why LSTM not enough for transformers

text: What we need: no sequence dependency. We need a model where: 1. Every word can directly look at every other word (no information loss through hidden states). 2. All words are processed in parallel (use all GPU cores). 3. Distance between words doesn't matter (word 1 and word 100 communicate equally well).
summary: Lists three requirements for the next architecture: direct any-to-any access, full parallelism, distance-independent communication.
queries: why every word must see every word

text: Chapter 4.4 introduces the Transformer from the 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need': it drops RNN and CNN entirely and uses attention so every word can directly look at every other word in parallel. A side-by-side with RNN on 'I love cats' shows sequential vs parallel. The core mechanism uses Query-Key-Value with attention scores (e.g., love→love=0.8, love→cats=0.5). Concrete benchmark: 1000 words takes 1000ms on RNN vs 1ms on Transformer (1000× faster). Attention also eliminates the vanishing gradient problem since information flows directly between any two words.
summary: Overview of Chapter 4.4: Transformer replaces RNN/CNN with parallel attention, solving speed and vanishing gradients.

text: How attention works: the core idea. Each word (Query) compares itself to every word (Keys) and asks 'how relevant are you?' The answers (attention scores) weight the values (word meanings) to produce a context-aware representation. Example with Query 'love': K=I score 0.1 (less relevant), K=love score 0.8 (self - very relevant), K=cats score 0.5 (object - moderately relevant). Key difference from RNN: this happens all at once for all words and all positions. No sequential dependency. Full parallelization.
summary: Explains Query-Key-Value attention with concrete scores and contrasts with RNN's sequential dependency.

text: Chapter 5.1 introduces the full Transformer architecture from the original paper. The encoder stack processes inputs through embedding plus positional encoding, then repeats Nx blocks of multi-head attention, add & norm, point-wise FFN, and add & norm with residual connections around each sub-layer. The decoder stack mirrors this but adds a masked self-attention sub-layer and a cross-attention sub-layer that pulls K and V from the encoder's output. On top of the decoder sits the Linear & Softmax output head producing token probabilities. The chapter sets up later zoom-ins on embeddings, positional encoding, attention, add & norm, and FFN.

text: The complete Transformer architecture, matching the original 'Attention Is All You Need' diagram. Two stacks side by side: ENCODER on the left and DECODER on the right. Residual skip connections wrap around the attention block and the FFN block. Decoder stack (xN repeats) bottom to top: Outputs (shifted right) -> Decoder Embedding -> circled-plus with Positional Encoding -> Masked Multi-Head Attention -> Add & Norm -> Multi-Head Attention (cross-attention) -> Add & Norm -> Point-wise FFN -> Add & Norm. Residual skips around all three sub-layers. The encoder's top output feeds into the decoder's cross-attention block as K and V (green arrow labeled 'K, V from encoder'). On top of the decoder sits the output head: Linear & SoftMax producing Output Probabilities.

text: Chapter 5.2 walks through how raw words get turned into the numerical vectors a Transformer can consume. Three stages: tokenization splits text into tokens (e.g., 'I love cats' becomes ['I','love','cats']; 'unbelievable' becomes ['un','believ','able']). Token-to-ID lookup maps each token to an arbitrary integer ID (e.g., 'I'->4, 'love'->6, 'cats'->2). The key step, ID-to-embedding, uses the ID to fetch a row from a learned embedding matrix - each row is 512 numbers representing meaning. Similar words end up with similar vectors. After this, every word is a 512-dim vector but still carries no position information, motivating positional encoding next.
summary: Three-stage pipeline (tokenize, ID lookup, embedding lookup) converts words into 512-dim learned vectors carrying meaning but not position.

text: Stage 3: ID to embedding (the KEY step). Each ID looks up a row in a learned matrix. Each row is 512 numbers representing that word's meaning. Examples: 'I' -> [0.12, -0.34, 0.78, ..., 0.91], 'love' -> [0.56, 0.23, -0.11, ..., 0.53], 'cats' -> [-0.45, 0.89, 0.33, ..., 0.67]. Similar words (cat/dog) get similar vectors. The embedding matrix is learned during training.

text: Chapter 5.3 - Positional Encoding, the Problem. Transformers process all tokens at once, so 'I love cats' and 'cats love I' produce the same set of embedding vectors and look identical to attention. Naive fixes fail: integer positions explode and overwhelm word meaning, pos/max_length depends on sentence length, and random vectors lose relative structure. The fix must give bounded values, a unique signature per position, and learnable relative distances - which sine and cosine waves provide.
summary: Chapter overview: transformers ignore word order, naive position schemes fail, and a good encoding must be bounded, unique per position, and preserve relative distance — motivating sine/cosine waves.

text:
The Transformer sees all words at the same time. It receives three embedding vectors simultaneously. Nothing tells it which is first, second, or third.

"I love cats" → [vec_I, vec_love, vec_cats]
"cats love I" → [vec_cats, vec_love, vec_I]

Same vectors, different order — but the model can't see order. Without a position signal, attention treats the input as an unordered set, so reversing or shuffling words gives the model the exact same view.
summary: Transformers process all tokens in parallel, so without an explicit position signal they cannot distinguish word order.

text:
Why simple solutions fail:

❌ Use [1,1,...], [2,2,...], [3,3,...]? Values grow forever. At position 1000, you add 1000 to every dimension - drowns out the word meaning.

❌ Use pos / max_length (0.0, 0.01, 0.02...)? Step size depends on sentence length. Model can't learn consistent patterns.

❌ Use random vectors? No relationship between positions. Can't learn 'pos 5 is near pos 6'.
summary: Three obvious position-encoding ideas — raw integers, normalized fractions, and random vectors — all fail because they explode, depend on sequence length, or destroy relative structure.

why bounded values matter in positional encoding

text:
We need: bounded values, unique per position, relative distances learnable. Solution: Sine and Cosine waves.

A good positional encoding must keep numbers in a fixed range so they don't drown out the embedding, give every position a distinct fingerprint, and let the model learn how far apart two positions are. Sine and cosine satisfy all three: outputs stay between -1 and +1, every position gets a unique pattern across many dimensions, and the smooth periodic structure makes relative distance learnable.
summary: A workable positional encoding must be bounded, unique per position, and preserve relative-distance structure — sine and cosine waves satisfy all three.

text: Chapter 5.4 introduces the sinusoidal positional encoding formula used in the Transformer. Even-indexed dimensions use sin(pos / 10000^(i/d_model)) and odd-indexed dimensions use cos(pos / 10000^(i/d_model)). pos is the position of the word, i is the dimension index, d_model is the embedding size (e.g. 512). The divisor 10000^(i/d_model) makes early dimensions oscillate fast and late dimensions oscillate slowly. Sin and cos keep values bounded in [-1, +1]. The clock analogy: fast seconds hand plus slow hours hand uniquely encodes time.
summary: Sinusoidal positional encoding formula with sin on even dims, cos on odd dims, frequency controlled by 10000^(i/d_model).

text: The Formula. Even dims (0,2,4...): sin( pos / 10000^(i/d_model) ). Odd dims (1,3,5...): cos( pos / 10000^(i/d_model) ).
summary: The sinusoidal positional encoding formula: sin for even dimensions, cos for odd dimensions, both divided by 10000^(i/d_model).

text: Each term in the formula explained: pos is the position of the word in the sentence (0, 1, 2, ...). i is which of the 512 dimensions we're computing. d_model is the size of the embedding (512). 10000^(i/d) controls wave speed - small i means fast, large i means slow. sin/cos output is always between -1 and +1, so it never explodes.
summary: Each variable in the positional encoding formula defined: pos, i, d_model, the 10000^(i/d) speed controller, and the bounded sin/cos output.

text: The KEY insight is the divisor 10000^(i/d_model). When i=0, divisor=1, so pos/1 = pos and the wave oscillates fast. When i=510, divisor is approximately 10000, so pos/10000 is tiny and the wave changes barely. This is like a clock: a fast seconds hand combined with a slow hours hand together give the exact time. Different dimensions oscillate at different speeds so that combining all 512 values produces a unique fingerprint per position.
summary: The 10000^(i/d_model) divisor makes early dimensions fast and late dimensions slow, like a clock combining seconds and hours hands.

text: Chapter 5.6 explains why positional encoding uses dimensions that oscillate at different speeds. Just like decimal counting uses ones, tens, and hundreds columns that change at different rates to give every number a unique fingerprint, positional encoding uses fast-changing early dimensions and slow-changing late dimensions. Using only the fast column repeats every 10 positions; using only the slow column makes 100 positions look identical. Combining all speeds gives every position a unique vector, and works for any sequence length.
summary: Why positional encoding mixes fast and slow oscillating dimensions, explained through the analogy of counting in ones, tens, and hundreds.

text: Think about how you count: ones, tens, hundreds. Each column changes at a different speed. The ones column changes every step, the tens column changes every 10 steps, the hundreds column changes every 100 steps. Counting through positions 0, 1, 2, 9, 10, 11, 99, 100, 101: the ones digit cycles fastest, tens cycles 10x slower, hundreds cycles 100x slower. Each column has its own job - ones distinguish neighbors, hundreds distinguish far-apart numbers.
summary: Decimal counting uses three columns that change at different speeds: ones change every step, tens every 10, hundreds every 100.
queries: how does counting in decimals work

text: What if you ONLY had the ones column? Position 0 = 0, position 1 = 1, position 2 = 2, ..., position 9 = 9, position 10 = 0 (same as pos 0!), position 11 = 1 (same as pos 1!). After 10 positions the ones column repeats. Position 0 and position 10 look identical. You can only count to 9. Stuck. A single fast-changing dimension cannot distinguish positions that are 10 or more apart.
summary: Using only the fast ones column makes positions wrap around every 10 steps, so position 0 and position 10 are indistinguishable.

text: What if you ONLY had the hundreds column? Position 0 = 0, position 1 = 0 (same!), position 2 = 0 (same!), ..., position 99 = 0 (still same!), position 100 = 1 (finally changes). Positions 0 through 99 all look identical - the hundreds column just shows 0 for all of them. You cannot tell neighboring words apart at all. A single slow-changing dimension cannot distinguish nearby positions.
summary: Using only the slow hundreds column makes the first 100 positions all look identical, hiding nearby distinctions.
queries: why does only slow column fail
what happens with only slow dimension positional encoding
why slow frequency cannot distinguish neighbors
why cant use only slow oscillation
slow dimension cannot tell neighbors apart

text: But COMBINE all three columns? Every position from 000 to 999 has a unique combination. That's 1000 unique positions using just 3 digits. Examples: pos 0 = 000, pos 1 = 001, pos 10 = 010, pos 11 = 011, pos 100 = 100, pos 101 = 101 - all unique. The fast column (ones) tells close positions apart (0 vs 1). The slow column (hundreds) tells distant positions apart (0 vs 100). Together, every position is unique.
summary: Combining fast, medium, and slow columns gives 1000 unique position fingerprints from just 3 digits, with fast columns separating neighbors and slow columns separating distant positions.
queries: why combining frequencies gives unique positions
how does combining digit columns make positions unique
why combine fast and slow positional encoding
why mix fast and slow oscillations
how do multiple speeds give unique fingerprint

text: This is EXACTLY what positional encoding does. Ones column (changes every step, repeats after 10) maps to fast dimensions (dim 0, 1) - sine wave changes fast, repeats quickly. Tens column (changes every 10 steps) maps to medium dimensions - sine wave at medium speed. Hundreds column (changes every 100 steps) maps to slow dimensions (dim 510, 511) - sine wave barely changes. Instead of 3 digits (0-9) giving 1000 unique positions, positional encoding uses 512 dimensions (sine waves -1 to +1), giving practically unlimited unique positions.
summary: The decimal counting analogy maps directly onto positional encoding: fast dimensions act like ones, slow dimensions like hundreds, and 512 sine waves give nearly unlimited unique positions.
queries: how does positional encoding match decimal counting

why dim 0 fast and dim 510 slow
how counting maps to sin cos encoding

text: That's the entire point of fast vs slow. Nothing more, nothing less. Different speeds exist so the combination is always unique, no matter how long the sentence is. Fast dimensions distinguish close-by positions, slow dimensions distinguish far-apart positions, and combining all 512 of them produces a unique vector for every position the model will ever see.
summary: Different oscillation speeds exist purely so that the combined positional encoding stays unique for any sentence length.

text: Chapter 5.7 (Positional Encoding - Final Addition): the embedding vector for each word is added element-wise to its sinusoidal positional encoding to produce the final input vector. A worked example for 'love' at position 1 shows embedding [0.56,0.23,-0.11,...] + PE [0.841,0.54,0.10,...] = final [1.40,0.77,-0.01,...]. The combined vector carries both meaning (embedding dominates) and position (small nudge). Four reasons this design is genius: bounded between -1 and +1, unique pattern per position across 512 dims, relative distances learnable via sin(a+b) identity, generalizes to longer sequences because sin/cos are smooth. The output is what enters the Transformer attention layers.
summary: Chapter-level summary of how embedding plus positional encoding produces the final transformer input and why the design works.

text: Four reasons sinusoidal positional encoding design is genius: 1) Bounded: sin/cos always between -1 and +1, so values never explode. 2) Unique: every position gets a unique pattern across 512 dimensions. 3) Relative distances: sin(a+b) is a linear combination of sin(a) and cos(a), so the model can learn relative positions. 4) Generalizes: works for longer sequences than seen during training because sin and cos are smooth and continuous.
summary: Lists the four key properties (bounded, unique, relative-distance-friendly, generalizable) that make sinusoidal positional encoding work.

text: Chapter 5.8 visualizes the full positional-encoding matrix as a heatmap: rows are positions, columns are embedding dimensions, color encodes the sin/cos value. The left columns flip fast (high frequency near 1.0) producing a checkerboard, the right columns barely move (frequency near 1/10000 = 0.0001) producing vertical stripes. Each row is a unique 128-number fingerprint for one position. Nearby positions share similar slow dimensions but differ in fast dimensions. The whole scheme is binary counting made smooth: fast dims act like low-order bits, slow dims like high-order bits, but with continuous values in [-1, +1] that give bounded, differentiable, geometrically meaningful position vectors.

text: Why the left side flips fast: each column uses its own frequency f_i = 1 / 10000^(2 · floor(i/2) / d). For small i (left side), the exponent is near 0, so f_i is close to 1.0. Each step in position moves the angle by about 1 radian (roughly 57 degrees), so sin and cos swing wildly. Worked example for column i = 0 with f = 1.0, computing sin(pos · 1.0) for positions 0 through 7: 0.00, 0.84, 0.91, 0.14, -0.76, -0.96, -0.28, 0.66. Every position is a very different number. That is the checkerboard on the left of the heatmap.
summary: Explains why low-index dimensions oscillate rapidly: their frequency is near 1.0 so each position step moves the angle ~1 radian.

text: Why the right side barely changes: for large i (right side), the exponent approaches 1, so f_i approaches 1 / 10000 = 0.0001. Across 40 positions the angle only moves by 40 · 0.0001 = 0.004 radians (about 0.23 degrees), so sin and cos barely move. Worked example for column i = 127 with f ≈ 1/10000, computing cos(pos · 0.0001) for positions 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 39: all values stay near 1.0000. Every position looks nearly identical here, producing the solid blue stripe on the far right. These slow dimensions encode very long-range position information: two tokens 1000 positions apart differ meaningfully in the slow columns even though they look identical across 40 positions.
summary: Explains why high-index dimensions barely change: their frequency ~0.0001 moves the angle by only ~0.004 radians across 40 positions, encoding very long-range positions.

text: Every position is a unique fingerprint. Pick any single row of the heatmap: that row is exactly the positional encoding vector for that position, 128 numbers acting as a unique barcode. Position 5 and position 6 are neighbors; their left-side cells (fast dims) differ strongly, but their right-side cells (slow dims) look identical because the slow dims barely moved. Position 25 looks very different from both on the left, but still nearly identical on the far right. This is exactly the property we want: each position gets a unique vector, AND nearby positions have similar vectors. The model can learn both 'which position is this' and 'how far apart are these two positions.'

text: Binary counting: the same idea, one bit at a time. Count in binary: every integer gets a unique bit pattern, and the bits flip at different speeds. Bit 0 (rightmost, fast) flips every step: 1, 0, 1, 0, .... Bit 1 flips every 2 steps, bit 2 every 4 steps, bit 3 (leftmost, slow) every 8 steps. Direct mapping to sin/cos encoding: bit 0 corresponds to fast dim 0 (f = 1.0) oscillating every step; bit 3 corresponds to slow dim 127 (f = 0.0001) barely moving. Binary values are 0 or 1 (discrete); sin/cos values are any number in [-1, +1] (smooth). Each binary number is a unique bit pattern; each position is a unique sin/cos signature. Sin/cos positional encoding is essentially binary counting made smooth and continuous: fast dimensions play the role of low-order bits, slow dimensions play the role of high-order bits. Using sin/cos instead of 0/1 gives nice geometric relationships - dot products capture relative distance, values stay bounded, and the encoding is differentiable so gradients flow through it cleanly during training.
summary: Sin/cos positional encoding is binary counting made smooth: fast dims are low-order bits, slow dims are high-order bits, with continuous bounded differentiable values.

text: RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) replaces additive sinusoidal position encoding by rotating each Q and K vector by an angle equal to position times frequency. Vectors are processed in 2D pairs, each pair rotating at its own frequency f_i = 1 / 10000^(2i/d) - fast pairs encode nearby distance, slow pairs encode long-range distance. Because rotation matrices are orthogonal, the absolute rotation cancels in the Q·K dot product, leaving only the relative position gap (n-m). This gives the model true relative position for free with zero parameters, preserves vector length, and supports extrapolation via NTK-aware frequency scaling. RoPE is used by LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, GPT-NeoX, and PaLM, and is the default position encoding in modern LLMs.
summary: Chapter overview: RoPE encodes position by rotating Q and K in pairs at multiple frequencies so attention sees only the relative gap, enabling extrapolation and zero extra parameters.
queries: Why use RoPE instead of sinusoidal

text: Recall chapters 5.3-5.7: sinusoidal positional encoding computes a position vector using sin/cos and ADDS it to each embedding, giving every token a unique position signal. But it has two fundamental problems. Problem 1: Absolute positioning. Position 5 always gets the same encoding vector regardless of context. 'Cats' at position 5 in a 10-word sentence and 'cats' at position 5 in a 1000-word sentence get identical position signals, even though relative context differs. Problem 2: Fixed length. If we train on sequences up to 2048 tokens, position 2049 has never been seen by the model. Performance degrades badly because the model cannot extrapolate to longer contexts. RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) solves both problems with a single elegant idea.
summary: Sinusoidal positional encoding fails because it is absolute and cannot extrapolate beyond training sequence length, motivating RoPE.

text: Picture every word's Query and Key vector as a clock hand. RoPE doesn't ADD anything to it - it TURNS the hand by an angle equal to position times frequency. Position 1 is a small turn, position 10 is bigger, position 100 is huge. No new numbers bolted on, no dimensions inflated - the hand just points somewhere else. Example with Q = [0.8, 0.6] and frequency f = 0.3 rad per step: pos 0 angle 0.0 gives [0.800, 0.600]; pos 1 angle 0.3 gives [0.587, 0.810]; pos 2 angle 0.6 gives [0.321, 0.947]; pos 3 angle 0.9 gives [0.027, 1.000]. Key property: turning preserves length. All four rotated vectors keep the same length (1.0 here). Rotation only changes direction, not length. Sinusoidal PE adds a position vector that changes both. The 2D rotation formula: R(t) · [q1, q2] = [q1 cos(t) - q2 sin(t), q1 sin(t) + q2 cos(t)] where t = position · frequency.
summary: RoPE rotates each Q and K vector by an angle proportional to its position, preserving length while encoding position as direction.

text: The attention score between two words is the dot product of their clock hands, and the dot product depends only on the angle between them. Same angle = same score, regardless of where the hands point absolutely. RoPE spins both Q and K by their own positions, so the common spin cancels and only the gap (position difference) survives. Example: Near scenario - Q at position 3, K at position 0, Q spun 0.9 rad, K spun 0 rad, gap = 0.9 rad, score = cos(0.9) ≈ 0.62. Far scenario - Q at position 10, K at position 7, Q spun 3.0 rad, K spun 2.1 rad, gap = 0.9 rad, score = cos(0.9) ≈ 0.62. Same answer. Turntable intuition: glue both Q and K to the same spinning turntable; the relative angle between them never changes regardless of absolute rotation. The model gets relative position for free, with zero extra parameters. Algebra: (R(m·f) Q) · (R(n·f) K) = Q · R((n-m)·f) K because rotation matrices are orthogonal so R(a)ᵀ R(b) = R(b-a). Only the gap (n-m) survives. For unit vectors at angular gap Δ, the dot product equals cos(Δ).
summary: Because rotation cancels in the dot product, RoPE makes attention scores depend only on the relative position gap between Q and K.
queries: Why does RoPE encode relative position

text: Real embeddings have 128 dimensions or more, not 2. RoPE handles this by processing dimensions in PAIRS, and each pair rotates at its own frequency - fast frequencies for early pairs, slow ones for late pairs. A 128-dim vector splits into 64 pairs: (d0, d1) fast, (d2, d3) fast, (d4, d5) medium, ..., (d124, d125) slow, (d126, d127) very slow. Each pair gets its own 2D rotation. Early pairs (d0, d1) rotate FAST: high frequency, sensitive to nearby position differences ('is the next word 1 or 2 positions away?'). Late pairs (d126, d127) rotate SLOW: low frequency, sensitive to distant position differences ('is this word 500 or 1000 positions away?'). The frequency formula matches sinusoidal PE: f_i = 1 / 10000^(2i/d), where i is pair index and d is total dimension. Pair 0 rotates fastest, pair 63 rotates slowest. After 10 position steps: pair 0 has spun 2.5 rad (143 deg), pair 10 spun 0.5 rad (29 deg), pair 60 spun 0.05 rad (3 deg).
summary: RoPE splits the embedding into 64 pairs and rotates each pair at a different frequency, fast pairs encode nearby positions and slow pairs encode distant ones.
queries: How does RoPE work in 128 dimensions

text: Why RoPE won. Comparison table: Sinusoidal is absolute, applied to embedding by addition, fixed at training, fails beyond max length, 0 parameters. Learned PE is absolute, added to embedding, fixed at training, fails beyond max, has pos × dim parameters. RoPE is relative, applied to Q and K only by rotation, can extrapolate, works with scaling tricks, 0 parameters. NTK-aware scaling: a model trained on 4096 tokens can handle 16384 by scaling rotation frequencies. The NTK-aware variant adjusts different frequency bands differently - slowing down fast-rotating pairs while keeping slow ones nearly unchanged. Extends context length 4× or more with minimal quality loss. Models using RoPE: LLaMA, LLaMA 2, LLaMA 3, Mistral, Qwen, GPT-NeoX, PaLM. Nearly every modern open-source LLM uses RoPE. It became the default choice for position encoding since 2022.
summary: RoPE wins because it gives relative position with zero parameters, applies only to Q and K, and extrapolates to longer contexts via NTK-aware frequency scaling, making it the default for modern LLMs.

text: Chapter 6.1 sets up the core problem attention solves: word embeddings are static, so the same word gets the exact same vector regardless of surrounding words. The chapter uses 'bank' (river bank vs financial bank) and 'love' ('I love cats' vs 'love is blind') to show meaning depends on context. The goal of attention: take a fixed embedding like love = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1] and transform it into a NEW vector that represents the word in its specific context, by letting each word look around, find which other words are relevant, and absorb information from them.
summary: Why attention is needed: embeddings are context-free, but word meaning depends on neighbors.

text: This isn't just about 'bank' - it's about every word. The model has a big dictionary (learned during training) that maps every word to a fixed list of numbers. Take 'love': after embedding, 'love' always gets the same numbers, no matter what sentence it's in. But 'love' in 'I love cats' is different from 'love' in 'love is blind.' These numbers capture the word's meaning in isolation. It has no idea what words are around it. That's a problem - because we need context. The goal: take 'love' = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1] and transform it into a NEW list of numbers that represents 'love in the context of I and cats.'

text: This is what Attention solves. It lets each word look around at the other words and ask 'which other words are relevant to me?' and then absorb context — absorbing information from the relevant ones. After attention: 'love' in 'I love cats' absorbs info from 'I' and 'cats' → now represents 'affection from me toward cats'. Same input vector, different output vector depending on context.

chapterTitle: How Does a Word Look At Others?
text: Chapter 6.2 — How Does a Word Look At Others? Attention mimics how humans read: when seeing 'it', your brain scans for what it refers to, focusing on nouns rather than every word equally. Attention computes a relevance score for every other word in the sentence. Worked example uses 'The cat sat because it was tired' — when 'it' is selected, 'cat' receives 55% attention (highest), so the model figures out 'it' = 'cat'. The chapter ends by motivating the dot product as the mathematical tool to measure relevance between two vectors.

text: Chapter 6.3 introduces the dot product as the mathematical tool that measures similarity between two vectors. Given vectors A and B, multiply matching pairs and sum: e.g. A=[1,2,3], B=[4,5,6] gives 1x4 + 2x5 + 3x6 = 4 + 10 + 18 = 32. The resulting single number reflects directional similarity: vectors pointing the same way produce a large positive number, opposite directions produce a large negative number, and perpendicular (unrelated) vectors produce a value near zero. Attention needs this tool to score relevance between words. However, the chapter ends by warning that we cannot dot-product raw word embeddings directly: words like 'I' (pronoun) and 'love' (verb) are related yet have dissimilar raw meanings, so we must compare them on a different basis - this motivates Query, Key, and Value.
summary: Dot product multiplies pairs and sums to get one number measuring directional similarity, the math tool attention uses for relevance scoring.

text: Given two vectors, multiply pairs and sum. A = [1, 2, 3], B = [4, 5, 6]. Multiply each pair: 1x4 = 4, 2x5 = 10, 3x6 = 18. Sum them up: 4 + 10 + 18 = 32. That's it - multiply pairs, add them up, get one number.

text: What does this number mean? It measures how similar two vectors are in direction. Think of you and a friend both pointing somewhere. Same direction (-> ->): large positive value, 'these are similar!' Opposite directions (-> <-): large negative value, 'these are opposites!' Perpendicular and unrelated (-> up): close to zero, 'nothing in common.' The dot product captures directional alignment between vectors.
summary: Explains the geometric meaning of the dot product as a directional alignment score: positive=same way, negative=opposite, ~zero=perpendicular.

We need to compare them on a different basis. This sets up why we transform embeddings into Query, Key, and Value before computing the dot product.

chapterTitle: Why Not Dot Product Embeddings Directly?
text: Chapter 6.4 explains why raw word embeddings cannot be dot-producted directly to measure attention relevance. Words like 'I' (pronoun) and 'love' (verb) have different raw meanings so a direct dot product is low, yet they are strongly related ('I' is the one loving). The fix: compare on a different basis - what each word is LOOKING FOR vs what each word OFFERS. 'love' looks for 'who is doing the loving?' (a subject, a person), and 'I' offers 'I am a subject, a person' - HIGH MATCH. This motivates needing three separate views of each word: Query, Key, Value.
summary: Why raw embedding dot products fail and why words need separate Query/Key/Value views.

chapterTitle: Why Not Dot Product Embeddings Directly?

chapterTitle: Q, K, V - The Classroom Analogy
text: Chapter 6.5 introduces the classroom analogy for Query, Key, and Value in attention. Imagine a classroom of 30 students. Student Riya has a question: 'who has yesterday's notes?' She looks around at everyone. Riya's question is the Query (what am I looking for?). Each other student's label or advertisement (e.g., Priya: 'I know the homework', Aman: 'I have yesterday's notes', Rahul: 'I know the canteen menu') is a Key (what can I be found for?). Aman matches, so he hands over the actual notes - physics equations, dates, diagrams - that real content is the Value (here's my actual info). Mapping back to attention: Query asks the question, Key advertises matchability, Value carries the real content delivered after a match.
summary: Classroom analogy maps Query to Riya's question, Key to each student's label, and Value to the actual notes handed over.

chapterTitle: Every Word is Both Asker and Answerer
text: Chapter 6.6 - Every Word is Both Asker and Answerer. Core idea: in self-attention, every token produces a Query, a Key, and a Value simultaneously. Using the classroom analogy, Riya, Aman, and Priya each have their own question (Query), their own label of what they offer (Key), and their own actual information (Value). The same is true for words in a Transformer: in "The cat sat because it was tired", "cat" asks its own question while also advertising itself as a noun-subject and carrying its own semantic content. This dual role is what makes self-attention symmetric across tokens, and motivates the next chapter's question of why Key and Value must remain separate.
summary: Chapter-level summary: in self-attention every token simultaneously plays asker (Q), advertiser (K), and information-carrier (V).

chapterTitle: Why Can't Key and Value Be the Same?
text: Chapter 6.7 explains why Key and Value must be separate vectors in attention. Uses a restaurant analogy: the menu description is the Key (what helps you match your craving to a dish), the actual food on the plate is the Value (what you actually consume). If Key and Value were the same, you would get a plate with the menu description printed on it - useless. Connecting back to the Transformer with 'The cat sat because it was tired': cat's Key encodes 'I'm a noun, subject, animate' which helps 'it' find cat; cat's Value carries the rich semantic content (small, furry, four-legged, alive, domesticated) that 'it' actually absorbs. Key is optimized for matching, Value is optimized for content delivery.
summary: Why Key and Value must be separate: Key is for matching (menu description), Value is the actual content (food on plate).

chapterTitle: Why Can't Key and Value Be the Same?

This helped you decide - yes, this matches what I'm craving. It's optimized for matching your preference (your Query) to the right dish. The actual food on your plate = Value: the smoky charred paneer cubes, the creamy gravy, the aroma, the nourishment. This is the actual content you receive. What you actually consume.

chapterTitle: Why Can't Key and Value Be the Same?
text: Now connect this back to the Transformer. Take the sentence: 'The cat sat because it was tired.' The word 'it' needs to find what it refers to. It finds 'cat'. cat's Key (the menu description) encodes: 'I'm a noun, I'm a subject, I'm animate' - this is what helped 'it' find 'cat' in the first place. Optimized for matching. cat's Value (the actual food) carries: rich semantic content - small, furry, four-legged, alive, domesticated - this is the actual information that 'it' absorbs after finding the match. Key helped 'it' FIND 'cat' (menu description). Value is what 'it' actually GOT from 'cat' (the actual food).

text: Another way to see Q, K, V is Google Search, broken into 4 steps. Step 1: Your search query, e.g. "best pizza near me" = Query. chapterTitle: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: Chapter 6.9 explains how Q, K, V vectors are created from a word's embedding. Each word starts as a single embedding vector (e.g., "love" = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1]). To get three different views (Query, Key, Value), the embedding is multiplied by three different W matrices: W_Q, W_K, W_V. Each W matrix is just a grid of numbers (a table/spreadsheet). Multiplying input by a table produces an output. Analogy: a photo with three filters - each filter highlights a different feature (eyes, nose, mouth). For real Transformers (GPT-style), embedding is 512 numbers, each W matrix is 512×64 = 32,768 numbers, producing 64-number Q/K/V vectors per head.
summary: How Q, K, V vectors are produced by multiplying a word's embedding through three learned weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V.

chapterTitle: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: So far, Q, K, V are just concepts in our head. We know each word needs: Query - "what am I looking for?", Key - "what do I offer?", Value - "what info do I carry?". But each word starts as just one list of numbers - its embedding. For example: "love" = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1]. That's one list. We need three different lists from it (Query, Key, Value). How do you get three different things from one thing? You transform it three different ways. And the tool for each transformation is: a grid of numbers.

chapterTitle: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: The grid of numbers is called a W matrix (W = "weights"). It's just a table of numbers. Like a spreadsheet. Rows and columns filled with numbers. Example: a tiny W grid: 0.02, -0.15, 0.31, 0.08, 0.41, 0.07, -0.22, -0.19, -0.09, 0.33, 0.14, 0.27, 0.18, -0.05, 0.42, 0.11. When you multiply a word's embedding by this grid, you get a new, smaller list of numbers - the Query (or Key, or Value, depending on which grid). Multiply input by a table → get an output. Nothing more complicated than that.
summary: Defines a W matrix as just a table of weights; multiplying an embedding by it produces a Q, K, or V vector.

chapterTitle: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: Think of it like a photo with three filters. You have a photo of a person and want three different views. View 1: highlight only the eyes → captures where the person is looking. View 2: highlight only the nose → captures the nose shape. View 3: highlight only the mouth → captures the expression. How do you create these views? Apply three different filters. Each filter is a grid of numbers that, when multiplied with the photo, produces a different view. That's EXACTLY what W_Q, W_K, W_V are. Word embedding: [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] ← the "photo". Grid 1 (W_Q): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what am I looking for?". Grid 2 (W_K): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what do I offer?". Grid 3 (W_V): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what info do I carry?". Multiply the same embedding by Grid 1 → Query (2-number list). By Grid 2 → Key. By Grid 3 → Value.
summary: Photo-with-three-filters analogy: each W matrix is a filter highlighting a different aspect of the same embedding to produce Q, K, or V.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: Chapter 6.10 explains why Q, K, V are smaller than the embedding. W_Q is a rectangular 4x2 (or 512x64 in real GPT) projection that down-projects the embedding into a compressed query space. The embedding itself keeps its full d_model size and continues through the network; Q, K, V are temporary lenses used only inside attention. The smaller size exists because multi-head attention splits a fixed output budget across many parallel heads: d_k = d_model / num_heads. After all heads finish, their outputs concatenate back to d_model and pass through W_O into the residual stream.
summary: Chapter overview: Q/K/V are smaller than the embedding because multi-head attention divides d_model across heads, with d_k = d_model / num_heads.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: The puzzle: why is W_Q shaped 4x2, not 4x4? In the prior chapter W_Q was written as a 4x2 grid. If the embedding has 4 numbers, shouldn't the query also have 4 numbers? Short answer: no. Q (and K and V) are smaller than the embedding. W_Q's job is to project the 4-number embedding down to a 2-number query. The input and output sizes do not have to match. Concrete shapes: embedding [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] (4 numbers) times W_Q (4x2 matrix) = query [0.3, 0.7] (2 numbers). 4 rows = input size, 2 columns = output size.
summary: Sets up why W_Q is a rectangular 4x2 projection rather than square, mapping a 4-number embedding down to a 2-number query.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: The embedding stays big. Q, K, V are small views. Think of the embedding as the word's full profile - it stays 4 numbers long for the entire journey through the Transformer. Q, K, V are three compressed lenses on that profile, built only for the attention step. Three different W matrices (W_Q, W_K, W_V), three different 2-number views. Example: embedding [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] becomes Query [0.3, 0.7], Key [0.9, 0.4], Value [0.6, -0.2]. The 4-number embedding itself is untouched; it continues through the rest of the network in its full original size.
summary: Explains that the embedding keeps its full size through the Transformer while Q, K, V are temporary compressed projections only used inside attention.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: Why smaller? Multi-head attention splits the budget. The Transformer runs several heads in parallel on the same embedding. Each head gets its own W_Q, W_K, W_V and works independently, looking at the embedding through its own 2-number lens. Two heads, each with W_Q (4x2). Each produces a different 2-number Query because W_Q1 and W_Q2 learned different things. Example: Head 1 Q1 = [0.3, 0.7], Head 2 Q2 = [-0.4, 0.2]. After both heads do their attention, outputs are concatenated back to 4 numbers total (2 + 2) and fed forward. Budget intuition: fixed 4 output numbers per token. Options: one big head with 4-number Q, or two small heads each with 2-number Q. Multi-head picks the second: more heads, each specialized. Formula: d_k = d_model / num_heads.
summary: Reveals that Q/K/V are small because the embedding budget is split across multiple parallel attention heads, with d_k = d_model / num_heads.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: Real Transformer numbers (GPT-style). Scale everything up. The chapter's 4 becomes 512. The chapter's 2 becomes 64. The chapter's 2 heads become 8. This chapter (teaching): d_model = 4 (embedding size), num_heads = 2, d_k = 4 / 2 = 2 (Q/K/V per head), W_Q per head = 4 x 2. Real GPT (typical): d_model = 512 (embedding size), num_heads = 8, d_k = 512 / 8 = 64 (Q/K/V per head), W_Q per head = 512 x 64. 8 heads x 64 numbers each = 512 numbers total, which fits exactly back into the embedding size. The budget is preserved across heads.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: Shapes recap. Embedding: d_model (4 here, 512 in real) - lives through the whole Transformer. Q, K, V (per head): d_k (2 here, 64 in real) - temporary, only inside attention. W_Q, W_K, W_V (per head): d_model x d_k (4x2 here, 512x64 in real) - projects embedding to Q / K / V. Multi-head concat + W_O: back to d_model (4 here, 512 in real) - returns to the main residual stream. Bottom line: W_Q is not square. It's a rectangular projection from embedding space into a smaller query space. The embedding keeps its original size; the query is a compressed view used only for the attention computation.
summary: Recap table of every shape in attention: embedding stays d_model, Q/K/V are d_k per head, W matrices are d_model by d_k, concat plus W_O returns to d_model.

text: Chapter 6.11 — W Matrices: Learned During Training. The Q/K/V projection matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V are not designed by humans. They start as random numbers on day 1. Training repeats billions of times: model predicts next word, computes error, error flows backward through every grid entry via backpropagation, each number gets nudged a tiny amount (e.g. -0.09 → -0.07). After billions of nudges, the random noise settles into meaningful values: W_Q produces Queries ("what am I looking for?"), W_K produces Keys ("what do I offer?"), W_V produces Values ("what info do I carry?"). Nobody programmed the entries; they emerged from gradient descent.
summary: W_Q, W_K, W_V start random and are nudged by backpropagation over billions of examples until they produce meaningful Q, K, V projections.

text: Day 1: Start with random garbage. Before training begins, every grid is filled with random numbers from a random number generator. Visual shows three 4×4 grids labeled W_Q (random), W_K (random), W_V (random) with values like 0.47, -0.82, 0.15, -0.63, 0.91, -0.24, 0.38, -0.71, 0.56, -0.09, 0.77, -0.45, -0.33, 0.62, -0.88, 0.19. With random grids the model computes: random W_Q × embedding = garbage Query; random W_K × embedding = garbage Key; random W_V × embedding = garbage Value. Garbage Q, K, V → garbage attention → garbage predictions. The model is terrible at this point. And that's completely fine.
summary: Before training, W_Q, W_K, W_V are filled with random numbers, so the resulting Q, K, V vectors and predictions are nonsense — and that's the expected starting point.

text: The training loop: predict, fail, nudge. Model reads millions of sentences and tries to predict the next word each time. Steps every attempt: 1) Model sees: "The cat sat on the". 2) Uses its current (bad) grids to compute attention. 3) Predicts next word: "elephant" (wrong! should be "mat"). 4) Calculates error: how wrong was that prediction? 5) Error flows backward through every grid number. 6) Each number gets nudged slightly to reduce the error. This loop repeats billions of times. Every repetition nudges the numbers a tiny bit closer to useful values.
summary: Six-step training loop where the model predicts, computes error, propagates it backward, and nudges every W matrix entry — repeated billions of times.

text: Zoom in: what does one nudge look like? Look at a single number in W_Q, row 3 column 2. Before: -0.09. After: -0.07. Change: +0.02. Reason: the error signal says "if this number were slightly larger, the Query for 'cat' would have paid more attention to 'sat' — and the prediction would have been a little better." That's it. Just +0.02. A tiny nudge. But multiply that by every number in every grid, across billions of training examples, and the grids slowly transform from random noise into something meaningful.
summary: Concrete example of one weight update: W_Q row 3 col 2 moves from -0.09 to -0.07 because the error signal says a slightly larger value would have improved the prediction.

text: After billions of examples: the grids have settled. The random numbers have been nudged millions of times into meaningful values. Visual: BEFORE (random) W_Q with values 0.47, -0.82, 0.15, -0.63, 0.91, -0.24, 0.38, -0.71, 0.56, -0.09, 0.77, -0.45, -0.33, 0.62, -0.88, 0.19 → AFTER (trained) W_Q with values 0.02, -0.15, 0.31, 0.08, 0.41, 0.07, -0.22, -0.19, -0.09, 0.33, 0.14, 0.27, 0.18, -0.05, 0.42, 0.11. Result: W_Q now produces Queries that correctly capture "what am I looking for?"; W_K produces Keys that capture "what do I offer?"; W_V produces Values that capture "what info do I carry?".

text: The key insight: nobody programmed these numbers. No human sat down and said "row 3, column 2 should be 0.14." The numbers emerged from the nudging process (backpropagation) over billions of examples. This is what "learned" means in machine learning. The model figured out, on its own, what numbers to put in each grid so that the resulting Q, K, V vectors produce useful attention patterns. Random noise in → billions of nudges → meaningful grids out.
summary: Defines what "learned" means: the W matrix entries emerge from backpropagation, not human design — random noise becomes meaningful through billions of small updates.

text: Chapter 6.12 traces a complete attention example end-to-end with concrete numbers. Sentence: "The cat sat". Embeddings: "The"=[0.1,0.8,-0.2,0.5], "cat"=[0.7,-0.3,0.6,0.1], "sat"=[-0.4,0.5,0.2,0.9]. For "cat", multiplying its embedding by W_Q, W_K, W_V produces Q_cat=[0.4,0.8] (looking for verbs/actions), K_cat=[0.9,0.3] (offering: noun, subject, animal), V_cat=[0.6,-0.2] (semantic content). For "sat", Q_sat=[0.2,0.7] (looking for a subject). Attention score = Q_sat dot K_cat = (0.2x0.9)+(0.7x0.3) = 0.18+0.21 = 0.39, a high match. "sat" absorbs cat's Value and learns "I'm an action performed by a cat."
summary: Walks through a complete attention computation between "sat" and "cat" using real numbers for embeddings, Q, K, V, and dot-product score.

chapterTitle: Step 1 - Compute Q, K, V for Every Word
text: Let's multiply 'I''s embedding by all three matrices. 'I' = [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8]. Each dim of the embedding multiplies the corresponding row of W, then we sum. For W_Q: dim 1 = (1.0 x 0.15) + (0.5 x 0.12) + (-0.3 x 0.10) + (0.8 x 0.78) = 0.8; dim 2 = (1.0 x -0.03) + (0.5 x 0.78) + (-0.3 x 0.51) + (0.8 x -0.01) = 0.2; Q_I = [0.8, 0.2]. For W_K: dim 1 = (1.0 x -0.12) + (0.5 x 0.51) + (-0.3 x 0.50) + (0.8 x 0.39) = 0.3; dim 2 = (1.0 x 0.16) + (0.5 x 0.37) + (-0.3 x 0.22) + (0.8 x 0.53) = 0.7; K_I = [0.3, 0.7]. For W_V: dim 1 = (1.0 x 0.59) + (0.5 x 0.32) + (-0.3 x -0.21) + (0.8 x 0.24) = 1.0; dim 2 = (1.0 x -0.49) + (0.5 x 0.64) + (-0.3 x 0.77) + (0.8 x -0.13) = -0.5; V_I = [1.0, -0.5]. 'love' and 'cats' go through the exact same three matrices - different embeddings in, different Q/K/V out.

text: Step 2: Compute attention scores. Each word's Query asks every Key 'how relevant are you to me?' Example with Q_I = [0.8, 0.2]. Dot product Q_I with every word's Key. Q_I · K_I where K_I = [0.3, 0.7]: (0.8×0.3) + (0.2×0.7) = 0.24 + 0.14 = 0.38 (lowest). Q_I · K_love where K_love = [0.6, 0.4]: (0.8×0.6) + (0.2×0.4) = 0.48 + 0.08 = 0.56 (medium). Q_I · K_cats where K_cats = [0.8, 0.5]: (0.8×0.8) + (0.2×0.5) = 0.64 + 0.10 = 0.74 (highest). From I's perspective: 'cats' most relevant, 'love' medium, 'I' itself lowest.

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: Chapter 7.3 explains why attention uses K transpose in the formula score = Q · K^T. T is not an exponent but the transpose operation that flips rows to columns. Without transposing K, Q (3×2) and K (3×2) cannot multiply because inner dimensions don't match. Transposing K to 2×3 makes (3×2) × (2×3) valid, producing a 3×3 score matrix where each cell is the dot product of a Query with a Key - the relevance score between two words. A classroom analogy (questions vs expertise) and a 3×3 example table reinforce the idea.
summary: Chapter 7.3 shows that transposing K aligns matrix shapes so Q · K^T yields a word-by-word relevance score matrix.

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: Transpose means rows become columns, columns become rows. No math changes - just a flip. Original K is 3 rows × 2 cols with row 1 = K_I [0.3, 0.7], row 2 = K_love [0.6, 0.4], row 3 = K_cats [0.8, 0.5]. After transpose K^T is 2 rows × 3 cols with col 1 = K_I, col 2 = K_love, col 3 = K_cats. Same numbers, same order within each word's vector, just rotated.
summary: Visual flip showing a 3×2 K matrix becoming a 2×3 K^T - rows become columns with identical values.
queries: what happens when you transpose K

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: The problem: shapes don't match. We want every Query to compute a dot product with every Key. Try Q · K directly: Q is 3×2 (3 words, 2 dims), K is 3×2 (3 words, 2 dims). 3×2 times 3×2 doesn't work. Matrix multiplication rule: the inner dimensions must match. (3×2) × (3×2) → 2 ≠ 3. Broken.
summary: Direct Q · K fails because the inner dimensions of two 3×2 matrices don't align for multiplication.
queries: why can't we multiply Q times K directly
why does Q K fail without transpose
shape error multiplying Q and K
why do shapes break in attention

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: The fix: transpose K, then multiply. Q is 3×2 (3 words, 2 dims). K^T is 2×3 (2 dims, 3 words). Q × K^T: (3×2) × (2×3) → inner dims both 2. Works! Result is a 3×3 score matrix - every word → every word. Exactly what we need.
summary: Transposing K to 2×3 lets (3×2) × (2×3) yield a 3×3 score matrix covering every word pair.

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: What does Q · K^T actually compute? Each cell in the 3×3 result = the dot product of one Query with one Key. That's the relevance score. Example scores: Q_I row [0.38, 0.56, 0.74] (best with K_cats), Q_love row [0.66, 0.42, 0.53] (best with K_I), Q_cats row [0.57, 0.54, 0.7] (best with K_cats). Row = one word asking. Column = one word answering. Cell = how relevant the answer-word is to the asker.
summary: Each cell of Q · K^T is the dot product of a Query with a Key - the relevance score for that word pair.

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: Classroom seating chart analogy: Q (Queries) = each student's question list - 3 students, each with a question that has 2 traits (topic, urgency). K (Keys) = each student's expertise card - same 3 students, each has expertise described by 2 traits. Q · K^T = the compatibility chart - every student's question matched against every student's expertise → a 3×3 grid of 'how well can you help me?'. The transpose makes this matching possible. Without it, shapes won't fit.

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: K^T in one sentence: K^T is just K with its rows and columns swapped - so that Q · K^T produces a score for every word pair. Quick recap: What is T? Transpose - flip rows ↔ columns. Why do it? So the matrix shapes align for multiplication. Does K change? No - same numbers, just rearranged. What's the result? A word×word score matrix (every pair gets a score).
summary: Recap: K^T flips rows and columns so Q · K^T aligns shapes and yields a word-by-word score matrix.

text: Mathematical notation diagram of the softmax formula. Left side: σ(z)_i. Right side: a fraction with numerator e^(z_i) labeled 'this word's e^score' and denominator Σ_{j=1}^{K} e^(z_j) labeled 'sum of all e^scores'. The diagram visually shows the exponential of one score divided by the sum of exponentials over all K scores, converting raw attention scores to probabilities.
summary: Diagram of the softmax formula as a fraction: e^(z_i) over the sum of e^(z_j) across all K positions.

text: Chapter 7.4 explains why attention needs softmax. Raw dot product scores can be negative or sum oddly, so plain division produces negative or over-100% weights. Softmax solves this in two steps: exponentiate (e^score) to make every value positive, then divide by the sum so weights add to 1. The formula is σ(z)_i = e^(z_i) / Σ_j e^(z_j). The exponential also amplifies the largest score, making the most relevant word dominate, but if scores are too large softmax saturates - motivating the next chapter's sqrt(d_k) scaling.
summary: Chapter overview: softmax turns raw attention scores into a valid probability distribution that sums to 1 while amplifying the winner.
queries: purpose of softmax in transformer attention
why softmax instead of simple division
from dot product to attention weights

text: After dot products in attention, we have raw scores. Example: I→I = 0.38, I→love = 0.56, I→cats = 0.74. Now we need to use these scores to blend the Values: output = ??? × V_I + ??? × V_love + ??? × V_cats. What should those ??? weights be? Can we just plug in the raw scores directly?
summary: Sets up the problem: raw attention scores from dot products need to be turned into blending weights for the Values.

text: Budget analogy: You have ₹100 to split among three friends based on helpfulness. Helpfulness scores: Riya=38, Aman=56, Priya=74. You can't give ₹38 + ₹56 + ₹74 = ₹168 because you only have ₹100. You need to convert scores into shares of ₹100 - percentages adding to 100%. Total = 38 + 56 + 74 = 168. Riya: 38/168 = 23% → ₹23. Aman: 56/168 = 33% → ₹33. Priya: 74/168 = 44% → ₹44. Total ₹100. That's basically what softmax does - convert raw scores into shares adding to 100%. But with one extra trick.
summary: Uses a ₹100 budget split between three friends to explain why scores need to be normalized into shares adding to 100%.
queries: why do attention weights add up to 1
dividing money based on scores analogy
what does softmax do in plain English

text: The problem: dot products CAN be negative. Example scores: word_A = -3, word_B = 1, word_C = 5. Simple division by total: total = -3 + 1 + 5 = 3. A: -3/3 = -100% (NEGATIVE - can't pay minus attention!). B: 1/3 = 33%. C: 5/3 = 167% (more than 100% - nonsense!). Simple division is broken when negatives are present. We need something better.
summary: Shows why plain division fails: negative dot products give negative or over-100% shares.
queries: why can't we just divide scores by sum

text: The Solution: Softmax. Before dividing, put each score through e^score (e ≈ 2.718). This function makes ANY number positive. Formula: σ(z)_i = e^(z_i) / Σ_{j=1}^{K} e^(z_j). The numerator is this word's e^score. The denominator is the sum of e^scores over all K words. In plain English: take e to the power of this score, then divide by the sum of e to the power of every score.
summary: Introduces the softmax formula σ(z)_i = e^(z_i) / Σ e^(z_j) and explains the role of the exponential.
queries: sigma z i equals e z over sum e z
e to the power of z_i divided by sum

text: Why e^x? It's ALWAYS positive. No matter what input - negative, zero, or huge - the output is always positive. e^(-3) = 0.05 (negative becomes small positive). e^(0) = 1.0 (zero becomes exactly 1). e^(1) = 2.72 (positive becomes bigger positive). e^(5) = 148.4 (large becomes much larger). Pattern: higher input → exponentially bigger output. Softmax naturally amplifies the winner.
summary: Demonstrates that e^x is always positive and grows exponentially, so it amplifies large scores.
queries: is e to the x always positive
why not just absolute value instead of e^x

text: Full walkthrough with scores [-3, 1, 5]. Step 1: Apply e^score to each. e^(-3) = 0.05 (red), e^(1) = 2.72, e^(5) = 148.4 (green). All negative numbers became positive. Step 2: Sum them all up (the denominator). 0.05 + 2.72 + 148.4 = 151.17.
summary: Walks through softmax steps 1 and 2 for scores [-3, 1, 5]: exponentiate to get 0.05, 2.72, 148.4 and sum to 151.17.

text: Step 3: Divide each e^score by the sum (151.17). A (score -3): 0.05/151.17 = 0.03%. B (score 1): 2.72/151.17 = 1.8%. C (score 5): 148.4/151.17 = 98.2%. Checks: all positive ✓, sum = 100% ✓, ranking preserved ✓. The highest raw score still gets the largest weight after softmax.
summary: Completes the softmax walkthrough by dividing by the sum, yielding 0.03%, 1.8%, 98.2% - all positive, summing to 100%, ranking preserved.
queries: what does softmax output look like

text: Softmax amplifies differences by design. Raw scores: -3, 1, 5 (gap from 1 to 5 is only 4). After e^x: 0.05, 2.72, 148.4 (gap from 2.72 to 148 is 145!). The highest score doesn't just win, it dominates. This is good: the most relevant word SHOULD stand out. But if scores are too huge (like 12, 18, 25), softmax amplifies TOO aggressively giving 99.99% on one word. That's why we scale first (next chapter) to keep scores in a useful range.
summary: Explains that softmax amplifies score gaps and motivates the sqrt(d_k) scaling step to prevent over-amplification.
queries: why scale attention scores before softmax
why divide by sqrt d_k motivation
exponential amplification softmax good or bad

text: Softmax complete picture, two steps: (1) e^score for each score - makes everything positive, even e^(-1000) is positive. (2) Divide each by the total sum - now everything adds up to exactly 1.0 (100%). Formula: softmax(z_i) = e^(z_i) / Σ e^(z_j). Output: valid percentages (all positive, sum to 100%) that respect the original ranking. But raw scores can be too big for softmax - that's the next problem to solve (scaling).
summary: Recap of the two-step softmax procedure with the formula and properties: positive, sums to 1, preserves ranking.
queries: all positive sum to 1 ranking preserved

text: Step 3 of attention is scaling the raw QK^T dot product scores by dividing by √d_k. Dot products grow with dimension d_k because more product terms are summed, producing huge raw scores in high-dimensional models (around ~25 at d_k=64, ~180 at d_k=512). Large scores cause softmax saturation - one token gets ~99.6% of attention and the model can't blend information. Dividing by √d_k brings variance back to 1 (since dot product variance scales with d_k and std dev = √d_k), restoring a focused-but-not-collapsed attention distribution.
summary: Scaled dot product attention divides QK^T by √d_k to prevent softmax saturation from dimension-inflated scores.
queries: what is the scaling step in attention
why divide attention scores by sqrt d_k
what does scaling do in self-attention
summary of scale by root d_k chapter
why scaling step exists in transformer
Vaswani et al scaling factor explanation

text: The problem: dot products grow with dimensions. The dot product multiplies each pair and sums all of them, so more dimensions means more pairs being added up. 2 dimensions sums 2 terms: 0.24 + 0.14 = 0.38. 64 dimensions sums 64 terms (term_1 + term_2 + ... + term_64) and totals around ~25. 512 dimensions sums 512 terms and totals around ~180. Each individual term might be small (like 0.3 or 0.5), but adding up 64 or 512 of them makes the total very large. The score grows just because of more dimensions - not because the words are more related.
summary: Dot product scores get bigger as dimensionality grows because more product terms are summed, inflating the score independent of true similarity.

text: Why are big scores a problem? Softmax uses e^score. When scores are big, e^big_number becomes astronomically huge, and one word gets 99.99% of the attention - the model becomes blind to everything else. Small scores (2-dim) example [0.38, 0.56, 0.74] give a balanced attention: I = 28%, love = 33%, cats = 39%. The model looks at all words and can blend info from multiple sources. Huge scores (64-dim) example [12.1, 17.8, 25.6] collapse the softmax: I = 0.1%, love = 0.4%, cats = 99.6%. 99.6% lands on one word, the others become invisible, and the model can't blend information.
summary: Large dot product scores cause softmax to collapse onto a single word, making attention degenerate into a hard pick instead of a soft blend.

text: The fix: divide by √d_k. If d_k = 64, then √64 = 8. Dividing brings scores back to a manageable range. Before scaling: [12.1, 17.8, 25.6]. Divide by √64 = 8. After scaling: [1.51, 2.23, 3.20]. Softmax of [1.51, 2.23, 3.20] gives I = 14%, love = 28%, cats = 58%. The model focuses on the most relevant word (58%) but still gathers info from others (14%, 28%). That's exactly what you want.
summary: Dividing dot products by √d_k rescales them to a manageable range so softmax produces a focused but not collapsed attention distribution.

text: Why √d_k specifically? Why not ÷2 or ÷100? There's a mathematical reason. When Q and K have random values, the dot product's variance grows proportionally to d_k. The standard deviation = √d_k. So dividing by √d_k brings the variance back to 1 - normalizing scores to a consistent range no matter the dimension. In simple terms: √d_k is the exact right amount to undo the growth caused by summing d_k terms. Not too much (all scores become equal), not too little (problem remains).
summary: Square root of d_k is chosen because dot product variance scales with d_k, so dividing by its standard deviation √d_k normalizes variance to 1.

text: Step 4 of attention: apply softmax to each row of scaled scores to convert them into attention probabilities. For each word, exponentiate the scores, sum them, and divide each by the sum. Each row becomes a probability distribution summing to 1.0 - the attention weights that decide how much each word borrows from every other word's Value vector.
summary: Chapter overview: softmax turns scaled attention scores into per-row probability distributions used as attention weights.

text: Apply softmax step-by-step to scaled scores. Word 'I' has scaled scores looking at each word: [0.27, 0.40, 0.52]. Step 1: compute e^score for each. e^0.27 = 1.31, e^0.40 = 1.49, e^0.52 = 1.68. Step 2: sum all e^scores. 1.31 + 1.49 + 1.68 = 4.48. Step 3: divide each by sum. 1.31 / 4.48 = 0.29, 1.49 / 4.48 = 0.33, 1.68 / 4.48 = 0.37. Result: [0.29, 0.33, 0.37]. Check: 0.29 + 0.33 + 0.37 = 1.0.
summary: Worked softmax calculation on scaled scores [0.27, 0.40, 0.52] producing attention probabilities [0.29, 0.33, 0.37].

text: Chapter 7.7 covers Step 5 of attention: the weighted sum of Value vectors. Each attention weight multiplies its corresponding V vector, and the scaled vectors are summed to produce a new output vector for the query token. Worked example for 'I' with weights 0.29, 0.33, 0.38 over V_I=[1.0,-0.5], V_love=[0.3,0.8], V_cats=[-0.2,0.9] yields [0.313, 0.461]. This output is a context-aware representation that blends information from other tokens, and the same computation runs for every word.
summary: Chapter summary: Step 5 of attention multiplies each Value vector by its attention weight and sums them to produce a new context-aware vector for every token.

text:
Step 5 of attention: use attention weights to blend Value vectors. For the word 'I' with attention weights 0.29, 0.33, 0.38 over 'I', 'love', 'cats':

0.29 × V_I = 0.29 × [1.0, -0.5] = [0.290, -0.145]
0.33 × V_love = 0.33 × [0.3, 0.8] = [0.099, 0.264]
0.38 × V_cats = 0.38 × [-0.2, 0.9] = [-0.076, 0.342]

Sum: [0.290, -0.145] + [0.099, 0.264] + [-0.076, 0.342] = [0.313, 0.461].

This weighted sum of Value vectors is the attention output for 'I'.
summary: Worked example multiplies each Value vector by its attention weight and sums them to produce the attention output [0.313, 0.461] for the word 'I'.

text: The resulting vector [0.313, 0.461] is the NEW vector for 'I'. It is no longer just about 'I' alone. It has absorbed context from 'love' (33%) and 'cats' (38%). It is now a context-aware representation. The same process happens for every word in the sequence, so every token ends up with a new vector that mixes information from all other tokens according to its attention weights.
summary: Explains that the weighted-sum output is a context-aware representation of the token, blending information from other words proportional to their attention weights.

text: Chapter 7.8 collapses the full attention pipeline into one equation: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(Q·K^T / √d_k) · V. Each piece has a job - Q·K^T computes similarity scores, dividing by √d_k stabilizes the softmax, softmax produces probability-like attention weights summing to 1 per row, and multiplying by V yields the context-aware output.

text: Everything above in one line: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax( Q·K^T / √d_k ) · V. This single equation packs the full scaled dot-product attention computation: compare every query to every key, scale by the square root of the key dimension, normalize with softmax into probabilities, then take a weighted sum of the value vectors.

text:
Breakdown of each piece of the formula:
- Q · K^T: Dot product of every query with every key → score matrix.
- / √d_k: Scale down to prevent extreme softmax → manageable scores.
- softmax: Convert to probabilities (rows sum to 1) → attention weights.
- · V: Weighted sum of values → context-aware output.

text: Chapter 7.9 motivates multi-head attention. A single head must produce one set of attention weights for each word, so when a word like 'sat' has many relationships at once (subject 'cat', location 'mat', time 'last week'), the head is forced to compromise and weaker relations like time can collapse to ~5%. The fix is to run several attention heads in parallel, each with its own W_Q, W_K, W_V. Each head learns to ask a different question (subject-verb, verb-location, temporal, ...) and captures one relationship type fully, so the model preserves all the relationships at once.
summary: Chapter overview: one attention head must compromise across many relations, so the model uses multiple heads with separate W_Q, W_K, W_V to specialize.

text: Single-head attention has a problem. Our short example 'I love cats' has only 3 words and 2 main relationships (who loves? what's loved?). To really see why one head isn't enough, use a longer sentence with more relationships: 'The cat that I adopted last week sat on the mat.' The word 'sat' needs answers to MULTIPLE questions at once: Who sat? -> 'cat' (subject <-> verb relationship). Sat where? -> 'mat' (verb <-> location relationship). Sat when? -> 'last week' (verb <-> time relationship). One word, one position, but three different kinds of relationships it must encode simultaneously.
summary: A single attention head struggles because one word like 'sat' must encode multiple relationship types (subject, location, time) at once.

text: One head produces one set of attention weights, so it is forced to compromise across all relationships. For 'sat' with one head the distribution looks like: cat 40% (got some), mat 30% (got some), last week 5% (almost nothing!), others 25% (scattered). Temporal info ('last week') got only 5% - it is almost lost. A single head cannot focus on all relationships simultaneously: spreading attention over subject, location, and time at once means none get full weight, and weak signals like temporal modifiers can vanish.
summary: With one head the attention weights compromise across all relations, so weaker signals like 'last week' get only 5% and are nearly lost.

text: Solution: give attention MULTIPLE heads - like multiple ears. Each head has its own W_Q, W_K, W_V, so each asks a different question. Head 1 (subject-verb): 'sat' asks 'who is my subject?' -> finds cat (70%). Head 2 (verb-location): 'sat' asks 'where am I happening?' -> finds mat (65%). Head 3 (temporal): 'sat' asks 'when am I happening?' -> finds week (55%). Now NO information is lost. Each head specializes in one relationship and captures it fully, instead of one head trying to average across all of them.
summary: Multi-head attention gives each head its own W_Q, W_K, W_V so different heads specialize in different relationships and no info is lost.

text: Multi-head attention splits the 512-dim embedding into 8 chunks of 64 dims. Each head has its own 512x64 W_Q, W_K, W_V matrices, producing 8 different Query arrows that ask 8 different questions (subject, location, time, syntax, coreference, etc.). Splitting costs the same as one full head because of diminishing returns past ~64 dims per head. Transformers stack many such layers (e.g., 96), and each layer's 8 heads specialize differently during training - giving 96 x 8 = 768 attention patterns from grammar at the bottom to reasoning at the top.
summary: Chapter 7.10 explains why and how multi-head attention splits the 512-dim embedding into 8 heads of 64 dims and stacks them across layers.
queries: what is the point of multiple heads
what does each head do in transformer

text: One head equals one Query arrow equals one direction. Each word's 512-dim embedding produces ONE Query vector through W_Q. That single arrow can only point one way, so it can only ask one type of question. Pipeline: 512-dim embedding through W_Q (512x512) yields one 512-dim Query. One arrow, one question, information lost. Solution: don't make one big arrow. Make 8 smaller arrows, each pointing in its own direction.
summary: Single-head attention gives each word only one Query direction so it can only ask one question, motivating splitting into 8 smaller arrows.

why eight arrows instead of one

text: Split the 512 dimensions into 8 chunks of 64. Imagine the embedding as a long bar sliced into 8 equal pieces labeled H1 through H8, each 64 dims wide. Total dimensions: 512. Number of heads: 8. Dims per head: 512 / 8 = 64. We don't run the full 512-dim attention 8 times. We split it - same total computation, but 8 independent perspectives.
summary: The 512-dim embedding is sliced into 8 chunks of 64 dims, giving 8 independent perspectives at the same total compute cost.

text: But wait - doesn't splitting lose precision? Compare options. Option A: 8 heads x 512 dims each. Cost: 8 x 512 = 4,096 dims total (8x the compute). Quality per head very high but diminishing returns past about 64 dims. Option B: 8 heads x 64 dims each (the split). Cost: 8 x 64 = 512 dims total (same budget as one head). Quality per head: 64 dims is plenty - 'is this the subject?' doesn't need 512 numbers to answer. The insight: more dims per head equals diminishing returns. Going from 64 to 512 dims per head barely improves quality but costs 8x more. The split gives 8 different perspectives for the price of 1 large one.
summary: Comparing 8x512 vs 8x64 heads shows the split costs the same as one full head while diminishing returns make 64 dims plenty per head.

text: Each head gets its own W_Q, W_K, W_V. 8 heads equals 8 independent sets of weight matrices. Each set is smaller: 512x64 instead of 512x512. Heads 1 through 8 each have W_Q W_K W_V at 512x64 each. Same embedding goes into all 8 heads. Each head's different W matrices produce a different Query arrow - pointing in a different direction, asking a different question.
summary: Each of the 8 heads has its own smaller 512x64 W_Q, W_K, W_V matrices that project the same embedding into different Query directions.

text: 8 arrows, 8 questions, nothing lost. For 'sat' in 'The cat sat on the mat last week' each head's Query arrow points differently. Head 1 asks 'who did the action?' finds cat at 70%. Head 2 asks 'where?' finds mat at 65%. Head 3 asks 'when?' finds week at 55%. Head 4 asks 'what comes next?' finds on at 60%. Heads 5-8 ask about grammar, coreference, proximity, etc. Each head specializes. Together they capture everything - no information lost.

text: Now zoom out: layers. A Transformer doesn't have just one set of 8 heads. It stacks multiple layers, each with its own 8 heads. Layer 1: word proximity, basic grammar. Layer 2: syntax, clause structure. Layer 3: meaning, sentiment. ... up to Layer 96: reasoning, generation. 96 layers x 8 heads = 768 attention patterns. Each layer reads the previous layer's output. Early layers see raw words. Later layers see increasingly abstract, context-rich representations. Nobody told the model which layer should handle grammar or reasoning. It organized itself this way during training.
summary: Transformers stack many layers each with 8 heads, with early layers learning grammar and later layers learning reasoning, totaling 96x8 = 768 attention patterns.

chapterTitle: Inside Each Head - Full Attention in 64 Dims
text: Inside each of the 8 attention heads, the full Steps 1-5 attention algorithm runs in 64 dimensions instead of 512: compute Q, K, V via head-specific W matrices, score = Q·Kᵀ/√64, softmax, weighted sum of V. Because each head has different W_Q/W_K/W_V, each produces different attention patterns - one head learns subject-to-verb, another verb-to-location, another temporal relations - so the model captures relationships a single head would miss.
summary: Chapter 7.11 shows that each of 8 heads runs the full attention computation in 64 dims and learns distinct relationship patterns through its own W matrices.

chapterTitle: Inside Each Head - Full Attention in 64 Dims
text:
Each head runs the FULL attention algorithm. Exactly Steps 1-5 we learned, but in 64 dims instead of 512.

For Head 1:
  Q₁ = embedding × W_Q₁  [3 words × 64 dims]
  K₁ = embedding × W_K₁  [3 words × 64 dims]
  V₁ = embedding × W_V₁  [3 words × 64 dims]

  scores₁ = Q₁ · K₁ᵀ / √64  [3×3 score matrix]
  weights₁ = softmax(scores₁)  [3×3 attention weights]
  output₁ = weights₁ × V₁  [3 words × 64 dims]

Same runs for all 8 heads. Each produces [3 words × 64 dims].
summary: Each of the 8 attention heads runs the full Step 1-5 attention algorithm independently in 64 dimensions, producing a [3 words × 64 dims] output.

chapterTitle: Inside Each Head - Full Attention in 64 Dims
text:
The KEY: each head's attention weights are different, because their W_Q, W_K, W_V are different. Here is what 'sat' sees in each head:

Head 1 - Subject to Verb: cat 70%, mat 8%, week 5%, others 17%.
Head 2 - Verb to Location: cat 10%, mat 65%, week 8%, others 17%.
Head 3 - Temporal: cat 8%, mat 7%, week 55%, others 30%.

Each head found what the single head missed. Head 3 got 'last week' at 55% - single head only gave it 5%.
summary: Different W_Q/W_K/W_V give each head different attention patterns - subject-verb, verb-location, temporal - capturing relations a single head would miss.

text: Chapter 7.12 - Concat + W_O - Blending All Heads. After multi-head attention runs, each word has 8 separate result vectors that are concatenated end-to-end into one 512-number list. But the sections are still 'sealed' from each other - Head 1's numbers don't know what Head 2 found. The output projection matrix W_O multiplies this concatenated vector so every output dimension becomes a learned blend of all 8 heads' findings. W_O is a learned weight matrix (512x512 = 262,144 weights) trained by backpropagation just like W_Q, W_K, W_V. Different layers learn different W_O matrices specializing in different combinations (syntax, semantics, reasoning). After W_O, 'love' becomes one rich integrated vector encoding subject + object + sentiment together.
summary: Concatenation glues 8 head outputs side by side but they stay isolated; W_O is a learned 512x512 matrix that mixes all heads into one integrated context-aware vector.

text: The outputs are separate - we need to combine them. After the 8 heads finish, each word has 8 separate results. For 'love': Head 1 gave [0.3, 0.5] - figured out 'I' is the one who loves. Head 2 gave [-0.2, 0.8] - figured out 'cats' is what's loved. Head 3 gave [0.7, -0.1] - figured out the sentiment is positive. ... 8 results total. We stick them end-to-end (concatenate): [0.3, 0.5, -0.2, 0.8, 0.7, -0.1, ..., 0.1, 0.4]. One long list - 512 numbers. But there's a problem.
summary: Each of the 8 heads produces a separate small vector for a word; concatenating them gives one 512-number list but the sections aren't yet mixed.
