what is concatenation step in attention

text: The problem - these results are like 8 sealed envelopes. 8 detectives investigated the same crime. Each wrote findings and sealed them: Head 1 - suspect is male, 30s. Head 2 - happened at the park. Head 3 - Tuesday evening. Head 4 - weapon was a knife. Tape them together (concatenation): thick black walls between each section - they can't see each other. They're physically side by side, but each section is still sealed. H1's numbers have no idea what H2 found. H3 can't see H4. They're taped together but not talking. Boss asks: 'one-line summary?' No envelope has the full picture. H1 only knows the suspect. H2 only knows the location. Nobody can combine them. We need someone to open all the envelopes, read everything, and write one combined summary.
summary: Concatenating head outputs leaves them isolated like sealed envelopes - each head's findings can't see each other, so we need a step to mix them.
queries: why concatenation alone is not enough
do heads share information after concatenation
what is the problem with concatenated head vectors
can H1 see H2's numbers after concat
why heads can't talk to each other after concat

text: W_O - the person who opens all the envelopes. W_O is just another weight matrix - a grid of numbers, same as W_Q, W_K, W_V. Multiply input by grid, get output. Nothing new in how it works. But what it does is special. When you multiply the concatenated list by W_O, every number in the output is computed from all numbers in the input. Before W_O: Position 1 = 0.3 (only knows Head 1's finding). After W_O: Position 1 = blend of 0.3 + (-0.2) + 0.7 + ... all positions = knows Head 1 + Head 2 + Head 3 + ... everything. It's like the combined summary: 'A male suspect in his 30s used a knife at the park on Tuesday evening.' One sentence that contains information from all envelopes. After W_O, every single position in the output carries a mix of what all 8 heads found. The envelopes have been opened, read, and combined.
summary: W_O is a weight matrix that mixes the concatenated head outputs so every output dimension contains information from all 8 heads.
queries: how does W_O blend head outputs
before and after W_O position values
W_O combines all positions of concatenated vector
how W_O is similar to W_Q W_K W_V
how does multiplication by W_O blend information

text: Where did W_O's numbers come from? Same exact process as all other grids: 1. Start with random numbers. 2. Model tries to predict next word, gets it wrong. 3. Error flows backward, nudges each number in W_O. 4. 'If this number were 0.31 instead of 0.30, the blending would have been better → change to 0.31'. 5. Repeat billions of times. 6. W_O settles into numbers that produce good blends. The training discovers the best way to combine the 8 heads' findings into one useful output. Nobody programs this - it emerges from training.
summary: W_O's weights start random and are tuned by backpropagation over billions of steps until the model learns the best way to blend the 8 heads.
queries: where do W_O weights come from
how does W_O learn good blends
small nudges to W_O weights example 0.30 to 0.31
W_O same training process as W_Q W_K W_V

text: Back to 'I love cats' - what W_O does for 'love'. After 8 heads run, 'love's concatenated vector has: Dims 1-64 - Head 1 found 'I' is the subject who loves. Dims 65-128 - Head 2 found 'cats' is the object being loved. Dims 129-192 - Head 3 found this is a positive sentiment. Without W_O: each section is isolated. Dim 1 has no idea 'cats' is involved. After W_O: every dimension of 'love's output encodes the combined meaning: 'affection from I directed at cats, with positive sentiment.' One rich, integrated vector. This is what we set out to do in Chapter 1 - transform 'love' from an isolated word into a context-aware representation.
summary: For 'love' in 'I love cats', dims 1-64/65-128/129-192 hold each head's findings; W_O fuses them so every output dim carries the full meaning of subject+object+sentiment together.

text: W_O is LEARNED - not hardcoded. The model discovers during training which combinations of head outputs are useful. Different layers learn different W_O matrices: Layer 1's W_O might focus on combining basic syntax patterns. Layer 3's W_O might focus on combining semantic relationships. Layer 6's W_O might focus on combining abstract reasoning. Nobody programs what to combine. W_O's 512x512 = 262,144 weights are all learned through backpropagation - the same process from chapters 1.3-1.9.
summary: W_O is learned not hardcoded; each transformer layer has its own W_O specializing in different combinations (syntax, semantics, reasoning), with 512x512=262,144 weights trained by backpropagation.

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: Why 8 heads, why not 4 or 16? Tradeoff: total 512 dims divided equally among heads. 16 heads = 512 / 16 = 32 dims each, more pattern types but each head weaker. 4 heads = 512 / 4 = 128 dims each, each head powerful but fewer patterns. 8 heads x 64 dims = sweet spot: enough capacity per head (64 dims) and enough perspectives (8 types).
summary: Explains 8 heads as tradeoff sweet spot between head capacity and pattern diversity.

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: Parameter count per head: W_Q (512x64) + W_K (512x64) + W_V (512x64) = 3 x 32,768 = 98,304. Across 8 heads: W_Q = 32,768 x 8 = 262,144; W_K = 32,768 x 8 = 262,144; W_V = 32,768 x 8 = 262,144; W_O = 512 x 512 = 262,144. Total: 1,048,576, about 1 million parameters per attention layer.
summary: Computes total attention parameters: ~1 million per layer across W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O.

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: Surprising: same count as single-head. Single-head with 512-dim Q, K, V needs 512 x 512 per matrix = same total. Multi-head doesn't add parameters - it reorganizes them into 8 independent groups. Same budget, much better results.
summary: Multi-head attention uses same parameter budget as single-head but reorganized into independent groups.

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: How does this scale in a full model? One attention layer = ~1 million parameters. Transformers stack multiple layers, each refining context further. Original Transformer (2017): 6 layers, ~6M attention params. GPT-2 (2019): 48 layers, ~48M attention params. GPT-3 (2020): 96 layers, 175 BILLION total params. More layers = deeper understanding. Each layer's attention asks different questions about the same words, building increasingly rich representations - same mechanism, just stacked.
summary: Shows how attention parameters scale across stacked layers from original Transformer to GPT-3.
queries: what do deeper transformer layers do

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: Chapter 7.14 answers whether the output projection matrix W_O (and other weight grids) is constant. W_O changes constantly during training as gradients nudge its numbers across billions of examples, but once training finishes it is frozen to disk and never changes again. At inference every user, every sentence, and every input uses the exact same W_O. The same is true for the embedding dictionary and all W_Q, W_K, W_V matrices. What does change per input are the live computed values: Q, K, V vectors, attention scores, softmax weights, blended outputs, and the final output. The coffee-machine analogy: W_O is the fixed machine, the sentence embeddings are the beans, so same machine plus different beans yields different coffee.
summary: W_O is updated during training but frozen at inference; only the per-input Q/K/V and attention values change.

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: During training: W_O changes. Every time the model sees a new batch of sentences and makes prediction errors, every number in W_O gets nudged slightly. Over billions of examples, W_O gradually improves. It changes millions of times during training - getting better and better at blending heads. After training is done: W_O is FROZEN. Completely constant. Once training is complete, the model is saved to a file. W_O's numbers are written to disk and they NEVER change again. When you use the model (send it a sentence), W_O is loaded from disk and used as-is.
summary: W_O is nudged by gradients during training over billions of examples, then frozen to disk forever after training finishes.

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: This is true for all grids, not just W_O. CONSTANT after training (stored on disk, never changes): Embedding dictionary; W_Q₁ through W_Q₈ ← same grid for every input; W_K₁ through W_K₈ ← same grid for every input; W_V₁ through W_V₈ ← same grid for every input; W_O ← same grid for every input; All other layer weights. Different for every input (computed live, then thrown away): Q, K, V vectors ← different because the words are different; Attention scores ← different because Q, K are different; Softmax weights ← different because scores are different; Blended outputs ← different because weights and V are different; Final output ← different because everything above is different.
summary: Lists which transformer pieces are constant on disk (all weight matrices, embeddings) versus which are recomputed per input (Q, K, V, scores, softmax, outputs).

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: Why do different sentences get different results if the grids are constant? Because the grids are like a coffee machine. The machine (W_O) is always the same. But the coffee beans you put in (your sentence) are different each time. Same machine + different beans = different coffee. Same W_O × 'I love cats' embeddings = one output. Same W_O × 'dogs are great' embeddings = completely different output. The grids don't need to change. They've already learned the GENERAL SKILL of 'how to blend heads' or 'how to extract queries.' That skill works on ANY input. Just like a coffee machine doesn't need to be rebuilt for each type of bean - it already knows how to grind and brew.
summary: The coffee machine analogy explains why a fixed W_O produces different outputs: the weights encode a general skill applied to different per-input embeddings.

chapterTitle: Attention Flow - Shapes at Every Step
text: Single-head flow with 3 tokens, d_model = 4, d_k = 2. Input X is 3 x 4 (rows: I, love, cats). Copy X and multiply by three different weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V each 4 x 2 to get Q, K, V each 3 x 2. Compute Q dot K^T then divide by sqrt(d_k) and softmax per row to get attention weights matrix 3 x 3 (rows sum to 1). Then weights (3 x 3) dot V (3 x 2) = head output (3 x 2). Each word keeps its row through the whole pipeline. That entire pipeline is one head; the model runs it in parallel for every head, each with its own W_Q, W_K, W_V.
summary: Single head: X (3x4) projects to Q, K, V (3x2 each), produces 3x3 weight matrix via softmax, then weights times V yields 3x2 head output.

chapterTitle: Attention Flow - Shapes at Every Step
text: Multi-head: with h = 2 heads each head produces a 3 x 2 output H_1 and H_2. Stack them side-by-side (concat along column axis) to get concat(H_1, H_2) of shape 3 x 4. Multiply by W_O of shape 4 x 4 to mix information across heads. Final attention output is 3 x 4 - the same shape as input X. Shape cheat sheet: X is N x d_model = 3 x 4; W_Q, W_K, W_V per head is d_model x d_k = 4 x 2; Q, K, V per head is N x d_k = 3 x 2; Q dot K^T is N x N = 3 x 3; softmax(/sqrt(d_k)) is N x N rows-sum-to-1 = 3 x 3; weights dot V = H_i is N x d_k = 3 x 2; concat(H_1...H_h) is N x (h * d_k) = N x d_model = 3 x 4; W_O is d_model x d_model = 4 x 4; Final is N x d_model = 3 x 4. In a real Transformer the same pattern runs with N = sentence length, d_model = 512, h = 8 heads, d_k = 64. Shape rules are identical.
summary: Two head outputs concat to 3x4, then W_O (4x4) gives Final (3x4) matching input shape; full cheat sheet of every matrix shape included.

chapterTitle: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text: Chapter 7.16 recaps the full attention computation in plain English as a 9-step pipeline: words become embeddings, multiplied by W_Q/W_K/W_V to make Queries, Keys, Values; Q dot K gives relevance scores; scale and softmax turn scores into percentages; percentages weight the Values into a context-aware output; steps 2-5 repeat across 8 heads with different grids; the 8 results are concatenated and multiplied by W_O for a combined summary; output is one blended vector per word ready for the next layer. The W matrices (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O) are tables of numbers trained from random and then frozen, each learning a distinct role. The grids are the machine; the sentence is raw material.
summary: Plain-English recap of the entire attention pipeline as 9 steps with the role of each W matrix.

chapterTitle: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text:
The complete picture in plain English, step by step:
1. Words become numbers (embedding lookup).
2. Those numbers times three grids (W_Q, W_K, W_V give Query, Key, Value).
3. Queries dot-product with Keys (find who is relevant to whom).
4. Scores get normalized (scale plus softmax give percentages).
5. Percentages blend the Values (weighted sum gives context-aware output).
6. Steps 2-5 happen 8 times with different grids (multi-head gives 8 separate results).
7. Stick the 8 results end-to-end (concatenate, like 8 sealed envelopes taped together).
8. That list times one more grid (W_O opens all envelopes and writes a combined summary).
9. Output: one blended vector per word, ready for the next layer.
summary: Lists the 9 plain-English steps of attention from embeddings to one blended vector per word.

chapterTitle: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text:
Every grid in attention (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O) is a table of numbers that started random, got nudged into useful values during training, and stays frozen forever after. All grids work the same way: multiply input by grid, get output. The only difference is what each one was trained to do:
- W_Q grids: learned to extract what a word is looking for.
- W_K grids: learned to extract what a word can be found for.
- W_V grids: learned to extract what actual info a word carries.
- W_O grid: learned to blend all heads' findings into one combined summary.
summary: Explains that all W matrices are frozen trained number grids that differ only in what they were trained to extract.

text: Chapter 8.1 introduces the original 2017 Transformer's encoder-decoder design for machine translation. The Encoder reads input in parallel and builds rich contextual representations; the Decoder generates output token by token. Cross-Attention bridges them: decoder Queries attend to encoder Keys and Values. The full stack is 6 encoder layers (Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm) and 6 decoder layers (Masked Attention, Add & Norm, Cross-Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm). Three model families emerge from this design: Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART) for translation, Encoder-Only (BERT, RoBERTa) for classification, Decoder-Only (GPT, Claude) for generation.
summary: Summarizes the encoder-decoder Transformer, cross-attention, the 6+6 layer stack, and the three model-family variants.

text: The original Transformer from the 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" was built to solve machine translation. Translation needs two different operations: take an English input like "I love cats" and produce a Hindi output like "Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain". English and Hindi are different languages, so the model must fully understand the English input before it can generate the Hindi output. These are two separate tasks, which motivates splitting the architecture into two halves.
summary: Introduces the 2017 Transformer paper and why machine translation requires two distinct stages: understanding the source and generating the target.

text: Solution: two separate components. The Encoder reads the input and builds a deep understanding, outputting a compressed representation. The Decoder reads the encoder's output and generates the translation one word at a time. Encoder properties: processes input in parallel, all words attend to all words, builds context for each word, outputs rich representations of "I", "love", "cats". Decoder properties: reads encoder output, generates translation token by token, uses attention to look back at input, producing "Mujhe" then "billiyaan" then "pasand" then "hain".
summary: Splits the Transformer into Encoder (parallel understanding) and Decoder (token-by-token generation) and lists their distinct jobs.

text: Cross-Attention is the bridge between encoder and decoder. The decoder doesn't generate from nothing; it looks back at the encoder's output through a special connection called cross-attention. Flow: Encoder output (rich understanding of "I", "love", "cats") provides Keys and Values, which feed into the Cross-Attention Layer, where the decoder's Query asks the encoder "What's relevant now?". The decoder then generates "Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain". When generating a Hindi noun, the decoder's Query asks "what noun am I translating?" and the encoder's Keys/Values answer with the meaning of "love" and "cats", so the decoder picks the right Hindi word.
summary: Explains cross-attention as the mechanism where decoder Queries attend to encoder Keys/Values to ground generation in the source.

cross attention queries keys values source

text: The full encoder-decoder stack: the 2017 Transformer stacks both halves with 6 layers each. Encoder Stack (6 layers) per layer: Attention, Add & Norm, Feed-Forward, Add & Norm. Decoder Stack (6 layers) per layer: Masked Attention, Add & Norm, Cross-Attention, Add & Norm, Feed-Forward, Add & Norm. Masked Attention means the decoder can only look at past tokens; Cross-Attention means it looks at encoder output. The encoder feeds into the decoder.
summary: Lays out the 6-layer encoder and 6-layer decoder stacks and the sublayers inside each, including masked and cross-attention in the decoder.

text: Why two halves? Different model types. The encoder-decoder design is great for translation, but other tasks need different shapes: Encoder-Decoder - Translation (T5, BART), input English, output Hindi. Encoder-Only - Classification (BERT, RoBERTa), input Sentence, output Sentiment score. Decoder-Only - Generation (GPT, Claude), input Prompt, output Continued text.
summary: Maps the three Transformer variants (encoder-decoder, encoder-only, decoder-only) to representative models and tasks.

text: Add & Norm is the stabilizer inside every Transformer block, appearing twice per block - once after Multi-Head Attention and once after the Feed-Forward Network. The Add (residual connection) preserves the original input by adding it back to the sub-layer output, creating a gradient highway. The Norm (Layer Normalization) rescales values to a stable range using mean, variance, epsilon, and learnable gamma/beta parameters. Together they prevent values from vanishing toward zero or exploding to thousands across deep networks like GPT-3's 96 layers, allowing both forward signals and backward gradients to flow without degradation.
summary: Add & Norm stabilizes deep Transformers by combining a residual skip connection with Layer Normalization, applied twice per block.

text: Inside one Transformer block, there are 4 steps. After attention inside a single Transformer block, the flow is: Input Embeddings -> Multi-Head Attention -> Add & Norm -> FFN (Feed-Forward Network) -> Add & Norm -> Output. Add & Norm appears twice in every block - once after Attention, once after FFN. It's the stabilizer that keeps everything in check.

text: The Problem: Values Drift in Deep Networks. GPT-3 has 96 layers. Each layer multiplies values by weights. The result depends on the weights - if they're slightly below 1.0, values shrink. If slightly above, values explode. Keeping them perfectly balanced across 96 layers is nearly impossible. Take 0.7 from "cats" embedding. If weights average 0.9 (shrink): Start 0.7 -> Layer 10: 0.24 -> Layer 50: 0.004 -> Layer 96: 0.00003 (value vanishes to nearly zero). If weights average 1.1 (grow): Start 0.7 -> Layer 10: 1.8 -> Layer 50: 82 -> Layer 96: 4,838 (value explodes to thousands). Both are broken. Whether the value goes to 0.00003 or 4,838 - it's no longer a useful embedding. And gradients during backpropagation drift the same way, so the model can't learn either.
summary: Without stabilization, values either vanish toward zero or explode to thousands across 96 layers of a deep Transformer.
queries: Why do values drift in deep networks?
What is the vanishing values problem?

How do values change across 96 layers?

Concrete example of value drift across layers

text: The "Add" - Keep the Original. The fix is beautifully simple: add the original input back to the output. The fancy name is "residual connection" - residual just means leftover. For "cats" with embedding [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]: original input [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] goes through Multi-Head Attention to produce attention output [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]. The Add Step: [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] + [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4] = [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0]. Think of it as a highway: even if the attention layer learns nothing useful, the original embedding still passes through untouched. During backpropagation, the error can travel backward along this highway without shrinking to zero. Without the Add: Input -> Attention -> Output, if attention messes up, values are lost. With the Add: Input -> Attention -> Output + Input, original values always survive.
summary: The Add step (residual connection) adds the original input back to the attention output, preserving the signal even if attention contributes nothing useful.

How does the residual help backpropagation?
Worked example of residual connection on cats

How do gradients survive without shrinking?

text: The "Norm" - Layer Normalization. After adding, values can drift to unpredictable ranges. Layer Norm rescales them to a stable range. The Layer Normalization Formula: LayerNorm(x_i) = gamma * (x_i - mu) / sqrt(sigma^2 + epsilon) + beta. Parameters: x_i = each value in the vector (the raw input we want to normalize). mu (mean) = average of all values (centers the data around zero). sigma^2 (variance) = variance of all values (measures how spread out values are). epsilon = tiny number (1e-5), prevents division by zero if all values are identical. gamma = learnable scale (starts at 1.0), lets the model stretch or shrink the range. beta = learnable shift (starts at 0.0), lets the model move the center up or down. Gamma and beta are learned during training - they let the model undo the normalization if needed and stretch, shrink, or shift the normalized values to whatever range works best for each dimension.
summary: Layer Normalization formula rescales values using mean, variance, epsilon, and learnable gamma/beta parameters per dimension.
queries: What is the Layer Normalization formula?

What is gamma in Layer Normalization?

Why are gamma and beta learnable?

Real formula for Layer Normalization in Transformers

text: Computing Layer Norm - Step by Step. Normalizing [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] (the result from Add). Step 1: Compute the mean (mu). mu = (-0.2 + 0.2 + 0.9 + 1.0) / 4 = 0.475. Step 2: subtract the mean from each value (x_i - mu). -0.2 - 0.475 = -0.675; 0.2 - 0.475 = -0.275; 0.9 - 0.475 = 0.425; 1.0 - 0.475 = 0.525. Centered: [-0.675, -0.275, 0.425, 0.525]. Step 3: Compute variance, then divide by sqrt(sigma^2 + epsilon). variance = mean of squared deviations = (0.675^2 + 0.275^2 + 0.425^2 + 0.525^2) / 4 = 0.2469. sqrt(0.2469 + 0.00001) = 0.4969. -0.675 / 0.4969 = -1.36; -0.275 / 0.4969 = -0.55; 0.425 / 0.4969 = 0.86; 0.525 / 0.4969 = 1.06. After this step: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] - centered at 0, unit variance. Step 4: Scale by gamma, shift by beta. Initially gamma=1.0 and beta=0.0, so at first this step does nothing. With initial gamma=1.0, beta=0.0: 1.0 * -1.36 + 0.0 = -1.36; 1.0 * -0.55 + 0.0 = -0.55; 1.0 * 0.86 + 0.0 = 0.86; 1.0 * 1.06 + 0.0 = 1.06. Final output: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Why have gamma and beta if they start as identity? During training, the model may learn that some dimensions need more spread (gamma > 1) or a non-zero center (beta != 0). Without them, normalization would be too rigid.
summary: Worked example of Layer Norm on [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] showing mean 0.475, variance 0.2469, and final normalized output [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06].

Attention Output: [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4] -> Add: input + output. After Add: [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] -> Normalize. After Norm: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] -> ready for FFN. To FFN: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. This exact same Add & Norm happens again after FFN. Every Transformer block does this twice - once after Attention, once after FFN.

text: Why It Matters - The Big Picture. Without Add & Norm: Layer 1: 0.7, Layer 10: 0.24, Layer 50: 0.004, Layer 96: ~0. Values drift to extremes. Model stops learning. With Add & Norm: Layer 1: 0.7, Layer 10: 0.67, Layer 50: 0.62, Layer 96: 0.57. Values stay stable. Gradients flow. Training works even at 96 layers deep. In one sentence: Add & Norm is the reason Transformers can be 96 layers deep without losing information. The "Add" preserves the original values, and the "Norm" keeps them in a stable range for the next layer.
summary: Side-by-side comparison shows values vanishing without Add & Norm versus staying stable with it, enabling 96-layer deep training.

text: Feed-forward network architecture diagram showing expand-then-compress structure: 512-dim input through W1 to 2048-dim hidden with GELU activation, then W2 back to 512-dim output. Three columns of neurons - Input (512, shown as x1, x2, x3), Hidden (2048, shown as h1, h2, h3, h2048) with + GELU, Output (512, shown as y1, y2, y3). W1 is 512x2048 between input and hidden; W2 is 2048x512 between hidden and output. Edges show full connectivity from each input neuron to every hidden neuron, then every hidden neuron to every output neuron.
summary: Diagram of the FFN's three-column expand-compress neural network with W1 (512x2048), GELU, and W2 (2048x512).
queries: feed forward network neural network picture

text: Line graph of the ReLU activation function over x from -3 to 3. The curve is flat at y equals zero for all negative x, forming a horizontal line along the x-axis, then rises with a hard kink at the origin and continues linearly as y equals x for all positive x. Dead region for negatives where the gradient is exactly zero. Annotations call out the dead region (gradient = 0) on the negative side and the hard kink at the origin.
summary: ReLU plot showing zero output for negatives, hard kink at origin, and y=x for positives - illustrates the dead-gradient region.

text: Line graph of the GELU activation function over x from -3 to 3. The curve is a smooth S-shape: nearly zero for large negative x, dips slightly below zero around x equals minus one forming a small leak region, passes smoothly through the origin with a continuous derivative, and asymptotically matches y equals x for large positive x. Smooth shape keeps gradients alive for negative inputs. Annotations call out the leak region (gradient alive) below zero and the smooth S curve at the origin.
summary: GELU plot showing the smooth S-curve with a small negative leak region and y≈x asymptote, contrasting with ReLU's hard cutoff.
queries: why GELU keeps gradient alive visual

text: Chapter 8.3 - FFN, the Feed-Forward Network. The FFN sits between two Add & Norm layers in every Transformer block and processes each token independently. Its formula is FFN(x) = W2 · GELU(W1 · x + b1) + b2 - just two linear layers with a GELU activation between them. The first layer expands d_model to d_ff (4× wider, e.g., 512 → 2048), and the second compresses back. GELU(x) = x · Φ(x) is a smooth alternative to ReLU that keeps gradients alive for negative inputs. While Attention routes information between tokens, FFN stores knowledge: W1 rows act as pattern detectors, W2 columns write corresponding outputs - the key-value memory view. This explains implicit knowledge, factual recall, and disambiguation. Each Transformer block has ~2/3 of its parameters in FFN, ~1/3 in Attention.
summary: Recaps the FFN's two-layer structure, expand-compress shape, GELU activation, knowledge-storage role, and parameter share.
queries: what is the feed forward network in transformer

text: Where does the Feed-Forward Network sit? Inside one Transformer block: Input Embeddings → Multi-Head Attention → Add & Norm → FFN (Feed-Forward Network) → Add & Norm → Output. The FFN sits between the two Add & Norm layers. It processes each token independently - no communication between words here. That's what makes it different from Attention.
summary: Locates the FFN as the third stage inside a Transformer block, processing each token independently between two Add & Norm layers.

text: FFN = Two Linear Layers with Activation. The FFN is two layers stacked from chapter 1.19 with an activation in between. Formula: FFN(x) = W2 · GELU(W1 · x + b1) + b2. Where x = input from Add & Norm; W1, b1 = first layer weights & bias; GELU = activation function; W2, b2 = second layer weights & bias. No attention, no softmax, no multi-head - just two matrix multiplies with an activation in between.
summary: States the exact two-layer FFN formula FFN(x) = W2·GELU(W1·x + b1) + b2 with each component labeled.
queries: FFN(x) formula W1 W2 b1 b2
is FFN just two matrix multiplies
what are W1 and W2 in FFN
why GELU between W1 and W2

text: The Expand-Then-Compress Shape. The first layer expands the dimension, the second layer compresses it back. In GPT-2 (d_model = 512): hidden layer has 4x more neurons than input or output. Every input neuron connects to every hidden neuron (W1, 512x2048), GELU activates each hidden neuron, then every hidden neuron connects to every output neuron (W2, 2048x512). Data expands into a wider thinking space (512 → 2048), then compresses back (2048 → 512). Why 4x? Imagine spreading notes across a big desk (expand to 2048), working things out, then summarizing on a small card (compress to 512). The 4x ratio comes from the original 'Attention is All You Need' paper and is standard across GPT-2, GPT-3, and most Transformers.
summary: Explains the FFN's expand-then-compress shape with d_ff = 4 × d_model and the intuition for why widening creates a thinking space.
queries: why does FFN expand then compress
why is hidden dim 4x model dim
Attention is All You Need FFN ratio

text: Step 1: Multiply by W1 + b1 (expand 4 → 8 dims). Tracing 'cats' through FFN. Add & Norm output [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] for 'cats'. We use a tiny 4-dim version (real models use 512). Compute [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] · W1 + b1. Each of the 8 output values is a dot product of the input with one column of W1, plus a bias - exactly like chapter 1.19. Result (8 dims): [0.42, -1.80, 0.95, 2.10, -0.33, 1.47, -0.88, 0.61].
summary: Walks through Step 1 of FFN on the cats vector: matrix multiply by W1 plus bias to expand from 4 to 8 dimensions.
queries: how does W1 expand the input

text: Step 2: Apply GELU activation (each value independently, similar to ReLU but smoother). Inputs/outputs: 0.42→0.28, -1.80→-0.04, 0.95→0.77, 2.10→2.07, -0.33→-0.12, 1.47→1.37, -0.88→-0.17, 0.61→0.44. Result: [0.28, -0.04, 0.77, 2.07, -0.12, 1.37, -0.17, 0.44]. Step 3: Multiply by W2 + b2 to compress 8 → 4 dims. The 8-dim expanded vector compresses back to the original 4 dims. FFN output for 'cats': [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]. Same size in, same size out - but values transformed through a thinking space 4x wider. Output goes to the second Add & Norm.
summary: Continues the FFN trace through GELU and W2 to compress back to the original 4-dim output [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22].

text: GELU - The Activation Function (Gaussian Error Linear Unit). Modern Transformers use GELU instead of ReLU. Formula: GELU(x) = x · Φ(x), where Φ(x) is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal: Φ(x) = (1/2)[1 + erf(x/√2)]. x = input value; Φ(x) = probability x is 'kept' (ranges 0 fully blocked to 1 fully passed); erf = Gauss error function (smooth S-curve mapping any number to [-1,1]). In plain English: GELU multiplies x by the probability that x would be considered 'large' under a standard normal bell curve. Worked examples: x=2.0, Φ=0.977, result=1.95 (large positive almost fully passed); x=0.5, Φ=0.691, result=0.35 (69% passed); x=0.0, result=0.00; x=-0.5, Φ=0.309, result=-0.15 (31% leaked); x=-2.0, Φ=0.023, result=-0.05 (almost fully blocked). ReLU vs GELU: ReLU has hard cutoff (negative=dead, gradient=0 forever). GELU has smooth transition - small negatives leak through, gradient never fully dies. During backpropagation, ReLU's gradient is exactly 0 for negatives - the neuron is dead and can never recover. GELU's smooth curve means slightly negative values get a small gradient, so neurons can recover. GPT-2 and GPT-3 both use GELU.
summary: Defines GELU's exact formula GELU(x) = x · Φ(x), works five example values, and contrasts the smooth GELU curve with ReLU's dead-region cutoff.

text: What FFN Stores - Knowledge vs Routing. Attention decides which words to listen to; FFN decides what to do with what it heard. Inside every block, Attention runs first, then FFN. FFN never sees the raw input - it always receives attention's output. Example 1: 'The area of this rectangle depends on its length' - notice 'breadth' is never mentioned but the model knows it matters. Attention runs first: 'area's Query asks 'what determines me?'; 'length's Key scores 0.55 after softmax; 'rectangle's Key scores 0.30. Weighted sum: Output = 0.55 · V_length + 0.30 · V_rectangle + ... 'area' vector now carries 'I'm the area of a rectangle and length is involved.' But 'breadth' is not in the sentence - attention can only mix what's there. Then FFN adds stored knowledge: W1 row 1203 ('rectangle-area' detector) was trained on millions of math texts: W1[1203] · x = 3.9. GELU(3.9) = 3.89, neuron 1203 fires strongly. Neuron 502 ('circle-area-pi') got -0.8, GELU suppresses it. W2 column 1203 shifts the vector toward a region encoding 'area = length × breadth, breadth is the missing factor.' The model implicitly knows breadth matters even though the word never appeared. During training, the FFN saw 'area = length × breadth' thousands of times - that relationship got baked into W1 row 1203 (detector) and W2 column 1203 (output).
summary: Contrasts attention's routing role with FFN's stored-knowledge role using the area/length/breadth example to show how FFN injects implicit facts.
queries: does attention or FFN store knowledge
how does FFN inject training data
attention as routing FFN as memory

text: Example 2: 'The capital of India is ___' - Attention gathers context, FFN recalls the fact. Attention runs first: '___'s Query asks 'what country am I about?'; 'India's Key scores 0.72 after softmax; 'capital' gets 0.18. Weighted sum: Output = 0.72 · V_India + 0.18 · V_capital + ... '___' vector encodes 'I need the capital of India.' But attention can only average existing vectors - it cannot look up a fact. It assembled the question, not the answer. Then FFN recalls: W1 row 847 ('capital-of-India' detector) trained on millions of geography texts: W1[847] · x = 4.2. GELU(4.2) = 4.19, neuron 847 fires. GELU(-1.3) = -0.05, neuron 200 ('capital-of-France') is suppressed. Of 2048 neurons, maybe 100-300 fire meaningfully for any given input. W2 column 847 was trained to shift vectors toward 'Delhi': output += 4.19 · W2[:,847]. The answer is now in the vector. Key insight: W1 rows = 'what to detect', W2 columns = 'what to output' - the key-value memory interpretation. The FFN is a lookup table: 2048 entries, each asking 'does this pattern match?' and outputting the corresponding knowledge.
summary: Uses 'capital of India is ___' to show FFN performing factual recall via a W1 detector row and matching W2 output column (key-value memory).
queries: W1 row detector W2 column writer

text: Attention + FFN Across Multiple Blocks. Example 3: 'She sat by the river bank' - does 'bank' mean money or riverbed? Block 1 (basic context): Attention - 'bank's Query matches 'river's Key (adjacent words). 'bank' absorbs ~40% of 'river's Value, ~20% of 'sat'. FFN sees 'bank + some river context'. W1 row 310 ('river-location') fires weakly at 1.2; W1 row 744 ('financial-institution') fires weakly at 0.8. GELU passes both. W2 nudges slightly toward both meanings. Ambiguity not yet resolved. Block 5 (deeper understanding): Attention - 'bank' attends to 'sat', 'by', 'river' with much higher scores than Block 1, because Blocks 1-4 FFNs already strengthened nature/location features. FFN: row 310 fires at 3.8 (strong match). Row 744 ('financial') fires at -1.1, GELU(-1.1) = -0.03, effectively dead. W2 column 310 shifts vector hard toward geography features; column 744 contributes nothing. 'Bank' now means 'riverbank' - ambiguity resolved. Why multiple blocks: each round, attention provides better context because FFN improved the vectors, and FFN makes better decisions because attention gathered more context. They bootstrap each other across all 96 blocks. Three types of FFN knowledge: (1) Implicit knowledge (area/breadth) - adds related concepts; (2) Factual recall (India/Delhi) - looks up stored facts; (3) Disambiguation (bank/river) - suppresses wrong meanings, boosts right ones.
summary: Traces 'river bank' disambiguation across Blocks 1 and 5 to show attention and FFN bootstrapping each other, then summarizes three FFN knowledge types.

why one block is not enough

text: Deep Questions You Should Be Able to Answer. (1) Why can't attention replace FFN? Attention computes weighted averages of Value vectors - a linear operation. It can mix information from different tokens, but never create new features. 'If river AND bank are both present, output riverbank' requires non-linearity (GELU). Only FFN has that. (2) Why can't FFN replace attention? FFN processes each token in complete isolation. It never sees any other word. Without attention first injecting 'river' into 'bank's vector, FFN would process 'bank' identically in 'river bank' and 'investment bank' - same input, same output, unable to disambiguate. (3) What exactly does 'FFN stores knowledge' mean? Each row of W1 is a pattern detector trained during pre-training. Row 847 learned to fire when input looks like 'capital-of-India.' The corresponding column of W2 learned to output a vector that shifts toward 'Delhi.' This row-column pair is literally one stored fact. GPT-3 has 2048-49152 such detectors per block across 96 blocks. (4) How do researchers prove this isn't a metaphor? Ablation studies: zero out specific FFN neurons and watch 'The capital of India is' stop producing 'Delhi' while grammar stays intact. Activate those same neurons artificially and watch 'Delhi' appear in unrelated contexts. Knowledge is localized to specific neurons - not distributed across the whole network.
summary: Answers four deep questions about why attention and FFN are non-interchangeable and how ablation studies prove FFN neurons store localized facts.
queries: is attention linear or non linear
does FFN need non linearity GELU
what does FFN stores knowledge mean exactly
how many detectors per block GPT-3
linear vs non linear operations transformer

text: Where Do the Parameters Live? Parameters are all the learnable numbers - every weight and bias tuned during training (chapter 1.4). They are the model's memory. More parameters = more capacity to store knowledge. Split between Attention and FFN in one Transformer block (~3.15M parameters total): Attention takes ~1/3 (~1.05M), FFN takes ~2/3 (~2.1M). Attention parameters: W_Q, W_K, W_V (3 matrices that create Queries, Keys, Values - chapter 6.9), W_O (matrix that blends multi-head outputs - chapter 7.12). These learn how to find relationships between words. FFN parameters: W1 (512 x 2048) expands to thinking space, W2 (2048 x 512) compresses back to model size. These learn facts and transformations - the actual knowledge. GPT-3 has 96 blocks: 96 × 3.15M = ~302M just in this layer pattern - and GPT-3's total is 175 billion because d_model is 12,288 (not 512), making each matrix vastly larger.
summary: Quantifies the per-block parameter split (~1/3 attention, ~2/3 FFN) and scales it up to GPT-3's 175B total.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Chapter 8.4 shows why the Feed-Forward Network in a Transformer block can handle any number of input words without changing its weights. The same W1 and W2 matrices process 1 word, 6 words, or 1000 words. Words are stacked as rows of an input matrix and multiplied once: (num_words x d_model) times W1 (d_model x d_ff) gives (num_words x d_ff); GELU; then times W2 (d_ff x d_model) gives back (num_words x d_model). num_words is a free row dimension. Each word is transformed independently by the same weights, which is why the FFN is embarrassingly parallel on GPUs and why KV cache only stores attention K and V, never FFN outputs.
summary: Why FFN weight count is independent of word count, and why that enables GPU parallelism.

One word: 1x3 times 3x6 equals 1x6. W1 has 3 x 6 = 18 weights. These 18 weights are the same for every single word that ever goes through this FFN.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: The trick: stack 3 words into a matrix. With 'I', 'love', 'cats' as three 3-dim vectors, stack into a 3x3 matrix and multiply by the same W1 (3x6) to get a 3x6 output, one row per word. 3x3 times 3x6 equals 3x6. Still the same W1. Still 18 weights. Row 1 of the input only interacts with W to produce row 1 of the output. No mixing between words - that's how matrix multiplication works.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Six words: 'The cat sat on the mat'. Stack the six 3-dim word vectors into a 6x3 matrix and multiply by the same W1 (3x6) to get a 6x6 output, one row per word. 6x3 times 3x6 equals 6x6. The W1 matrix is still 3x6 = 18 weights. Going from 3 words to 6 doesn't change the weight matrix size. Row 1 ('The') hits W and produces its own output. Row 4 ('on') hits the exact same W and produces its own output. The rows never interact. Each word is independently transformed by the same 18 weights.
summary: Six-word example shows W1 stays at 18 weights regardless of going from 3 to 6 words.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: 10 words, 1000 words, same 18 weights. The number of words is just the number of rows in the input matrix. The weight matrix W never changes. 1 word: 1x3 times 3x6 = 1x6. 3 words: 3x3 times 3x6 = 3x6. 6 words: 6x3 times 3x6 = 6x6. 10 words: 10x3 times 3x6 = 10x6. 1000 words: 1000x3 times 3x6 = 1000x6. W1 always = 18 weights. The general pattern: (num_words x d_model) times W1 (d_model x d_ff) = (num_words x d_ff). num_words is a free variable; W1 only cares about d_model (3 in our toy example, 512 in real transformers). In GPT-2 (d_model=512, d_ff=2048), W1 has 512 x 2048 = 1,048,576 weights. That number stays the same whether you feed it 10 words or 100,000 words.
summary: num_words is a free row dimension; W1 only depends on d_model and d_ff, e.g. 512 x 2048 in GPT-2.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Scaling back down: W2 has the inverse shape. After GELU activation, compress back to the original dimension. For 6 words: Input 6x3 times W1 3x6 = Expanded 6x6, GELU 6x6, times W2 6x3 = Output 6x3. Wait, W2 is shaped d_ff x d_model = 6x3. For 10 words: 10x3 times W1 3x6 = 10x6, GELU 10x6, times W2 6x3 = 10x3. Same W1, same W2. The general pattern: Layer 1: (words x d_model) times W1 (d_model x d_ff) = (words x d_ff). GELU: (words x d_ff) -> (words x d_ff). Layer 2: (words x d_ff) times W2 (d_ff x d_model) = (words x d_model). Word count flows through as the row dimension. W1 and W2 only depend on d_model and d_ff. In, expand, compress back out - same size going in and coming out, regardless of how many words.
summary: W2 has the flipped d_ff x d_model shape so FFN expands then compresses back, with word count just flowing through as rows.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Why this matters: massive GPU parallelism. Without stacking (for loop): 'for word in words: output = word x W'. 1000 words = 1000 sequential matrix multiplies. Each waits for the previous. The GPU sits mostly idle because each multiply is tiny (1x3 times 3x6). With stacking (one multiply): 'output = all_words x W'. 1000 words = one massive parallel matrix multiply (1000x3 times 3x6). GPU processes all rows simultaneously across thousands of cores. One instruction, all words done. The deep insight: this parallel trick works because FFN treats each word independently. No word's output depends on any other word. That's the fundamental difference from Attention, which creates cross-word dependencies (Q dot K). You can't parallelize Attention the same way because token 3's output depends on tokens 1 and 2. FFN is embarrassingly parallel - the GPU's dream workload. This is also why the KV cache during generation only caches attention K, V, not FFN outputs. FFN has no cross-word state to remember; run it on the new word, get the answer.
summary: Stacking enables GPU to do one parallel matmul instead of many tiny ones, possible because FFN is token-independent unlike attention.

text: Chapter 8.5 covers the second Add & Norm in a Transformer block - the stabilizer that follows FFN. It mirrors the first Add & Norm: residual addition (FFN input + FFN output) followed by Layer Normalization with gamma and beta. Worked through with 'cats' embedding: FFN input [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] + FFN output [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22] = [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28], then normalized to block output [-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72]. The chapter ends by explaining why Add & Norm appears twice: each transforming sub-layer (Attention, FFN) gets its own residual + Layer Norm to prevent value drift, enabling stacks of 96 blocks.

text: Second Add & Norm sits after FFN in the Transformer block. Pipeline: Multi-Head Attention -> Add & Norm (first) -> FFN -> Add & Norm (second) -> Block Output. FFN just transformed each token's representation, but values drift in deep networks (problem from chapter 8.2), so we stabilize again. This second Add & Norm works exactly like the first - same mechanism - except its input is the FFN output instead of the Attention output.
summary: The second Add & Norm follows FFN in every Transformer block and stabilizes FFN's output the same way the first Add & Norm stabilizes attention's output.

text: The Add step around FFN works the same as in chapter 8.2: keep the original input by adding it back. The input to FFN was the first Add & Norm's output. Worked example for 'cats': FFN input = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Goes through FFN (chapter 8.3). FFN output = [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]. Add step: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] + [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22] = [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28]. The residual connection ensures that even if FFN distorts values, the original signal from Add & Norm #1 survives by being added back.
summary: The Add step around FFN adds FFN's input vector to FFN's output element-wise, preserving the original signal as a residual.

text: Same Layer Normalization formula as chapter 8.2: compute mean, subtract, divide by standard deviation, scale by gamma, shift by beta. Worked example normalizing [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28]: Step 1 mean mu = (-0.85 + -1.28 + 2.00 + 1.28) / 4 = 0.2875. Step 2 variance = 1.9172, sqrt(variance + epsilon) = 1.3846. Step 3 normalize each: (x - mu) / 1.3846 -> -0.85 -> -0.82, -1.28 -> -1.13, 2.00 -> 1.24, 1.28 -> 0.72. Block output for 'cats': [-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72] with gamma=1.0 and beta=0.0 (initial values). The model learns optimal gamma and beta during training.
summary: Layer Norm rescales the post-Add vector using mean, variance, gamma, and beta - same formula as the first Add & Norm but applied to FFN output.

text: Add & Norm appears twice because each Transformer block has two sub-layers: Attention and FFN. Each sub-layer gets its own Add & Norm because each can independently cause value drift. Sub-layer 1: Input x -> Attention(x) -> Add & Norm #1 (stabilizes after attention changes). Sub-layer 2: From A&N #1 -> FFN(x) -> Add & Norm #2 (stabilizes after FFN changes). The Rule: in Transformers, every sub-layer that transforms values gets wrapped with its own residual connection and layer norm. This is what makes it possible to stack 96 blocks deep without the signal degrading. If you only normalized once at the end of the block, FFN's output could already be in a bad range before it gets stabilized.
summary: Each transforming sub-layer (Attention and FFN) needs its own residual + LayerNorm so that value drift from either sub-layer is corrected before stacking 96 blocks deep.

text: Chapter 8.6 explains why Transformer blocks repeat N times. One block only learns simple patterns; deep models stack many identical blocks (called Nx) to build from grammar up to reasoning. Each block runs Attention then Add & Norm then FFN then Add & Norm. Every block has the same structure but its own unique learned weights (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, W1, b1, W2, b2, gamma1, beta1, gamma2, beta2). Real model sizes: GPT-2 Small N=12 (d=768), GPT-2 Medium N=24 (d=1024), GPT-2 Large N=36 (d=1280), GPT-3 N=96 (d=12288). Research on internals shows depth-based specialization: blocks 1-4 surface patterns, 5-12 syntax, 13-24 semantics, 25-96 abstract reasoning. The encoder uses bidirectional attention (no masking).
summary: Transformer blocks stack N times with identical structure but unique weights per block, enabling deep models to learn from grammar to reasoning.

text: One block does not repeat enough. We've seen all 4 steps inside one Transformer block: Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm. But one block can only learn simple patterns. To understand language, the model needs to repeat this entire block many times. Think of it like this: 1 block learns basic word associations ("cat" relates to "sat"); 4 blocks learn grammar structure (subject-verb agreement); 12 blocks learn context and nuance (sarcasm, tone); 96 blocks learn deep reasoning, world knowledge, complex inference. Each repeat of the block refines the token representations further. The output of block 1 becomes the input of block 2, and so on.
summary: One Transformer block is too shallow; repeating it many times unlocks deeper capabilities from word associations up to reasoning.

text: The stack: how many blocks? Different models use different numbers of blocks. In the original paper and in practice, this number is called N (hence "Nx" in architecture diagrams). Real model sizes: GPT-2 Small = 12 blocks, d=768. GPT-2 Medium = 24 blocks, d=1024. GPT-2 Large = 36 blocks, d=1280. GPT-3 = 96 blocks, d=12288. What this means: in GPT-3, every single token in your prompt passes through 96 identical blocks in sequence. Each block runs the full Attention -> Add & Norm -> FFN -> Add & Norm pipeline. That's 96 rounds of attention, 96 rounds of FFN, and 192 Add & Norm operations per token.
summary: Real model block counts and d_model sizes from GPT-2 Small (12 blocks, d=768) up to GPT-3 (96 blocks, d=12288).

text: Same structure, different weights. Every block has the same structure: Attention -> Add & Norm -> FFN -> Add & Norm. But every learnable weight inside each block is unique - learned separately during training. Multi-Head Attention weights: W_Q creates Queries ("what am I looking for?"), W_K creates Keys ("what do I contain?"), W_V creates Values ("what info do I pass along?"), W_O blends all 8 heads back together. Add & Norm #1 weights: gamma1 (learnable scale per dimension), beta1 (learnable shift per dimension). FFN weights: W1, b1 (first layer expands 512 to 2048 dims), W2, b2 (second layer compresses 2048 back to 512). Add & Norm #2 weights: gamma2 and beta2 (separate copies learned independently). Every block has its own copy of ALL these weights. Block 1, Block 2, ... Block 96 each have completely different weights. If they shared the same weights, stacking 96 blocks would be no better than having one - you'd just repeat the same transformation. Each block's unique weights let it specialize: Block 1 learns different patterns than Block 50.
summary: Each block has identical architecture but its own unique copy of W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, W1, W2, gammas, and betas, enabling per-block specialization.

text: What each layer learns - shallow to deep. Research on Transformer internals has revealed a clear pattern: early blocks learn simple things, deep blocks learn abstract things. Blocks 1-4 (Surface Patterns): part-of-speech (noun, verb, adjective), basic grammar and word order, simple co-occurrence ("New" near "York"). Blocks 5-12 (Syntax & Structure): subject-verb agreement across distance, clause boundaries and nesting, pronoun resolution ("she" refers to "Alice"). Blocks 13-24 (Semantics & Meaning): word sense disambiguation ("bank" = river vs money), sentiment and tone detection, entity relationships and facts. Blocks 25-96 (Abstract Reasoning): multi-step inference and logic, world knowledge and common sense, task-specific meaning and generation. This is why depth matters - you need many blocks to build from grammar all the way up to meaning and reasoning. Same principle as deep networks from chapter 1.21, but now each block has both attention (for context) and FFN (for knowledge).
summary: Empirically, early blocks learn POS and grammar, middle blocks handle syntax and semantics, deep blocks do abstract reasoning and world knowledge.

text: The complete picture - token to output through N blocks. Diagram shows the full journey of a single token from input to output: Token Embedding + Positional Encoding (Sections 5.2-5.7) -> Block 1 (Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm) -> Block 2 -> ... -> Block N -> Encoder Output (contextual hidden states for each token). The N blocks are bracketed with an Nx label on the right. Sizes: GPT-2 Small N=12 blocks, 117M parameters. GPT-3 N=96 blocks, 175B parameters. The full recipe of a Transformer encoder: Tokenize -> Embed -> Add positions -> Pass through N blocks (each: Self-Attention -> Add & Norm -> FFN -> Add & Norm) -> Rich contextual hidden states. The encoder uses bidirectional attention - every word can see every other word, no masking. This gives the encoder a full understanding of the entire input.
summary: Full encoder pipeline: embedding plus positional encoding, then N stacked blocks each with attention/Add&Norm/FFN/Add&Norm, producing contextual hidden states with bidirectional attention.

text: Residual connections (skip connections) solve the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks by computing output = F(x) + x. The +x creates a shortcut path whose gradient is always exactly 1, so even when F'(x) shrinks toward zero across 96 layers, the gradient on the skip path arrives at layer 1 undiminished. Every Transformer block has two residual connections (one around Attention, one around FFN), giving GPT-3's 96 blocks 192 gradient highways. Without them, deep Transformers would be untrainable.
summary: Residual connections add input back to layer output so gradients flow through deep stacks via a skip path with derivative 1.

text: The Problem: Gradients Vanish in Deep Networks. From chapter 1.15, gradients shrink as they travel backward through layers because each layer multiplies the gradient by its derivative. If that derivative is below 1.0, the gradient shrinks exponentially. Multiplying through 96 layers: with F'(x)=0.99, 0.99^96 = 0.38 (lose 62% even with near-perfect derivatives). With F'(x)=0.9, 0.9^96 = 0.0000396 (effectively zero by layer 1). With F'(x)=0.5, 0.5^96 = 1.3 x 10^-29 (completely gone, no learning possible). GPT-3 has 96 layers. Without a fix, the gradient arriving at layer 1 from layer 96 would be essentially zero. Layer 1 would never learn. The model would be dead on arrival.
summary: Gradients in deep networks shrink exponentially through layer multiplications, killing learning in the early layers of 96-layer models.

text: The Residual Connection: output = F(x) + x. The fix is deceptively simple: add the input directly to the output. The +x creates a shortcut path that bypasses the entire layer. Path A (Without Residual): input x -> weight1 -> activation -> weight2 -> activation -> output. Gradient must travel through every layer and shrinks at each step. Path B (With Residual): input x -> weight1 -> activation -> weight2 -> activation -> F(x) + x. The +x adds a direct highway that bypasses all layers. The skip arrow runs alongside the layer stack and rejoins at the bottom.
summary: A residual block computes F(x)+x, adding the raw input back to the layer's transformed output to create a shortcut around the layer.
queries: what does output equals F of x plus x mean
how does a skip connection work visually
residual block diagram path A vs path B
what is a shortcut path in a neural network
why add input back to output of layer
residual function F of x explanation

text: Why It Solves Vanishing Gradients - The Math. Take the derivative of the residual output: d/dx [F(x) + x] = F'(x) + 1. F'(x) is the gradient through the layer weights and can shrink toward 0. The +1 is the gradient through the skip connection - ALWAYS exactly 1, no matter what. That +1 is everything. Even if F'(x) approaches 0, the total gradient is at least 1. The gradient through the skip connection is always 1, regardless of what the layer weights do. Without residual: gradient = F'(x); if F'(x) = 0.01, gradient = 0.01. With residual: gradient = F'(x) + 1; if F'(x) = 0.01, gradient = 1.01.
summary: The derivative of F(x)+x equals F'(x)+1, so the +1 from the skip path guarantees the gradient never vanishes even when layer derivatives approach zero.
queries: derivative of F(x) plus x formula
why is residual gradient always at least 1
math proof skip connection prevents vanishing gradient
what is the skip path derivative
why the plus 1 saves gradient flow

text: The Gradient Highway visual. A 6-layer transformer with gradients traveling backward from Layer 6 to Layer 1 via two paths shown side by side at each layer. Through weights: gradient is 0.7^idx, decaying as 1.00, 0.70, 0.49, 0.34, 0.24, 0.17 - lost 83% of the signal across 5 layers. Through skip: always 1.00 at every layer, full signal preserved. Bars visualize the two channels. Conclusion: through weights 0.7^5 = 0.17, through skip = 1.00. The skip path delivers the gradient from layer 6 to layer 1 without any multiplication.
summary: Side-by-side visualization shows the weight path gradient decaying to 0.17 across 6 layers while the skip path stays at 1.00.

text: Why This Is Critical for Transformers. Modern transformers are absurdly deep. Without residual connections, training them would be impossible. GPT-2: 12 layers, 24 gradient highways. GPT-3: 96 layers, 192 gradient highways. GPT-4 (estimated): 120 layers, 240 gradient highways. TWO residual connections per block: every transformer block has two sub-layers (Attention and FFN). Each gets its own residual connection (chapter 8.2). That means GPT-3's 96 blocks have 192 gradient highways - one around every Attention layer and one around every FFN layer. Per-block flow: Input -> Attention + Residual #1 -> FFN + Residual #2 -> Block Output. Without residual connections, gradients would vanish by layer 3. With them, gradients flow freely through all 96 layers on the skip path, letting even the earliest layers learn from the loss signal. This is what makes deep transformers trainable.
summary: Each transformer block has two residual connections (around Attention and FFN), so GPT-3's 96 blocks contain 192 gradient highways enabling deep training.

text: Chapter 8.8 contrasts Post-Norm (the original 2017 'Attention Is All You Need' arrangement where LayerNorm comes after the residual Add) with Pre-Norm (the modern arrangement where LayerNorm comes before each sub-layer and the residual Add receives the raw, unnormalized input). Pre-Norm wins for two reasons: (1) cleaner gradient flow because the skip path is pure addition with derivative 1 at every step instead of passing through LayerNorm, and (2) better training stability that tolerates larger learning rates without careful warmup. Post-Norm appears in the original paper and early BERT; Pre-Norm is used by GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, LLaMA family, Mistral/Mixtral, Claude, and virtually all modern LLMs since ~2019.
summary: Pre-Norm puts LayerNorm before sub-layers leaving a clean residual highway, replacing the original Post-Norm arrangement and now standard in all modern LLMs.

text: Post-Norm (Original 2017 Paper). In the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper, LayerNorm comes after the residual Add. The exact order per sub-layer is: Input x -> Attention(x) -> Add: x + Attention(x) -> LayerNorm -> FFN -> Add: prev + FFN(prev) -> LayerNorm. Key feature: LayerNorm sits after the residual add. The residual path passes through LayerNorm before reaching the next sub-layer.
summary: Post-Norm is the original 2017 transformer ordering where LayerNorm is applied after the residual Add, so the skip path itself flows through LayerNorm.

text: Pre-Norm (Modern Approach). In pre-norm, LayerNorm comes before each sub-layer. The residual add gets the raw, unnormalized input - not the normalized version. Order: Input x -> LayerNorm(x) -> Attention(LayerNorm(x)) -> Add: x + Attention(LayerNorm(x)) -> LayerNorm(prev) -> FFN(LayerNorm(prev)) -> Add: prev + FFN(LayerNorm(prev)). Key difference: the Add step receives the raw input x, not the normalized version. LayerNorm only feeds into the sub-layer, not into the skip path.
summary: Pre-Norm places LayerNorm before each sub-layer so the residual Add operates on the raw input, leaving the skip path untouched by normalization.

text: Side-by-Side Comparison. Trace input [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] (the 'cats' embedding) through the Attention sub-layer in both approaches. Post-Norm: Input x = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] -> Attention(x) = [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4] -> Add: x + Attn = [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] -> LayerNorm = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Norm is last; the skip path passes through Norm. Pre-Norm: Input x = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] -> LayerNorm(x) = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] -> Attn(LN(x)) = [0.28, -0.12, 0.18, 0.38] -> Add: x + Attn = [-0.22, 0.18, 0.88, 0.98]. Norm is first; the skip path is just pure addition. Critical difference: in post-norm the skip path goes through LayerNorm; in pre-norm the skip path is pure addition - no transformations on the highway.

text: Why Pre-Norm Wins. Two key advantages make pre-norm the clear winner for deep models. (1) Cleaner Gradient Flow: in post-norm, the gradient on the skip path must pass through LayerNorm at every block. LayerNorm has its own derivative that modifies the gradient - not as bad as vanishing, but it adds noise and makes optimization harder. In pre-norm, the skip path is pure addition. The gradient flows from the last block to the first block on a clean highway with derivative = 1 at every step. No LayerNorm interference on the residual stream. (2) Training Stability: post-norm requires very careful learning rate warmup - start with a tiny learning rate and slowly increase it over thousands of steps. Without warmup, gradients in early training are unstable and the model diverges (loss goes to infinity). Pre-norm is more forgiving. It works with larger learning rates from the start. The clean gradient highway stabilizes training even without careful warmup schedules. This makes training faster and less finicky.
summary: Pre-norm wins because the residual highway has derivative exactly 1 (clean gradient flow) and because training tolerates larger learning rates without delicate warmup schedules.

text: Who Uses What. Post-Norm: 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017), early BERT models, some early translation models - mainly historical, used in the original paper. Pre-Norm: GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, LLaMA / LLaMA 2 / LLaMA 3, Mistral, Mixtral, Claude, virtually all modern LLMs - the industry standard since ~2019. The Field Converged: the shift happened because pre-norm trains faster, scales better to deep models, and is more robust. When you hear about a modern LLM with 96+ layers, it is almost certainly using pre-norm. The original paper used post-norm, but practice proved pre-norm superior for large-scale training.
summary: Post-norm appears in the 2017 paper and early BERT, while pre-norm is used by GPT-2/3/4, LLaMA, Mistral, Mixtral, Claude, and effectively every modern LLM since 2019.

text: Chapter 8.9 contrasts Batch Normalization and Layer Normalization. Batch Norm (Ioffe and Szegedy, 2015) normalizes across the batch dimension - for each feature, it computes mean and variance across all examples in the batch. It works for CNNs because images share structure, have fixed sizes, and provide meaningful per-position statistics, and it sped up CNN training 10-14x. But Batch Norm fails for language because sequences have variable lengths (position 5 may not exist in shorter sentences), inference batch size is often 1 (no batch to compute statistics over), and tokens at the same position across different sentences share nothing semantically. Layer Norm (Ba et al., 2016) instead normalizes across the feature dimension - each token computes mean and variance across its own features, independent of the batch. This is why Transformers use Layer Norm: each token is its own universe, normalization works at any batch size or sequence length, and statistics are semantically meaningful per token.
summary: Compares Batch Norm (normalizes down the batch column, used in CNNs) with Layer Norm (normalizes across one token's features, used in Transformers) and explains why language demands Layer Norm.

text: Batch Normalization (Ioffe and Szegedy, 2015) normalizes across the batch dimension. Given a batch of examples, for each feature, compute the mean and variance across all examples in the batch. Concrete grid: a batch of 4 examples, each with 3 features. Example 1 = [0.5, 1.2, -0.3]. Example 2 = [0.8, 0.9, 0.1]. Example 3 = [0.3, 1.5, -0.5]. Example 4 = [0.6, 0.6, 0.2]. Batch Norm picks one feature column and computes mean and variance across the 4 examples. For Feature 2: mean = (1.2 + 0.9 + 1.5 + 0.6) / 4 = 1.05. Then it normalizes each example's value for that feature using that mean and variance.
summary: Defines Batch Normalization as normalizing each feature column across all examples in the batch, with a worked 4-example, 3-feature example computing mean 1.05 for Feature 2.

text: Why Batch Norm works for images and CNNs. Three reasons. (1) Shared statistics: images in a batch share similar structure - the feature 'edge detector at position (3,5)' has a meaningful distribution across a batch of cat photos because they all have edges in roughly similar places. (2) Fixed input size: images are always the same dimensions (e.g., 224x224). Position (3,5) means the same thing in every image, so computing statistics across the batch at that position makes sense. (3) Training speedup: BN reduced internal covariate shift (layer inputs changing distribution during training), letting CNNs train 10-14x faster, and it acted as a mild regularizer. Batch Norm revolutionized CNN training in 2015. But language is a fundamentally different domain.
summary: Explains why Batch Norm succeeds for CNNs: shared image structure across batch, fixed 224x224 input size, and 10-14x training speedup via reduced internal covariate shift.

text: Why Batch Norm fails for language. Three fundamental problems. (1) Variable Sequence Lengths: sentences have different lengths. In a batch of 4 sentences - 'I love cats' (3 tokens), 'The cat sat on the mat last week' (8 tokens), 'Hello' (1 token), 'Deep learning is fascinating' (4 tokens) - what is 'position 5' across this batch? Only one sentence even has a position 5. Normalizing across the batch at position 5 is meaningless because there's nothing to compare. (2) Batch Size 1 at Inference: when you chat with an LLM, your prompt is batch size 1. There's no batch to compute statistics over. Batch Norm must fall back to running averages stored from training - a fragile approximation that can drift from the true distribution. (3) Tokens Are Fundamentally Different: normalizing feature 42 across 'the', 'cat', 'sat', and 'love' from different sentences treats them as comparable, but they're completely different words with different roles. Unlike pixels at the same position in different images, tokens at position 3 across different sentences share nothing semantically.
summary: Lists three reasons Batch Norm fails for language: variable sequence lengths make positions non-comparable, batch size 1 at inference forces fragile running averages, and tokens at the same position across sentences share no semantic meaning.

text: Layer Normalization (Ba et al., 2016) normalizes across the feature dimension. For each token independently, compute mean and variance across its own features. Same 4x3 grid as before. Layer Norm picks one example row and computes mean and variance across its 3 features. For Example 2 = [0.8, 0.9, 0.1]: mean = (0.8 + 0.9 + 0.1) / 3 = 0.60. Each token normalizes itself independently. Three properties matter. No batch dependency: each token normalizes using only its own features, works with batch size 1. Any sequence length: token 5 normalizes itself the same way whether the sentence has 5 or 500 tokens. Semantically meaningful: normalizing across a single token's features rescales its representation without mixing different words' statistics.
summary: Defines Layer Normalization as per-token normalization across feature dimensions, with worked example mean 0.60 for [0.8, 0.9, 0.1], highlighting batch independence, length independence, and semantic validity.

text: The key difference: which dimension is normalized? Batch Norm normalizes down columns (across examples in the batch); the highlighted column = one BN operation. Properties: needs a batch of examples, used in CNNs (image models), fragile at batch size 1. Layer Norm normalizes across rows (across features of one token); the highlighted row = one LN operation. Properties: independent per token, used in Transformers (LLMs), works with any batch size. The Key Insight: Layer Norm treats each token as its own universe. Its mean and variance depend only on that token's own feature values - not on what other sentences happen to be in the same batch, not on how long other sequences are, and not on what other tokens exist. This independence is exactly what Transformers need: each token's normalization should be a property of that token alone.
summary: Side-by-side summary: Batch Norm normalizes down columns (CNNs, fragile at batch=1), Layer Norm normalizes across rows (Transformers, batch-independent), with each token as its own universe.

text: Chapter 9.1 Decoder-Only - How Modern LLMs Work explains why ChatGPT, Claude, LLaMA, and Gemini use only the decoder half of the original 2017 Transformer. Translation needs encoder + decoder because input (English) and output (Hindi) are different languages. Chatting is one continuous English stream so encoder is unnecessary. Decoder-only relies on causal masking: token N attends only to tokens 0..N-1. The chapter compares three architectures: Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART) for translation, Encoder-Only (BERT, RoBERTa) for classification, Decoder-Only (GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini) for generation. Concludes the CNN -> RNN -> Transformer -> Decoder-only evolution.
summary: Modern LLMs use only the Transformer decoder half with causal masking because chatting is one continuous stream, not two-language translation.

text: Plot twist: Modern LLMs are decoder-only. ChatGPT, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini all use only the Decoder half. They threw away the Encoder. Why throw away half the architecture? Because translation and chatting are fundamentally different.
summary: Modern LLMs like ChatGPT, Claude, LLaMA, and Gemini drop the encoder and use only the decoder.

text: Translation vs Chatting: different tasks. Translation: input (English) and output (Hindi) are different languages - you must fully process the input before generating output. Chatting: input (your prompt) and output (my response) are both English. They are part of one continuous conversation, not two separate things. Translation has two separate sides: English input (complete) goes to Hindi output (generated). Chatting is one continuous stream: prompt 'What is 2+2?' to response 'The answer is 4.'
summary: Translation needs two-sided encoder-decoder because languages differ; chatting is one continuous English stream so a single decoder suffices.
queries: is chatting one stream or two
two languages vs one language transformer

text: Causal masking: the key difference. In a decoder-only model, when generating token N, you can only look at tokens 0 through N-1 (past and present). You cannot look at future tokens. This is called causal masking - causality: effect cannot precede cause. Processing 'What is 2+2?' sequentially: Step 1 generate 'What' looking at nothing []; Step 2 generate 'is' looking at [What]; Step 3 generate '2+2' looking at [What, is]; Step 4 generate '?' looking at [What, is, 2+2]. This is the fundamental constraint of LLMs: they generate one token at a time, and each new token is based only on what came before.
summary: Causal masking forces each generated token to attend only to previous tokens, never the future.
queries: why can't LLM see future tokens
token N attends to which tokens
why no future attention in GPT
why one token at a time

text: Why decoder-only wins. Decoder-only is simpler (half the architecture) but a large decoder trained on enough data can: 1) Read and understand your prompt (implicit encoder task), 2) Generate natural, coherent responses (explicit decoder task), 3) Reason, code, translate, summarize (with prompting/fine-tuning). The result: decoder-only models are more general-purpose than any other architecture. They are simpler, faster, and more scalable.
summary: A large decoder-only model implicitly handles understanding and generation, making it simpler, faster, and more general-purpose than encoder-decoder.

text: Three architectures: the complete picture. Encoder-Decoder (E + D): task Translation; models T5, BART, mT5; best for when input and output are different languages or modes. Encoder-Only (E): task Understanding; models BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT; best for classification, sentiment, similarity - no generation. Decoder-Only (D): task Generation; models GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini; best for chat, writing, reasoning, all modern LLMs.
summary: Three transformer flavors map to three task types: encoder-decoder for translation (T5/BART), encoder-only for understanding (BERT), decoder-only for generation (GPT/Claude).
queries: BERT vs GPT vs T5 architecture

text: You now understand the complete evolution: CNN (local patterns) -> RNN (sequential memory, slow) -> Transformer (attention, parallel) -> Decoder-only (the architecture of modern AI). You now have the full history. The upcoming sections go deep into each piece of the Transformer: how input enters, how attention works, and how the complete encoder and decoder blocks are built.
summary: Recap of the architectural evolution from CNN to RNN to Transformer to decoder-only LLMs.

text: Chapter 9.2 explains causal masking in transformer attention. During text generation, future tokens don't exist yet, but during training the model sees the whole sentence at once for efficiency. The causal mask artificially hides future positions by adding -∞ to the upper triangle of the Q·K^T/√d_k score matrix, so softmax turns those scores into exactly 0. The chapter walks through the score matrix, the mask matrix, masked scores, softmax output, bidirectional vs causal comparison, the training insight that masking lets N tokens produce N honest next-token predictions in one forward pass, and the three architectures: encoder-only (BERT, no mask, bidirectional understanding), decoder-only (GPT/Claude/LLaMA, causal mask, generation), and encoder-decoder (T5/BART, both plus cross-attention).
summary: Causal masking hides future tokens by adding -∞ to upper triangle of score matrix so softmax produces 0; enables honest training and powers encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures.

text: The problem: during generation, future words don't exist yet. When the model generates text one token at a time, it has only produced the tokens so far - the next word hasn't been generated, so it can't attend to something that doesn't exist. Generating 'The cat sat on the mat': step 1 'The' can only see itself and predicts 'cat'; step 2 'cat' sees 'The' and itself and predicts 'sat'; step 3 'sat' sees 'The', 'cat', and itself and predicts 'on'; step 4 'on' sees all 4 previous tokens and predicts 'the'. But during training, the model sees the entire sentence at once for efficiency - so we need to artificially hide future tokens. That's what the causal mask does.
summary: Generation produces one token at a time so future tokens don't exist, but training feeds the whole sentence at once - causal mask artificially recreates the generation constraint during training.

text: Who can look at whom? Each row is a word asking 'who can I attend to?' Each column is a word being looked at. A word can only see words generated before it plus itself. Using words ['The', 'cat', 'sat', 'on']: 'The' (1st) - only itself exists; 'cat' (2nd) sees 'The' + itself; 'sat' (3rd) sees 'The', 'cat' + itself; 'on' (4th) sees everything before + itself. The diagonal = each word seeing itself (always allowed). Below diagonal = looking at earlier words (allowed - they already exist). Above diagonal = looking at future words (forbidden - not generated yet). The actual Q·K^T/√d_k score matrix is shown with the same color coding: green cells (past + self, allowed) keep their scores, red cells (future) must be blocked. Raw scores row 1: [2.1, 0.5, 0.3, 0.8]; row 2: [1.2, 2.4, 0.7, 0.4]; row 3: [0.6, 1.8, 2.0, 1.1]; row 4: [0.9, 0.3, 1.5, 2.3].
summary: Lower triangle plus diagonal of the score matrix is allowed (past + self), upper triangle is forbidden (future tokens) - shown as checkmarks/crosses and color-coded scores.

text:
The mask: a triangle of negative infinity. We add a mask matrix to the scores. The mask has 0 where a word is allowed to look, and -∞ where it's forbidden. Formula: masked_scores = scores + mask, where mask[i][j] = 0 if j ≤ i, else -∞. Adding -∞ to a score makes it -∞. When softmax sees -∞, it outputs exactly 0 - zero attention, as if that word doesn't exist. The lower triangle (0s) keeps scores unchanged. For 4 tokens the mask matrix is:
row 'The' → [0, -∞, -∞, -∞]
row 'cat' → [0, 0, -∞, -∞]
row 'sat' → [0, 0, 0, -∞]
row 'on'  → [0, 0, 0, 0]

text: Softmax turns masked scores into attention weights (probabilities). Applying softmax row-by-row to the masked scores yields the attention weight matrix. 'The' puts 100% attention on itself: [1.00, 0.00, 0.00, 0.00]. 'cat' splits between 'The' and itself with weights summing to 1.0 across visible tokens only. 'sat' splits across 'The', 'cat', 'sat'. 'on' attends across all 4 tokens. Every -∞ became exactly 0.00 after softmax. Each row still sums to 1.0 - but only across the visible (past + self) tokens. The future positions are dead.
summary: Row-wise softmax on masked scores produces attention probabilities where future positions are 0.00 and each row sums to 1.0 across visible tokens only.
