Left: 'No mask (bidirectional)' shows full 4x4 grid where every word sees every word - all 16 cells active with softmax probabilities. Right: 'Causal mask' shows only the lower triangle filled with probabilities, upper triangle dark/zero - each word sees only past + self. Same Q, K, V matrices, same formula, same softmax. The mask is a single triangle of -∞ values and it completely changes what the model can do.

text: The Training Insight: Every Position Makes a Prediction. 4 tokens go in, 4 output vectors come out - always, regardless of masking. Each output vector v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4 passes through Linear + Softmax to predict the next token. The mask determines whether those predictions are honest. Without mask (bidirectional): every position saw the full sequence including the answer it was supposed to predict → 0 usable training examples, every prediction 'saw the answer'. With causal mask: position 1 ('The') saw ['The'] → predicts 'cat' (HONEST); position 2 ('cat') saw ['The','cat'] → predicts 'sat' (HONEST); position 3 ('sat') saw ['The','cat','sat'] → predicts 'on' (HONEST); position 4 ('on') saw all 4 → predicts 'the' (HONEST). Result: 4 usable training examples from one forward pass. This is why causal masking exists during training - from one forward pass with N tokens you get N honest next-token predictions. Without the mask you'd get zero - the model would 'cheat' at every position, then completely fail at actual generation.
summary: Causal mask gives N honest next-token training examples from one forward pass; without it the model cheats by seeing the answer and fails at generation.

text: Encoder-only architecture - BERT, RoBERTa. No mask - every word sees every other word (bidirectional). The attention grid is the full 4x4 matrix with all 16 cells active. Why? The encoder's job is to understand the full input. When classifying 'The bank by the river', 'bank' NEEDS to see 'river' (which comes after it) to know it means riverbank, not a financial bank. Hiding future words would cripple understanding. Used for: text classification, sentiment analysis, question answering, named entity recognition.
summary: Encoder-only models like BERT use no mask so every token sees every other token bidirectionally - needed for full-input understanding tasks like classification and NER.
queries: why does BERT use no mask
why does bank need to see river
why no causal mask for understanding

text: Decoder-only architecture - GPT, Claude, LLaMA. Causal mask - each word only sees past words + itself. The attention grid shows only the lower triangle active (10 cells of 16 for 4 tokens), upper triangle masked. Why? The decoder generates text one token at a time. At generation step 3, token 4 doesn't exist yet - attending to it would be looking at something that isn't there. During training, the full sentence is fed at once for speed, so we use the mask to simulate the generation condition. Without it, the model would 'cheat' by seeing the answer, then fail at actual generation. Used for: text generation, chatbots, code completion, reasoning - all modern LLMs.
summary: Decoder-only LLMs (GPT, Claude, LLaMA) use causal mask so each token sees only past + self - matches generation constraint and enables N honest predictions during training.
queries: why does GPT use causal mask
why mask simulates generation during training
all modern LLMs use what mask

text: Encoder-decoder architecture - Original Transformer, T5, BART. Both - encoder uses no mask, decoder uses causal mask, plus cross-attention connecting them. Encoder shows full 4x4 matrix, decoder shows lower triangle, joined by '+'. Why? The input (e.g., English sentence) already exists fully - the encoder should see all of it. But the output (e.g., Hindi translation) is generated word by word - the decoder must not peek ahead. Cross-attention lets the decoder look at the encoder's full understanding while still generating left-to-right. Used for: translation, summarization, any 'input → different output' task. The core rule: if the text already exists (input you're reading) → no mask needed, let every word see everything for maximum understanding. If the text is being generated (output you're writing) → causal mask required, because future words don't exist yet.
summary: Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART, original Transformer) use no mask in encoder + causal mask in decoder + cross-attention; core rule: existing text uses no mask, generated text uses causal mask.

text: Cross-attention is the encoder-decoder bridge in a Transformer. The decoder produces the Query (Q) while the encoder's final output produces the Key (K) and Value (V). The same Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(Q·K^T / sqrt(d_k)) · V formula applies, but the sources differ. The encoder runs once; K and V are projected once from its final layer output and cached, then reused by every decoder layer. Each decoder layer computes a new Q (since its representation refines layer by layer) while K and V stay identical. The score matrix is rectangular (M decoder tokens x N encoder tokens) rather than square. Decoder-only models like GPT, Claude, LLaMA have no cross-attention; encoder-decoder models like the original Transformer, T5, and BART use cross-attention in every decoder layer between masked self-attention and the feed-forward network.
summary: Cross-attention links decoder Queries to encoder Keys/Values using the standard attention formula with rectangular score matrices.

text: Setup: translating "I love cats" into Hindi "Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain". The encoder has already processed the English sentence, so each English word is now a rich contextual 768-dimensional vector - "love" doesn't just mean love, it means love-in-the-context-of-I-love-cats. The encoder's final output (3 vectors, one per English token) feeds into the decoder's cross-attention. The decoder is generating Hindi word by word; the first three Hindi tokens (Mujhe, billiyaan, pasand) are generated and the fourth (hain) is next. The decoder's self-attention lets Hindi tokens look at other Hindi tokens, but to look at the English source the decoder needs cross-attention.
summary: Motivates cross-attention with an English-to-Hindi translation where the decoder must look at the encoder's contextual English vectors.

text: Key idea: Q from decoder, K and V from encoder. In self-attention, Q, K, and V all come from the same sentence. In cross-attention they come from different sources. Query (Q) comes from the Decoder (Hindi) - the decoder is asking "what English word should I pay attention to right now?" Key (K) comes from the Encoder (English) - the encoder's words offer their labels for matching ("I'm the subject", "I'm the verb"). Value (V) also comes from the Encoder (English) - the encoder's words offer their actual content, the rich contextual meaning to borrow from. The formula is identical: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(Q·K^T / sqrt(d_k)) · V. Only the source of Q, K, V changes.
summary: Cross-attention sources Q from the decoder and both K and V from the encoder, while keeping the same attention formula as self-attention.

text: Concrete trace: each Hindi word's Query asks "which English word is most relevant to me?" The score matrix is not square - it is 4 Hindi rows x 3 English columns. Apply softmax per row to get attention weights. Result: "Mujhe" puts most weight on "I" (subject/experiencer), "billiyaan" on "cats" (direct object), "pasand" on "love" (core verb meaning), "hain" on "love" (auxiliary aligns with verb to complete predicate). Rectangular 4x3 matrix shows decoder tokens attending to encoder tokens.

text: Self-attention vs cross-attention comparison. Self-attention: Q, K, V all from the same sentence; words attend to each other within one language; score matrix is square (N x N). Cross-attention: Q from decoder, K and V from encoder; decoder attends to encoder's output; score matrix is rectangular (M x N). Each decoder layer has BOTH types of attention: (1) Masked self-attention - Hindi words look at other Hindi words with a causal mask; (2) Cross-attention - Hindi words look at English words with no mask, because the full English sentence already exists.
summary: Side-by-side contrast: self-attention is square within one sentence, cross-attention is rectangular between decoder and encoder, and decoder layers use both.

text: Critical detail: the encoder runs ONCE. A common misconception is that encoder layer 1 feeds decoder layer 1, encoder layer 2 feeds decoder layer 2, and so on. That is wrong - there is no layer-to-layer mapping. CORRECT: only the final encoder layer matters. Encoder runs all 6 layers once on "I love cats", producing 3 contextual vectors from layer 6. K and V are computed once from that final output using W_K and W_V projection matrices, then cached and reused. Decoder layers 1 through 6 each compute their own Q (because the decoder's representation changes layer by layer as it gets refined), but K and V are always the same encoder final output.
summary: The encoder is not paired layer-by-layer with the decoder; only its final layer's K and V are computed once and reused by every decoder layer.

Settings: d_model = 512, d_k = 64, 8 heads.

text: What happens at each decoder layer: every layer performs cross-attention with the exact same K and V but a different Q. Layer 1: Attn(Q_1, K, V) = softmax(Q_1 · K^T / sqrt(64)) · V. Layer 2: same K, V. ... Layer 6: Attn(Q_6, K, V) = softmax(Q_6 · K^T / sqrt(64)) · V. Q_1 comes from the decoder's layer 1 output; Q_6 comes from layer 6's output. Q captures "what I'm looking for" at each stage of refinement; K and V capture "what the encoder said" and never change. Dimension walkthrough (d_model = 512, heads = 8, d_k = 64): Encoder output [3 x 512] (3 English tokens, 512 dims each); W_K projection [512 x 64] (per head, learned); K cached [3 x 64]; V cached [3 x 64]; Decoder layer n [4 x 512] (4 Hindi tokens); Q_n per layer [4 x 64] (changes each layer); Q_n · K^T scores [4 x 3] (every Hindi word scores every English word). Efficiency: encoder runs once, K and V computed once, only Q changes per decoder layer - for a 1000-token input this saves computing K and V six times, a 6x reduction in encoder-side computation. Each decoder layer has 6 separate learned matrices: W_Q, W_K, W_V for masked self-attention (Q,K,V all from Hindi) plus W_Q, W_K, W_V for cross-attention (Q from Hindi, K,V from English). All learned independently.
summary: Per-layer formula and dimension table showing K,V fixed and Q_n changing, plus efficiency gains and the 6 distinct weight matrices each decoder layer learns.

text: Where does cross-attention live? Decoder-Only models (GPT, Claude, LLaMA) have NO cross-attention at all - there's no encoder, so nothing to cross-attend to. The decoder handles everything with just self-attention; prompt and response are one continuous sequence. Encoder-Decoder models (original Transformer, T5, BART) have cross-attention in every decoder layer. Each such decoder layer has three sub-layers in order: (1) Masked self-attention - Hindi looks at Hindi (causal); (2) Cross-attention - Hindi looks at English (full, no mask); (3) Feed-forward network - process each position independently. This matches the architecture diagram from chapter 5.1 where the green "K, V from encoder" arrow connects to the cross-attention layer. Cross-attention is elegant but optional - modern LLMs prove world-class results are possible without it, but for tasks with clear input/output separation (translation, summarization) the encoder-decoder architecture with cross-attention remains powerful.
summary: Decoder-only LLMs skip cross-attention entirely; encoder-decoder models like T5 and BART place it as the middle sub-layer in every decoder layer.

text: Chapter 9.4 explains the Transformer's output head: the final stage that converts a 512-dim hidden state into a predicted token. It covers the Linear layer (a 512 x 50,257 weight matrix W producing logits via dot products with each vocabulary word), Softmax (exponentiating logits and normalizing so probabilities sum to 1), Temperature scaling (dividing logits by T to control how peaked the distribution is), Sampling strategies (Greedy, Top-k, Top-p / nucleus), Weight tying (reusing the embedding matrix transposed as W to save 25.7M parameters), and how the head behaves differently during training (every position predicts) vs inference (only the last position).
summary: Chapter-level overview of the Transformer output head: Linear, softmax, temperature, sampling, weight tying, and training vs inference behavior.

text: After all transformer layers, every position holds a rich, context-aware vector of 512 numbers. This vector encodes everything the model understood about that position. But we need an actual word, not 512 numbers. The model's vocab has 50,257 possible tokens. How does it choose one? Decoder output is [0.82, -0.31, 0.57, 0.14, ...] (512 numbers); we must map this to one of 50,257 words. That is exactly what the output head does. It has two parts: a Linear layer and Softmax. These are the two blocks sitting at the very top of the architecture diagram.
summary: Frames the gap: a 512-dim hidden state must be turned into one of 50,257 vocabulary words via the Linear + Softmax output head.

text: The Linear layer is a weight matrix W with 512 rows and 50,257 columns. Each column represents one word in the vocabulary. The matrix multiplication: hidden state [1 x 512] times W [512 x 50,257] equals logits [1 x 50,257]. One matrix multiply. That is it. No activation function, no non-linearity - just multiply the 512-dim hidden state by this giant matrix and you get 50,257 raw scores, one for every word in the vocabulary. This single matrix has 512 x 50,257 = 25.7 million parameters. It is the second largest weight matrix in the model (after the embedding table).
summary: The Linear layer is a single 512 x 50,257 matrix multiply with no activation, producing one raw logit per vocabulary word.

text: Each logit is a dot product between the hidden state and one column of W. A high dot product means the hidden state is pointing in the same direction as that word's template. Computing one logit: hidden state [0.82, -0.31, 0.57, 0.14, ...]; W column for 'cats' [0.71, -0.25, 0.63, 0.08, ...]; logit = (0.82)(0.71) + (-0.31)(-0.25) + (0.57)(0.63) + ... = 4.2. This happens for all 50,257 words simultaneously: cats +4.2, dogs +3.1, the +1.5, love -0.3, banana -2.1, ... and 50,252 more words. High logit = the model's hidden state is similar to that word's representation. Low or negative = dissimilar. But these raw numbers are hard to interpret - we need probabilities.
summary: Each logit is a dot product between the hidden state and a vocabulary column of W; worked example shows cats=4.2, dogs=3.1, banana=-2.1.

text: Raw logits can be any number: positive, negative, huge, tiny. Softmax converts them into probabilities that are all positive and sum to exactly 1. The softmax formula: P(word_i) = e^(logit_i) / sum over j of e^(logit_j). Exponentiate each logit, then divide by the sum of all exponentiated logits. Watch the transformation: raw logits cats +4.2, dogs +3.1, the +1.5, love -0.3, banana -2.1 become after softmax cats 70.8%, dogs 23.6%, the 4.8%, love 0.8%, banana 0.1%. Notice how softmax amplifies differences. A logit gap of just 1.1 between 'cats' (4.2) and 'dogs' (3.1) becomes a 3x probability difference (71% vs 24%). The exponential function makes the winner win big.
summary: Softmax formula P(i)=e^logit_i / sum e^logit_j turns raw logits into a probability distribution and amplifies gaps between top scores.

text: Before softmax, divide each logit by a number called temperature (T). This controls how peaked or spread out the resulting probabilities are. Formula: softmax( logit / temperature ). T = 0.2 (Focused, almost deterministic): cats 100%, dogs 0%, the 0%. T = 1.0 (Default, balanced): cats 71%, dogs 24%, the 5%. T = 2.0 (Creative, more spread out): cats 50%, dogs 29%, the 13%. Low temperature (0.2): divides logits by 0.2, making differences 5x larger before softmax. The top word dominates. High temperature (2.0): divides by 2.0, shrinking differences. More words get a fair chance. Temperature = 0 would be pure greedy (pick the top word every time).
summary: Temperature T divides logits before softmax; low T sharpens to greedy, high T flattens for creativity, T=0 is pure greedy.

text: After softmax gives us probabilities, we still need to decide which word to actually pick. Three main strategies. Greedy decoding: always pick the highest probability word. Simple but boring - same input always produces same output (e.g., always pick cats 71%). Top-k sampling (e.g., k = 3): keep only the k highest-probability words, re-normalize their probabilities, randomly sample from them. Balances quality with variety. Example with k=3: keep cats 71%, dogs 24%, the 5%; drop love 1%. Top-p sampling / nucleus (e.g., p = 0.9): keep adding words (highest probability first) until cumulative probability reaches p. More adaptive than Top-k - uses fewer words when model is confident, more when uncertain. Example: cats 71% (cum 71%), dogs 24% (cum 95%) - stop at 95%. In practice, most APIs combine temperature + Top-p. ChatGPT defaults: temperature = 1.0, Top-p = 1.0. For more focused output: temperature = 0.7, Top-p = 0.9.
summary: Three sampling strategies - Greedy, Top-k, Top-p (nucleus) - decide which word to pick from the probability distribution; APIs typically combine temperature with Top-p.

text: Surprising fact: the output Linear layer's weight matrix W is the same matrix as the embedding table from the input - just transposed. Embedding (input): word -> vector, shape [50,257 x 512]. Linear (output): vector -> word scores, shape [512 x 50,257]. SAME MATRIX. Why does it work? The embedding table maps each word to a point in 512-dim space. The output layer asks: 'which word's point is closest to this hidden state?' That is the same operation in reverse. If embedding for 'cats' is [0.71, -0.25, 0.63, ...] and hidden state is [0.82, -0.31, 0.57, ...], the dot product is high because they point in similar directions. The model is literally asking: which embedding vector is this hidden state most similar to? Parameter savings: without tying, 50,257 x 512 = 25.7M extra parameters. With tying: zero extra parameters - the output layer reuses the embedding matrix. GPT-2 and most modern LLMs use this trick.
summary: Weight tying reuses the input embedding matrix (transposed) as the output Linear weights, saving 25.7M parameters because both ops measure similarity in the same space.

text: Complete output pipeline end to end: hidden state (512 dims) -> Linear (W transposed, 512 -> 50,257) -> logits (50,257 scores) -> divide by T (temperature) -> softmax (probabilities) -> sample (Top-k / Top-p) -> next token ('cats'). During training: the model computes this output head at every position simultaneously. At position 1 ('I'), it should predict 'love'. At position 2 ('love'), it should predict 'cats'. Cross-entropy loss measures how wrong each prediction was, and backprop updates the weights. During inference: only the last position's hidden state goes through the output head. That is the position where the model predicts the next token. This single prediction gets sampled, appended to the input, and the whole process repeats - one token at a time. Big picture: the entire transformer - embeddings, positional encoding, attention, FFN, layer stacking - exists to produce one good hidden state at the final position. The output head then converts that single 512-dim vector into a probability distribution over 50,257 words. One matrix multiply, one softmax.
summary: End-to-end output pipeline (Linear -> /T -> softmax -> sample) plus contrast between training (all positions, cross-entropy) and inference (only the last position, then loop).

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Chapter 9.5 walks through what a Transformer actually does end-to-end on the example sentence "I love cats". Six pipeline steps: (1) tokens become embedding vectors that capture meaning in isolation; (2) positional encoding is added so order matters; (3) self-attention via Q/K/V lets each word look at others and reshapes its vector by context; (4) Add and Norm plus a feed-forward network refine each word independently with residual connections; (5) the block repeats N times (GPT-2 has 12 layers, GPT-3 has 96, LLaMA 3 70B has 80) with early layers learning syntax and later layers learning semantics and reasoning; (6) the last position's vector goes through Linear plus Softmax to produce a probability distribution over the vocabulary, predicting the next token ("very" at 35%). The key insight: a generic embedding for "love" exits as a context-specific vector meaning "first-person affection directed at cats."
summary: End-to-end six-step Transformer pipeline from tokens to next-token prediction using "I love cats."

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: What is a Transformer actually doing? You give it a sentence: "I love cats". It needs to understand what each word means in context. The word "love" alone could mean many things - romantic love, love of food, a tennis score. But in "I love cats" it specifically means personal affection for cats. The Transformer figures this out through a pipeline of steps. Visual: three colored word boxes (I red, love purple, cats cyan) flow into a green "Contextual Understanding" box.
summary: The Transformer's job is to turn each word into a context-specific meaning, like resolving "love" to affection-for-cats.

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 1: Each word becomes a vector (embedding). The model's embedding table maps each token to a vector of numbers. These numbers capture meaning in isolation - "love" doesn't know about "I" or "cats" yet. Examples shown with 4 numbers each (real models use 512 dims): "I" -> [0.2, -0.5, 0.8, 0.1, ...]; "love" -> [0.9, 0.1, -0.3, 0.6, ...]; "cats" -> [0.4, 0.7, 0.2, -0.4, ...]. Similar words get similar vectors - "cat" and "dog" are nearby in the 512-dimensional space. But "love" here is generic - it could be any kind of love.
summary: Embeddings convert each token into a 512-dim vector that encodes meaning but not yet context.
queries: what is an embedding in a transformer
what does an embedding table do
why do similar words have similar embeddings
why is love generic without context
do embeddings know about other words

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 2: Add position information. Without position, "I love cats" and "cats love I" look identical to the model - same words, same embeddings. Order matters. Positional encoding adds WHERE each word sits in the sentence. Example for "love" at position 1: embedding [0.9, 0.1, -0.3, ...] + position [0.84, 0.54, 0.00, ...] = positioned [1.74, 0.64, -0.30, ...]. Result: each word now knows WHAT it means AND WHERE it sits. "love" at position 1 is different from "love" at position 5.
summary: Adding a positional encoding vector to each embedding injects word order so identical words at different positions become distinguishable.
queries: why do transformers need positional encoding
I love cats vs cats love I
how is position added to embeddings
why same words different positions matter

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 3: Self-attention - words look at each other. This is the magic step. Each word sends a Query ("what am I looking for?"), every word offers a Key ("here is what I can offer"), and the model computes attention scores - how relevant is each word to every other? Attention scores for "I love cats" (rows = querying word, columns = attended-to word): I -> [I:0.60, love:0.25, cats:0.15]; love -> [I:0.15, love:0.30, cats:0.55]; cats -> [I:0.10, love:0.55, cats:0.35]. "love" attends strongly to "cats" (0.55) - loves WHAT? And "cats" attends to "love" (0.55) - cats in what context? After the weighted sum of Values: "love" is no longer generic. It now means "I-have-affection-for-cats" - encoded as a completely new vector shaped by its context.
summary: Self-attention uses Q/K/V to compute relevance scores between every pair of words and rewrites each word's vector as a weighted sum of context.

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 4: Add & Norm + FFN. Two important things happen after attention. Pipeline shown: Attention Output (context-aware vectors) + Original residual (pre-attention vectors) -> Add & Norm (stabilize values) -> FFN (two linear + GELU) -> Add & Norm (stabilize again). Residual connection: adds the ORIGINAL vector (before attention) back to the output. This prevents information loss and helps gradients flow during training. Without it, deep networks cannot learn. FFN (Feed-Forward Network): two linear layers with GELU activation. Processes each word INDEPENDENTLY. Attention mixed words together - the FFN thinks about each word alone, creating richer features.
summary: After attention, residual + LayerNorm stabilize values and an independent two-layer FFN with GELU enriches each word's representation.

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 5: Repeat N times. One attention + FFN block is ONE transformer layer. Real models repeat this block many times. Each layer refines understanding further. Layer 1-2 (~15%): Basic syntax, word boundaries. Layer 3-6 (~35%): Grammar, subject-verb agreement. Layer 7-10 (~65%): Semantic meaning, analogies. Layer 11-12 (~95%): Complex reasoning, long-range dependencies. GPT-2: 12 layers. GPT-3: 96 layers. LLaMA 3 70B: 80 layers. After all layers: "love" has been refined from "generic positive emotion" to "specific first-person affection directed at small furry animals in this particular sentence."
summary: Stacking transformer blocks N times progressively refines representations from syntax in early layers to semantics and reasoning in later layers.

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 6: Predict the next token. After all layers, each position holds a rich context-aware vector (512 numbers). The model takes the LAST position's vector and projects it through a Linear layer. Linear: 512 dims -> 50,257 (one score per word in vocabulary). Softmax: converts raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1. Top predictions after "I love cats": very 35%, so 22%, a 12%, the 8%, really 5%. The highest probability word becomes the prediction. The model picks "very" - "I love cats very..." and then repeats the whole process for the next token.
summary: The last position's hidden state goes through Linear (512 -> 50,257) and softmax to produce next-token probabilities, then the cycle repeats.

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 7: The complete pipeline end to end. "I love cats" -> Tokenize -> Embed -> +Position -> Self-Attention -> Add & Norm -> FFN -> Add & Norm -> xN layers -> Linear -> Softmax -> "very" (35%). The key insight: the word "love" entered as a generic 512-number vector and exited as a completely different 512-number vector that encodes "first-person affection directed at cats in a casual sentence." This is what "understanding context" means - every word's vector is reshaped by all the other words around it. That reshaping is the pipeline you just saw.
summary: End-to-end the Transformer reshapes a generic word vector into a context-specific one through tokenize/embed/position/attention/FFN repeated N times then Linear+Softmax.

text: Chapter 9.6 walks through the full encoder-decoder Transformer training (prefill) flow on the example sentence 'The cat sat on the mat' encoded and '<s> The cat' decoded. It progressively reveals 10 stages: tokenization, embedding lookup, positional encoding, encoder self-attention (Q/K/V/scores/softmax/W_O), Add & Norm + FFN + Add & Norm producing the encoder output, decoder input + masked self-attention, cross-attention using encoder K,V, decoder FFN + final Add & Norm, and the output head producing predictions at every decoder position. Key insight: during training all tokens flow as one matrix through every layer in parallel (teacher forcing), the causal mask makes every position a valid next-token prediction, so a length-N decoder produces N-1 training signals from a single forward pass.
summary: Full training-time pipeline of an encoder-decoder Transformer, end-to-end with parallel matrix flow and per-position loss.

text: Training / Prefill Mode. All tokens flow as one matrix through every layer simultaneously. Step 0: Raw input string 'The cat sat on the mat'. Step 1: Tokenization is a lookup table with no neural network. Split text into tokens and map each to an integer ID: 'The' -> 1042, 'cat' -> 5829, 'sat' -> 3820, 'on' -> 322, 'the' -> 1042, 'mat' -> 9382. Each word becomes one integer. Nothing neural yet. Notice 'The' and 'the' get the same ID 1042 because tokenization is case-sensitive only if the tokenizer is built that way; here they share the row.
summary: Tokenization turns the raw sentence into integer IDs via a lookup table, no neural net involved.

text: Step 2: Embedding Lookup. Each token ID selects a row from the embedding matrix, which is learned during training. Example vectors (shown as 3-dim for clarity, normally 512-dim): 'The'(1042)=[0.21,-0.87,0.45], 'cat'(5829)=[0.62,0.34,-0.18], 'sat'(3820)=[-0.55,0.91,0.12], 'on'(322)=[0.08,-0.43,0.77], 'the'(1042)=[0.21,-0.87,0.45], 'mat'(9382)=[0.73,0.15,-0.61]. ALL 6 lookups happen at once - just row selection from a table. 'The' (ID 1042) and 'the' (ID 1042) get the SAME embedding because same ID = same row.
summary: Embedding lookup selects one learned vector row per token ID, all 6 done in parallel.

text: Step 3: Add Positional Encoding (pure math, not learned). sin/cos values based on position index are added element-wise to embeddings. After adding: pos 0 'The'=[0.21,-0.87,1.45], pos 1 'cat'=[1.47,0.34,-0.17], pos 2 'sat'=[-0.46,0.91,0.13], pos 3 'on'=[0.16,-0.43,1.77], pos 4 'the'=[0.30,-0.87,0.46], pos 5 'mat'=[1.58,0.15,-0.6]. ALL 6 additions happen at once - element-wise, no dependencies. 'The' (pos 0) = [0.21,-0.87,1.45] vs 'the' (pos 4) = [0.30,-0.87,0.46] - now different because position-dependent values were added even though embeddings were identical.
summary: Positional encoding adds sin/cos position values element-wise so identical tokens at different positions become distinguishable.

text: Encoder Block (x6 in original Transformer, x96 in GPT-scale). Input is a 6x512 matrix (6 tokens, each a 512-dim vector), shown as 6x3 for clarity. Encoder Self-Attention is bidirectional - NO causal mask. Every token can attend to EVERY other token, including future ones. 'The' can look at 'mat', 'sat' can look at 'cat'. Projections: Q = X * W_Q (one matrix multiply 6x3 * 3x3 = 6x3). K = X * W_K. V = X * W_V. Each W is learned. Q.K^T produces attention scores: every token compared to every other token. Then scale by 1/sqrt(d_k), apply Softmax, weighted Sum of V: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(Q*K^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V. Then multiply by W_O (learned) for Multi-Head Output. All operations are batched matrix multiplications happening in parallel across all 6 tokens.
summary: Encoder self-attention: project to Q,K,V (no causal mask), compute scaled dot-product attention, then W_O, all as batched matrix multiplies.
queries: how does encoder self attention work
Q K V projection matrices in encoder
softmax QK^T over sqrt dk times V
W_Q W_K W_V W_O learned matrices

text: Add & LayerNorm (residual + normalize): LayerNorm(X + Attention(X)) applied independently per vector, all at once. Residual line carries the positional-encoded input around the attention block. Then the Feed-Forward Network (FFN): Layer 1 expands 3 -> 6 dims with ReLU, Layer 2 compresses 6 -> 3 dims. Same FFN weights applied to EACH vector independently - no cross-token mixing. All 6 tokens go through simultaneously as a batched matrix multiply. Then a second Add & LayerNorm (residual around FFN). The ENCODER OUTPUT is 6 context-enriched vectors (6x512 matrix). These become K and V for the decoder cross-attention. Computed ONCE, reused every decoder step. Each vector now 'knows' about all other words thanks to bidirectional self-attention.
summary: Encoder finishes with Add&Norm, FFN (no cross-token mixing), another Add&Norm, producing context-enriched vectors reused as K,V by the decoder.
queries: what does Add and LayerNorm do
encoder output is K and V for decoder

text: Decoder Block (x6 in original Transformer). During TRAINING all decoder tokens are processed in parallel via teacher forcing. Decoder input is the target sequence shifted right plus positional encoding: '<s>', 'The', 'cat' (predicting the next token at each position). Masked Self-Attention is causal - can only look backward. 'cat' can see '<s>' and 'The' but NOT future tokens, preventing cheating during training. Decoder projects via its own W_Q -> Decoder Query, W_K -> Decoder Key, W_V -> Decoder Value. Q.K^T with CAUSAL MASK keeps only the lower triangle: position 0 attends to position 0 only; position 1 to positions 0,1; position 2 to positions 0,1,2. Then Softmax + Weighted Sum of V, followed by Add & LayerNorm.
summary: Decoder runs masked self-attention with a lower-triangular causal mask so each position only attends to itself and earlier positions.

text: Cross-Attention: Decoder asks questions, Encoder provides answers. Q comes from decoder. K and V come from encoder output (computed once, reused). This is how the decoder 'reads' the input sentence. Decoder x W_Q -> Cross-Attention Query (3 vectors for the 3 decoder tokens). K and V come from the 6 context-enriched encoder vectors - no mask, full access. Cross-Attention computes decoder Q . encoder K^T, then weighted sum of encoder V. Each decoder token can attend to ALL 6 encoder tokens (no causal mask here). Example: 'cat' can now see encoder 'The','cat','sat','on','the','mat' - the full input. Output is 3 decoder vectors enriched with input info. Then Add & LayerNorm.
summary: Cross-attention lets each decoder token attend to all encoder output vectors using decoder Q with encoder K,V.

cross attention example cat reads input

text: Decoder FFN has the same structure as the encoder FFN. Each vector is processed independently with the same weights for all positions. FFN Layer 1: 3 -> 6 dims with ReLU (expand). FFN Layer 2: 6 -> 3 dims (compress back). No cross-token mixing. Then Add & LayerNorm produces the final decoder layer output. This is the residual + normalized representation that feeds the output head.
summary: Decoder FFN mirrors the encoder FFN: per-token expand-ReLU-compress, then a final residual Add & LayerNorm.
queries: is decoder FFN same as encoder FFN
same FFN weights all positions decoder

text: Output Head - TRAINING: Every Position Predicts the Next Token. During training we compute loss at ALL positions simultaneously, not just the last one. Why use all positions? Position 0 ('<s>') predicts 'The'. Position 1 ('The') predicts 'cat'. Position 2 ('cat') predicts 'sat'. This gives 3 training signals from 1 forward pass. The causal mask ensures each position can only see past tokens so every prediction is a fair test. Linear + Softmax produces probabilities at each position. Pos 0: 'The' 78%, 'A' 12%, 'cat' 4% -> Target 'The' -> Loss = -log(0.78) = 0.25. Pos 1: 'cat' 71%, 'dog' 12%, 'sat' 8% -> Target 'cat' -> Loss = -log(0.71) = 0.34. Pos 2: 'sat' 72%, 'is' 5%, 'ran' 11% -> Target 'sat' -> Loss = -log(0.72) = 0.33. Total Loss = avg(0.25 + 0.34 + 0.33) = 0.31 -> backprop updates ALL weights. During PRODUCTION (generation) we DO only use the last position output because we already know all previous tokens. 1000 tokens = 999 free training signals.
summary: Output head emits Linear+Softmax probabilities at every decoder position; cross-entropy losses are averaged for backprop, giving N-1 free training signals.

text: Summary: Training Phase Flow. Encoder: ALL 6 input tokens flow as one 6x512 matrix through every layer. Bidirectional attention (no mask). Decoder: ALL 3 target tokens ('<s>', 'The', 'cat') flow together (teacher forcing). Causal mask prevents cheating. Cross-Attention: Decoder Q attends to encoder K,V. Each decoder token can see ALL encoder tokens. Self-Attention: MIXES information between tokens. Q.K^T creates cross-token connections. FFN: Does NOT mix tokens. Same weights applied independently to each vector. No cross-talk. Add & Norm: Per-vector operation. Adds residual, normalizes. No cross-token interaction. Output Head (train): ALL positions produce predictions. Loss computed everywhere. 3 signals from 1 pass. Output Head (gen): Only LAST position predicts next token. Others already known. Encoder runs ONCE; its output is reused as K,V for every decoder step. Decoder runs once (train) or N times (gen).
summary: Recap of which operations mix tokens vs not, masking rules, and the once-vs-N-times distinction between training and generation.

text: Progressive encoder-decoder inference flow diagram showing autoregressive generation: encoder runs once on full input ('The cat sat on the mat'), its 6-vector output is cached as cross-attention K,V; the decoder runs in a loop processing one new token at a time. Diagram is built up across 9 sub-steps: (0) big-picture summary box, (1) encoder phase with 6 output vector nodes badged CACHED, (2) decoder state at step 3 with cached <s> and 'The' shown as dashed nodes and new 'cat' shown as solid, (3) W_Q/W_K/W_V projections producing only one new Q,K,V vector each, with prior K,V drawn from cache, (4) attention edges from new Q to all K (cached + new), single output vector, Add & LayerNorm, (5) cross-attention layer pulling K,V from 6 cached encoder outputs, (6) decoder FFN running only on the new token through a 3->6->3 layer, (7) output head linear projection over a 5-word vocab and softmax giving 80.7% for 'sat', (8) summary of remaining steps and KV cache memory/speed tradeoff table.
summary: Describes the progressive inference flow diagram covering encoder-once + decoder-loop with KV cache across 9 sub-steps.

text: Encoder-decoder inference (autoregressive generation) runs the encoder ONCE on the full input sequence and caches its output as cross-attention K,V. The decoder then runs in a loop, generating one new token per step. At each step, only the newest token flows through the decoder's layers; previously generated tokens' K,V vectors are read from the KV cache instead of being recomputed. Walked example: input 'The cat sat on the mat', decoder steps feed <s> -> The -> cat -> sat -> on -> the -> mat -> <end>. Self-attention cache grows by one entry per step; cross-attention cache is fixed (encoder output, 6 vectors). Without cache: O(N^2). With cache: O(N). Cost: GPT-4 stores ~1.57M floats per token (96 layers x 128 heads x 128 dims for K+V); 100K context = ~300 GB KV cache in fp16.
summary: Overview of encoder-decoder inference: encoder runs once, decoder loops one token at a time with KV cache.

text: PRODUCTION / INFERENCE MODE (autoregressive generation). Only ONE new token flows through the decoder at each step. Previous K,V are cached. HOW INFERENCE WORKS - THE BIG PICTURE: 1. ENCODER runs ONCE on the full input 'The cat sat on the mat' (6 tokens, all in parallel). 2. Encoder output (6 vectors) becomes K,V for cross-attention. Cached permanently. 3. DECODER runs in a LOOP, one new token at a time: Step 1: feed <s> -> predict 'The'. Step 2: feed 'The' -> predict 'cat' (KV cache has <s>). Step 3: feed 'cat' -> predict 'sat' (KV cache has <s>, 'The'). Step 4: feed 'sat' -> predict 'on' (KV cache has <s>, 'The', 'cat'). ...until <end> token is produced. 4. At each step, ONLY the new token runs through all layers. Previous tokens' K,V are read from cache.
summary: Introduces inference mode: encoder runs once, decoder loops one token at a time with previous K,V cached.

text: PHASE 1: ENCODER (runs ONCE, identical to training). 'The cat sat on the mat' -> 6 tokens flow through all encoder layers in parallel. Self-attention (bidirectional, no mask) -> Add&Norm -> FFN -> Add&Norm -> repeat x6. The encoder produces 6 output vectors (one per input token), shown with values like [-0.8, -0.5, 1.3] for 'The', [-0.7, -0.3, 1.0] for 'cat', [-0.5, -0.1, 0.6] for 'sat', etc. ENCODER OUTPUT CACHED - reused as cross-attention K,V for every decoder step. The encoder pass is identical to training: bidirectional attention with no causal mask, parallel processing of all input tokens through 6 stacked encoder blocks.

text: PHASE 2: DECODER - showing Step 3: generating after '<s> The cat'. Already generated: '<s>', 'The', 'cat'. Now predicting the next token. DECODER STATE AT STEP 3: KV Cache (self-attention) contains K,V for: <s> (step 1), 'The' (step 2). These were computed in earlier steps. NEW token entering now: 'cat' (the token we just generated in step 2). Only 'cat' flows through the full decoder. <s> and 'The' are NOT recomputed. DECODER STEP 3: Only 'cat' flows through all layers. FROM KV CACHE (not recomputed): '<s>' and 'The' shown as dashed/faded nodes. K,V already in cache from steps 1-2. NEW TOKEN (flows through network): 'cat' with embedded vector [1.47, 0.34, -0.17]. Embedded + positional encoded.

text: Masked Self-Attention (with KV Cache). Only 'cat' computes Q. K,V for <s> and 'The' come from cache. Three projections happen ONLY for the new token 'cat': (1) x W_Q -> Query: only 1 Q vector computed (not 3). Example Q for 'cat': [0.4, 0.2, 0.6]. (2) x W_K -> Key: new 'cat' K joins cached <s>, 'The' K. Cached K vectors example: [0.4, 0.0, 0.3] for <s>, [0.7, 0.2, 0.1] for 'The'. New K for 'cat': [0.3, 0.4, 0.5] - then ADDED to cache. (3) x W_V -> Value: new 'cat' V joins cached <s>, 'The' V. Cached V vectors example: [0.5, -0.2, 0.6] for <s>, [0.8, 0.1, 0.4] for 'The'. New V for 'cat': [0.6, 0.3, 0.7] - then ADDED to cache. The K and V for the new token are appended to the running KV cache so future steps can read them.
summary: Step 3 self-attention with cache: only new token computes Q,K,V; its K,V are appended to the cache.

text: Attention computation: 'cat' Q attends to ALL K (cached + new). 'cat' Q dots with K_<s>, K_The, K_cat -> 3 attention scores -> softmax -> weighted V sum. Without cache: compute Q,K,V for ALL 3 tokens, run attention on 3x3 matrix. O(n^2). With cache: compute Q,K,V for 1 token, read cached K,V, attention on 1x3. O(n). At 100K tokens, that is 100,000x cheaper per step. KV cache is essential for fast generation. Followed by Add & LayerNorm on just 1 vector, producing example [-0.3, 0.3, 0.0].

Encoder K,V: 6 vectors from encoder output (cached, never recomputed) - one per input token: 'The', 'cat', 'sat', 'on', 'the', 'mat'. ENCODER OUTPUT - computed once in Phase 1, reused every step. 'cat' Q dots ALL 6 encoder K -> weighted sum of 6 encoder V. Cross-attention output example [0.5, 0.2, 0.3]. 'cat' now incorporates information from ALL input words. text: Decoder FFN (just 1 vector through - 'cat' only). FFN processes each vector independently, so only the new token needs to go through. FFN Layer 1: 3 -> 6 + ReLU. FFN Layer 2: 6 -> 3, example output [0.1, 0.1, 0.0]. WHY ONLY RUN FFN ON THE NEW TOKEN? FFN does NOT mix information between tokens. It applies the same function to each vector independently. Previous tokens' FFN outputs are already 'baked into' the K,V vectors stored in the cache. So running FFN on <s> or 'The' again would produce the EXACT same output. No point recomputing. Followed by Add & LayerNorm (decoder block output for 'cat'), example [0.1, 0.2, -0.3].

text: Output Head ('cat' vector -> next word prediction). Linear Projection (3 -> vocab size). Vocab words and logits example: 'sat' 3.5, 'is' 0.8, 'ran' 1.2, 'sees' -0.3, 'went' 0.5. Softmax -> Probabilities: 'sat' 80.7%, 'is' 5.4%, 'ran' 8.1%, 'sees' 1.8%, 'went' 4.0%. Predicted next token: 'sat'. 'sat' feeds back as decoder input for Step 4. WHY ONLY THE LAST POSITION? During generation, there is only one position flowing through - the new token. <s> and 'The' were processed in Steps 1-2. Their predictions are done. We already used them. The single vector for 'cat' at the output is all we need to predict the next word 'sat'.

text: What Happens Next. Step 4: 'sat' enters the decoder. KV cache now has <s>, 'The', 'cat'. Predicts 'on'. Step 5: 'on' enters. KV cache has <s>, 'The', 'cat', 'sat'. Predicts 'the'. Step 6: 'the' enters. KV cache has <s>, 'The', 'cat', 'sat', 'on'. Predicts 'mat'. Step 7: 'mat' enters. Predicts <end>. Generation stops. Final output: 'The cat sat on the mat' (7 decoder steps, encoder ran once). KV Cache: Memory vs Speed Tradeoff. What is stored: K and V vectors at every attention layer, for every past token. Self-attention cache: Grows by 1 entry per step. At step N, holds N-1 K,V pairs per layer. Cross-attention cache: Fixed size (6 vectors K + 6 vectors V). Set once from encoder output. Real scale (GPT-4): 96 layers, 128 heads, 128 dims/head. Each token = 96*128*128 = 1.57M floats for K+V. At 100K context: 100K * 1.57M * 2 bytes (fp16) = ~300 GB just for the KV cache. Without cache: Each step would recompute ALL tokens. Step N costs O(N). Total: O(N^2). Impossibly slow. With cache: Each step costs O(1) for FFN + O(N) for attention lookup. Total: O(N). Much faster. Tradeoff: KV cache uses lots of GPU memory but makes generation feasible. No cache = no fast LLMs.
summary: Remaining steps generate 'on', 'the', 'mat', <end>; then details KV cache memory cost (300 GB at 100K context for GPT-4) and the O(N) vs O(N^2) tradeoff.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: Chapter 10.1 explains the KV cache: the inference-time trick that makes LLM generation fast. Naive generation recomputes Q, K, V for every previous token at each step, scaling as O(N^2) work per step. Because inputs (frozen embeddings) and weights (frozen W_Q, W_K, W_V) never change during inference, the K and V vectors for past tokens are identical every step. The cache stores K and V (never Q, since old queries are never reused), appending one row per generation step. Only the new query row is computed, giving 1 new score row and 1 new output row instead of the full matrix. Trade-off: memory cost (LLaMA 70B at 4K tokens = 10.7 GB per sequence; 32K = 86 GB) buys roughly 660x speedup at 1000 tokens. Every concurrent user needs their own cache. Cache exists only at inference; training processes all tokens in parallel.
summary: Overview of the KV cache: why it works, what gets saved, the memory-for-speed trade-off, and why every fast LLM relies on it.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The problem: naive generation redoes history. To generate each new word, the naive approach re-processes every previous word from scratch. Across three steps of generating 'I love cats': Step 1 does 1 new block (compute Q,K,V for 'I'). Step 2 redoes 'I' (1 red redo block) plus computes 'love' (1 green new block). Step 3 redoes 'I' and 'love' (2 red blocks) plus computes 'cats' (1 green block). Each block = one token's Q, K, V computed. Total work across 3 steps = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6 blocks. Actually useful: 3 blocks. Wasted: 3 blocks (50%). At 1000 tokens: 500,500 blocks of work, only 1000 are useful. 99.8% wasted. Naive generation recomputes identical numbers every step. This chapter shows the one-line fix that makes ChatGPT feel instant.
summary: Naive autoregressive generation recomputes every previous token's Q,K,V at every step, wasting 99.8% of work at 1000 tokens.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The lightbulb: same input, same output, always. When step 2 re-processes the token 'I', it gets the same numbers as step 1. Step 1 computed q_I = W_Q · x_I = [0.5, 0.2]. Step 2 re-computed q_I = W_Q · x_I = [0.5, 0.2]. Identical every single time. Why identical? Because neither input has changed. x_I (embedding for 'I') is frozen: tokens don't change their embedding mid-generation. W_Q (weight matrix) is frozen: trained weights don't change during inference. Same input + same weights = same output, ALWAYS. So stop recomputing. Save the answer the first time. That's a cache.
summary: Because token embeddings and weight matrices are both frozen during inference, recomputed K and V vectors are bit-identical to the originals, justifying caching.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The matrix view: only the last row is new. Attention is matrix multiplication. At step 3 (generating 'cats'), draw the full matmul Scores = Q · Kᵀ where Q is 3xd and Kᵀ is dx3, giving a 3x3 Scores matrix. Rows for q_I and q_love are dimmed because they were already computed at earlier steps; only the q_cats row glows - 3 dot products (not 9). Then Output = softmax(Scores) · V where V is 3xd (all rows needed) and Output is 3xd: out_I and out_love rows are old and discarded; only out_cats is new - 1 weighted sum (not 3). Counting cells at step 3: Without cache = 18 cells (9 score + 9 output). Actually needed = 6 cells (3 score + 3 output). Wasted = 12 cells (66%). Cost per step scales as N² without cache. With cache, cost per step stays O(N). Now we just need to figure out what to save so we can skip the gray rows.
summary: At each generation step, only the last row of Scores and Output is new; everything above is wasted recomputation, reducing per-step cost from O(N^2) to O(N) with caching.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: What to save, what to throw away. We compute three things per token: Q, K, V. Q - don't cache (dead weight): Only the new query q_cats appears in the new output row's formula. Old queries q_I and q_love are never used again. K - cache it: The new row of Scores = q_new · every old K. Missing one key = missing one attention score. V - cache it: The new output row = weighted sum of every old V. Missing one value = losing that token's contribution. That is why it is called the KV cache. We save K and V. Never Q.
summary: Old K and V vectors are needed every step (one per past token) but old Q is never reused, so the cache stores K and V but not Q.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The cache is a growing notebook. The KV cache is literally two tables (K cache and V cache) that grow by exactly one row at every step. Nothing moves, nothing gets recomputed. We just append. After 'I': K = [[0.3, 0.4]], V = [[0.8, 0.3]]. After 'love': K = [[0.3, 0.4], [0.7, 0.2]], V = [[0.8, 0.3], [0.1, 0.9]] (older row dimmed). After 'cats': K = [[0.3, 0.4], [0.7, 0.2], [0.5, 0.3]], V = [[0.8, 0.3], [0.1, 0.9], [0.45, 0.6]] (only newest row highlighted). One append per step. The existing rows don't move, don't change, don't get recomputed. They just sit there in memory, ready to be read.
summary: The KV cache behaves like two append-only tables; each generation step adds exactly one new K row and one new V row, leaving previous rows untouched.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: Before vs after - step 3 side by side. Generating 'cats' with and without the cache produces identical output but very different work. Without cache (recompute everything): q_I, q_love, q_cats; k_I, k_love, k_cats; v_I, v_love, v_cats - 9 projections, of which 6 are wasted (the I and love ones). Plus 9 score cells + 3 output rows = 15 wasted operations. With cache: Read from cache: k_I, k_love, v_I, v_love. Compute only for new token: q_cats = W_Q · x_cats, k_cats = W_K · x_cats, v_cats = W_V · x_cats. Append k_cats, v_cats to cache. Then scores = q_cats · Kᵀ (last row only, 1×N) and output_cats = softmax(scores) · V (1×d). 3 projections + 3 score cells + 1 output row, 0 operations wasted. The output vector for 'cats' is identical in both. The cache just changes where the old K and V values come from (freshly recomputed vs memory read).
summary: Side-by-side trace of step 3 shows the cache cuts 15 wasted operations to zero while producing the exact same output for the new token.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: Trace it with real numbers (d = 2). Embeddings: x_I = [1.0, 0.0], x_love = [0.0, 1.0], x_cats = [0.5, 0.5]. W_Q rows: [0.5, 0.1] [0.2, 0.6]. W_K rows: [0.3, 0.7] [0.4, 0.2]. W_V rows: [0.8, 0.1] [0.3, 0.9]. Step 1 ('I'): q_I = [0.5, 0.2], k_I = [0.3, 0.4], v_I = [0.8, 0.3]. Cache: K = [[0.3, 0.4]], V = [[0.8, 0.3]]. Step 2 ('love'): q_love = [0.1, 0.6], k_love = [0.7, 0.2], v_love = [0.1, 0.9]. Cache: K = [[0.3, 0.4], [0.7, 0.2]], V = [[0.8, 0.3], [0.1, 0.9]]. Step 3 ('cats'): q_cats = [0.3, 0.4], k_cats = [0.5, 0.3], v_cats = [0.45, 0.6]. Scores (last row only): q_cats · k_I = 0.3×0.3 + 0.4×0.4 = 0.25; q_cats · k_love = 0.3×0.7 + 0.4×0.2 = 0.29; q_cats · k_cats = 0.3×0.5 + 0.4×0.3 = 0.27. Scale by √2, then softmax = [0.329, 0.338, 0.333]. Weighted sum of values: 0.329 × [0.8, 0.3] + 0.338 × [0.1, 0.9] + 0.333 × [0.45, 0.6] = output_cats = [0.447, 0.603]. This exact [0.447, 0.603] would also come out without the cache. The cache never changes what is computed; it only changes where k_I, k_love, v_I, v_love came from (cached vs recomputed).
summary: Worked d=2 trace of three steps showing exact Q, K, V values, score dot products, softmax, weighted sum, and the final output [0.447, 0.603] that the cache delivers identically.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The cost - 10.7 GB per conversation. The formula: cache_bytes = 2 (K+V) × layers × d_model × seq_len × bytes_per_param. Example: LLaMA 70B. layers = 80. d_model = 8,192. seq_len (max) = 4,096 tokens. precision = FP16 (2 bytes). 2 × 80 × 8,192 × 4,096 × 2 bytes = 10.7 GB per sequence. KV cache size grows with sequence length: 1K tokens = 2.7 GB, 4K tokens = 10.7 GB, 32K tokens = 86 GB. Every concurrent user needs their own cache: 1 user = 10.7 GB, 10 users = 107 GB, 100 users = 1,070 GB. Long-context models (128K tokens) consume enormous memory. This is a major reason why serving LLMs is expensive, and why techniques like grouped-query attention exist to shrink the cache.
summary: KV cache memory equals 2 · layers · d_model · seq_len · bytes_per_param; for LLaMA 70B at 4K tokens this is 10.7 GB per sequence and scales linearly with users and length.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The deal - memory for speed. The KV cache is a trade: it costs memory and pays back speed. Without KV cache: Speed = slow, O(N²) per step, O(N³) total. Memory = low, nothing stored. With KV cache: Speed = fast, O(N) per step, O(N²) total. Memory = GB-scale, grows per token. At 1000 tokens: Without cache ~333 million ops. With cache ~500 thousand ops. ~660× faster. Every fast LLM you've ever used runs on this trick. Without it, ChatGPT would take minutes per response instead of seconds. Scope note: the cache only exists at inference time. During training, all tokens are processed in parallel, so there's nothing to cache.
summary: Final trade-off ledger: KV cache cuts per-step cost from O(N^2) to O(N), giving ~660x speedup at 1000 tokens at the price of GB-scale memory, and exists only at inference.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) shrinks the KV cache during inference. Standard MHA gives each head its own Q, K, V (24 matrices for 8 heads), creating a ~10 GB KV cache per user in LLaMA 2 70B. GQA shares K and V across groups of query heads: 8 Q heads with 2 KV groups means 8 W_Q + 2 W_K + 2 W_V = 12 projections, 4x smaller cache. MQA shares one K/V across all heads (8x smaller) but loses 2-5% quality. GQA-8 matches MHA within 1% and is used by LLaMA 2 70B, LLaMA 3, Mistral 7B, and Gemma.
summary: GQA shares K and V across query-head groups to cut KV cache memory 4x while preserving quality, making it standard in modern LLMs.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: Recall: Multi-Head Attention. In chapters 7.9-7.14, we learned standard MHA. With 8 heads, the model maintains 8 independent sets of Q, K, V weight matrices: Queries W_Q1...W_Q8, Keys W_K1...W_K8, Values W_V1...W_V8. That is 8 W_Q + 8 W_K + 8 W_V = 24 weight matrices per attention layer. Each head has its own complete set. This works great for quality, but there is a hidden cost during inference.
summary: Standard MHA gives each of the 8 heads its own Q, K, V projection, totaling 24 weight matrices per layer.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: The inference problem: KV cache memory. During generation, we cache the K and V vectors for all previous tokens to avoid recomputing them at every step. But this KV cache grows fast. KV cache memory per layer = 2 x num_heads x seq_length x d_head x bytes_per_param. Concrete example LLaMA 2 70B: num_heads=64 (64 attention heads), d_head=128 (128 dims per head, 8192/64), seq_length=4096 (4096 token context window), layers=80 (80 transformer layers), bytes=2 (2 bytes per param float16). Total KV cache per batch item: 2 x 64 x 4096 x 128 x 2 bytes = 134,217,728 bytes per layer. 134,217,728 x 80 layers = 10,737,418,240 bytes ~ 10 GB per batch item. Serving 8 users simultaneously = 80 GB just for KV caches. That is an entire A100 GPU of memory before the model weights themselves.
summary: KV cache balloons to ~10 GB per user in LLaMA 2 70B, making memory the bottleneck during inference.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: GQA solution: share K and V across query groups. Instead of giving every head its own K and V, we share K and V across groups of query heads. Each head still has its own unique Q - queries stay personal. Example: 8 query heads, 2 KV groups. Group 1: Heads 1-4 share K_1, V_1 (Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4 all use K_1 and V_1). Group 2: Heads 5-8 share K_2, V_2 (Q_5, Q_6, Q_7, Q_8 all use K_2 and V_2). Weight matrices needed: 8 W_Q + 2 W_K + 2 W_V = 12 projections (down from 24). The K and V computations and their caches shrink by 4x.
summary: GQA keeps unique Q per head but shares K and V across groups, cutting projections from 24 to 12 and KV cache by 4x.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: Visual comparison: MHA vs GQA vs MQA. MHA (Multi-Head Attention): 8 Q heads, 8 K heads, 8 V heads, 24 projections total, 8 K + 8 V cached per layer. GQA (Grouped-Query Attention, 4 heads per group): 8 Q heads, 2 K heads, 2 V heads, 12 projections total, 2 K + 2 V cached per layer (4x less). MQA (Multi-Query Attention): 8 Q heads, 1 K head, 1 V head, 10 projections total, 1 K + 1 V cached per layer (8x less). KV cache savings for LLaMA 2 70B at 4096 context: MHA (64 KV heads) ~10 GB, GQA-8 (8 KV groups) ~1.25 GB, MQA (1 KV shared) ~160 MB.
summary: MHA, GQA, and MQA compared head-by-head: GQA-8 cuts cache from 10 GB to 1.25 GB, MQA to 160 MB.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: Why does sharing K and V work? The query is the question each head asks. Each head needs its own unique question to capture different patterns (syntax, semantics, coreference, etc.). But keys and values are the answers - and it turns out adjacent heads often attend to similar positions anyway. The K and V vectors across heads are highly correlated. Sharing them loses very little information. Research result: GQA matches standard MHA quality within 1% on most benchmarks while using 4x less KV cache memory. The quality-to-efficiency tradeoff is exceptionally favorable. Models using GQA: LLaMA 2 70B (8 KV groups), LLaMA 3 (8 KV groups), Mistral 7B (8 KV groups), Gemma (varies by size). Why not always use MQA (1 shared KV)? MQA saves the most memory but loses quality - about 2-5% on harder tasks. GQA is the sweet spot: most of the memory savings with almost no quality loss. That is why most modern large models use GQA with 8 groups, not full MQA.
summary: Sharing K/V works because they correlate across heads; GQA matches MHA within 1%, while MQA loses 2-5%, making GQA-8 the sweet spot used by LLaMA 2/3, Mistral, Gemma.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Mixture of Experts (MoE) replaces the single FFN in a transformer block with a router and N expert FFNs. The router picks the top-k experts per token (e.g., top-2 of 8) and produces a weighted sum of their outputs. This decouples total parameters from active parameters: Mixtral 8x7B holds 47B params in memory but uses only ~13B per token. An auxiliary load-balancing loss prevents one expert from hogging all tokens. Memory holds every expert (94 GB at fp16), but compute per token drops to about 26 GFLOPs. Production examples include Mixtral 8x7B, Mixtral 8x22B, DeepSeek-V3 (671B total / 37B active, top-8 of 256+1 shared), and Qwen3-Next (80B / 3B, top-10 of 512). MoE wins for data-center training and high-throughput serving but struggles on edge devices and latency-critical single-user inference.
summary: Chapter overview of Mixture of Experts: router plus N expert FFNs, top-k routing, load balancing, parameter math, memory-vs-compute tradeoff, and where MoE wins or loses.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: The problem - more brain, same effort per token. Training a bigger model means more knowledge, but every token pays the full compute cost. What if most of the brain could nap while the relevant part does the work? Dense 47B model: active params per token = 47B (all of it). Every FFN, every attention head, every layer fires for every token. MoE model (Mixtral 8x7B): active params per token = ~13B (only 2 of 8 experts). Same 47B total capacity loaded in memory, but only a slice runs per token.
summary: Motivates MoE by contrasting a dense 47B model that activates all params per token against Mixtral 8x7B which has 47B total but only ~13B active.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Replace one FFN with a router + N expert FFNs. Before (standard transformer block): LayerNorm -> Attention -> Add; LayerNorm -> FFN -> Add. After (MoE block): LayerNorm -> Attention -> Add; LayerNorm -> [Router -> top-k experts -> weighted sum] -> Add. Each 'expert' is just a copy of the FFN architecture (two matrices, an activation). 8 experts means 8 independent sets of FFN weights. The router is a tiny linear layer that decides which experts to invoke.
summary: Shows how an MoE block swaps the standard FFN for a router that selects top-k expert FFNs and combines their outputs.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Each token picks its top 2 experts. The router is a tiny linear layer. For each token, it produces one score per expert, applies softmax, and picks the top-2 highest. Token = 'cat' (from 'I love cats'). router_scores = softmax(cat . W_router) = [0.03, 0.02, 0.80, 0.01, 0.04, 0.05, 0.02, 0.03] over E1..E8. Top-2 picks: E3 (0.80) and E6 (0.05). Normalize: E3 = 0.80 / 0.85 = 0.94; E6 = 0.05 / 0.85 = 0.06. Output = 0.94 . E3('cat') + 0.06 . E6('cat'). The router matrix W_router has shape d_model x num_experts (e.g., 4096 x 8 = 32K params). Negligible cost compared to an expert's ~650M params.
summary: Worked top-2 routing example: softmax scores over 8 experts pick E3 and E6 for token 'cat', normalize their weights to 0.94 and 0.06, then weighted-sum.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Without a fix, one expert hogs everything. Early in training the router has no reason to spread load. It often collapses to sending almost every token to one 'lucky' expert - the others never get gradient updates and go unused. Without load balancing example histogram: E3 = 92%, others ~1-2%. With auxiliary loss: E1..E8 roughly 12-13% each. Auxiliary loss formula: L_aux = alpha . N . sum_i (f_i . P_i). f_i = fraction of tokens routed to expert i; P_i = total router probability mass sent to expert i. Product is minimized when routing is balanced. The auxiliary loss is added to the main training loss.
summary: Explains routing collapse, why one expert dominates without intervention, and the auxiliary load-balancing loss L_aux = alpha * N * sum(f_i * P_i).

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Why it is called 8x7B but totals 47B, not 56B. Attention is shared (not duplicated per expert). Only the FFN is replaced by experts. So 8 experts of ~7B FFN-size each do not multiply to 56B - most of each '7B' is the shared attention. Per layer: Attention (GQA) ~41M; 8 experts x ~176M = ~1.41B; Router + norms ~50K (negligible); per layer total ~1.45B. x 32 layers + shared embeddings = 46.7B total. Active per token: Attention 41M; 2 of 8 experts = 352M per layer; x 32 layers + embeddings = ~13B active. The '8x7B' name is marketing-ish. It hints at '8 experts, 7B-class model.' The literal total is 47B because attention is shared across all experts (not replicated eight times).
summary: Parameter math for Mixtral 8x7B explaining shared attention and per-expert FFN size to reach 46.7B total and ~13B active per token.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Memory holds all experts. Compute only runs two. This is the core tradeoff. MoE does NOT reduce memory - every expert has to be loaded so the router can pick any of them. What it reduces is compute per token. GPU memory (fp16) = 94 GB (47B x 2 bytes per param). All 8 experts must be loaded - the router could pick any. Compute per token = 26 GFLOPs (13B x 2 fwd-pass cost per param). Only 2 of 8 experts actually multiply anything for this token. Takeaway: MoE is great for data-center serving where memory is abundant but compute is precious. It struggles on edge devices where memory is the bottleneck.
summary: Quantifies the MoE tradeoff: 94 GB GPU memory holds every expert while only ~26 GFLOPs of compute fires per token.

text: MoE models in production. Mixtral 8x7B: total 47B, active 13B, 8 experts, top-2 routing. Mixtral 8x22B: total 141B, active 39B, 8 experts, top-2. DeepSeek-V3: total 671B, active 37B, 256 + 1 shared experts, top-8 routing. Qwen3-Next: total 80B, active 3B, 512 experts, top-10 routing. DeepSeek-V3 is the extreme case - only 5.5% of its 671B params activate per token. That is why it is cheap to serve despite being enormous on disk.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: MoE is not free lunch. Different deployments have different bottlenecks. MoE trades memory for compute - that wins in some contexts and loses in others. Wins: large-scale data-center training; high-throughput serving (many users at once); compute-constrained + memory-rich settings; when model capacity matters more than latency. Struggles: edge and on-device deployment; small-batch single-user inference; memory-constrained environments; latency-critical apps (the router adds hops). If you can afford the memory to hold a huge model but not the compute to run it, MoE is the answer.
summary: Honest tradeoff list: MoE wins for data-center training and high-throughput serving but loses on edge devices and latency-sensitive workloads.

text: Reasoning models like o1, o3, and DeepSeek-R1 are the same transformer architecture as regular LLMs - tokenizer, attention, FFN, KV cache, autoregressive loop all unchanged. What differs is training: stage 1 standard pre-training, stage 2 SFT on ~50K reasoning traces with <think>/</think> special tokens, stage 3 RL with a verifier over millions of rollouts using PPO or GRPO. The model learns to emit <think> and </think> as ordinary vocabulary tokens; thinking is just the same autoregressive loop running 120+ iterations instead of 3. More thinking tokens means more compute means better accuracy on checkable problems - test-time compute scaling. Training data comes from existing CoT, synthetic generation, and rejection sampling - no humans label reasoning steps. Self-correction, verification, and confidence calibration emerge purely from outcome rewards. Reasoning lifts math, code, logic, and proofs; it barely helps creative writing, empathy, or casual conversation.
summary: Reasoning LLMs are vanilla transformers trained with SFT plus outcome-reward RL so the autoregressive loop runs longer inside <think> tags - test-time compute scaling that helps verifiable tasks only.

text: A reasoning model is the exact same transformer. Nothing new is added to the architecture. Every component from Sections 1-9 is unchanged: Tokenizer, Embeddings, Positional encoding (RoPE), Causal mask, KV cache, Multi-head Attention, FFN, Layer norms, Residual connections, Output head, Softmax, Autoregressive loop. A reasoning model and a regular LLM could share the same code file.

text: Not a new loop. The same loop, running longer. Autoregressive generation always loops - the transformer can only produce one token per forward pass. Regular LLM example: FWD -> 10, FWD -> 81, FWD -> <EOS>, about 3 loop iterations. Reasoning LLM example: FWD -> <think>, FWD -> Let, FWD -> me, ... FWD -> </think>, FWD -> 1081, about 120 loop iterations. Same mechanism either way.
summary: Reasoning models run the standard autoregressive loop many more times - 120 iterations vs 3 - rather than introducing any new generation mechanism.

text: How the model switches between thinking and answering. There is no thinking mode switch in the code. The vocabulary has two special tokens: <think> and </think>. The model learned to emit them at the right moments, the same way it learned any other word. The softmax output is a probability distribution over all tokens; the special tokens simply win at the right moments. Examples: after 'User: What is 23 x 47? Assistant:' the next-token distribution is <think> 0.87 (winner), 1081 0.03, The 0.02. Inside thinking after '... = 1081 OK' the distribution is </think> 0.62 (winner), Let 0.09, Also 0.07.
summary: <think> and </think> are ordinary vocabulary tokens; the model learned when to emit them via softmax probabilities, no architectural mode switch exists.

text: More thinking tokens means better answers on hard problems. This is the new scaling axis: instead of training a bigger model, you let the same model think longer. More thinking tokens means more forward passes, which means more compute spent on the problem. Representative accuracy on hard math vs thinking budget: 100 tokens 30%, 1,000 tokens 55%, 10,000 tokens 78%, 100,000 tokens 89%. For decades, better AI meant a bigger model. Reasoning models added a second knob: spend more compute at inference time instead - this is called test-time compute scaling. (Numbers represent trends across o1/o3/DeepSeek-R1, not a single published result.)
summary: Test-time compute scaling: accuracy on hard math climbs from 30% at 100 tokens to 89% at 100,000 thinking tokens, a new axis beyond model size.

text: What is actually different is the training, not the model. Reasoning models go through three training stages. Stage 1 Pre-training: predict the next token on trillions of internet tokens (trillions of tokens scale). Stage 2 SFT: fine-tune on curated reasoning traces with <think> tags (~50K curated examples). Stage 3 RL with verifier: generate rollouts, reward correct final answers, PPO/GRPO update (millions of rollouts). Stage 1 is identical to a regular LLM. The magic is entirely in stages 2 and 3.
summary: Reasoning capability comes from a three-stage pipeline: standard pre-training, SFT on ~50K reasoning traces, then RL with a verifier over millions of rollouts.
queries: training pipeline for o1 style models
three stages of reasoning model training

is pre-training different for reasoning models
what makes a reasoning model different in training
how does reasoning emerge from training

text: How reasoning improves, one reward at a time. The model generates many rollouts per problem. A verifier checks each final answer. PPO or GRPO nudges weights: tokens in winning rollouts become more likely, tokens in losing rollouts less likely. Example - Problem '23 x 47 = ?' (ground truth 1081), generate 64 rollouts. #1 '... 23 x 50 - 23 x 3 = 1081' answer 1081 OK r=+1. #2 '... (20 + 3) x 47 = 940 + 141 = 1081' answer 1081 OK r=+1. #3 '... estimate: roughly 1000' answer 1000 X r=-1. #4 '... 23 x 47 = 1081' answer 1081 OK r=+1. #5 '... quick mental math: 1104?' answer 1104 X r=-1. PPO/GRPO update: winning rollouts -> token probabilities UP, losing rollouts -> token probabilities DOWN. The reward only checks the FINAL answer. The model figures out on its own what kinds of reasoning lead to correct answers. Nobody hand-labels reasoning steps.
summary: RL with verifier: 64 rollouts per problem, +1 for correct final answer, -1 otherwise; PPO/GRPO upweights winning chains, no per-step labels needed.
queries: how does RL with verifier work
rollouts per problem in RL training
how does verifier check LLM answer
what is a rollout in LLM training

text: You do not need humans to write millions of reasoning traces. The only thing you need is problems with checkable answers. Three data sources: 1. Existing chain-of-thought - already in pre-training data: math textbooks, Stack Overflow answers, competitive programming editorials, tutorial posts. 2. Synthetic generation - a big LLM generates 64 reasoning attempts per problem, a verifier checks each final answer, keep the ones that got it right and use them as training data. 3. Rejection sampling - have the base model itself try each problem 64 times, keep the attempts that got the right answer, model learns from its own best work. Domains with checkable answers are abundant: GSM8K, MATH, AIME, olympiad problems, LeetCode, Codeforces, scientific multiple-choice.
summary: Reasoning training data comes from existing CoT, synthetic generation filtered by a verifier, and rejection sampling - no humans writing millions of traces.

text: The tricks nobody programmed. Reasoning chains that include self-correction and verification tend to produce correct answers more often. The reward signal pushes those patterns up over millions of rollouts. Nobody writes code that says 'self-correct' - it emerges from the rewards. Example trace: '23 x 47 = 23 x 50 - 23 x 3 = 1150 - 69 = 1081. Wait, let me double-check. (<-- emerged: self-doubt) Try another way: 20 x 47 + 3 x 47 = 940 + 141 = 1081. (<-- emerged: verification) Both methods give 1081. Confident. (<-- emerged: confidence calibration)'. The DeepSeek-R1 papers documented an 'aha moment' during training where thinking length spontaneously grew and phrases like 'wait' and 'let me reconsider' appeared - purely from the reward signal.
summary: Self-doubt, verification, and confidence calibration emerge purely from outcome-based rewards - the DeepSeek-R1 'aha moment' phenomenon.

text: Reasoning models are not universally better. Thinking helps when there is a right answer to check. It barely helps when there is not. Big lift domains: Math, arithmetic, algebra; Competitive programming; Logic puzzles; Scientific Q&A with verifiable answers; Constraint-satisfaction problems; Formal proofs. No real gain domains: Creative writing; Poetry, storytelling; Open-ended opinions; Empathy, emotional support; Summarization; Casual conversation. Reasoning helps when there is a right answer to check. It barely helps when there is not.
summary: Reasoning lifts performance only on tasks with verifiable answers (math, code, logic, proofs); creative, empathic, and open-ended tasks see no real gain.

text: Chapter 11.1 introduces the retrieval problem at the heart of every vector database: given N pre-computed embedding vectors and one query vector, return the top-k most similar. It establishes the running cat corpus (10 documents, 4-dim illustrative vectors, query 'Information about cats'), shows that brute-force scan costs N times d multiplications per query (about 1.5 trillion at N = 1 billion, d = 1536), surveys where vector search shows up (semantic search, recommendation, image retrieval, duplicate detection, anomaly detection, RAG), and reframes Section 11 as a systems problem - storage, indexing, retrieval - rather than a training problem. The embeddings already exist; the work now is the index.
summary: Overview of chapter 11.1: the top-k nearest-neighbor retrieval problem, the cat corpus, scaling pain, where it appears, and the systems-not-training framing.

text:
From embeddings to a retrieval problem. Back in chapter 5.2 the transformer turned every word into an embedding - a dense vector capturing meaning. Now picture a library: 10 documents, each pre-encoded into its own vector. A user types a query. How do we find documents whose meaning matches?

Query: 'Information about cats', vector [0.85, 0.14, 0.44, 0.21].

Stored database: 10 documents, each with a 4-dim embedding:
#1 Cats are small domesticated carnivores [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, 0.22]
#2 Dogs are loyal pets [0.33, 0.68, 0.29, 0.41]
#3 Lions are big cats that live in Africa [0.76, 0.18, 0.52, 0.31]
#4 My cat sat on the mat [0.72, 0.22, 0.48, 0.26]
#5 Tigers are striped cats [0.79, 0.15, 0.41, 0.28]
#6 Python is a programming language [0.09, 0.88, 0.12, 0.74]
#7 Kittens grow up to be cats [0.83, 0.10, 0.47, 0.19]
#8 The dog chased the cat [0.55, 0.44, 0.36, 0.39]
#9 Birds can fly [0.18, 0.31, 0.74, 0.62]
#10 Fish live underwater [0.21, 0.29, 0.68, 0.81]

Real embeddings have 768 or 1536 dimensions instead of 4, and production databases hold billions of rows instead of 10. The shape of the problem is the same.

text:
Find the 10 most similar documents. The core retrieval task: one query vector, N stored vectors, return the top-10 nearest. For the tiny 10-doc corpus, top-10 is everything, so the diagram highlights top-3 (docs 1, 3, 7 - all cat-related) to make 'closest' visible. The real problem scales: hundreds of millions of vectors, top-10 closest in milliseconds.

Formal statement:
  Given: N stored vectors V = {v_1, v_2, ..., v_N}
  Given: one query vector q
  Return: the k vectors in V most similar to q

This is the retrieval problem at the heart of every vector database. k is usually 10 or 20 - we want a shortlist, not the whole library.
summary: Defines the formal top-k retrieval task: given N stored vectors V and a query q, return the k most similar; k is typically 10 or 20.

text:
Now make it one billion. With 10 docs the naive approach - compare the query against every vector - is instant. Real systems have millions or billions of vectors and the cost of 'check every vector' explodes.

Toy corpus: N = 10, scan ~0.01 ms (this chapter)
Wikipedia-scale: N = 1,000,000 (all English Wikipedia chunked), scan ~500 ms
Internet-scale: N = 1,000,000,000 (web-scale search index), scan ~500,000 ms (~8 min)

Naive cost = N times d multiplications per query
At N = 1 billion, d = 1536: ~1.5 trillion multiplies per query

Checking every vector works for 10 docs. At 1B it is an 8-minute wait per search. Users expect answers in tens of milliseconds.
summary: Scales the brute-force scan cost from 10 docs (0.01 ms) through Wikipedia (500 ms) to 1 billion (8 minutes), giving the formula N times d multiplications per query.
