knowledge base article in multiple systems

text: Exact-hash dedup is the first line of defense: cheap and catches the easy 80%. Normalize text (strip whitespace, lowercase, remove punctuation), run SHA-256, keep a hash table of every hash seen. If new doc hash matches existing hash, byte-identical content, drop it. Three-step flow: Step 1 Normalize - "Reset Your Password. Click The Link." becomes "reset your password click the link". Step 2 Hash SHA-256 - sha256(normalized) produces hex digest a3f2b9.... Step 3 Dedup lookup - hash table check, match = drop, miss = keep + store. Formula: hash = sha256(normalize(doc.content)). Worked example: Zendesk hash a3f2b9... kept (first); Confluence hash a3f2b9... dropped; Notion hash a3f2b9... dropped. Cost: O(N) hash compute + O(1) hash-table lookup. Miss: "same article with one typo fixed" produces a different hash, so byte-identical only catches the easy 80%.
summary: Normalize then SHA-256 hash with table lookup catches byte-identical duplicates in O(N) time.
queries: why exact hash misses typo fixes
lowercase strip whitespace remove punctuation dedup

text: MinHash + LSH catches near-duplicates that exact hash misses (one typo flips SHA-256). Need "almost the same" without comparing every pair of N docs. Standard library: datasketch, text-dedup. Three-step pipeline: 1. Shingle - cut each doc into overlapping 3-word windows. Doc becomes a set of shingles. 2. MinHash - compress each set into a fixed 128-number fingerprint where matching positions estimate Jaccard overlap (shared / union). 3. LSH bucket - split fingerprint into bands, hash each band. Docs landing in the same bucket are candidate near-duplicates. Skips the O(N^2) compare-everything scan. Tunable: Jaccard threshold (default 0.85). Catches typo-fixes, re-words, copy-paste edits that exact hashing misses. Scales to billion-doc corpora.
summary: MinHash compresses shingle sets into fingerprints; LSH buckets find near-duplicate candidates without O(N^2) scan.
queries: how MinHash LSH works for dedup
why MinHash beats brute force pairwise

text: Embedding dedup is the only way to catch paraphrases - two docs saying the same thing with almost no shared words. MinHash misses these because no shingles overlap. Only embedding cosine catches paraphrases (same meaning, different words). Paraphrase pair example: Support doc (formal): "To reset your password, click the password reset link delivered to your registered email address." Forum post (casual): "Yo if you forgot your password just hit the reset thing they sent to your email." MinHash Jaccard: 0.08 (miss). Embedding cosine: 0.94 (flagged as semantic duplicate). Cosine similarity ladder: Cosine >= 0.97 = definite duplicate, drop one keep the other; 0.90 <= cosine < 0.97 = near-duplicate, send to human review queue; 0.85 <= cosine < 0.90 = topically similar, keep both; cosine < 0.85 = distinct documents, keep all. Cost reality: embedding full corpus to dedup adds $0.10 per 1M tokens x corpus size. For 10M docs at 512 tokens each = 5B tokens = about $500 just to dedup. Cheaper than re-embedding twice but a real line item. Use embedding dedup when corpus has paraphrases (support docs + forum posts, multi-author docs, translations). Skip it when content is byte-stable (PDFs from authoritative sources) and exact hashing + MinHash already cover the threshold.
summary: Embedding cosine catches paraphrases (formal vs casual wording) that MinHash misses, with band-based actions and ~$500 cost per 10M docs.
queries: how to detect paraphrase duplicates RAG
human review queue near duplicate band
when to use embedding dedup vs MinHash

text: Cleaning is the unglamorous 30% that decides whether eval stabilizes. Dedup catches duplicates; cleaning fixes the bytes themselves. Four steps turn raw extracted text into something embedder and tokenizer can read consistently. 1. Unicode Normalization (NFC) - two strings that look identical can have different byte sequences. Before: café (composed, 5 bytes) vs café (decomposed, 6 bytes). After: NFC pass collapses both to the same 5-byte form. Hash dedup and embedding lookup now agree. 2. Encoding Fix-Up - mojibake from latin-1/UTF-8 mismatches breaks tokenization. Before: raw bytes rendered wrong, 'naïve résumé' shown as 'naïve résumé'. After: ftfy or chardet detects the encoding mismatch and restores 'naïve résumé'. 3. Strip Headers, Footers, Page Numbers, Watermarks - boilerplate repeated on every page bloats chunks with noise. Before: every chunk starts with 'CONFIDENTIAL - DRAFT 2026 - Page 3 of 47'. After: layout-aware parser strips the repeating per-page header, footer, watermark band. 4. PII Scrubbing - emails, SSNs, phone numbers, credit cards leak into the index unless redacted. Before: "Contact john@acme.com or call 555-1234 with SSN 123-45-6789." After: "Contact [EMAIL_REDACTED] or call [PHONE_REDACTED] with SSN [SSN_REDACTED]." Tool: regex + LLM NER (Presidio). Cleaning is invisible when it works and catastrophic when skipped. It stabilizes eval scores by removing irrelevant tokens that fool similarity metrics, and keeps PII out of the index entirely.
summary: Four cleaning steps (NFC, encoding fix, boilerplate strip, PII scrub) stabilize embeddings and keep PII out of the index.

Six steps each shown as a labeled card on the left and a body description on the right, connected to a central time axis. T=0:01 Webhook Fires - Confluence POSTs {event: 'page.updated', page_id: 'doc-1', revision: 42} to ingestion service. text: Three-row versioning timeline. Row 1 (green, t=0): doc-1 v1 indexed, chunks=[c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1], status=active. Queries hit v1 chunks, source-of-truth and index agree. Row 2 (yellow, t=+1min): v2 ingested, v1 deprecated. Chunks=[c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2] active + [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1] deprecated. status=active OR status=deprecated. Both versions live during re-embed window so there is no retrieval gap. Row 3 (cyan, t=+15min): v1 dropped after grace period. Chunks=[c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2], status=active. Only v2 active, old chunks tombstoned and removed.
summary: Three-row timeline showing chunk versioning states at t=0, t=+1min, and t=+15min that keep both versions live during the re-embed grace period.

text: Chapter 12.6 covers Refresh & Sync Schedules for RAG. Docs change but indexes don't, so stale chunks cause confident hallucinations with no automatic stale signal. Three sync strategies: (1) full re-index on a schedule using shadow-index pattern with atomic pointer swap, cheap at 10K docs ($30/month) but dominates budget above 100K docs ($3,000/month at 1M docs); (2) webhook-driven incremental sync, ~10-second propagation, only re-embeds changed docs, needs idempotent upserts and retry/DLQ; (3) polling with per-source watermark and updated_at filter, fallback for sources without webhooks, lag bounded by poll interval. Delete propagation via tombstones and chunk versioning (status=active vs deprecated) with a 5-15 minute grace period prevent ghost chunks and retrieval gaps. Both are silent failures invisible to users.
summary: Overview of why RAG indexes go stale and the three sync strategies plus delete propagation and chunk versioning required to keep retrieval fresh.
queries: how to keep RAG index fresh
why does my RAG return stale answers
full re-index vs incremental sync vs polling

text: Stale RAG hallucinates with confidence. Docs change, the index does not. When source is updated but chunks in the vector DB still reflect the old version, RAG retrieves stale content and the LLM answers with confidence. The user sees a fluent, wrong answer. There is no automatic stale signal - the only way to know is to compare against the live source, which RAG never does at query time. Example: password-reset article v1 published 2026-04-12, edited to v2 on 2026-05-01 adding MFA-preservation note. On 2026-05-10, query 'Do I lose my MFA after password reset?' hits v1 chunk because the index never re-ingested v2. LLM answers 'MFA may need re-setup' - wrong. Every doc has an expiry date. Stale answers feel correct because the LLM is confident.
summary: Stale indexes cause confident hallucinations because retrieval and generation proceed as if chunks were fresh, with no automatic stale-detection signal.
queries: why does RAG hallucinate after doc update
what happens when source doc changes but index doesn't
RAG returns old version of document
password reset MFA example stale RAG
retrieve old chunk after source edit
how to detect stale RAG answers
why LLM doesn't know index is stale

text: Strategy 1: Full Re-Index On A Schedule. The simplest sync strategy - heavy hammer. Every night, rebuild the entire index from scratch. No change detection, no event plumbing - just a cron job that re-ingests everything. Common starting point for in-house RAG. Mechanism: nightly cron at 2 AM fires. Job fetches all 10,000 docs, re-parses, re-chunks, re-embeds. Output written into a SHADOW index (parallel collection). At 6 AM, atomic pointer swap promotes shadow to primary. Retrieval traffic flips instantly with zero gap. Without shadow-index pattern, retrieval is degraded for the full 4-hour rebuild window - difference between clean swap and service incident. Cost math: 10K docs x 1K tokens/doc x $0.10/1M tokens = $1/night, roughly $30/month for 10K corpus, trivial. But at 1M docs: $3,000/month just for re-embedding. Re-embedding dominates the budget once corpus crosses 100K docs - above that, full re-index is the wrong default. Use full re-index if: corpus small (<10K docs), changes infrequent (weekly batch), simplicity beats freshness. Avoid if: real-time updates needed, corpus large enough for re-embed to dominate budget.
summary: Nightly cron rebuilds the entire index into a shadow collection then atomically swaps it in, cheap for small corpora but cost-prohibitive above 100K docs.
queries: how to do full nightly re-index RAG
cron job for vector index rebuild

text: Strategy 2: Webhook-Driven Incremental Sync. When source supports webhooks (Confluence, Notion, GitHub, Slack), index stays fresh in seconds instead of overnight. Each edit fires an event; ingestion service handles only docs that actually changed. No wasted re-embedding on the 99.99% of docs that did not change. 10-second propagation sequence: T=0:00 user edits doc (support author updates 'Reset Your Password' to add MFA-preservation note). T=0:01 webhook fires - Confluence POSTs {event: 'page.updated', page_id: 'doc-1', revision: 42}. T=0:02 fetch latest - ingestion service calls Confluence API to pull doc-1 revision 42 in full. T=0:05 re-parse + re-chunk + re-embed end-to-end, 3 chunks produced in ~150ms. T=0:08 upsert in vector DB with same doc_id, new chunks; old chunks dropped, new chunks inserted (Section 11.22 deletes-updates pattern). T=0:10 queryable - fresh chunks ready, ~10-second end-to-end propagation lag. Pros: real-time freshness, no wasted re-embedding, upsert lines up with Section 11.22, linear cost scales with edit volume not corpus size. Cons: requires webhook support (Confluence yes, raw S3 no), webhook failures need retry/DLQ handling, upserts must be idempotent to avoid duplicate writes on retries, out-of-order events need revision-number guards. Default for SaaS sources with event hooks - build idempotent upserts and a retry queue from day one.
summary: Webhook-driven incremental sync reacts to per-doc edit events for ~10-second propagation with linear cost in edit volume, requiring idempotent upserts and retry queues.
queries: how does webhook sync work in RAG
out of order webhook events revision number
when does webhook sync not work
linear cost edit volume not corpus size

text: Strategy 3: Polling - The Fallback For Webhook-Less Sources. Many sources (REST APIs without event hooks, SQL databases, internal services) cannot push events. The ingestion service must pull. Pattern: track per-source watermark, ask source on a timer what has changed since the watermark, advance the watermark, repeat. Worked example: last_synced_at = 2026-05-10T14:00:00Z (service tracks per-source watermark). interval = every 15 minutes (tune to freshness budget, throttle to source's rate limits). query = WHERE updated_at > 2026-05-10T14:00:00Z LIMIT 1000 (REST or SQL filter the source API exposes). result = 7 changed docs since last poll (process them, then advance last_synced_at to max(updated_at) in batch). Freshness lag edge case: doc-12 edited at t=2 min just after a poll at t=0. Change picked up at t=15 min (next poll). Lag = 13 minutes on average for that doc, 15 minutes worst case across the corpus. Tighter interval = lower lag, higher API cost. Looser interval = higher lag, lower API cost. Tune poll interval to match freshness budget. Use polling when: source has no webhooks but exposes a queryable updated_at field (most REST APIs, SQL databases, internal services). Cost: API calls every interval whether or not anything changed. Throttle the poller to source's rate limits. Lag is bounded by the poll interval.
summary: Polling tracks a per-source watermark and queries updated_at every interval to fetch changes, with freshness lag bounded by the poll interval.
queries: how does polling sync work RAG
SQL database sync to vector index
when to use polling instead of webhook
API cost of polling at fixed interval

text: Delete Propagation And Versioning: Where 90% Of In-House RAG Breaks. Sync is not just 'add new docs.' The hard parts are propagating deletes (when source removes a doc) and versioning chunks (so retrieval never sees a gap during a re-embed). Both are silent failures - eval sets catch them when chunks reference ghost content or queries miss during a swap, but customers do not see a stack trace, they just get the wrong answer. Three-row versioning timeline: Row 1 t=0 doc-1 v1 indexed, chunks=[c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1], status=active. Queries hit v1 chunks, source-of-truth and index agree. Row 2 t=+1min v2 ingested, v1 deprecated, chunks=[c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2] active + [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1] deprecated, status=active OR status=deprecated. Both versions live during re-embed window, no retrieval gap. Row 3 t=+15min v1 dropped after grace period, chunks=[c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2], status=active. Only v2 active, old chunks tombstoned and removed. Delete Propagation: source removes doc-1, webhook fires event 'page.deleted', ingestion service writes tombstone in vector DB, tombstone propagates to all replicas, all chunks for doc-1 removed (Section 11.22 multi-replica pattern). Without delete propagation: deleted docs still served - ghost chunks retrievable but pointing to content no longer in source. Versioning + Grace Period: new chunks land as status=active, old chunks flip to status=deprecated, both queryable during grace period (typically 5-15 minutes), after grace period deprecated chunks dropped. Without versioning: old chunks dropped before new land, retrieval gap, queries during re-embed window return empty or stale results. Silent failures: ghost chunks served as fact, or retrieval gap during re-embed - invisible to user, eval catches them, customers do not. Build delete propagation + versioning from day one - retro-fitting costs 10x the dev time because every downstream component (retrieval, eval, monitoring) already assumes chunks are immutable.
summary: Delete propagation via tombstones and chunk versioning with a 5-15 minute grace period prevent ghost chunks and retrieval gaps - silent failures that must be built from day one.
queries: how to handle deleted docs in vector DB
why old chunks must stay during re-embed

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Chapter 12.7 establishes why RAG must chunk and introduces fixed-size chunking as the baseline. Embedding a whole 500-page document fails because it overflows the 8,192-token embedding ceiling and one vector for 500 pages carries no specific signal. Three forces make chunking unavoidable: context limits, retrieval granularity, and signal dilution. Fixed-size chunking slides a fixed token window (e.g., size=128, overlap=16) across the document. The window ignores grammar so cuts land mid-sentence, breaking retrieval for cross-boundary facts. Overlap duplicates N tokens between adjacent chunks to preserve sentence-level cuts at ~12% extra vector cost, but does not fix long-range references. Fixed-size is the right default only for short, homogeneous docs or as a baseline; structured or heterogeneous content should move to structural or semantic chunking.
summary: Chapter 12.7 explains why RAG must chunk and introduces fixed-size sliding-window chunking with overlap as the baseline strategy to beat.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Why not embed the whole document? The intuitive first idea is to take a full 500-page product manual, embed it as one vector, and search. That instantly fails for two hard reasons. First, the document is too long to fit through the embedding model. A 500-page product manual is roughly 250,000 tokens, but embedding models like OpenAI ada-3 cap at 8,192 tokens. Only 8,192 tokens fit; 241,808 tokens overflow the ceiling. Second, even if it fit, one vector like [0.21, 0.84, 0.12, ...] representing 500 pages would mean nothing specific - it averages everything and carries no signal for any particular fact.
summary: Embedding a whole 500-page document fails because it overflows the 8,192-token embedding ceiling and even one vector for 500 pages carries no specific signal.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Three reasons production RAG always chunks. Chunking is not a workaround - it is the fundamental design choice. Three forces make it unavoidable. (1) Context Limits: embedding models cap at 8K tokens; LLMs cap at context-window length. A 500-page manual cannot fit in either. (2) Retrieval Granularity: top-K retrieval returns chunks, not whole docs. Smaller chunks = more focused matches. (3) Signal Dilution: averaging 500 pages into one vector buries any specific fact. Smaller chunks keep semantic density. Bottom line: chunking is the highest-leverage decision in RAG. Get the chunk strategy wrong and every downstream stage compounds the error.
summary: Production RAG always chunks because of context limits, retrieval granularity needs, and signal dilution from averaging.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Fixed-size chunking is a sliding window with overlap. The simplest possible strategy: slide a fixed token window across the document. Each window becomes one chunk. Adjacent chunks overlap by a few tokens so a sentence cut in half still appears whole somewhere. Formula: chunks = slide(doc, size=128, overlap=16). Example doc-1 is a Password Reset Article of ~480 tokens explaining how to reset a password, click the reset link valid for 24 hours, choose a strong password of at least twelve characters with letters/numbers/symbols, and what to do if the email doesn't arrive. Sliding 128-token window with 16-token overlap produces four chunks: Chunk 1 = tokens 0-128, Chunk 2 = tokens 112-240, Chunk 3 = tokens 224-352, Chunk 4 = tokens 336-464. The 16-token overlap zones (112-128, 224-240, 336-352) are shared between adjacent chunks.
summary: Fixed-size chunking slides a fixed token window across the document producing overlapping chunks like size=128 overlap=16 over a 480-token article giving four chunks.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Fixed-size cuts land anywhere - including mid-sentence. The token window does not care about grammar. The 128th token is wherever the 128th token is. Zoom into the boundary between chunk 1 and chunk 2 of doc-1. Chunk 1 ends mid-phrase: '...click the reset link in the email within 24 hours to set a new'. Chunk 2 starts mid-phrase: 'password. The link expires after that and you must request a new one...'. The boundary cut breaks retrieval: the chunk ending with 'set a new' has no idea what the user is setting; the chunk starting with 'password.' has no idea what action was being taken. Retrieval cannot match the query 'password reset expiry' to either piece alone - both lose the cross-boundary fact.
summary: Fixed-size chunking cuts mid-sentence because token windows ignore grammar, breaking retrieval when a fact spans the boundary.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Overlap helps - but only partially. The standard fix is overlap: duplicate the last N tokens of each chunk into the start of the next. A sentence cut at the boundary now appears whole in one of the two adjacent chunks. Overlap = 0 (naive) has a hard cut at the boundary: cuts can lose context entirely and recall drops on cross-chunk facts. Overlap = 16 tokens shares a 16-token band like 'set a new password. The link expires' across both chunks. Costs: overlap = duplicate storage cost; ~12% more vectors at size 128 / overlap 16; still breaks long-range references (entity defined earlier in doc, pronoun later). Overlap fixes the easy case (sentence-level cuts) but not the hard case (a definition on page 3 referenced by 'it' on page 17).
summary: Overlap duplicates N tokens between adjacent chunks to keep boundary sentences whole, costing ~12% more vectors and still failing on long-range references.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Fixed-size: the baseline to beat. Fixed-size chunking is the right default in a narrow band of cases. Use Fixed-Size If: (1) Docs are short (< 2K tokens each). (2) Content is homogeneous (uniform paragraphs). (3) You need a baseline to measure other strategies against. (4) Budget = zero engineering time. Move To Structural / Semantic If: (1) Docs have clear structural markers (markdown, HTML headings). (2) Content is heterogeneous (one doc = manual + FAQ + changelog). (3) Cross-chunk facts hurt recall in your eval set. Fixed-size is the baseline. Every chunking strategy in production is measured against it. Chapters 12.8-12.12 each fix one weakness of fixed-size.
summary: Fixed-size chunking is the baseline default for short, homogeneous docs but loses to structural or semantic chunking when content is structured, heterogeneous, or cross-chunk facts hurt recall.

text:
Chapter 12.8 Recursive Structural Chunking: Instead of slicing at arbitrary token positions, try the most structural separator first - paragraph break "

" - and only fall back through line break "
", sentence break ". ", and word break " " if the chunk still exceeds the size limit. Compared on doc-7 (login troubleshooting markdown), fixed-size with size=128 creates a chunk 2 that mixes Browser Compatibility with Account Lockout. Recursive structural with separators=["
## ", "

", "
", ". ", " "] uses the heading break and produces three single-topic chunks (~120, ~95, ~115 tokens). When a section like doc-12's 300-token Authentication Header Format exceeds the limit, recursion falls back to paragraph break and emits three sub-chunks of 100, 110, 90 tokens. It's the 80% baseline default in LangChain RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter and LlamaIndex SentenceSplitter - zero embedding cost at chunk time, respects markdown/HTML structure for free, but doesn't understand semantic boundaries (fixed in 12.9) or parent-child relationships (fixed in 12.11).

text:
Split by structure, not by token count. Fixed-size chunking cuts the password reset doc mid-phrase. Recursive structural chunking instead tries the most structural separator first - a paragraph break - and only falls back to smaller separators when the resulting chunk still exceeds the size limit. The cut lands at a boundary the writer already chose. Separator priority tree (try in order): paragraph break "

" (most structural, one blank line between blocks), then line break "
" (newline inside a paragraph still respects layout), then sentence break ". " (period followed by space - smallest grammar unit), then word break " " (plain space - never cuts mid-word). The recursive rule: try the most structural separator first; only fall back if the resulting chunk still exceeds the size limit. Separators = ["

", "
", ". ", " "]. The newline character "
" is what the writer typed to end a line. Two newlines mark a paragraph boundary.
summary: Recursive structural chunking tries paragraph breaks first, falling back through line, sentence, and word separators only when chunks remain too big.

text: Fixed-size breaks doc-7 in the wrong places. doc-7 is a login troubleshooting article (markdown, ~380 tokens) with three distinct topic sections: ## Common Login Errors (wrong password, account locked after 5 failed attempts in 15 minutes, session expired), ## Browser Compatibility (Safari blocks third-party cookies breaking SSO redirect, Chrome strict tracking protection same effect, fix is add auth domain to site exceptions), and ## Resetting Account Lockout (open account-admin panel, search by user email, click reset lockout, user can log in immediately, audit log captures admin action with timestamp). Apply fixed-size chunking with size=128, overlap=0: Chunk 1 (tokens 0-128) = all of "Common Login Errors" - one clean topic. Chunk 2 (tokens 128-256) = tail of "Browser Compatibility" + head of "Account Lockout" - spans two unrelated topics. A retrieval query for "Safari cookie SSO" matches this chunk, but the answer is half-Safari, half-lockout-admin. Neither topic is retrievable cleanly. Chunk 3 (tokens 256-380) = tail of "Resetting Account Lockout" - missing the heading, missing the first sentence, topic context is gone. Chunk 2 mixes browser compatibility with account lockout - retrieval cannot answer either cleanly.
summary: Fixed-size chunking on doc-7 ignores section structure and produces a middle chunk that mixes browser compatibility with account lockout topics.

Session expired shows up when an idle tab is reopened). Chunk 2 (~95 tokens) - Browser Compatibility (## Browser Compatibility. Safari blocks third-party cookies... Chrome with strict tracking protection has the same effect. The fix is to add the auth domain to the site exceptions list). Chunk 3 (~115 tokens) - Resetting Account Lockout (## Resetting Account Lockout. Open the account-admin panel... click reset lockout and confirm... Audit log captures the admin action with a timestamp). 3 chunks, each is exactly one topic. Retrieval matches the topic cleanly: a Safari cookie query goes to chunk 2 and nowhere else.

text: If a section is too big, recurse into paragraphs. Sometimes a single section blows past the size limit. doc-12 (API keys reference) has an "Authentication Header Format" section that runs 300 tokens - more than double the 128-token target. Recursion handles it cleanly. Recursion trace: Step 1: Try heading split. The "Authentication Header Format" section comes out at 300 tokens. Limit is 128. Too big. Step 2: Fall back to paragraph break "\n\n". The section has three paragraphs of 100, 110, and 90 tokens. Each fits under 128. Step 3: Emit three chunks (Chunk A 100 tok, Chunk B 110 tok, Chunk C 90 tok). Recursion stops as soon as every produced chunk fits. No need to drop to sentence or word splits. If a paragraph were still > 128, recursion would try ". " (sentence) next, then " " (word). The recursion goes deeper only when needed. Most chunks stop at the paragraph level.
summary: When a heading-level section exceeds the size limit, the recursive chunker falls back to paragraph break and emits sub-chunks that each fit under the target.

text: Recursive structural: the 80% baseline. Recursive structural is the default chunker in every major RAG framework. It covers around 80% of production cases out of the box. Pros: Preserves sentence + paragraph boundaries. Respects markdown / HTML structure for free. Zero embedding cost at chunk time (just string ops). Implemented by every major RAG framework (LangChain RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, LlamaIndex SentenceSplitter). Cons: Doesn't understand semantic boundaries (a topic spanning 4 paragraphs gets split). Heading-aware but not heading-equivalent (two ## sections on the same theme are split into two chunks). Plain-text docs without structural markers fall through to sentence/word splits (closer to fixed-size). Chapter 12.9 fixes the semantic-boundary gap. Chapter 12.11 fixes the parent-child gap.
summary: Recursive structural chunking is the default 80% baseline in every major framework but misses semantic boundaries and degrades on plain-text docs.

text: Chapter 12.9 Semantic Chunking. When a document has no structural markers (no headings, no blank lines), recursive structural chunking degrades to word splits. Semantic chunking instead: (1) splits the doc into sentences, (2) embeds each sentence to get vectors, (3) computes cosine similarity between adjacent sentence vectors, (4) emits a chunk break wherever similarity dips below a threshold like 0.7. On doc-12 (12-sentence API keys reference: 6 creating sentences then 6 revoking sentences) within-cluster similarity stays at 0.85-0.92 and the create-to-revoke shift at boundary 6-7 drops to 0.41 - producing exactly one clean topic break and two chunks that don't mix sub-topics. Cost: 10-50x more expensive than structural chunking because every sentence must be embedded before chunking (~60 ms per 12-sentence doc; ~$13,000 to chunk 10M docs), then chunks are re-embedded for the index. Use semantic chunking when structural markers are absent and retrieval quality matters more than indexing budget.
summary: Semantic chunking detects topic shifts in marker-less prose by splitting wherever adjacent-sentence cosine similarity dips below a threshold, at 10-50x the cost of structural chunking.

text: What if a document has no structural markers? doc-12 is an API keys reference written as pure flowing prose - no headings, no bullet lists, no blank-line separators. Six paragraphs of plain narrative. Recursive structural chunking falls all the way through paragraph break, line break, sentence break, and lands on word splits - essentially fixed-size. doc-12 contains 12 sentences: the first six describe creating an API key (developer settings page, New Key button, human-readable label, audit logs, scope dropdown, optional expiry, Generate button - key shown once). The last six describe revoking a key (same developer settings page, find by label in active keys list, trash icon, revocation takes effect within sixty seconds, in-flight requests return 401, revoked keys remain in audit log for compliance). The topic shift lives in the middle and nothing in the markup tells the splitter where. The structural gap: no headings (no ##, no #), no blank lines (no double newline - recursive splitter sees one long paragraph), topic shift is invisible (creating-to-revoking switch is a semantic boundary with no surface marker; structural splitter cuts mid-topic at the 128-token mark). Need a chunker that reads meaning, not markup.
summary: Flat narrative docs with no headings or blank lines defeat structural chunkers because the topic shift has no surface marker.

text: Step 1: Embed every sentence independently. Split doc-12 into individual sentences using a sentence tokenizer (period-plus-space heuristic, or a real NLP library). Twelve sentences come out. Send each sentence through the embedding model and get back a vector. Now there are 12 vectors capturing the meaning of each sentence. Formula: embed(sentence_i) -> v_i  |  12 sentences -> 12 vectors. Example vectors (8-dim, illustrative): s1 'Creating an API key starts in the developer settings page.' = [0.82, 0.14, 0.46, 0.71, 0.09, 0.55, 0.23, 0.66]; s6 'Click Generate and the key value is shown one time only - copy it now.' = [0.86, 0.13, 0.50, 0.72, 0.12, 0.57, 0.24, 0.70]; s7 'Revoking an API key is done from the same developer settings page.' = [0.21, 0.78, 0.16, 0.34, 0.74, 0.18, 0.69, 0.27]; s12 'Revoked keys remain in the audit log forever for compliance review.' = [0.24, 0.82, 0.13, 0.32, 0.78, 0.17, 0.73, 0.26]. Sentences s1-s6 (creating cluster) have vectors pointing in similar directions. Sentences s7-s12 (revoking cluster) have vectors pointing in a different direction. The 8-dim vectors shown are illustrative; production models use 768 or 1536 dims.
summary: Tokenize the doc into sentences then embed each one independently to get 12 meaning vectors per the doc-12 example.

text: Step 2: Plot cosine similarity between adjacent sentences. For each pair of adjacent sentences, compute cosine similarity between their vectors. Plot it along the doc. Inside a topic cluster similarity stays high (0.85-0.92). At the topic shift between sentence 6 (creating) and sentence 7 (revoking) it drops to 0.41. Set a threshold like 0.7 - any boundary below the threshold is a chunk break. Per-boundary cosine values: 1-2 = 0.91, 2-3 = 0.89, 3-4 = 0.87, 4-5 = 0.90, 5-6 = 0.88, 6-7 = 0.41 (DROP), 7-8 = 0.86, 8-9 = 0.85, 9-10 = 0.91, 10-11 = 0.83, 11-12 = 0.90. Algorithm: For i in 1..11: sim_i = cosine(v_i, v_i+1). If sim_i < 0.7 -> emit chunk break at boundary i. The red dashed threshold line at 0.7 marks the chunk-break cutoff. Only boundary 6-7 dips below 0.7.
summary: Compute adjacent-sentence cosine similarities, plot them, and emit a chunk break wherever similarity dips below 0.7.

text: Step 3: Each cluster of high-similarity sentences becomes one chunk. One boundary fell below the threshold (boundary 6-7 at sim 0.41). That single break splits the doc into exactly 2 chunks. Chunk 1 (creating cluster, sentences 1-6): 'Creating an API key starts in the developer settings page. Click the New Key button and provide a label. The label appears in audit logs. Choose a scope. Optional expiry sets a date. Click Generate and the key value is shown one time only.' Chunk break at boundary 6-7, sim = 0.41. Chunk 2 (revoking cluster, sentences 7-12): 'Revoking an API key is done from the same developer settings page. Find the key by its label. Click the trash icon. Revocation takes effect within sixty seconds. In-flight requests return 401 after propagation. Revoked keys remain in the audit log for compliance.' The win: semantic chunking found the topic shift that structural chunking would miss - no headings, no double newlines, no markers of any kind. Retrieval behavior: a query for 'how do I revoke a key' matches chunk 2 cleanly; a query for 'creating a scoped key' matches chunk 1. Neither chunk mixes the two sub-topics. Structural chunkers would have cut at the 128-token mark (somewhere inside sentence 5 or 6) and emitted a chunk that mixes 'optional expiry' with 'revoking the key'.
summary: The single cosine dip at boundary 6-7 splits doc-12 into a clean creating chunk and revoking chunk where structural chunkers would have cut mid-topic.

text: The cost: embed-before-chunk is 10-50x more expensive. Semantic chunking has to embed every sentence before it can decide where to chunk. That makes it an order of magnitude (or more) slower and costlier than structural chunking, where chunk-time work is just string splitting. Then you still re-embed the final chunks for the index. Structural / Fixed-Size cost: per doc 1 pass of string-splitting, about 0.1 ms. Embedding cost at chunk time is $0 - embedding happens later, once, on the final chunks. Pure CPU string ops. Semantic cost: per doc split into sentences (~0.5 ms) + embed each sentence (~5 ms per sentence x 12 = ~60 ms) + cosine math (~0.1 ms). At $0.10 per million tokens, a 30-doc corpus chunked semantically costs about $0.04. At 10M docs that is about $13,000 just to chunk - then you re-embed the final chunks for the index. Use semantic chunking when retrieval quality matters more than indexing budget, and when structural markers are absent.
summary: Semantic chunking is 10-50x costlier than structural because every sentence must be embedded before chunking, plus the final chunks are re-embedded for the index.

text: Side-by-side comparison diagram: left panel shows chunk-then-embed with three independent embedding passes per chunk producing v1, v2, v3 where each vector sees only its own chunk. Right panel shows late chunking with one whole-doc attention pass over all 384 tokens producing token hidden states (N x hidden_dim), followed by mean-pool at chunk boundaries to emit v_chunk1, v_chunk2, v_chunk3 where every chunk vector sees the whole doc.
summary: Diagram contrasts three separate per-chunk embeddings against a single whole-doc embedding pass that mean-pools into chunk vectors.
queries: chunk-then-embed vs late chunking comparison diagram
side by side late chunking visualization
three embedding passes vs one pass diagram
token hidden states pooled into chunk vectors diagram
v1 v2 v3 vs v_chunk1 v_chunk2 v_chunk3

text: Token-attention sweep diagram over doc-1 password reset: single attention arc spans all 12 token cells (Sarah ... she ...) with mean-pool boundary lines at token positions 128 and 256. Each token has a hidden state h1..h12; mean-pool over h1..h4 = v_chunk1, h5..h8 = v_chunk2, h9..h12 = v_chunk3. Annotation: v_chunk3 pools h9 to h12 and h10 ('she') attended to h1 ('Sarah'), so v_chunk3 still encodes 'Sarah' even though the word lives in chunk 1.
summary: Diagram of single attention arc covering all tokens with pool boundaries at 128 and 256, showing chunk 3's vector inherits the Sarah signal through attention.
queries: token attention sweep diagram late chunking

text: Chapter 12.10 Late Chunking (Jina 2024): chunk-then-embed strips cross-chunk references like pronouns ('she' in chunk 3 doesn't know it means Sarah from chunk 1). Late chunking reverses the order - run one embedding pass over the whole doc so attention links every token to every other token, then mean-pool token hidden states at chunk boundaries. v_chunk_i = mean-pool(H[chunk_i.start : chunk_i.end]). Result: chunk 3's vector still encodes Sarah, flipping a 0.34 retrieval miss into a 0.81 Top-1 hit on the same query. Same compute as chunk-then-embed. Requires a model that exposes token-level hidden states (jina-embeddings-v2) and a doc that fits one embedding pass (8K-32K). Doesn't work with OpenAI/Cohere APIs that only return pooled vectors. Use on anaphora-heavy corpora (support, legal, narrative); otherwise stick with chunk-then-embed plus hybrid/rerank.
summary: Late chunking embeds the whole doc first then pools by chunk so cross-chunk references survive, boosting recall on anaphora-heavy docs without extra compute.
queries: when does late chunking help RAG
mean pool token hidden states summary

text: Chunk-then-embed loses cross-chunk context. doc-1 password-reset article split into 3 chunks. Chunk 1 (tokens 0-128) names 'Sarah' and sets up reset flow: 'When a user named Sarah forgets her password, the system generates a one-time token. The reset flow begins on the login page when she clicks the Forgot Password link.' Antecedent 'Sarah' lives here. Chunk 2 (tokens 128-256): token hashed, stored in password_reset_tokens table, emailed as single-use link, subject 'Reset Your Password', arrives within 30 seconds. No mention of who requested it. Chunk 3 (tokens 256-384): 'The link expires after 24 hours; she must request a new one if she misses the window. The old token is invalidated immediately on reuse.' Pronoun 'she' has no antecedent in this chunk. When you embed each chunk in isolation, the chunk-3 vector has zero knowledge that 'she' refers to Sarah from chunk 1. Cross-chunk references (pronouns, 'the system', 'this approach', 'that field') bleed context the moment you slice the doc.
summary: Embedding chunks in isolation strips cross-chunk references like pronouns, leaving the chunk-3 vector unable to know 'she' means Sarah.

text: Chunk-then-embed vs embed-then-chunk (late chunking). Baseline (chunk-then-embed): doc-1 sliced into 3 chunks, each chunk independently fed through embedding model, producing v1, v2, v3 - 3 separate passes, each vector sees only its own chunk. Late chunking (embed-then-chunk, Jina 2024): doc-1 (384 tokens) feeds a single embedding pass that runs attention over all 384 tokens, producing token hidden states (N x hidden_dim). Pool boundaries at the chunk cut points mean-pool the token states into v_chunk1, v_chunk2, v_chunk3. One pass plus pool - every chunk vector sees the whole doc. Same compute as chunk-then-embed for the same doc; vastly different vectors. Formulas: Baseline: v_i = embed(chunk_i). Late: H = embed_tokens(doc); v_chunk_i = mean-pool(H[chunk_i.start : chunk_i.end]).
summary: Late chunking reverses the order - embed the whole doc first so attention links all tokens, then mean-pool token hidden states at chunk boundaries.
queries: embed then chunk vs chunk then embed
single embedding pass over whole doc
mean pool token hidden states for chunks
difference between chunk-then-embed and late chunking

text: Late chunking on doc-1: chunk 3's vector now encodes Sarah. Embedding model runs once over all 384 tokens. Attention links every token to every other token. The hidden state for 'she' in chunk 3 has attended to 'Sarah' in chunk 1. Pool boundaries at token 128 and token 256 split the token hidden states into 3 chunks. v_chunk_i = mean-pool(h_start...h_end). v_chunk3 pools h9 to h12 - and h10 ('she') attended to h1 ('Sarah'), so v_chunk3 still encodes 'Sarah' even though the word lives in chunk 1. Trace: H = embed_tokens(doc-1); H.shape = [384, 768]; v_chunk3 = mean(H[256:384]).
summary: Because attention runs across the whole doc before pooling, the 'she' token in chunk 3 carries the Sarah signal into v_chunk3.

text: Query 'Did Sarah get her reset email?' now matches chunk 3 with late chunking. Same query, same 3 chunks, same embedding model - only embedding order changes. Chunk-then-embed (baseline) scores: Chunk 1 (mentions Sarah, forgets password) = 0.78 Top-1; Chunk 2 (token generation, email send) = 0.65; Chunk 3 (link expiry, 'she' re-request) = 0.34. Top-1 is chunk 1, misses the actual answer because chunk 3's 'she' vector has no idea who 'she' is. Late chunking scores: Chunk 1 = 0.72; Chunk 2 = 0.68; Chunk 3 = 0.81 Top-1. Hits the answer because chunk-3 token hidden states attended to 'Sarah' in chunk 1 before pooling. Retrieval scores flip - chunk 3 goes from 0.34 miss to 0.81 hit because its vector now carries the Sarah signal.
summary: Same query and chunks; late chunking flips chunk 3 from a 0.34 miss to a 0.81 Top-1 hit because its vector encodes the cross-chunk Sarah antecedent.

text: Late chunking: pros, cons, when to use. Wins: preserves cross-chunk pronouns and references (anaphora resolution); single embedding pass = same compute as chunk-then-embed for the same doc; significant recall gains on docs with anaphora (he, she, it, this, that, the X); released 2024 by Jina AI for the jina-embeddings-v2 model family. Limits: requires an embedding model that exposes token-level hidden states (most OpenAI/Cohere APIs return only the pooled final vector - you cannot late-chunk with them); requires the whole doc to fit in one embedding pass (8K-32K token ceilings); pooling strategy matters (mean-pool vs CLS vs attention-weighted - jina-embeddings-v2 uses mean-pool). Reach for late chunking when corpus has heavy anaphora (support docs, legal text, narrative articles), docs fit in one embedding pass, and you can run an open-weights model like jina-embeddings-v2 that exposes token-level hidden states. Otherwise keep chunk-then-embed and lean on reranking or hybrid search to recover cross-chunk recall.
summary: Late chunking is free recall on anaphora-heavy docs but only works with open models that expose token hidden states and docs that fit one embedding pass.
queries: why most OpenAI models can't late chunk
when not to use late chunking

Each summary card contains a one-sentence LLM-generated summary ('Who qualifies and refund window', 'Filing steps and SLAs', 'Late requests and appeals'). The index now stores summary vectors (one per section) instead of leaf vectors. Leaves still appear faintly below as the eventual generation target. Bottom annotation: 'Index stores summary vectors. Retrieval finds a summary. Swap to the full parent section on a hit.'

text: Hierarchical / parent-child chunking resolves the tug-of-war between small chunks (precise retrieval) and large chunks (rich generation context) by chunking the document twice. A tree is built with a root (whole doc), mid-level section nodes (~300 tokens each), and small leaf chunks (~64 tokens each). Only the leaf vectors are indexed; parent texts live in a separate parent-text store keyed by leaf id. Retrieval searches the leaf index and finds the most precise match. Before generation, the system looks up the retrieved leaf's parent_id and swaps in the full parent section, giving the LLM 4-5x more context than the leaf alone. A summary-chunk variation indexes LLM-generated 1-2 sentence section summaries instead of leaves for denser semantic matching. Storage cost is ~2-3x the raw corpus; latency adds one sub-millisecond lookup per hit. LangChain (ParentDocumentRetriever) and LlamaIndex (RecursiveRetriever) ship the pattern out of the box.
summary: Hierarchical chunking indexes small leaves for precise retrieval and swaps in full parent sections for generation, getting both precision and context.
queries: how does parent document retrieval work
RAG chunking with retrieval and generation tradeoff
chunking strategy that gives both precision and context
framework support for parent child chunking

text: Chunk size is a tug-of-war between two phases of RAG. A retriever finds the best matching chunk; an LLM answers from the chunk text. Small chunks (64 tokens) give high retrieval precision because each chunk is one focused fact, but low generation context because the LLM may need surrounding paragraphs to answer fully. Large chunks (1024 tokens) give low retrieval precision because many topics per chunk make the vector average them, but high generation context because the LLM has the whole section. Pick one and you are forced to compromise. What if you did not have to?
summary: Small chunks retrieve precisely but starve the LLM of context; large chunks give context but blur retrieval - a fundamental tug-of-war.
queries: why does chunk size matter in RAG
small chunks vs large chunks tradeoff
what chunk size should I use for RAG
why do small chunks help retrieval
why do large chunks help LLM generation
can I have small retrieval and large context

text: Hierarchical chunking: chunk the doc twice and build a tree. The root is the whole doc. Mid-level nodes are sections of around 300 tokens each. Leaves are tiny 64-token chunks that fit one fact apiece. Only the leaf vectors live in the index - they are what we search. Parents live in a separate parent-text store keyed by id, ready to be swapped in at generation time. Doc-4 refunds policy example: 1 root (900 tokens total) + 3 sections (Eligibility, Process, Edge Cases, ~300 tokens each) + 12 leaves (64 tokens each, 4 per section). Index: { leaf_id -> vector }. Parent Store: { leaf_id -> parent_text }.
summary: Build a tree of root + sections + leaves; index only the small leaves but keep parents in a separate store for swap-in at generation time.
queries: do I index parents or leaves

text: Step 1: retrieval finds the best leaf. The user asks 'Can I get a refund if I cancel after 30 days?' The query is embedded once. We search the leaf index. Top-3 leaves come back ranked by cosine similarity: L7 wins at 0.89 ('...no refunds after 30 days...'), L3 at 0.74 ('Refund eligibility window: 14 days full, 15-30 days prorated...'), L12 at 0.68 ('Appeal process for denied refund requests...'). L7 wins because its 64-token text directly states the 30-day cutoff - a precise hit on the query.
summary: Embed the query once, search the leaf index, and the top-1 leaf is the precise 64-token snippet that answers the question.
queries: how does retrieval work in hierarchical chunking
why do small chunks win retrieval
what does the retriever return hierarchical
leaf wins on precise phrase match

text: Step 2: swap the leaf for its parent before the LLM. We retrieved a leaf but we do not send the leaf to the LLM. Each leaf carries the id of its parent section. A parent-lookup pulls the full 300-token Edge Cases section from the parent-text store. That section enters the prompt so the LLM has the eligibility nuance, the appeal path, and the carve-outs - not just one sentence in isolation. Flow: Retrieved Leaf L7 (64 tokens, '...no refunds after 30 days...', parent_id: sec-edge-cases, score 0.89) → parent_store[L7.parent_id] → Parent Section Edge Cases (300 tokens: 14-day full refund window, 15-30 day prorated refund, no refunds after 30 days, manual-review carve-out, appeal path Form A-12, special-case refunds; ~4.7x more context than leaf) → LLM Prompt Context. Retrieval precision comes from the leaf; generation context comes from the parent.
summary: After retrieval, look up the leaf's parent_id and send the full ~300-token parent section to the LLM instead of the leaf.
queries: how does parent swap work in hierarchical chunking
what does the LLM actually see hierarchical
why send parent not leaf to LLM
why retrieval precision and generation context together

text: Variation: index summary chunks instead of leaves. Same tree, different retrieval target. Run an LLM over each section once at index time to generate a 1-2 sentence summary. Embed and index those summary chunks instead of the leaves. On a hit, swap to the full parent section (or even the whole doc). Doc-4 example: each section node gets a yellow summary card placed beside it ('Who qualifies and refund window' for Eligibility; 'Filing steps and SLAs' for Process; 'Late requests and appeals' for Edge Cases). Index stores 1 summary vector per section. Summaries pack more meaning per token so the vector match can be more semantic. Trade-off: summaries are denser semantically (one vector encodes the gist of a 300-token section) but require an LLM pass at index time to generate them.
summary: Instead of indexing leaves, run an LLM once per section to make a 1-2 sentence summary, index those, and swap to the parent on a hit.
queries: cost of summary chunking index time

text: Hierarchical chunking is a workhorse pattern in production RAG: precise retrieval and rich generation context at the price of storing parent texts. Use it when: docs are long and have natural section structure (manuals, policies, RFCs); queries need surrounding context to answer well (legal, medical, technical docs where one sentence is rarely enough); storage cost for parent texts is acceptable (roughly 2-3x raw doc size). Cost & implementation: Storage = index + parent-text store, roughly 2-3x raw corpus size. Latency = one extra lookup per hit (parent-from-leaf-id), sub-millisecond. Implementation: every major framework has this pattern - LangChain ParentDocumentRetriever, LlamaIndex RecursiveRetriever - working in a few lines.
summary: Use hierarchical when docs have section structure and queries need context; pay ~2-3x storage and one extra lookup; LangChain and LlamaIndex ship it.
queries: when not to use hierarchical chunking

text: Horizontal bar chart titled 'Top-20 Retrieval Failure Rate (Lower Is Better)' showing four bars from Anthropic's September 2024 Contextual Retrieval benchmark. Bars from top to bottom: Naive Embedding Only at 5.7% (Baseline, gray #9aa0a6), +Contextual Embedding at 3.7% (35% Reduction, cyan #80deea), +Contextual BM25 at 2.9% (49% Reduction, green #a5d6a7), +Reranking at 1.9% (67% Reduction, orange #ffcc80). X-axis labeled 'Failure Rate (%)' with ticks at 0%, 2%, 4%, 6%. Chart title 'Method · Failure Rate' at top. Values and reduction percentages displayed at end of each bar.
summary: Horizontal bar chart of Anthropic's four-method failure rate benchmark: 5.7% naive, 3.7% contextual embedding, 2.9% contextual BM25, 1.9% with reranking.
queries: what does the contextual retrieval chart show

text: Contextual Retrieval (Anthropic, September 2024) prepends a 1-2 sentence LLM-generated context line to each chunk before embedding and indexing. This disambiguates chunks that share surface phrases across different documents. The augmentation runs once at index time using a cheap model like Claude Haiku (about $0.001 per chunk, ~$100 per 100K chunks, with prompt caching reusing the document prefix). Anthropic's benchmark on top-20 retrieval failure rate: naive embedding 5.7%, +contextual embedding 3.7% (35% reduction), +contextual BM25 hybrid 2.9% (49% reduction), +reranking 1.9% (67% reduction). The technique composes with hybrid search and rerankers - each layer attacks a different failure mode. Skip when corpus is small/homogeneous or recall isn't the bottleneck; use when many docs share surface phrases.
summary: Contextual Retrieval prepends LLM-written context to chunks at index time, cutting Anthropic's benchmark retrieval failure rate by 35-67% when stacked with hybrid search and rerankers.
queries: prepend context to chunks before embedding
fix chunks that look identical to embedder

text: Chunks out of context look identical to the embedding model. Three chunks from three unrelated docs (Doc-2 Email Change Guide, Doc-15 Role Permissions Guide, Doc-18 Notifications Settings) all end with the exact same sentence 'click Save to confirm.' Query: 'How do I change my email?' -> q_vec. The embedding model sees only the token sequence. With nearly identical tokens, the three chunks produce nearly identical vectors (e.g., [0.31, 0.42, 0.18, 0.55, 0.27, 0.39, 0.46, 0.22] vs [0.30, 0.41, 0.19, 0.54, 0.29, 0.38, 0.45, 0.23] vs [0.32, 0.40, 0.18, 0.55, 0.28, 0.39, 0.47, 0.21]). All three score 0.71 similarity to the query. Recall@1 collapses to guessing - the wrong chunk is returned roughly two times out of three.
summary: Three unrelated chunks ending in the same phrase produce near-identical vectors and tie at 0.71 similarity, making retrieval effectively random.
queries: why do unrelated chunks have identical embeddings

chunks with same surface tokens collide

text: Solution: prepend an LLM-generated context line. Before each chunk is embedded, run a cheap LLM call once at index time to write a short 1-2 sentence context: what doc the chunk is from, what section, and what it is about. Prepend that context line to the chunk text. Example for Chunk A: prefix 'Document: Email Change Guide. Section: Step 4 - Verify New Address. This chunk is about confirming the new email after verification.' followed by the original body '...verify your new email address, then click Save to confirm.' Full augmented chunk = [Prefix] + [Body], which is what gets embedded and indexed. The body never changes - only the index-side representation gets richer. The original tokens are still available for the LLM to read at generation time.
summary: Augmentation prepends a 1-2 sentence LLM-written context describing the doc/section to each chunk before embedding, enriching the index-side vector without changing the body.
queries: what is prepended to chunks in contextual retrieval
