text:
The augmentation prompt template (run once per chunk). Exact prompt fired at index time:

Here is a chunk from a documentation page.
<document_title>{document_title}</document_title>
<section_heading>{section_heading}</section_heading>
<chunk>
{chunk}
</chunk>
Write a short (1-2 sentence) context that situates this chunk within the document and section so retrieval models can find it. Output only the context, no preamble.

Placeholders {document_title}, {section_heading}, {chunk} are filled in for every chunk. Model choice: Anthropic recommends Claude Haiku - a cheap model is enough; the task is short and structured. Cost at index time: roughly $0.001 per chunk. For 100K chunks that is about $100 one-time. With prompt caching the document prefix is paid once across all its chunks.
summary: Exact augmentation prompt template uses Claude Haiku to generate a 1-2 sentence context per chunk at ~$0.001/chunk (~$100 per 100K chunks), with prompt caching reusing the document prefix.
queries: cost of contextual retrieval per chunk
how much does contextual retrieval cost
prompt caching saves money per document

text: Before vs. after recall on the email-change query. Same query, same three chunks, same embedding model - only difference is augmentation. Row 1 Without Augmentation: Chunk A (Doc-2 Email Change) 0.71, Chunk B (Doc-15 Role Permissions) 0.71, Chunk C (Doc-18 Notifications) 0.71. All three tied at 0.71. Top-1 effectively random. Recall@1 = 33%. Row 2 With Augmentation: Chunk A 0.93 (Top-1, augmentation explicitly mentions email change - direct hit), Chunk B 0.42 (augmentation pulls vector toward roles, away from email), Chunk C 0.39 (augmentation pulls vector toward notifications, away from email). Chunk A wins at 0.93. Recall@1 = 100%. The augmented vectors are no longer near-duplicates - Chunk A's prefix mentions email change explicitly, pulling its vector toward the query; B and C drift away.
summary: Before/after comparison: without augmentation all three chunks tie at 0.71 (recall@1 = 33%); with augmentation Chunk A jumps to 0.93 while B (0.42) and C (0.39) drift away, lifting recall@1 to 100%.
queries: before and after contextual retrieval scores
why does augmented chunk score higher
why does augmentation pull vectors apart

text: Anthropic's 2024 benchmark: 49% recall improvement. Anthropic published a paper-length blog post in September 2024 measuring contextual retrieval over multiple corpora. Top-20 retrieval failure rate (lower is better): Naive Embedding Only 5.7% (baseline), +Contextual Embedding 3.7% (35% reduction), +Contextual BM25 2.9% (49% reduction), +Reranking 1.9% (67% reduction). The 49% headline number is the failure rate reduction from naive embedding (5.7%) to contextual embedding plus contextual BM25 hybrid (2.9%). Stacking a reranker on top brings the combined stack to 67% fewer failures than naive embedding retrieval.
summary: Anthropic's September 2024 benchmark shows top-20 failure rate falling from 5.7% (naive) to 3.7% (+contextual embedding, 35%), 2.9% (+contextual BM25, 49%), and 1.9% (+reranking, 67%).
queries: top-20 failure rate naive vs contextual
does adding reranker help contextual retrieval
how much better is contextual retrieval
reranker on top of contextual retrieval

text: When contextual retrieval is overkill. It pays for itself when many docs share surface phrases and recall is a real bottleneck. On a small homogeneous corpus the one-time augmentation cost is hard to justify - the recall problem can be solved more cheaply by hand-tuning chunks or by adding a reranker first. Skip If: Corpus is fully homogeneous (one product manual; no risk of cross-doc duplicate phrases); Index size is small (under 1K chunks; recall problems can be solved by hand-tuning); Index budget is hard (the one-time $100-$1,000 augmentation cost is too high for a prototype). Use If: Corpus has many docs with similar surface phrases (FAQ + support + product = lots of click Save to confirm overlap); Recall is the bottleneck in your eval metrics; Hybrid and reranking are already in place and you want one more lever. Rule of thumb: if recall is not your bottleneck, do not add the augmentation pass. The 49% headline number is a ceiling, not a guarantee - it only shows up when chunks would otherwise collide on surface tokens.
summary: Skip contextual retrieval on small/homogeneous corpora or tight budgets; use it when many docs share surface phrases, recall is the bottleneck, and hybrid+reranking are already in place.
queries: when not to use contextual retrieval
use if many docs share phrases

text: Stacks with hybrid search and rerankers for the full 67%. Contextual retrieval is not a replacement for hybrid search or rerankers - it composes with them. Step 1: Contextual Augmentation (Index Time) - Run an LLM over each chunk once. Prepend a 1-2 sentence context line saying what doc and section the chunk lives in. Embed and index the augmented chunk. Step 2: Hybrid Retrieval (Query Time) - Dense embedding search and BM25 lexical search run in parallel over the augmented chunks. Their top-20 results are merged into a single candidate list. (Hybrid covered in Section 11.25.) Step 3: Reranker (Top-20 To Top-3) - A cross-encoder scores each query-chunk pair jointly and reorders the candidates. The top 3 go into the LLM prompt. (Rerankers covered in Section 11.26.) Combined: 67% failure-rate reduction over naive (Anthropic 2024). Augmentation lifts dense and BM25 recall in parallel; the reranker fixes ordering on whatever the hybrid stack surfaces. Each component is independently useful, but the full stack is where the 67% number lives.
summary: The full stack chains contextual augmentation at index time, hybrid dense+BM25 retrieval at query time, and a cross-encoder reranker that picks top-3 from top-20 - delivering the 67% failure reduction.
queries: how to stack contextual retrieval with hybrid and reranker
full RAG stack with contextual retrieval
does contextual retrieval replace hybrid search
when do you embed augmented chunks
how does the 67% number come about
contextual augmentation plus BM25 plus reranking
composing contextual retrieval with other techniques

text: Horizontal cost-budget scale with three tier markers labeled Lab / Prototype, Startup, and Enterprise spread left-to-right along a dashed spine with arrow on right. Each marker is a colored circle (green/cyan/orange) with the tier name above and dollar cost below: Free, ~$100 one-time, $100-$10K one-time. Axis label reads 'Indexing Budget Grows Left To Right.' Below the scale, three cards list the recommended chunking stack for each tier: Lab uses Recursive Structural only; Startup adds Hierarchical for long-form; Enterprise stacks Recursive Structural plus Hierarchical plus Contextual Retrieval plus Semantic where prose dominates, with hybrid plus rerankers.
summary: Cost-budget scale diagram showing three tiers and recommended chunking stacks per indexing budget.

text: Chapter 12.13 synthesizes chunking strategies from 12.7-12.12 into a decision framework along three axes: document structure (markdown/HTML/PDF/code/flat narrative/mixed), query type (factual/relational/comparative), and indexing budget (lab/startup/enterprise). Six strategies compared on quality, cost, implementation: Fixed-Size (12.7), Recursive Structural (12.8), Semantic (12.9), Late (12.10), Hierarchical (12.11), Contextual Retrieval (12.12). Production chunking is rarely one strategy - mix per doc-type. Walkthrough uses customer support corpus with three doc-types getting three different recipes: Contextual for FAQ billing, Hierarchical for product features, Semantic for troubleshooting runbooks.
summary: Framework for choosing chunking strategy by doc structure, query type, and budget, with mixed-strategy production walkthrough.
queries: how do I choose a chunking strategy for RAG
which chunking method should I use
fixed-size vs semantic vs hierarchical chunking
best chunking for markdown vs PDF
chunking strategy for factual vs relational queries
mix chunking strategies per doc type

text: Six Strategies, One Page. Chapters 12.7 to 12.12 each introduced one chunking strategy. Lined up side by side, each has its own quality, cost, and implementation profile. Meters: retrieval quality (higher better), embedding cost at chunk time, implementation difficulty. Strategies: Fixed-Size (12.7) - sliding window, no structure awareness, quality=1, cost=1, difficulty=1. Recursive Structural (12.8) - tries paragraph then line then sentence then word, quality=2, cost=1, difficulty=1. Semantic (12.9) - embed sentences, split where cosine dips, quality=3, cost=2, difficulty=2. Late (12.10) - one whole-doc attention pass, then pool per chunk, quality=3, cost=2, difficulty=3. Hierarchical (12.11) - small chunks retrieve, parents go to LLM, quality=3, cost=2, difficulty=2. Contextual Retrieval (12.12) - LLM-generated context prefixed to each chunk, quality=4, cost=3, difficulty=2. No strategy wins on all three axes. Fixed-Size cheapest but loses quality; Contextual gives highest retrieval quality but adds index-time LLM cost.
summary: Side-by-side comparison of six chunking strategies on quality, cost, and implementation meters.
queries: fixed-size vs recursive structural vs semantic
quality cost difficulty of chunking methods
which chunking gives best retrieval quality
why no chunking wins all axes

text: Axis 1: What structure do your docs have? Structure constrains the chunker more than content does. Markdown and HTML come with built-in split points; PDFs and code have predictable shapes; flat narrative prose has none and forces a content-aware strategy. Recommendations by doc type: Markdown / HTML → Recursive Structural (uses heading and paragraph markers natively). PDF (scanned or extracted) → Recursive Structural after extraction, or Semantic if extraction quality is bad. Code → Recursive Structural with language-aware separators (function defs, class declarations). Flat-text narrative (no headings, no markdown) → Semantic Chunking (only way to find topic boundaries). Mixed / heterogeneous corpus → Apply different strategies per doc-type. Don't force one strategy. The biggest signal is whether your corpus has explicit split points. If yes, Recursive Structural is almost always the right starting baseline. Only flat narrative forces you up the cost ladder to Semantic on day one.
summary: Picking chunking by document structure - markdown gets recursive structural, flat prose needs semantic.
queries: when to use semantic over recursive

text: Axis 2: What kinds of queries will you get? Query shape changes which chunk size and which retrieval pattern wins. Factual lookups want small, high-precision chunks; relational and comparative questions want enough surrounding context to reason across entities. Factual ("What is X?") → Small chunks are fine. Recursive Structural plus Contextual if FAQ-style overlap. Hierarchical optional for context-heavy answers. Relational ("How does X relate to Y?") → Hierarchical helps (LLM needs whole relationship section). Late Chunking helps when entities referenced across chunks. Comparative ("Compare X and Y") → Multi-hop retrieval (chapter 12.28) on top of any chunking. Bigger chunks help. Hierarchical preferred. Hierarchical is the safest middle bet: serves factual queries through leaves and relational through parents. Comparative queries on top of any chunking still need multi-hop retrieval.
summary: Picking chunking by query type - factual wants small chunks, relational and comparative want context.
queries: what chunk size for FAQ queries
small chunks vs big chunks for queries
best chunking for compare X and Y queries
why hierarchical is safe middle bet

text: Axis 3: What's your indexing budget? Chunking cost is a one-time index-time spend, but scales with corpus size. Three tiers on a horizontal cost-budget scale: Lab / Prototype (Free) → Recursive Structural only. Zero embedding cost at chunk time. Iterate fast. Startup (~$100 one-time) → Recursive Structural plus Hierarchical for long-form docs. Skip semantic and contextual until you see recall problems in your eval. Enterprise ($100 - $10K one-time) → Recursive Structural baseline plus Hierarchical plus Contextual Retrieval plus (Semantic where prose dominates). Stack with hybrid plus rerankers. Budget for the one-time $100 - $10K augmentation cost. Cost climbs with augmentation, not with chunking itself. Recursive Structural is free to run; Contextual Retrieval is the big spike because it fires an LLM call per chunk. Treat the spike as a one-time index cost amortized across every future query.
summary: Three budget tiers (lab/startup/enterprise) with recommended chunking stacks scaled to indexing cost.
queries: when can I afford contextual retrieval

text: Walkthrough: Picking Strategies For The Customer Support Corpus. The 30-doc support corpus splits into three doc-types with three different profiles. The right answer is not one strategy: it is a mix. Account & Billing (10 docs, FAQ-style, lots of repeated phrases like 'click Save') → Recursive Structural plus Contextual Retrieval. Contextual disambiguates duplicate phrases across email, role, and notifications docs. Product Features (10 docs, longer technical pages with sections and code samples) → Recursive Structural plus Hierarchical. Sections give clean splits; parent-swap gives the LLM context for technical answers. Troubleshooting (10 docs, free-form runbooks, narrative paragraphs) → Recursive Structural plus Semantic for the longer narratives. Semantic catches topic shifts within a runbook. Production Chunking Is Rarely One Strategy: mix per doc-type, measure recall, iterate. Chapters 12.14-12.17 move to embedding model choice and how chunking interacts with embedding quality.
summary: Customer support corpus walkthrough mixing Contextual, Hierarchical, and Semantic chunking across three doc-types.

text: Four-step decision flowchart for picking an embedding model. Step 1: Need multilingual? Yes -> Use Cohere v3 / Voyage / OpenAI 3-large. No -> go on. Step 2: Specialized domain? Yes -> Domain-tuned BGE or fine-tune (see 12.15). No -> go on. Step 3: Budget sensitive? Yes -> Self-host BGE. No -> go on. Step 4: Default -> OpenAI text-embedding-3-large or Cohere embed-v3. Each question card connects via right arrow to a yes-branch card; each 'No' falls down to the next step. Rendered as a centered SVG with cyan question cards, green default card.
summary: SVG decision flowchart: four sequential questions (multilingual, domain, budget, default) branching right on Yes and falling down on No to pick an embedding model.
queries: how to choose embedding model diagram
picking embedder step by step visual
when to use Cohere v3 vs OpenAI

text: Chapter 12.14 covers how to pick an embedding model for RAG. The embedding model sets a hard recall ceiling that no reranker can recover - on the same support corpus, OpenAI ada-002 gets recall@10 of 0.62 while Cohere embed-v3 reaches 0.83 and BGE-Large reaches 0.81. Five axes matter: dimension, multilingual coverage, domain fit, cost per million tokens, and max context length. Higher dimensions cost more memory (d=384 -> 1.5GB, d=3072 -> 12GB at 1M vectors with float32) with diminishing quality returns past ~1024. Multilingual models waste capacity on English-only data. Cost ranges from ~$50 self-hosted BGE on g5.xlarge to $13,000 for OpenAI 3-large on a 100M-token ingest. MTEB is a hint, not an answer - models can over-fit the leaderboard. A four-step decision flow: need multilingual? -> specialized domain? -> budget sensitive? -> default to OpenAI text-embedding-3-large or Cohere embed-v3.
summary: Chapter overview of picking embedding model for RAG: recall ceiling, five tradeoff axes, dimension/memory math, multilingual fit, cost tiers, MTEB caveat, four-step decision flow.
queries: how do I pick an embedding model for RAG
which embedding model is best for retrieval
OpenAI vs Cohere vs BGE embedding comparison
does the embedding model matter for RAG
multilingual vs english only embedding model
Voyage-2 vs Cohere vs OpenAI embeddings

text: The embedding model decides the recall ceiling. Chunking decides how text is split. The embedding model decides what those chunks mean as vectors (recap from Section 5.2). Two systems on the same corpus, with the same chunks and the same vector DB, can have wildly different recall just because one uses a stronger embedding model. The chosen model sets a hard ceiling: no reranker or hybrid trick can recover documents your embedder failed to bring close enough in the first place. Recall@10 on the support corpus (same chunks, same index): OpenAI ada-002 (older) = 0.62, Cohere embed-v3 = 0.83, BGE-Large = 0.81. Recall is 'did the right doc land in the top 10?' Higher is better.
summary: Embedding model sets a hard recall ceiling no downstream trick can recover; ada-002 0.62 vs Cohere v3 0.83 vs BGE-Large 0.81 on identical chunks and index.
queries: why does embedding model matter for retrieval
what is recall ceiling in RAG
same corpus different embedding model results
does choice of embedding model matter
how much can embedding model improve recall
hybrid search cannot fix embedding ceiling

text: Five axes that matter. Every embedding model trades off the same five dimensions. Memorize this table and most 'which embedder should I use?' questions answer themselves. Table: Model | Dim | Multilingual | Domain | Cost | Max Context. OpenAI text-embedding-3-large: 3072, Yes, General, $0.13/M tokens, 8191. Cohere embed-v3: 1024, Yes, General, $0.10/M tokens, 512. BGE-Large: 1024, EN+CN, General, Free self-host, 512. SBERT all-mpnet-base: 768, English mostly, General, Free self-host, 384. Voyage-2: 1024, Yes, Domain variants available, $0.10/M tokens, 16000. Dim = output vector length. Multilingual = trained on multiple languages. Domain = general purpose vs specialized. Cost = price per million input tokens (or self-host). Max Context = longest single input.
summary: Five axes for choosing embedder - dim, multilingual, domain, cost, max context - with concrete values for OpenAI 3-large, Cohere v3, BGE-Large, SBERT all-mpnet-base, Voyage-2.
queries: what dimensions does OpenAI text-embedding-3-large output

text: Higher dim costs more memory and compute. Every dimension is a 4-byte float. Memory scales linearly with dim. At 1 million vectors, the difference between d=384 and d=3072 is 1.5 GB versus 12 GB - same corpus, 8x the RAM bill and 8x the dot-product cost at query time. Memory footprint at 1M vectors (float32): d=384 -> 1.5 GB, d=768 -> 3 GB, d=1024 -> 4 GB, d=3072 -> 12 GB. Formula: dim x 4 bytes x 1e6 vectors. Bigger dim helps quality up to a point. Beyond ~1024 the marginal gain shrinks fast - returns diminish while memory and compute keep growing linearly. 1024 is the sweet spot for most production systems.
summary: Memory scales linearly with dimension at 4 bytes per float32; 1M vectors range from 1.5GB (d=384) to 12GB (d=3072); quality plateaus past d=1024.
queries: how much memory does 1M embeddings take

text: Multilingual models underperform on English-only data. A multilingual model spends representation capacity on every language it was trained on. If your corpus is only English, that extra capacity is wasted and the English representation is slightly weaker than a same-size English-only model. Match the model's language coverage to your data. 2x2 matrix: English-only model (e.g., SBERT) + English-only data = best quality, smallest dim, cheapest. English-only model + multilingual data = will fail on non-English queries. Multilingual model (e.g., Cohere v3) + English-only data = quality slightly worse, extra capacity wasted. Multilingual model + multilingual data = right tool for the job. If your corpus is medical, legal, or code, off-the-shelf general models often underperform a domain-tuned model. Domain adaptation and fine-tuning are covered in chapter 12.15.
summary: Match model language coverage to data: multilingual models waste capacity on English-only corpora; specialized domains (medical/legal/code) need domain-tuned embedders.
queries: should I use multilingual embedding for english data
matching embedding model to data language

text: Cost math at 100M token ingest. Ingesting a 100M-token corpus is a one-time charge but it dominates the bill. The same corpus runs from ~$50 (self-hosted GPU for two days) to $13,000 (managed API on the largest OpenAI model). Three options: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large: 100M / 1M x $0.13 = $13,000. Cohere embed-v3: 100M / 1M x $0.10 = $10,000. Self-hosted BGE on g5.xlarge (2 days): $1.006/hour x 48 hours = ~$50. Self-hosting is dramatically cheaper at scale. Managed APIs win on operational simplicity for small corpora where the per-token rate is rounding error compared to engineering time.
summary: Cost worked example at 100M tokens: $13,000 for OpenAI 3-large, $10,000 for Cohere v3, ~$50 for self-hosted BGE on g5.xlarge over 2 days.
queries: how much does it cost to embed 100M tokens
cost of OpenAI text-embedding-3-large at scale
managed embedding API vs self-host cost

text: MTEB is a hint, not an answer. MTEB is a public benchmark with 50+ retrieval and classification tasks. It is the best public signal we have for ranking embedding models - and it is also the trap most teams fall into. The MTEB caveat: MTEB is a public leaderboard. Models can be over-fit to it: trained on data resembling the test sets, tuned for MTEB metrics, or both. The leader on MTEB may underperform on YOUR data. Always run a small eval on your own queries before committing. The four-step decision flow: Step 1 - Need multilingual? Yes: use Cohere v3 / Voyage / OpenAI 3-large. Step 2 - Specialized domain? Yes: domain-tuned BGE or fine-tune (see 12.15). Step 3 - Budget sensitive? Yes: self-host BGE. Step 4 - Default: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large or Cohere embed-v3. Follow the flow top to bottom. Each 'No' sends you to the next question. Land on the default only if none of the above pulled you off. Then run your eval on your own data before locking it in.
summary: MTEB leaderboard can be over-fit so always run your own eval; four-step decision flow: multilingual? domain? budget? else default to OpenAI 3-large or Cohere v3.
queries: how to pick embedding model step by step

text: Triplet-loss diagram: an anchor point A in the center (purple circle), with a green arrow labeled 'Pull Close' pulling a positive sample P (green circle) close, and a red arrow labeled 'Push Away' pushing a negative sample N (red circle) farther away. Visualizes the contrastive update direction in embedding space.
summary: Visual of anchor, positive, and negative points with pull/push arrows illustrating triplet loss.
queries: what does triplet loss look like

text: Before-versus-after bar chart comparing Recall@5, MRR, and Latency for off-the-shelf vs fine-tuned embeddings on the support corpus. Red 'Before' bars beside green 'After' bars per metric. Recall@5 0.78 to 0.91, MRR 0.62 to 0.84, Latency stays at 30 ms.
summary: Bar chart showing recall and MRR gains while latency stays flat after fine-tuning.
queries: off the shelf vs fine-tuned bars

text: Training loss curve showing triplet loss dropping from 0.50 at epoch 0 to 0.22 at epoch 1, 0.10 at epoch 2, and 0.05 at epoch 3 over a fine-tuning run on 50k support-corpus triples. Pink line plot with labeled data points; x-axis epochs, y-axis loss 0.00 to 0.50.
summary: Loss curve illustrating triplet loss convergence over three epochs of fine-tuning.
queries: how fast does triplet loss drop

text: Domain Adaptation - Fine-Tuning Embeddings: general-purpose embedders miss domain-specific semantics (medical abbreviations like MI, tPA). Contrastive fine-tuning with triplet loss L_triplet = max(0, d(A,P) - d(A,N) + margin), margin = 0.2 typical, pulls positives closer and pushes negatives away. Training data = (anchor query, positive chunk, negative chunk) triples; hard negatives mined from top-20 retrieval results teach the boundary. Fine-tune when vocabulary is specialized, recall < 80% on a 200-500 pair golden set, and 5k+ labeled pairs exist. Example: 50k support-corpus triples on BGE-large, ~$200 managed cost, lifts Recall@5 from 0.78 to 0.91 and MRR from 0.62 to 0.84 while latency stays at 30 ms. Re-train every 3-6 months for drift.
summary: Overview of fine-tuning embedding models with triplet contrastive learning for domain-specific RAG retrieval.
queries: what is embedding fine-tuning for RAG
when should I fine-tune an embedding model
how does domain adaptation for embeddings work
how to adapt embeddings to medical legal code domain
fine-tune vs off the shelf embedder

text: Off-the-shelf embeddings sometimes miss domain semantics. General-purpose embedding models are trained on web text - they know cat and dog are similar but have barely seen MI (myocardial infarction) or tPA (tissue plasminogen activator). On a medical corpus this shows up as low cosine similarity between an abbreviation and its full term, which wrecks retrieval. Cosine similarity on a medical corpus: 'MI' vs 'Myocardial infarction' - off-the-shelf 0.42, fine-tuned 0.95. 'tPA contraindicated' vs 'Tissue plasminogen activator should not be given' - off-the-shelf 0.51, fine-tuned 0.90. Recall@10 on a held-out medical query set: off-the-shelf embedder 0.62, fine-tuned on medical corpus 0.88 - a 26-point recall lift. That is the value of teaching the embedder your domain.
summary: Off-the-shelf embedders fail on specialized vocabulary, causing huge recall drops on domain corpora.
queries: why do general embedders fail on medical text
why off the shelf embedder bad for legal docs
how much does fine-tuning improve recall

text: Contrastive fine-tuning: pull positives close, push negatives away. We do not retrain the embedder from scratch - we take the existing model and nudge its vector space using triples: an anchor query, a positive document that should match, and a negative document that should not. Each training step pulls anchor closer to positive and farther from negative. Triplet loss formula: L_triplet = max(0, d(A, P) - d(A, N) + margin), where d = distance, A = anchor, P = positive, N = negative, margin = 0.2 typical. The loss is zero only when the negative is at least 'margin' farther from the anchor than the positive. If the negative is too close, the gradient pushes it away. The margin prevents the loss from going to zero just because positives are slightly closer than negatives.
summary: Triplet loss formula and contrastive learning mechanism that warps embedding space to favor true matches.
queries: what is the margin in triplet loss
why use a margin in triplet loss

text: Pair construction decides quality - the training data is the model. Each row is one triple: anchor (real user query), positive (chunk that should match), negative (chunk that should not). Example triples from a support corpus: anchor 'How do I reset my password?' / positive '[doc-1, chunk 1] - To reset your password, visit the account settings page and click Forgot Password' / negative '[doc-3, chunk 1] - Refunds are processed within 5-7 business days of approval'. Anchor 'I can't sign in' / positive '[doc-25] Login Troubleshooting: clear cookies, verify caps lock, and check for typos in your email' / negative '[doc-7] Dashboard tour: the home screen shows your usage metrics and recent activity'. Anchor 'Cancel my subscription' / positive '[doc-15] Account Deletion Flow: go to billing settings and click Cancel Plan to stop renewal' / negative '[doc-2] Email Change: update your contact email from the profile settings page'. Hard negatives teach the most: a hard negative sits NEAR the positive in vector space but is irrelevant - same vocabulary, wrong topic. Mining hard negatives (top-20 retrieval results minus the true positive) teaches the actual semantic boundary. Easy negatives (random docs from unrelated topics) waste compute because the gap is already obvious to the base model.
summary: How to construct anchor/positive/negative triples and why hard-negative mining matters more than random sampling.
queries: how to build training data for embedding fine-tune
examples of training triples for support corpus
mine hard negatives from top-20 retrieval
how to label query positive pairs

text: When is fine-tuning worth the cost? Fine-tuning takes labeled data, compute time, and a recurring re-training budget as your corpus drifts. Fine-tune when: highly specialized vocabulary (medical, legal, code); off-the-shelf recall below 80% on your golden set; you have 5k+ query-positive pairs labeled; budget for periodic re-training as embeddings drift. Skip and use off-the-shelf when: general English domain; common domain (e-commerce, support, news); fewer than 5k labeled pairs available; recall already above 80% on the golden set. The cleanest signal: build a small golden set (200-500 query-positive pairs) and measure recall with the off-the-shelf model. Below 80%, fine-tuning is usually worth it. Above 80%, the marginal gain rarely pays for the recurring training cost.
summary: Decision rubric for when to fine-tune embeddings vs sticking with off-the-shelf based on domain, data, and golden-set recall.
queries: fine-tune vs off the shelf decision

text: Before vs after on the support corpus: fine-tuning on 50k support-ticket triples on BGE-large. Recall and MRR jump, latency does not - fine-tuning changes vector geometry, not the index or query path. Recall@5: before 0.78, after 0.91. MRR: before 0.62, after 0.84. Latency: before 30 ms, after 30 ms. Training loss over 3 epochs (50k triples): epoch 0 loss 0.50, epoch 1 loss 0.22, epoch 2 loss 0.10, epoch 3 loss 0.05. Cost: ~$200 for a 50k-pair fine-tune on BGE-large via a managed service. Quality delta: usually 10-20 points on recall. Re-evaluate every 3-6 months as the corpus drifts and new query patterns appear.
summary: Concrete before/after metrics, loss curve, and cost for a 50k-triple fine-tune on the support corpus.
queries: before after fine-tune recall MRR latency

text: Three-bar chart of recall at five comparing BM25-only at 0.68 (red), dense-only at 0.74 (yellow), and hybrid RRF at 0.86 (green) on a 50-query held-out set from the support corpus. Baseline y-line under bars, labels above each bar showing the recall value, label below each bar naming the method. Caption: 'Recall@5 On 50 Held-Out Queries'. Hybrid is the tallest bar showing +12 points over dense and +18 over BM25.
summary: Bar chart visualizes Recall@5 across BM25 (0.68), dense (0.74), and hybrid RRF (0.86) on 50 held-out queries.
queries: bar chart recall@5 hybrid vs dense vs BM25

text: Chapter 12.16 Hybrid Retrieval for RAG. Dense embeddings miss exact terms (API key, account IDs); BM25 misses synonyms (Subscription cancel vs Account Deletion). Hybrid runs both retrievers and fuses ranked lists. Two fusion strategies: Reciprocal Rank Fusion RRF(d) = sum_r 1/(k + rank_r(d)) with k=60 (parameter-free), and Weighted Score Fusion Score(d) = alpha*bm25 + (1-alpha)*dense (tunable, needs min-max normalization). Worked RRF example for query 'I can't sign in' ranks doc-25 first (0.03252), doc-7 (0.03200), doc-12 (0.01639), doc-13 (0.01587). On 50-query held-out set Recall@5: BM25 0.68, dense 0.74, hybrid RRF 0.86 (+12 points over dense, +30 ms latency). Per-query-type alpha tuning: factual/lookup alpha=0.7 (lean BM25), conceptual alpha=0.3 (lean dense), mixed alpha=0.5 (balanced). LLM classifier adds ~150 ms + $0.0002 per query for 4-8 points recall. Defaults: start with RRF, move to weighted-alpha only with query-type labels, add classifier for split workloads, re-evaluate alpha quarterly.
summary: Hybrid retrieval for RAG fuses BM25 and dense rankings using RRF or weighted-alpha to lift recall@5 from 0.74 to 0.86.
queries: what is hybrid retrieval for RAG
why combine BM25 and dense search
recall at 5 hybrid vs dense vs BM25
when to use hybrid search RAG

text: Dense misses exact terms; BM25 misses synonyms. Hybrid search (Section 11.25) ships dense vectors and BM25 side by side and fuses rankings. Example 1: Query 'How do I get an API key?' Truth doc-13 API Keys. Dense top-3 [doc-7, doc-12, doc-5] missed doc-13 - embedding of 'API key' sat closer to generic dev-tools docs than the actual API-Keys page. BM25 top-3 [doc-13, doc-7, doc-12] hit it. Example 2: Query 'Subscription cancel' Truth doc-15 Account Deletion. Dense top-3 [doc-15, doc-19, doc-3] hit it. BM25 top-3 [doc-3, doc-19, doc-25] missed - the cancel page is titled 'Account Deletion' and does not contain the token 'cancel'. Hybrid keeps both retrievers and fuses their rankings. Each one catches what the other misses.
summary: Dense misses exact-term matches and BM25 misses synonym matches, so hybrid keeps both retrievers to catch failures of either.
queries: why dense retrieval misses exact terms
example of dense missing a query
example of BM25 missing a query

text: Two fusion strategies dominate production RAG. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): RRF(d) = sum over retrievers r of 1 / (k + rank_r(d)), where k = 60 (standard). Robust default. No weights to tune. Each retriever contributes a rank-reciprocal, so doc-1 always outweighs doc-2 regardless of underlying score scales. Weighted Score Fusion: Score(d) = alpha * bm25_score(d) + (1 - alpha) * dense_score(d), where alpha is in [0, 1]. Tunable per query type. Requires score normalization first - BM25 scores are unbounded positive while dense cosine sits in [-1, 1], so min-max scaling each list to [0, 1] is the standard prep.
summary: Two fusion strategies: parameter-free RRF using rank reciprocals with k=60, or weighted-alpha that combines normalized BM25 and dense scores.
queries: what is k in RRF k=60

text: RRF worked example. Query: 'I can't sign in'. BM25 top-3: doc-25 rank 1, doc-7 rank 2, doc-13 rank 3. Dense top-3: doc-12 rank 1, doc-25 rank 2, doc-7 rank 3. Compute RRF(d) with k = 60. doc-25: BM25 term 1/(60+1) = 0.01639, Dense term 1/(60+2) = 0.01613, sum = 0.03252, final rank 1. doc-7: BM25 term 1/(60+2) = 0.01613, Dense term 1/(60+3) = 0.01587, sum = 0.03200, final rank 2. doc-12: BM25 term 0 (not in BM25 top-3), Dense term 1/(60+1) = 0.01639, sum = 0.01639, final rank 3. doc-13: BM25 term 1/(60+3) = 0.01587, Dense term 0 (not in dense top-3), sum = 0.01587, final rank 4. Final ranking: doc-25 (0.03252) > doc-7 (0.03200) > doc-12 (0.01639) > doc-13 (0.01587). doc-25 wins because both retrievers ranked it in their top-2. doc-7 is second because both kept it in the top-3. Singletons trail behind.
summary: Worked RRF example shows doc-25 winning over doc-7, doc-12, doc-13 because documents appearing in both retrievers' top ranks accumulate higher scores.
queries: I can't sign in query example
how to compute RRF with k=60
doc appearing in both retrievers wins
why does doc-25 rank first RRF
compute hybrid ranking step by step

text: Complementary recall: hybrid catches what either misses. Recall@5 measured on a 50-query held-out set against the support corpus. BM25 only: 0.68. Dense only: 0.74. Hybrid (RRF): 0.86. Hybrid is not just an average of the two retrievers - the failures of BM25 and dense are largely uncorrelated, so fusion picks up the union of their wins. Hybrid is rarely worse than the best single method, often noticeably better. The complementary recall gain here is +12 points over dense alone. Latency cost: +30 ms for the BM25 lookup on top of the dense search. For most RAG systems that is well below the LLM generation tail, so the recall lift is almost always worth it.
summary: On a 50-query held-out set Recall@5 jumps from 0.74 dense to 0.86 hybrid because BM25 and dense failures are uncorrelated, at only +30 ms latency cost.
queries: recall at 5 hybrid vs dense vs BM25
fusion picks up union of wins

text: Tune alpha by query type. One alpha for everything leaves recall on the table. Factual/Lookup queries (example 'What is my account ID?') use alpha = 0.7, lean BM25 - exact tokens like account IDs, error codes, and SKUs dominate the answer. Conceptual/How-Does-X-Work queries (example 'How does our pricing model work?') use alpha = 0.3, lean dense - synonyms and paraphrase dominate, the answer rarely uses the same words as the query. Mixed/Ambiguous queries (default, example 'Why is my charge wrong?') use alpha = 0.5, balanced - mix of keywords (charge, refund) and intent (something is wrong), hedge with 0.5. A simple LLM-based classifier (one call with a 3-class prompt) can label each query at runtime before retrieval. Cost: 1 extra LLM call (~$0.0002 with a small model). Adds ~150 ms but unlocks 4-8 points of recall on mixed query workloads.
summary: Per-query-type alpha tuning uses 0.7 for factual lookups, 0.3 for conceptual questions, 0.5 for mixed, with a small LLM classifier routing queries for 4-8 recall points.
queries: why is my charge wrong balanced alpha
tuning hybrid for different query types

text: Hybrid defaults for RAG. Hybrid is the production default for RAG. Start simple with RRF and only add complexity if evaluation metrics demand it. Rules: 1) Always start with RRF (no tuning needed). 2) Move to weighted-alpha only if you have query-type labels. 3) Add a classifier when your queries naturally split into factual vs conceptual. 4) Re-evaluate alpha quarterly as your corpus and query mix drift. The cheapest big win in RAG retrieval. If you only do one thing after picking an embedding model, ship hybrid with RRF first - it almost always beats either retriever alone with a tiny latency cost.
summary: Default playbook: start with RRF, upgrade to weighted-alpha only with labels, add classifier for split workloads, re-tune quarterly as corpus drifts.

text: Chapter 12.17 covers the reranker cascade in RAG: a 3-stage pipeline (vector retrieval -> cross-encoder reranker -> LLM) where each stage cuts candidates roughly 5x and spends more compute per remaining item. Concrete numbers: 1M -> 50 -> 10 -> 3 chunks; latency 30 + 80 + 800 = 910 ms p50; cost $0.0001 + $0.0003 + $0.0120 = $0.0124 per query. LLM generation dominates both latency and cost. Two tuning knobs (top-K retrieved, top-K reranked) trade recall against latency/cost, with 50 -> 10 -> 3 as the default sweet spot. Caching the LLM call gives the biggest latency and cost wins.
summary: Chapter-level summary of the 3-stage cascade, its latency and cost budget, and the recall/cost tradeoffs.

text: A reranker cascade is a 3-stage RAG retrieval pipeline where each stage cuts the candidate set and pays more compute per remaining item. Stage 1: vector retrieval narrows 1M chunks to top-50 (cheap and lossy). Stage 2: a cross-encoder reranker re-scores the 50 candidates by reading the query and document together, narrowing to top-10 (medium cost, sharp scores). Stage 3: the LLM reads the top-3 chunks and generates the final answer (expensive). The vector retriever is cheap and lossy, the reranker is expensive and precise, the LLM is most expensive and uses the smallest set. Each stage filters the work for the next one. Rerankers were introduced in Section 11.26; here that idea is applied as a 3-stage RAG cascade.
summary: Introduces the 3-stage RAG cascade where each stage shrinks candidates and spends more compute per remaining item.

text: Funnel diagram showing the 3 cascade stages with 5x shrinkage at each level. Stage 1 Vector Retrieval: input 1M chunks, output top-50, cost ~30 ms / $0.0001, uses HNSW or IVF-PQ scan, approximate but blazing fast. Stage 2 Cross-Encoder Reranker: input 50 candidates, output top-10, cost ~80 ms / $0.0003, reads query plus each candidate jointly, 50 forward passes through a small transformer. Stage 3 LLM reads top-3 and generates: input 10 reranked, output answer, cost ~800 ms / $0.0120, top-3 chunks stuffed into the prompt and the LLM does the reasoning and writes the answer. The candidate pool shrinks by 5x at each stage, and each shrink lets us spend more compute per remaining item.
summary: Per-stage funnel with sizes, costs, and roles for vector retrieval, cross-encoder rerank, and LLM generation.

text: Two tuning knobs control the cascade: top-K retrieved (vector stage) and top-K reranked (reranker stage). Three operating points on the running corpus: Config 20 -> 5 gives recall 0.82, latency 850 ms, cost $0.011 (narrow, saves a touch of latency and cost but recall slips). Config 50 -> 10 (default) gives recall 0.89, latency 910 ms, cost $0.012 (default sweet spot, best recall per millisecond). Config 100 -> 20 gives recall 0.92, latency 990 ms, cost $0.013 (wider, buys +3 points of recall for +80 ms, diminishing returns). Sweet spot is usually 50 -> 10 -> 3. Going wider buys diminishing returns; going narrower trades recall for latency.

text: Chapter 12.18 motivates query rewriting in RAG. User-typed questions are rarely the optimal retrieval query: the right doc exists in the corpus but the retriever misses it because the user's wording and the doc's wording diverge. Three failure shapes ruin naive retrieval - (1) lexical mismatch (sign-in vs log-in, cancel vs delete), (2) ambiguity (one short query has multiple plausible intents), and (3) multi-intent (one sentence packs two distinct asks). Four query-transformation strategies map onto these failures: HyDE (12.19) fixes lexical mismatch by embedding a hypothetical answer, multi-query expansion (12.20) fixes ambiguity via N paraphrases plus RRF, query decomposition (12.21) splits multi-intent queries, and query routing (12.21) classifies queries to the right index.
summary: Chapter overview: why user queries fail at retrieval and the four query-transformation strategies that fix the three failure shapes.

text: User queries are rarely optimal for retrieval. The user types a sentence in their own words; the retriever embeds it and finds the nearest chunks. But the user's wording is rarely the wording that lives in the index, so the right doc is in the corpus and the retriever still misses it. Concrete example: User query = "I can't sign in". Best doc in corpus = "Login Troubleshooting". Cosine(user, best_doc) = 0.61. Top-3 misses it; the correct doc lands at rank 7. Three failure shapes ruin naive retrieval and four query-transformation strategies fix them.

text: Failure 1: Lexical mismatch. Users and docs share an idea but not vocabulary. The retriever sees two strings of tokens that look unrelated and the right chunk falls out of the top-k. Examples on the support corpus: (a) User "I can't sign in" vs doc "Login Troubleshooting" - mismatch: sign in vs log in. (b) User "How do I cancel?" vs doc "Account Deletion Flow" - mismatch: cancel vs delete. (c) User "My screen is frozen" vs doc "Browser Performance Issues" - mismatch: screen frozen vs browser performance. Dense embeddings help a bit (sign-in and log-in sit close in vector space) but only partially. HyDE (12.19) and multi-query (12.20) close the gap by rewriting the user query into wording that matches the doc.
summary: Lexical mismatch: user words and doc titles use different vocabulary (sign in vs log in, cancel vs delete, screen frozen vs browser performance) and dense embeddings only partially bridge it.

text: Failure 2: Ambiguity. One short question has multiple plausible intents. A naive retriever picks one direction and quietly drops the other two. Worked diagram: the query "How do I export?" fans out into three branches: (1) Export data to CSV -> doc-9 (Export Formats), (2) Export user list for admin -> doc-26 (Bulk Operations), (3) Cancel an export job that failed -> doc-29 (Export Failures). A naive retriever picks one branch and misses the other two. Multi-query (12.20) generates paraphrases that cover each branch, and routing (12.21) sends each branch to the index that fits best.
summary: Ambiguity: the query 'How do I export?' has three plausible interpretations mapping to three different docs; naive retrieval picks one and silently misses the others.

text: Failure 3: Multi-intent. The user packs two asks into one sentence. The retriever embeds the whole thing and lands on docs that cover one ask, missing the other entirely. Worked example: User query = "Cancel my subscription and get a refund". Sub-intent 1: How to cancel subscription -> doc-15 (Account Deletion Flow). Sub-intent 2: How to get a refund -> doc-4 (Refunds). Naive retrieval finds docs for one intent and misses the other. Decomposition (12.21) splits the query into two sub-queries, retrieves for each, then combines the results.
summary: Multi-intent: a single sentence like 'Cancel my subscription and get a refund' carries two distinct asks; naive retrieval covers one and misses the other, motivating query decomposition.

text: Four strategies map to three failures. Each transformation tackles a different failure shape. (1) HyDE (12.19) fixes lexical mismatch: ask an LLM to draft a hypothetical answer, then embed the answer instead of the question. (2) Multi-Query Expansion (12.20) fixes ambiguity: generate N paraphrases of the query, retrieve for each, then merge results. (3) Query Decomposition (12.21) fixes multi-intent: split a compound query into independent sub-queries, retrieve for each, then combine. (4) Query Routing (12.21) picks the right index: classify the query and send it to the index, tool, or filter that best matches. Picking the right transformation is itself a routing decision - in production, a small classifier or LLM call inspects the query and chooses which strategy (or combination) to run.
summary: Preview grid: HyDE fixes lexical mismatch, multi-query expansion fixes ambiguity, decomposition fixes multi-intent, routing picks the right index; choice itself is made by a classifier or LLM call.

text: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) is a query-transformation technique that improves dense retrieval by asking an LLM to draft a plausible hypothetical answer to the user's question, then embedding that answer instead of the question, then retrieving against the index. The hypothetical answer shares vocabulary and shape with real docs, so cosine similarity scores higher. The 5-stage flow: user query, LLM generates hypothetical answer, embed the answer, vector retrieval, return top-K real docs. Helps with long descriptive queries, lexical mismatch, and conceptual 'why' questions. Hurts on short factual queries and adds 200-400ms latency plus one LLM call (~$0.0005). Includes a prompt template with a {query} placeholder.
summary: Chapter overview of HyDE: embed an LLM-drafted hypothetical answer instead of the user question to improve dense retrieval recall.
queries: Why embed the answer instead of the question?
How much does HyDE cost per query?
How to fix lexical mismatch in RAG retrieval?

text: Don't embed the question - embed the hypothetical answer. A user's question and the doc that answers it often live far apart in vector space because they use different words and have different shapes. HyDE flips the order: have an LLM draft a plausible answer first, then embed THAT, then retrieve. Normal RAG: Embed("Why is my dashboard slow?") -> retrieve. HyDE: LLM("Why is my dashboard slow?") = "The dashboard may be slow due to large data volumes, browser extensions, or network latency." -> Embed that ANSWER -> retrieve. The hypothetical answer is text-shape-closer to actual docs than the question is, so cosine similarity wins.
summary: Core HyDE insight: questions and answers sit far apart in vector space, so embed an LLM-drafted answer instead of the raw question.
queries: Question vs answer shape in vector space
Why are questions and docs far apart in vector space?

text: The HyDE flow: Query -> LLM -> Hypothetical Answer -> Embed -> Retrieve. HyDE adds one extra step before retrieval: an LLM call that writes a hypothetical answer. That answer is what gets embedded and sent to the vector index. The user never sees it directly. The five stages are User Query, LLM Generates Hypothetical Answer, Embed Hypothetical Answer, Vector Retrieval, Top-K Real Docs. The hypothetical answer is throwaway - only its embedding goes to the index. The user gets the real top-K docs back, never the LLM's draft.
summary: HyDE pipeline walkthrough: an extra LLM step drafts a throwaway answer whose embedding (not the raw query) feeds the vector retriever.
queries: What are the steps in HyDE?

text: Worked example: "Why is my dashboard slow?". Three panels show what the user typed, what the LLM drafted, and what the retriever returned. LLM hypothetical answer: "The dashboard may be slow due to large data volumes, browser extensions interfering with rendering, network latency, or background sync jobs consuming resources. Try refreshing, disabling extensions, or checking the network panel." Top-3 retrieved docs: doc-22 Slow Page Load Troubleshooting, doc-23 500 Errors, doc-27 Browser Compatibility Issues. The LLM's draft pulled in vocabulary like "page load", "browser extensions", and "network latency" - exactly the words in the docs that actually help. Without HyDE, retrieval returned doc-7 (Dashboard Tour), a generic doc that doesn't help. The question "why slow" embedded poorly; the answer embedded well.
summary: Concrete dashboard-slow query example showing the LLM's hypothetical answer pulling in doc vocabulary to retrieve doc-22, doc-23, doc-27.
queries: How HyDE retrieves the right docs
Why does HyDE find better docs?

text: Why HyDE works - three reasons the hypothetical-answer trick beats embedding the raw question. (1) Different shapes in vector space: questions look different from answers in vector space. (2) Borrows doc vocabulary: hypothetical answers borrow doc vocabulary even if the LLM doesn't know the actual answer. (3) Robust to lexical mismatch: robust to lexical mismatch because the LLM does the rewriting for you. Even if the LLM's draft is factually wrong, the SHAPE of the answer matches the SHAPE of real docs - and that is what cosine similarity scores.
summary: Three reasons HyDE outperforms raw-query embedding: matching shapes, borrowed doc vocabulary, and robustness to lexical mismatch.
queries: Does HyDE need a correct answer?
Why factually wrong LLM draft still helps
Why questions and answers differ in embeddings

text: When HyDE helps vs when it hurts. HyDE is not free and is not universal. Helps: long descriptive queries; lexical mismatch (sign-in vs log-in); conceptual / 'why does X happen' questions; queries that don't share vocabulary with docs. Hurts: short factual queries with good embeddings (e.g., 'API key'); queries where the LLM might hallucinate the wrong answer shape; latency-sensitive pipelines (HyDE adds an LLM call: +200-400 ms). If recall on your golden set is already above 0.85, HyDE likely won't help and will add latency.
summary: Decision guide for HyDE: helps on long, conceptual, lexical-mismatch queries; hurts on short factual lookups and high-recall systems.
queries: Should I use HyDE for short queries

text: The HyDE prompt template. The exact prompt fired at the LLM: "You are a helpful assistant. Write a single concise paragraph that answers the user's question as if you knew the answer. The paragraph will be used to search a documentation index, so include domain terms and likely doc vocabulary. Do not say 'I don't know' - guess plausibly. Question: {query} Hypothetical Answer:" The {query} placeholder gets filled with the user's question. The LLM's response is the hypothetical answer that gets embedded. Cost: +1 LLM call per user query (~$0.0005 + ~250ms). Cache the hypothetical answer by query hash to amortize. Caching is covered in chapter 12.36.
summary: Concrete HyDE prompt template with {query} placeholder, plus cost and latency overhead (~$0.0005 / ~250ms) and caching guidance.

text: Three-box horizontal pipeline diagram for multi-query expansion. Box 1 (orange): LLM Generates 3-5 Query Variants. Box 2 (yellow): Run Vector Retrieval For Each Variant. Box 3 (green): Fuse Rankings With RRF. Arrows connect each box left to right, showing the sequential pipeline flow.

text: Multi-Query Expansion (also called RAG-Fusion) turns one user query into 3-5 LLM-generated variant phrasings, runs vector retrieval for each variant in parallel, and fuses the rankings with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) to produce one final top-K. RRF formula: RRF(d) = sum over variants v of 1 / (k + rank_v(d)), with k = 60 standard. Helps on ambiguous, multi-intent, or multi-clause queries. Skip on short factual lookups. Adds ~300 ms (one LLM call) plus N parallel retrievals. Step-back prompting is a niche variant that adds a more general rewording. Pairs well with HyDE.
summary: Chapter overview: expand one query into N LLM-rewritten variants, retrieve per variant, fuse with RRF.

text: One query becomes many; fuse the results. If the user's exact wording missed the right doc, one of the variant wordings might catch it. RAG-Fusion is the same idea under a different name; this chapter absorbs it.

text: Three steps: generate variants -> retrieve each -> fuse. Stage 1: LLM generates 3-5 query variants. Stage 2: run vector retrieval for each variant. Stage 3: fuse rankings with RRF. Stage 2 runs in parallel - N retrievals fire at once, not in sequence. Latency is dominated by stage 1 (the LLM call), not stage 2. A three-box horizontal pipeline diagram shows the LLM generating 3-5 query variants, vector retrieval running for each variant, and rankings fused with RRF into one final list.

text: Worked example: 'Cancel my subscription and get a refund'. One query, two intents. LLM-generated variants: Variant 1: 'How to cancel my subscription'. Variant 2: 'How to get a refund after cancellation'. Variant 3: 'Stop billing immediately'. Top-3 retrieved docs per variant: Variant 1: [doc-15, doc-7, doc-3]. Variant 2: [doc-4, doc-15, doc-3]. Variant 3: [doc-5, doc-15, doc-21]. Final fused top-3 after RRF: Rank 1: doc-15 (covers cancellation). Rank 2: doc-4 (covers refund). Rank 3: doc-3 (subscription tiers). Each variant brings a different doc to the top; fusion captures both intents - doc-15 for cancellation, doc-4 for refund.

text: Fuse with RRF (same formula as chapter 12.16). Reciprocal Rank Fusion takes N rankings and produces one. Each doc's score is the sum of 1 / (k + its rank) across every ranking it appears in. The constant k = 60 dampens the influence of any single list so agreement across variants matters more than a top-1 appearance. Formula: RRF(d) = sum over variants v of: 1 / (k + rank_v(d)) where k = 60 (standard). Same fusion math as hybrid search (chapter 12.16); different inputs - here the rankings come from N variants of the same query rather than from BM25 + dense.
summary: RRF formula: sum of 1/(k+rank) across variants, k=60, rewards agreement across rankings.

text: Step-back prompting: add a more general variant. Step-back is a specific variant style: ask a broader, less specific version of the question. The intuition: a doc might not match the user's exact narrow wording, but it almost certainly matches a more general phrasing of the same problem. Example - Original (very specific): 'Why is the user role page failing on Chrome v124 with error E_PERM?' Step-back (more general): 'Why does the user role page fail?' The original is too specific - the doc might not mention Chrome v124. The step-back catches more general docs. Reasoning models reduce the need for explicit step-back; include it when your queries are unusually specific. This is one of many possible variant prompts; the chapter's main approach covers the common case; step-back is a niche addition.
summary: Step-back prompting generates a more general variant of a too-specific query to catch broader docs.

text: When multi-query helps. Multi-query expansion is not a default - it earns its keep on hard, ambiguous, or multi-intent queries; on short factual lookups it just burns budget. Helps: ambiguous queries with multiple plausible intents; complex multi-clause queries; recall is the priority over latency; reranker can absorb the wider candidate set. Skip: short factual lookups ('What is my account ID?'); latency-critical paths (adds 1 LLM call + N parallel retrievals); when HyDE already solves the same failure. Adds ~300 ms (1 LLM call) + N parallel retrievals (cheap). Often pairs well with HyDE for hard cases.
summary: Helps vs skip table: use multi-query on ambiguous/multi-intent queries, skip on short factual lookups.

text: Chapter 12.21 covers two related query-transformation strategies in RAG: routing (which index or tool should this query hit) and decomposition (does this query need to be split into sub-queries). A router classifies each query and sends it to the matching index - or skips retrieval entirely for chitchat. Decomposition splits complex queries like comparisons, multi-hop questions, and disjunctions into 2-3 sub-queries, retrieves for each, then assembles. Routers can be implemented as fast embedding-based semantic routers (~10 ms, 85-90% accuracy) or as slower LLM classifiers (~150 ms, 92-97% accuracy). The four production combinations are route only, decompose only, both, or neither - pick by query complexity. Combined overhead: ~300-450 ms per query.
summary: Query routing picks the right index/tool while decomposition splits complex queries into sub-queries; choose route, decompose, both, or neither based on query complexity.

text: Routing decides where; decomposition decides how many. Two related query-transformation strategies share this chapter. Routing answers a where question - which index or tool should the query hit. Decomposition answers a how-many question - does the query need to be split into smaller sub-queries first. Routing: pick one target. Skip retrieval entirely for off-topic chitchat. Decomposition: split complex queries into 2-3 sub-queries; retrieve and assemble. Both rely on an LLM (or a cheaper classifier) to inspect the query and decide. They compose - a router can send a query to a decomposition step, or a decomposed sub-query can itself be routed to a different index.
summary: Routing chooses which index a query hits while decomposition decides whether to split it into sub-queries; the two strategies compose.
queries: what is the where vs how-many framing

when do you split a query in RAG

text: Routing sends each query to the right target. On the running support corpus, queries split cleanly into four buckets. A router classifies each query into one of them, then sends it to the matching index - or skips retrieval entirely for chitchat. The four leaves: Account & Billing -> Account-Billing Index, Top-K=5; Product Features -> Product-Features Index, Top-K=5; Troubleshooting -> Troubleshooting Index, Top-K=8; Chitchat / Off-Topic -> Skip Retrieval, Reply Directly. Routing avoids retrieving from indexes that can't help. Skipping retrieval on chitchat saves latency and cost - and prevents the LLM from hallucinating about a doc it doesn't need to read.
summary: A router classifies each support query into one of four buckets (billing, features, troubleshooting, chitchat) and sends it to the matching index or skips retrieval.
queries: what indexes does a router target
route a query to billing index

text: Two router implementations: embedding-based vs LLM classifier. The routing decision can be made by a tiny embedding lookup or by a small LLM call. Semantic Router (embedding-based): embed the query, compare to embeddings of route prototypes ('Billing question', 'Troubleshooting question'). Pick highest cosine. Cost: ~$0.0001/query. Latency: ~10 ms. Accuracy: 85-90%. LLM Classifier: pass the query + route labels to a small LLM ('Classify this query: billing / feature / troubleshoot / chitchat'). Pick the label. Cost: ~$0.0002/query. Latency: ~150 ms. Accuracy: 92-97%. Semantic router scales cheaper; LLM classifier handles edge cases better. Start with semantic; switch to LLM if routing accuracy stalls below your bar.
summary: Routers can be implemented as fast embedding-based semantic routers (~10 ms, 85-90% accuracy) or slower LLM classifiers (~150 ms, 92-97% accuracy).
queries: how does a semantic router work

trade off between routing cost and accuracy

text: Decomposition splits 'Compare X and Y' into two sub-queries. User query: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' One query, two subjects. A single vector lookup gets pulled toward whichever subject has stronger phrasing and misses the other. LLM splits into sub-queries: Sub-Query 1: 'What's in the Pro plan?' retrieves doc-3 (Subscription Tiers), doc-10 (Team Seat Management). Sub-Query 2: 'What's in the Enterprise plan?' retrieves doc-3 (Subscription Tiers), doc-10 (Team Seat Management), doc-21 (Quota Exceeded - Enterprise-Only Feature). LLM Assembly Step: union of retrieved docs (doc-3, doc-10, doc-21) is passed to a single LLM call that writes the side-by-side comparison. Without decomposition, retrieval finds one tier's docs and misses the other.
summary: Worked example: 'Compare Pro and Enterprise plans' splits into two sub-queries; each retrieves its own docs, and the union (doc-3, doc-10, doc-21) feeds one LLM assembly call.
queries: how to compare two products with RAG

why single retrieval misses one subject
Pro vs Enterprise plan RAG example
compare X and Y query split

text: When to decompose: multi-hop, comparison, disjunction. Three query shapes consistently benefit from being split before retrieval. Comparison: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' Split: Sub-1: What's in the Pro plan? + Sub-2: What's in the Enterprise plan? Multi-Hop: 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' Split: Sub-1: How to recover email? + Sub-2: How to reset password? Disjunction (Mixed Types): 'Why is the dashboard slow and showing 500 errors?' Split: Sub-1: Dashboard slow troubleshooting + Sub-2: 500 error troubleshooting. If the query has 'and' / 'compare' / 'or' between two distinct subjects, decomposition usually helps. Single-subject queries don't benefit and just pay the extra LLM call for nothing.
summary: Decomposition helps for comparison, multi-hop, and disjunction query shapes; single-subject queries don't benefit and just pay the extra LLM cost.
queries: three shapes that benefit from decomposition
how to spot a decomposable query

text: The decision: route, decompose, both, or neither. Four combinations cover every production case. Route Only: most production RAG systems start here. Cheap, easy, high-impact. Decompose Only: use when queries are consistently complex (multi-hop, comparisons). Adds 1 LLM call + N parallel retrievals. Both: route first to pick the right index, then decompose if the query is complex. They compose - they don't interfere. Neither: default for simple lookup queries. Don't add complexity you don't need. Cost summary: Routing +10-150 ms / +$0.0001-0.0002 per query. Decomposition +200-300 ms / +$0.0005 per query. Combined: ~+300-450 ms total query-transformation overhead.
summary: Four combinations - route only, decompose only, both, or neither - cover every production case; combined overhead is roughly 300-450 ms and ~$0.0007 per query.
queries: cost of routing and decomposition combined

text: Chapter 12.22 Context Packing covers how to fit retrieval into an LLM context window. Every prompt has a token budget split across system prompt, retrieved context, user question, reserved completion, and headroom (8000-token example: 200 + 5000 + 50 + 1500 + 1250). Three ordering strategies: relevance-first (sort by score), chronological (sort by date/position), and MMR (deduplicate with MMR = lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked))). The final packed prompt combines system instructions, [doc-N, chunk-M] citation markers, the user question, and an authorized refusal phrase, all within the budget (~60% of 8k).
summary: Chapter 12.22 teaches token budget allocation, three chunk-ordering strategies (relevance-first, chronological, MMR), and how to assemble a complete packed prompt within budget.

text: Every prompt has a token budget. An 8000-token context window splits across four jobs: the system prompt that frames the model (200 tokens, 2.5%), the retrieved context with the answer (5000 tokens, 62.5%), the user question (50 tokens, 0.6%), and the reserved completion tokens the model will use to write its answer (1500 tokens, 18.8%). Headroom (1250 tokens, 15.6%) is the slack you keep so a long answer never overflows. Slot / Tokens / Share Of Budget: System Prompt 200 (2.5%); Retrieved Context 5,000 (62.5%); User Question 50 (0.6%); Reserved Completion 1,500 (18.8%); Headroom 1,250 (15.6%). Total = 8,000.

text: Ordering Option 1: Relevance-First. Sort by retrieval score, highest first. The most-relevant chunk lands at position 1 where the model attends best. Default for single-best-answer queries. Packed order: 1. [doc-1, chunk 1] (Score 0.91); 2. [doc-1, chunk 2] (Score 0.84); 3. [doc-1, chunk 3] (Score 0.77). Most relevant first. Cheap to do (just sort by score). Risk: tail-position chunks lose attention weight.
summary: Relevance-first ordering sorts chunks by descending retrieval score so the highest-scored chunk sits in position 1 where model attention is strongest.
queries: How should I order chunks in a RAG prompt?

Default chunk ordering strategy for RAG
Sort chunks by retrieval score descending

text: Ordering Option 2: Chronological. Sort by publication date or document position. Useful for time-sensitive docs - changelogs, policy histories, incident timelines - where the natural reading order helps the model follow cause-effect. Packed order: 1. [doc-1, chunk 1] - Document Position 1; 2. [doc-1, chunk 2] - Document Position 2; 3. [doc-1, chunk 3] - Document Position 3. Time-sensitive ordering preserves the natural sequence so the model reads cause then effect.
summary: Chronological ordering sorts chunks by date or document position so the model reads cause then effect, useful for changelogs and incident timelines.
queries: When should chunks be sorted by date?
How to pack changelogs into a prompt?
How do I preserve document position when packing?
Sort chunks by date in RAG prompt

text: Ordering Option 3: Deduplicate with MMR (Maximal Marginal Relevance). Keeps high-score chunks while dropping near-duplicates. Same budget, more unique information. Before MMR (5 chunks, redundant): chunk 1 'reset password go to Settings then Security'; chunk 2 'email with reset link will be sent'; chunk 3 'click link within 24 hours'; chunk 4 'reset email is sent with a clickable link' (duplicate of 2, wastes budget); chunk 5 'set a strong new password to finish'. After MMR (4 chunks, diverse): chunks 1, 2, 3 plus doc-9 chunk 1 'MFA must be re-verified after a reset (different but related)'. The MMR formula: MMR = lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked)). Lambda = 0.7 weights relevance over diversity. Lambda = 0.3 weights diversity over relevance. Tune per workload.
summary: MMR deduplicates retrieved chunks using MMR = lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked)), trading relevance against diversity.

text:
The final packed prompt - all pieces in one prompt: system instruction, retrieved chunks with citation markers, user question, refusal phrase the model is allowed to use. Prompt template:

You are a helpful support assistant. Use the documentation below to answer.
If the docs don't contain the answer, say "I don't have enough information".

Documentation:
[doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, go to Settings then Security.
[doc-1, chunk 2] An email with a reset link will be sent to your address.
[doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link within 24 hours to set a new password.

Question: How do I reset my password?
Answer:

Within budget: ~4,800 / 8,000 tokens (60%).

text: Chapter 12.23 explains the lost-in-the-middle problem from Liu et al. 2023: LLMs attend best to the start and end of a long context window and dip in accuracy when the answer-bearing chunk sits in the middle. The chapter shows a U-shaped accuracy curve over 20 positions, demonstrates how the same retrieved chunk produces different answers depending on its position, and presents two reordering strategies (front-load and sandwich) that move top-scored chunks into the high-attention regions. It closes with failure modes where reordering alone is not enough: truly long multi-fact queries, tail-buried critical details, and model-specific curve shapes.
summary: Overview of the lost-in-the-middle effect and the front-load and sandwich reordering strategies that mitigate it.
