text: Strategy 1: Front-load the most relevant chunk. Relevance-first ordering: sort by retrieval score descending. The top-scored chunks land in positions 1, 2, 3 - the high-attention region. Tail positions hold the lowest-scored chunks. Scores by position: pos 1 = 0.91 (Top-1), pos 2 = 0.88 (Top-2), pos 3 = 0.84 (Top-3), pos 4 = 0.78, pos 5 = 0.71, pos 6 = 0.66, pos 7 = 0.59, pos 8 = 0.52, pos 9 = 0.45, pos 10 = 0.38. Cheap. Helps single-best-answer queries. Risk: tail context still ignored, but the top-scored chunks land in the attention sweet spot.
summary: Front-load strategy sorts chunks by descending retrieval score so top-3 land in positions 1 to 3 of the prompt.
queries: how to place best chunk at position 1
is front-load enough for multi-fact queries
how front-load helps lost in the middle

text: Strategy 2: Sandwich the best chunks at start and end. Put the top-2 chunks at the front and the next-best two at the back. The middle dip on the U-curve becomes the least-relevant region. Same total tokens; reordering is negligible compute. Slot layout: pos 1 = 0.91 (Top-1), pos 2 = 0.88 (Top-2), pos 3 = 0.66, pos 4 = 0.59, pos 5 = 0.52, pos 6 = 0.45, pos 7 = 0.38, pos 8 = 0.31, pos 9 = 0.84 (Top-3), pos 10 = 0.78 (Top-4). Best chunks bookend start and end. More robust for multi-fact queries. Same total tokens. Costs reordering compute (negligible).
summary: Sandwich strategy places top-2 chunks at the front and next-best 2 chunks at the back so low-relevance chunks fall in the middle dip.
queries: bookend chunks at start and end
top-2 chunks at front next 2 at back
is sandwich more robust than front-load
how to exploit U-shaped attention curve
what fills the middle in sandwich strategy

text: When reordering isn't enough. Three failure modes need different fixes than position tricks. (1) Truly long multi-fact queries: 30 chunks all needed; even sandwich leaves middle facts ignored. Fix: hierarchical summarization or multi-hop retrieval (chapter 12.25). (2) Tail-buried critical detail: the killer fact lives at chunk 18; both strategies miss it. Fix: rerank harder, fetch fewer. (3) Model-specific curve shape: some models have flatter curves than others; benchmark on your own model. Lost-in-middle is a model fingerprint, not a universal law. Always benchmark on your own model and workload.
summary: Three failure modes where front-load and sandwich are insufficient, and the alternative fixes for each.
queries: model-specific lost in the middle curve
is lost in the middle universal
benchmark attention curve on your own model
when reordering chunks is not enough
lost in middle as model fingerprint
what to do when position tricks fail

text: Chapter 12.24 covers Citations, Refusal & Groundedness for RAG. Without citations a user cannot verify answers and reviewers cannot audit them, so hallucinations slip through. Inline [doc-N] markers make every claim traceable. The chapter shows: a citation prompt template (literal [doc-N] markers tied to the Documentation block), a structured JSON output schema pairing answer with citations and confidence, an explicit refusal instruction with the exact sentence 'I don't have enough information to answer that.', a 3-step faithfulness audit (parse claims, locate cited chunk, score via LLM judge), a regex parser (\[doc-\d+\]) that resolves markers to a citation table with doc, chunk, quoted span, and a production template combining numbered RULES for citations + refusal because structured constraints survive long context better than prose.
summary: Chapter 12.24 teaches inline [doc-N] citations, refusal instructions, faithfulness auditing, and a production prompt template that combines both for grounded RAG answers.
queries: how to make RAG cite sources
how to prevent hallucination in RAG
production RAG prompt with citations and refusal
I don't have enough information sentence

text: Why citations are a production requirement. Without citations the user cannot verify the answer and a reviewer cannot audit it; hallucinations slip through silently. With inline [doc-N] markers every claim is traceable. Same answer with citations becomes an artifact you can review, replay, and trust. Comparison on query 'Why is my account locked?'. No-citations answer: 'Your account may be locked due to too many failed logins or a billing issue.' - user cannot verify, reviewer cannot audit, hallucination has nowhere to land. With-citations answer: 'Your account locks after 5 failed logins [doc-12]. Billing issues trigger a 24-hour hold [doc-7].' - every claim traces to a specific chunk, audit-friendly, hallucinations exposed.
summary: Citations turn an opaque LLM answer into an auditable artifact where every claim ties back to a retrieved chunk.
queries: why are citations important in RAG

text:
Tell the model to cite inline. The citation instruction lives in the system prompt. Use a literal [doc-N] marker the model can emit inline. Specify that N must come from the Documentation block, not be invented. Prompt template:

You are a helpful support assistant. Use the documentation below
to answer the user's question.

For every claim you make, cite the source as [doc-N], where N is
the doc number from the Documentation section. Do NOT cite a doc
you did not use.

Documentation:
{context}

Question: {query}
Answer:
summary: A citation-instruction prompt template tells the model to emit literal [doc-N] markers tied to doc numbers from the Documentation block.
queries: how to instruct LLM to cite

summary: Structured JSON output pairs the answer with doc_id, chunk_id, quote, and a confidence score so audit pipelines can parse citations without regex.

text:
Tell the model to refuse when context is missing. Without an explicit refusal instruction, models tend to invent. With one, the model has an authorized exit for 'I don't know'. Exact wording matters - models follow the most precise instructions. Refusal template:

If the documentation does not contain enough information to answer,
respond with EXACTLY this sentence and nothing else:
"I don't have enough information to answer that."

Do not invent facts. Do not guess. Do not cite a doc you didn't use.

Example on query 'What's the refund window for monthly plans?' with no matching doc. Without refusal instruction: 'Monthly plans have a 7-day refund window.' - hallucinated, model guessed because it had no exit. With refusal instruction: 'I don't have enough information to answer that.' - safe, user knows the system was honest about its limit.
summary: An explicit refusal instruction gives the model an authorized exit phrase so it stops inventing answers when retrieved docs don't cover the question.
queries: I don't have enough information sentence

text: Faithfulness: every claim must trace to a chunk. Faithfulness is the share of claims in an answer that are supported by the retrieved chunks. A 3-step audit converts citations into a measurable score. Chapters 12.31-12.35 cover RAGAS faithfulness in depth. Step 1: Parse answer into atomic claims (example: '5 failed logins triggers a lock' / 'Billing issues hold for 24 hours'). Step 2: Locate the cited chunk for each claim by following the [doc-N] marker back to retrieved chunk text. Step 3: Score claim_supported = LLMJudge(claim, chunk); aggregate per-claim scores into a faithfulness number from 0 to 1. Output is a single number from 0 (none supported) to 1 (every claim has evidence in the retrieved chunks).
summary: Faithfulness is a 0-1 score measuring how many atomic claims in an answer are actually supported by the retrieved chunks, computed via parse-locate-LLMJudge.

text:
Parse citations back out of the answer. After generation, parse [doc-N] markers with regex \[doc-\d+\]. Map each marker to retrieved chunk and render as footnotes or hover tooltips. Model output highlighted: 'Your account locks after 5 failed logins [doc-12]. Billing issues trigger a 24-hour hold [doc-7].' Resolved citation table:

[doc-12] | doc-12 (Account Lockouts) | chunk 3 | "5 failed login attempts"
[doc-7]  | doc-7 (Billing Holds)     | chunk 1 | "24-hour hold after a failed charge"

Render as footnotes or inline tooltips. Now the user can click a citation and see exactly what the model used to write that sentence.
summary: A regex \[doc-\d+\] extracts citation markers from the answer and resolves each to a table of doc, chunk, and quoted span for UI footnotes or tooltips.

text:
Production template: citations plus refusal. One combined template that does both. Numbered RULES make the contract explicit so the model treats citations and refusal as non-optional. Template:

You are a helpful support assistant. Use ONLY the documentation
below to answer the user's question.

RULES:
1. Cite every claim inline as [doc-N], where N is the doc number.
2. Quote 3-7 words from the cited chunk after each [doc-N].
3. If the docs do not answer the question, respond with EXACTLY:
   "I don't have enough information to answer that."
4. Do not invent facts. Do not paraphrase what isn't in the docs.

Documentation:
{context}

Question: {query}
Answer:

Numbered rules survive long context better than prose instructions. The model attends to short structured constraints more reliably than a paragraph.
summary: A production prompt template combines citations and refusal into 4 numbered RULES, which the model follows more reliably than prose instructions.

text: Multi-hop retrieval control-loop flowchart: starts with User Query, enters Retrieve Top-K For Current Query, then a sufficiency diamond 'Does Context Answer The Question?' Three branches: YES leads right to Generate Answer (green); NO + Hops < max_hops leads left to Reformulate Query (orange) then loops back up to the Retrieve box; NO + Hops >= max_hops leads down to Refuse: 'I Don't Have Enough Information' (red). Arrow markers color-coded to indicate each path.
summary: Diagram of the multi-hop control loop with retrieve, sufficiency diamond, and three branches (generate, reformulate-and-loop, refuse) for hitting max_hops.

text: Chapter 12.25 - Multi-Hop Retrieval. Some queries depend on chained facts that live across multiple docs; one retrieval misses half. Multi-hop retrieval is a control loop: retrieve top-k, run a sufficiency check, then either generate, reformulate-and-loop, or refuse when max_hops is hit. The sufficiency check is a structured prompt forcing SUFFICIENT or INSUFFICIENT+follow-up. Worth it for compound, cross-doc, step-dependent queries; not for single-doc FAQ lookups. Cost scales linearly with hops, so cap max_hops (typically 3-5), cache reformulations, and use a smaller judge. Watch three failure modes: infinite loops (cap + log), divergence (anchor original query), stuck sufficiency judges (separate calibrated model).
summary: Multi-hop retrieval iterates retrieve-check-reformulate with a hard max_hops cap to handle compound queries, trading cost and latency for accuracy while guarding against loops, drift, and overconfident judges.
queries: how to chain retrievals in RAG
when to use multi-hop in RAG

text:
Some queries need more than one retrieval. Single-hop retrieval works when the answer fits in one chunk. Multi-hop retrieval kicks in when the question depends on a chain of facts that live in different docs. Signal you need it: even a perfect retrieval returns only half of what is needed.

Single-hop works example: Query 'How do I reset my password?' - 1 retrieval finds doc-1 (password reset). Answer: 'Go to Settings then Security, click Reset Password.'

Multi-hop needed example: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' - 1 retrieval finds doc-1 (password reset) but the question also needs doc-3 (email change / recovery). One retrieval misses half; multiple retrievals needed.
summary: Multi-hop retrieval applies when answering a query requires chaining facts from multiple docs that one retrieval cannot fetch together.
queries: when do I need multi-hop retrieval
question needs facts from multiple documents
password reset if forgot email RAG example
signal that you need multiple hops

text:
Two hops on the running query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?'

Hop 1: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' Retrieved: doc-1 (password reset). Reasoning: Reset requires email access. The user says they forgot email - need email recovery info before answering. Decision: INSUFFICIENT. Reformulate as: 'How do I recover access if I forgot my email?'

Hop 2: Query 'How do I recover access if I forgot my email?' Retrieved: doc-3 (email change / recovery). Reasoning: Now both pieces are in hand: the reset flow (doc-1) and the email-recovery path (doc-3). Decision: SUFFICIENT.

Final answer combines doc-1 + doc-3 with citations. Each hop reformulates the query to fetch the missing piece.
summary: Worked two-hop trace showing the LLM marking hop 1 INSUFFICIENT, reformulating, then marking hop 2 SUFFICIENT and combining doc-1 with doc-3.
queries: how does LLM decide to do another hop
combining doc-1 and doc-3 with citations

text:
The multi-hop control loop flowchart. Loop structure: retrieve top-k for current query -> check if context answers the question (diamond) -> if YES generate answer; if NO and hops < max_hops, reformulate query and loop back to retrieve; if NO and hops >= max_hops, refuse with 'I don't have enough information'.

max_hops typically 3-5. Each hop adds 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call latency. Tune based on workload and budget. A hard cap on max_hops prevents infinite loops. If the cap is hit without sufficiency, the system refuses.
summary: Control-loop flowchart: retrieve, check sufficiency, then either generate, reformulate-and-loop, or refuse when max_hops cap is hit.
queries: loop back to retrieve after reformulation

text:
Asking the model: do you have enough? The sufficiency check is a small structured task. The model decides whether the retrieved text answers the question. SUFFICIENT means 'I can answer now'; INSUFFICIENT means 'fetch this follow-up'.

Prompt template:
You are an assistant deciding whether you have enough information.
Question: {query}
Retrieved so far: {context}
Respond with EXACTLY one of:
- SUFFICIENT (you can answer the question from the retrieved text)
- INSUFFICIENT: <one short follow-up query to retrieve more>
Do not answer the question yet.

Example trace:
Hop 1: 'INSUFFICIENT: How do I recover access if I forgot my email?'
Hop 2: 'SUFFICIENT'
summary: Sufficiency-check prompt forces the model to emit either SUFFICIENT or INSUFFICIENT with a follow-up query, without prematurely answering.
queries: how to ask LLM if it has enough info
prompt to decide more retrieval needed

text:
When to reach for multi-hop. Multi-hop is not free. Each hop multiplies latency and cost. Use only when query genuinely needs chained facts, always with a hard cap.

Worth It: Compound questions (password reset if you forgot your email); cross-doc reasoning (compare Pro vs Enterprise plans); step-dependent flows (cancel subscription then refund).

Not Worth It: Single-doc lookups; simple FAQ-style queries; cost outweighs benefit when one hop already answers.

Cost Multiplier: Each hop = 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call; 3 hops = ~3x latency, ~3x cost vs single-hop; budget per query becomes a hard constraint.

Mitigations: Cap max_hops at 3; cache reformulated queries; use a smaller model for the sufficiency check.
summary: Multi-hop is worth it for compound, cross-doc, and step-dependent queries but multiplies cost ~Nx, so cap hops, cache reformulations, and use a cheap sufficiency judge.

text:
Failure modes: infinite loops and divergence. Three known failure modes show up in production multi-hop systems; each has a known mitigation that costs either an extra check or stricter caps.

Infinite Loop: Model never says SUFFICIENT. Each hop reformulates without making progress. Mitigation: hard cap on max_hops + log every reformulation.

Divergence: Each reformulation drifts farther from the original intent. 'How do I reset my password?' becomes 'How do I configure SSO?' by hop 3. Mitigation: keep the ORIGINAL query in the reformulation prompt as an anchor.

Stuck Sufficiency Judge: Model always says SUFFICIENT after hop 1 even when context is incomplete (overconfident). Mitigation: separate judge model, calibrate via a golden dataset.
summary: Three multi-hop failure modes - infinite loops, divergence, and stuck sufficiency judges - each fixed with caps, anchoring the original query, or a calibrated judge model.
queries: model drifts off topic across hops

text: Self-RAG token-emission timeline diagram: horizontal line with six markers showing the model alternating between special control tokens and answer text across the generation sequence. Markers in order: <retrieve> (model gates retrieval), Reformulate (tokens 2-3 produce search query), doc-1 In (retriever returns top doc), <isrel> (model judges doc-1 relevant), Answer (tokens 6-20 generate answer text), <issup> (model judges answer supported). Generation order left to right.
summary: Diagram showing the left-to-right order of Self-RAG control tokens interleaved with answer generation.
queries: what does Self-RAG timeline look like

text: Retrieve / no-retrieve decision diamond diagram: User Query box at top flows into yellow diamond labeled "Need external knowledge?". YES branch on right exits to green box "<retrieve> -> <isrel> -> <issup>". NO branch on left exits to red box "<no-retrieve> -> Answer". Below: "Trained via instruction tuning + RL. Reduces unnecessary retrievals ~40% (Asai et al. 2023)".
summary: Diagram of the Self-RAG gate diamond routing queries to either retrieve-plus-critique or direct-answer.

text: Self-RAG (Asai et al. 2023) trains the LLM to decide per-query whether to retrieve, then self-critique what comes back. Model emits four special control tokens inline: <retrieve>, <no-retrieve>, <isrel> (judges each retrieved doc's relevance), and <issup> (judges whether final answer is supported by kept docs). Runtime intercepts tokens and acts. Cuts unnecessary retrievals ~40%. Wins on mixed-knowledge corpora, latency-sensitive workloads, adaptive depth. Limits: needs a fine-tuned model, larger vocab, classifier calibration is the bottleneck.
summary: Self-RAG teaches the LLM to gate retrieval and self-critique results using four special control tokens.
queries: special tokens RAG retrieve isrel issup

text: Should the model retrieve, or just answer? Always-retrieve wastes tokens and latency. Never-retrieve risks hallucination. Self-RAG decides per-query. Example queries: "What is 2 + 2?" -> <no-retrieve> (factoid already known from pre-training). "How do I reset my password?" -> <retrieve> (custom-corpus knowledge model cannot answer without retrieval). "Summarize what we just discussed." -> <no-retrieve> (context already in conversation; no external lookup needed).
summary: Self-RAG gates retrieval per-query so the model only fetches when external knowledge is genuinely needed.

text: Self-RAG extends model vocabulary with four control tokens emitted inline during generation; runtime intercepts each. <retrieve>: retrieve a document now. <no-retrieve>: do not retrieve, answer from model's own knowledge. <isrel>: is this retrieved doc relevant? Emitted after each retrieved doc. <issup>: is the answer supported by the retrieved doc? Emitted with the answer.
summary: Four special tokens (<retrieve>, <no-retrieve>, <isrel>, <issup>) extend the model vocabulary to control retrieval and grade results.

text: Token emission timeline for query "How do I reset my password?": <retrieve> (model gates retrieval) -> Reformulate (tokens 2-3 produce a search query) -> doc-1 In (retriever returns top doc) -> <isrel> (model judges doc-1 relevant) -> Answer (tokens 6-20 generate answer text) -> <issup> (model judges answer supported). Each marker is a decision point the runtime watches. Generation order left to right.
summary: Walk through a real query showing the sequence of control tokens and answer text Self-RAG emits.

text: Retrieve / no-retrieve gate sits at top of every generation. User query enters decision diamond "Need external knowledge?" YES branch -> <retrieve> -> <isrel> -> <issup>. NO branch -> <no-retrieve> -> Answer. Trained via instruction tuning + RL on retrieval decisions. Reduces unnecessary retrievals ~40% in original Asai et al. 2023 paper.
summary: The gate diamond routes queries to retrieval-plus-critique or direct-answer paths, trained with instruction tuning plus RL.

text: Self-critique re-scores every retrieved doc. After retrieval, model emits <isrel> per doc to grade relevance. Per-doc critique example: doc-1 (Password Reset) RELEVANT -> Keep; doc-9 (Login Troubleshooting) PARTIALLY RELEVANT -> Keep but de-weight; doc-22 (Rate Limits) IRRELEVANT -> Drop. After answer, model emits <issup> final verdict: FULLY-SUPPORTED | PARTIALLY-SUPPORTED | NO-SUPPORT. Labels how grounded the answer is in the kept docs.
summary: After retrieval Self-RAG grades each doc with <isrel> and grades the final answer's groundedness with <issup>.

text: Self-RAG wins: mixed-knowledge corpora (some queries need retrieval, some don't); latency-sensitive workloads (skips retrieval for ~40% of queries); adaptive depth (decides how many docs to fetch). Limits: requires a model trained for Self-RAG (not plug-and-play); token vocabulary increases serialization cost; calibration of the special-token classifier is the bottleneck. Pick Self-RAG when fine-tuning is on the table and the workload mixes retrieval-needing and already-knew-it queries.
summary: Self-RAG wins on adaptive mixed-knowledge workloads but requires fine-tuning and classifier calibration.

text: CRAG three-branch decision tree: an evaluator score routes retrieved docs to a CORRECT path (use directly), an AMBIGUOUS path (combine internal + web), or an INCORRECT path (web-search fallback), all converging into knowledge refinement before generation.
summary: Diagram showing the CRAG evaluator routing docs into three branches that converge into knowledge refinement and generation.
queries: Visual showing CORRECT AMBIGUOUS INCORRECT branches

text: CRAG (Corrective RAG, Yan et al. 2024) inserts a retrieval evaluator between retrieval and generation. Each retrieved doc is scored and classified as CORRECT (use directly), AMBIGUOUS (combine internal docs with external web search), or INCORRECT (fall back to web search). All branches converge into a knowledge refinement step that decomposes docs into 100-150 token strips, drops off-topic strips, and packs only on-topic ones before generation. Threshold guidance: below 0.5 = INCORRECT, above 0.8 = CORRECT, between = AMBIGUOUS. Demonstrated on query 'What is our cancellation policy for monthly plans?' where doc-7 scored 0.92 (CORRECT), doc-22 scored 0.31 (INCORRECT), doc-15 scored 0.62 (AMBIGUOUS).
summary: CRAG adds an evaluator that routes retrieved docs into CORRECT, AMBIGUOUS, or INCORRECT branches and refines surviving docs into strips before generation.
queries: When does CRAG fall back to web search
How to handle bad retrieval in RAG
How to detect irrelevant retrieved docs
What threshold makes a doc CORRECT in CRAG

text: CRAG (Yan et al. 2024) adds a retrieval evaluator between retrieval and generation. Each doc is scored and classified: CORRECT (use it), AMBIGUOUS (combine with external sources), or INCORRECT (fall back to web). Running query: 'What is our cancellation policy for monthly plans?' Scored docs: doc-7 (Refunds + Cancellation) score 0.92 verdict CORRECT - snippet 'Customers can cancel monthly plans any time before the next billing cycle.' doc-22 (API Rate Limits) score 0.31 verdict INCORRECT - snippet 'Free tier allows 100 requests per minute; Pro tier 1000 per minute.' doc-15 (Subscription Tiers) score 0.62 verdict AMBIGUOUS - snippet 'Monthly and annual plans differ in pricing and feature access.' The evaluator can be a small classifier model or an LLM judge. Threshold below 0.5 = INCORRECT, above 0.8 = CORRECT, between = AMBIGUOUS.
summary: The CRAG retrieval evaluator scores each retrieved doc and labels it CORRECT, AMBIGUOUS, or INCORRECT using thresholds 0.5 and 0.8.
queries: What does the CRAG evaluator do
How are retrieved docs scored in CRAG
What is a CORRECT verdict in CRAG
What is an AMBIGUOUS verdict in CRAG
What is an INCORRECT verdict in CRAG
Can the CRAG evaluator be an LLM
Small classifier vs LLM judge for retrieval scoring
Example CRAG scores for cancellation policy query
How to classify retrieved doc relevance
Why score retrieved docs before generation
How to gate RAG generation on doc quality

text: CORRECT branch: highest-confidence path. Doc passes the evaluator; CRAG routes it through knowledge refinement and then to the LLM with citations. Flow: Retrieved Doc -> Knowledge Refinement -> Generate Answer With Citations. Same as baseline RAG once the docs pass the evaluator.
summary: On the CORRECT branch, passing docs go through knowledge refinement and into generation just like baseline RAG.
queries: What happens on the CRAG CORRECT branch
How does CRAG use docs that pass the evaluator
Is CRAG CORRECT branch same as baseline RAG
Flow after CORRECT verdict in CRAG
When evaluator gives high score what happens

text: INCORRECT branch: doc fails the evaluator. CRAG rewrites the query for web search, calls an external search API, and uses the web results in place of the internal doc. Flow: Failed Doc -> Rewrite Query -> External Web Search -> Use Web Results -> Generate Answer. Web fallback costs latency (300-800 ms) and requires a search API. Use when refusal is unacceptable. Trade-off versus admitting 'I don't know'.
summary: On the INCORRECT branch, CRAG rewrites the query and falls back to web search instead of using the failed internal doc.
queries: What does CRAG do when a doc is INCORRECT
Latency cost of web fallback in CRAG
When evaluator score is too low what happens
Query rewriting for web search in CRAG
Why use web search instead of refusal

text: AMBIGUOUS branch: doc partially answers. CRAG keeps it AND also fetches external web results, then merges both into the generation prompt. Flow: Partial Doc + Web Results -> Merge Into Context -> Generate Answer. The hedge bet: highest cost path, lowest miss rate. Use when refusal is unacceptable but confidence in internal docs is low.
summary: On the AMBIGUOUS branch, CRAG hedges by combining the partial internal doc with web results before generating.
queries: What does CRAG do on AMBIGUOUS verdict
CRAG combine internal docs and web
When CRAG merges internal and external sources
Why combine docs and web search

text: CRAG knowledge refinement splits each retrieved doc into 100-150 token strips and scores each strip individually. Off-topic strips are dropped; on-topic strips are packed. Doc-7 (Refunds) starts at 800 tokens; after refinement, only 3 of 6 strips are kept (~50% reduction). Strip breakdown: Strip 1 Refund Eligibility KEEP. Strip 2 Annual Plans Prorating KEEP. Strip 3 Refund Processing Time KEEP. Strip 4 Tax Handling DROP. Strip 5 Legal Disclaimers DROP. Strip 6 Chargeback Policy DROP. Final: 3 kept strips, ~50% of original tokens, all on-topic. Packed into the prompt for generation.
summary: Knowledge refinement splits each doc into 100-150 token strips, drops off-topic ones, and keeps only the on-topic strips for the prompt.
queries: How many strips are kept after CRAG refinement

text: The full CRAG decision tree: Query + Retrieved Docs feed into an Evaluator Score diamond, which branches three ways - CORRECT -> Use Docs (green), AMBIGUOUS -> Combine (yellow), INCORRECT -> Web (red). All three branches converge into a Knowledge Refinement (Strips) step, which feeds Generate Answer. One evaluator. Three branches. All converge into knowledge refinement and generation (Yan et al. 2024).
summary: Full CRAG pipeline diagram: one evaluator routes to three branches that all converge into knowledge refinement and final generation.
queries: From evaluator to generation in CRAG
Where does knowledge refinement sit in CRAG

Nodes show case names like Smith v Jones, Brown v Board, Apex v Beta, Aurora v Beacon arranged across the canvas with citation arrows linking citing cases to cited ones.
summary: Diagram of the full 15-node, 27-edge legal citation graph used throughout the chapter.

text: Subgraph retrieval highlight on the 15-node citation graph showing how a query selects a 5-node connected component (a precedent chain) as the retrieved context. The highlighted nodes are Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State, drawn in orange with their connecting citation edges emphasized while the remaining 10 nodes fade into the background.

text: Same 15-node citation graph re-colored into three communities (Civil Rights, Contract Law, IP Law) for global-query summarization. Civil Rights community (6 nodes, red), Contract Law community (5 nodes, cyan), and IP Law community (4 nodes, orange) are visually separated by color so global queries can route to per-community summaries.
summary: Diagram coloring the citation graph by three detected communities (Civil Rights, Contract Law, IP Law) used for global-query summaries.
queries: Civil Rights Contract Law IP Law clusters
what does a community look like in GraphRAG

text: GraphRAG (Microsoft 2024) replaces flat embedding retrieval with a knowledge graph built by LLM extraction. Offline, each doc is run through an LLM that emits entities and relations (cost ~$0.02/doc); these form a directed graph (here, 15 legal cases with 27 citation edges). Community detection (Leiden/Louvain) clusters the graph and an LLM summarizes each community. At query time, local queries retrieve a subgraph (e.g., 5 nodes for a precedent chain); global queries retrieve community summaries and map-reduce them. GraphRAG shines on entity-rich corpora with global queries (themes, comparisons) where naive RAG cannot answer without reading everything; it is wrong for self-contained FAQ-style docs. Mitigate cost via caching, nightly community detection, and hybrid routing.
summary: GraphRAG indexes docs as a knowledge graph and answers local queries from subgraphs and global queries from community summaries, paying off only on entity-rich corpora.
queries: how does GraphRAG work end to end
when does GraphRAG beat naive RAG

text: Why this chapter uses a different corpus. The customer-support corpus is mostly self-contained how-to pages with sparse relationships, so GraphRAG over it would build a thin graph with few edges and demonstrate nothing. This chapter switches to a 15-node legal-citation network because it has rich entity-relationship structure. Customer Support Corpus - Won't Fit: 30 docs, mostly self-contained how-to pages, sparse relationships. Legal Citation Network - Fits: 15 case decisions where each cites 2-5 prior cases, rich entities (Plaintiff, Defendant, Court, Statute) and real graph topology. The customer-support corpus returns in chapter 12.29; this chapter only uses the legal-citation secondary corpus.
summary: GraphRAG needs an entity-rich corpus, so this chapter swaps the customer-support docs for a 15-node legal citation network.
queries: why does GraphRAG chapter use a different corpus
why switch from customer support to legal citation corpus
when does GraphRAG not fit a corpus
why sparse graphs don't demonstrate GraphRAG
legal citation network corpus for RAG
is GraphRAG good for FAQ docs

text: Offline: extract entities and relations with an LLM. Each case runs through an LLM extraction prompt once at index time. The LLM emits entities (people, organizations, statutes) and relations (sued, decided-in, applies, holding). Cost is approximately $0.02 per doc. Example for 'Smith v Jones (2018)'. Entities: Smith (Plaintiff), Jones (Defendant), Northern District Court, Title VII (Statute), "Discrimination Found" (Holding). Relations: Smith -- sued --> Jones; Smith v Jones -- decided-in --> Northern District Court; Smith v Jones -- applies --> Title VII; Smith v Jones -- holding --> Discrimination Found.
summary: An LLM extracts entities and typed relations from each doc once offline at roughly $0.02 per doc.
queries: LLM entity extraction for graph indexing
cost per doc for GraphRAG extraction

sued decided-in applies holding relation types

text: The 15-node citation graph, built once offline. Each node is a case; each directed edge is a citation from the citing case to the cited case. 15 cases, 27 citation edges. Updated incrementally as new cases arrive. Nodes include Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State, Miller v EEOC, Apex v Beta, Cosmos v Stellar, Delta v Epsilon, Falcon v GoTech, Hera v Olympus, Aurora v Beacon, Cipher v Decode, Eagle v Falcon IP, Sigma v Tau.
summary: The running corpus is a 15-node, 27-edge directed citation graph built once offline and updated incrementally.

text: Query time: retrieve a subgraph. For the query 'What is the precedent chain for workplace discrimination cases?' the retriever finds a 5-node subgraph (Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State) and sends only those nodes plus their edges to the LLM. This is entity-aware retrieval; the graph topology is the signal. The LLM reads only the highlighted subgraph, not the whole 15-node corpus.
summary: Local queries retrieve a small connected subgraph of relevant nodes and edges instead of feeding the whole corpus to the LLM.
queries: how does GraphRAG retrieve at query time
how big is the retrieved subgraph
what does the LLM see in GraphRAG

text: Communities: cluster the graph for global queries. Community detection (Leiden or Louvain algorithms) groups densely-connected nodes into clusters. Each community gets an LLM-generated summary at index time. Global queries hit the community summaries; local queries hit the subgraph. Three communities for this corpus: Civil Rights (6 nodes) - cases established protected-class precedents under Title VII and the Equal Protection Clause. Contract Law (5 nodes) - cases interpreted breach-of-contract remedies, specific performance, and damages caps. IP Law (4 nodes) - cases shaped fair-use boundaries, trademark dilution, and software copyrightability.
summary: Community detection (Leiden/Louvain) clusters the graph and an LLM writes a summary per community so global queries answer from summaries instead of the full corpus.
queries: what is community detection in GraphRAG
how does GraphRAG answer global queries
how many communities in the legal corpus
Civil Rights Contract Law IP Law communities
Title VII Equal Protection Clause community

text: Global queries use communities, local queries use subgraphs. Two query patterns, two routing paths. Local Query example: 'How did the court rule in Smith v Jones?' Plan: retrieve the Smith v Jones node + 1-hop neighbors (5 nodes total). Output: direct answer from the subgraph. Global Query example: 'What are the main themes across this corpus?' Plan: retrieve all 3 community summaries; map-reduce summarize them. Output: high-level themes answer the question without retrieving every doc. Global queries are GraphRAG's killer feature - naive RAG cannot answer them without retrieving everything; GraphRAG answers from the community summaries.
summary: Local queries fetch a 1-hop subgraph for a direct answer while global queries map-reduce over community summaries to produce corpus-wide themes.
queries: local query vs global query GraphRAG
what is a global query in RAG
what is a local query in RAG
why naive RAG can't answer global queries
how did the court rule example query

text: When to reach for GraphRAG. GraphRAG is expensive to build and maintain. Use it when the corpus has rich entity-relationship structure and global queries are common. Worth It: rich entity-relationship corpus (legal, biomedical, code); global queries common (themes, summaries, comparisons); long-term value (build the graph once, query many times). Not Worth It: mostly self-contained docs (FAQ, support knowledge base); only local / factoid queries; schema churn (entities change weekly). Cost: per-doc LLM extraction at index time (~$0.02 per doc); re-extract on doc changes; graph storage + community detection compute. Mitigations: cache extractions; run community detection nightly, not per-query; hybrid - GraphRAG for global queries, naive RAG for local.
summary: GraphRAG pays off for entity-rich corpora with frequent global queries, but its extraction cost makes it wrong for self-contained FAQ-style docs.
queries: biomedical code legal GraphRAG use cases

text: Chapter 12.29 Tool-Augmented & Agentic RAG. Retrieval becomes one tool among many: vector search, SQL query, calculator, web search. The LLM uses function-calling to pick a tool per turn; runtime executes it and feeds the response back. Loop runs until model emits final answer or hits max_iterations (typically 5-10). Worked example query 'Compare Pro and Enterprise plans, then estimate cost for 25 users on Pro' uses 4 turns: vector_search, sql_query, calculator (25 x 29 = 725), then final answer with citations. Loop control: hard caps (max_iterations, wall-clock), soft signals (final-answer marker, repeated identical tool calls flag divergence), cost tracking per tool call. Mitigations: cap iterations, cache tool responses by exact args, use smaller orchestrator model. Orchestration last: DIY while-loop vs frameworks like LangGraph (graph state machine, checkpointing, retries, human-in-the-loop). Patterns are framework-agnostic.
summary: Agentic RAG treats retrieval as one tool among many; LLM picks tools per turn via function-calling loop until final answer or max_iterations.
queries: how does function calling work with retrieval
agent that uses RAG plus SQL
how to prevent runaway agent loops
agent calls vector search and SQL

text: Retrieval is one tool. Add more. Tool-augmented (agentic) RAG treats retrieval as one of several tools the LLM can invoke. SQL, calculators, web search, and internal APIs sit alongside it. The model picks the right tool per query. Four tools shown: Vector Search (semantic retrieval over embedded chunks, the RAG built so far); SQL Query (structured lookups over a relational store - pricing tables, user accounts); Calculator (precise arithmetic - models bad at math, calculators not); Web Search (fresh or out-of-corpus info, fallback or time-sensitive queries). The LLM decides which tool to call. Function-calling pattern. Each tool returns text the model can read.
summary: Introduces agentic RAG where vector search, SQL, calculator, and web search are tools the LLM chooses from per query.
queries: what tools can an LLM agent use
what does agentic mean in RAG

text: Function-Calling: How The Model Picks A Tool. The model emits structured tool_call tokens; the runtime intercepts, executes the tool, and feeds the response back. Loop continues until model emits final answer or hits max_iterations. Trace: User: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' Model: 'I need the pricing table. Calling tool.' tool_call: sql_query(table='plans', filter="name in ('Pro','Enterprise')"). tool_response: [{'plan':'Pro','price':29,'users':10}, {'plan':'Enterprise','price':199,'users':'unlimited'}]. Model: 'I also need feature comparison docs. Calling tool.' tool_call: vector_search(query='Pro vs Enterprise features', k=3). tool_response: [doc-15 chunk 1, doc-15 chunk 2, doc-19 chunk 1]. Model: 'Now I can answer. Generating response with citations.'
summary: Concrete function-calling trace where the model alternates tool_call emissions and tool_response inputs until it can answer.
queries: how does model emit tool call tokens

Pro vs Enterprise plan comparison agent
LLM emits tool call then waits for response

text: The Tool-Call Loop flowchart. Control loop runs until model declares done or max_iterations reached. Flow: User Query -> Model -> diamond 'Need A Tool?' -> YES branch calls tool_call(...), tool_response fed back to Model (loopback) -> NO branch goes to Final Answer. max_iterations = 10. Hard cap prevents runaway loops. Loopback path lets the model chain multiple tool calls.
summary: Flowchart of the agentic RAG tool-call loop: query, model, decision diamond, tool call with feedback, or final answer with max_iterations cap.
queries: loop until final answer or cap
model decision diamond need a tool
user query to final answer flow

text: Worked Example: Multi-Tool Trace. Query: 'Compare Pro and Enterprise plans, then estimate cost for 25 users on Pro.' Four turns, three tools, one final answer. Turn 1: vector_search('Pro Enterprise comparison') -> 3 chunks (doc-15 + doc-19). Turn 2: sql_query(SELECT price FROM plans WHERE name='Pro') -> $29 per seat. Turn 3: calculator(25 x 29) -> 725. Turn 4 Final Answer: 'Pro is $29/seat. 25 users => $725/month. Pro includes features X, Y, Z [doc-15]; Enterprise adds SSO + audit logs [doc-19].'
summary: Four-turn agentic trace: vector_search then sql_query then calculator then final cited answer estimating $725/month for 25 Pro users.
queries: agent uses vector search SQL calculator
trace of three tools then answer
agent answer with doc-15 and doc-19

text: Loop Control: Termination Criteria. Layered defense. Hard Caps: max_iterations (typically 5-10), total tool calls per query (rate-limit by tool), wall-clock budget (timeout seconds). Soft Signals: model emits 'final answer' marker; same tool called with same args twice in a row -> divergence flag; tool error rate above threshold -> abort and refuse. Cost Tracking: each tool call has fixed cost, sum across iterations; alert if over per-query budget; bill chargebacks to feature owners. Mitigations: cap iterations strictly; cache tool responses by exact args (especially vector_search); use a smaller orchestrator model.
summary: Termination uses hard caps, soft divergence signals, cost tracking, and caching/cheaper-model mitigations to keep agent loops safe.

text: Orchestration: Frameworks Like LangGraph. Pick orchestration last. The patterns above work without any framework. Frameworks are a productivity choice, not a correctness one. DIY (Custom While Loop): you implement function-calling parse, tool dispatch, max_iterations check, response merge yourself. Full control. More code. LangGraph And Similar Frameworks: graph-shaped state machine for tool flows. Nodes are tool calls, edges are transitions. Built-in checkpointing, retries, human-in-the-loop. Less code. Framework lock-in. Pick a framework when it saves more time than it costs. Patterns are framework-agnostic.
summary: Compares DIY while-loop orchestration vs LangGraph-style state-machine frameworks for tool flows; choose by productivity not correctness.
queries: should I use a framework for agentic RAG

Three metric groups (Cost per Query in dollars, Latency P50 in seconds, Accuracy as golden recall percentage), each with three colored bars: RAG-Only (green), Hybrid (yellow), Long-Context-Only (red). Bar values labeled above each bar; group labels below.

text: Chapter 12.30 compares three strategies for a 200-page product manual that fits in a 200k context window: long-context-only (pack all 120k tokens, $0.36/query, 5-8s, lost-in-middle risk), RAG-only (chunk into 240 pieces, retrieve top-5, $0.008/query, 1.5s, miss-rate risk), and hybrid (retrieve top-30, pack 50k tokens with reordering, $0.15/query, 3s, highest accuracy). Hybrid is the production default; RAG-only wins for cost/latency-critical workloads; long-context-only is niche for single-doc low-volume cases.
summary: Chapter-level summary: three strategies for a 200-page manual - long-context-only, RAG-only, and hybrid - with cost/latency/accuracy tradeoffs and decision rules.

text: This chapter switches corpora to compare retrieval against simply stuffing everything into the prompt. The customer-support corpus has 30 docs at 500-2000 tokens each, totaling ~50k tokens distributed across 30 distinct documents - retrieval is the natural pattern. The secondary corpus is a single 200-page product manual at ~120k tokens that fits inside a 200k context window. This is the 'do I even need RAG?' test case: when the entire knowledge base is one long document that fits in the context window, the retrieval question changes shape.

text: Long-context-only: pack ALL 120k tokens of the manual into the prompt. The LLM reads everything and answers. Pipeline: User Query -> Pack ALL 120k tokens -> LLM Reads -> Answer. Cost: at $3 per 1M input tokens, this is $0.36 per query. Latency: 120k tokens to read produces 5-8 second first-token latency. Simple. Expensive. Slow. And subject to lost-in-the-middle (chapter 12.23) for facts buried in the middle pages.
summary: Long-context-only stuffs the full 120k-token manual into the prompt; $0.36/query, 5-8s latency, lost-in-the-middle risk.

text: RAG-only: chunk the 200-page manual into 240 chunks of 512 tokens. At query time, embed the query, retrieve top-5 (2,560 tokens), pack into prompt. Pipeline: Chunk Manual Into 240 Chunks -> Retrieve Top-5 -> Pack ~2,560 tokens -> LLM Reads -> Answer. Cost: ~$0.008 per query, ~45x cheaper than long-context-only. Latency: 50ms retrieval + 1-2s generation = ~1.5s. Risk: if the relevant chunk isn't in top-5, the answer is wrong.
summary: RAG-only chunks the manual into 240 pieces, retrieves top-5 for ~$0.008/query and ~1.5s latency but risks missing the right chunk.

text: Hybrid: retrieve top-30 chunks (15,000 tokens), optionally rerank, then pack 50k tokens into the prompt. Reorder using lost-in-middle strategies (front-load or sandwich from chapter 12.23). Pipeline: Retrieve Top-30 -> Optional Rerank -> Pack 50k tokens -> Front-Load Or Sandwich -> LLM -> Answer. Cost: ~$0.15 per query (5x cheaper than long-context-only). Latency: 80ms retrieval + 3s generation = ~3s. Accuracy: highest because more candidate chunks lower the miss rate. The pragmatic middle - most production systems land here.
summary: Hybrid retrieves top-30 chunks and packs 50k tokens with reordering; ~$0.15/query, ~3s latency, highest accuracy - the production default.

text: Grouped bar chart compares three strategies on three metrics. Cost per query: RAG-Only $0.008, Hybrid $0.150, Long-Context-Only $0.360. Latency p50: RAG-Only 1.5s, Hybrid 3.0s, Long-Context-Only 6.0s. Accuracy (golden recall): RAG-Only 78%, Hybrid 92%, Long-Context-Only 84%. Hybrid wins on accuracy; RAG-only wins on cost and latency; Long-Context-Only loses on both cost and latency while landing in the middle on accuracy. Hybrid is the production default. Long-context-only is a niche path. RAG-only is for high-volume, cost-sensitive systems.

text: Decision: when each one wins. RAG Only: cost-critical at scale (~$0.008/query), latency-critical (~1.5s p50), large multi-doc corpus, citations required for chunk-level provenance. Hybrid: default for production, best accuracy on accuracy-critical workloads, acceptable cost + latency tradeoff, citations still feasible. Long-Context Only: single long document corpus, low query volume so cost not pressing, citations not required or post-extracted, no retrieval infrastructure available. Hybrid is the production default; RAG-only and long-context-only are intentional choices for specific shapes.
summary: Decision rules: RAG-only for cost/latency-critical workloads, hybrid for production accuracy, long-context-only for single-doc low-volume cases.

Each vertex is a rounded rectangle with a label and a one-line caption describing what that layer measures. Edges connect the three vertices, showing that the three layers together cover the full pipeline and a failure on the final answer must trace to one of the three corners.

text: Chapter 12.31 introduces the RAG Eval Triangle: three layers of evaluation - retrieval (did we find the right docs?), generation (did the model use them faithfully?), and end-to-end (did the final answer help the user?). Each layer has its own metrics so that a bad final answer can be traced to a specific root cause. Retrieval uses Recall@k, MRR, Precision@k, and nDCG@k. Generation uses Faithfulness and Answer Relevancy. End-to-end uses Correctness (vs gold) and Helpfulness (1-5 Likert). Three failure scenarios on a multi-hop password-reset query show how the triangle isolates faults to a retrieval, generation, or product cause. BLEU and ROUGE are deprecated for RAG because n-gram overlap does not measure faithfulness; use RAGAS (12.33) and LLM-as-judge (12.32) instead.
summary: Three-layer evaluation framework for RAG: retrieval, generation, end-to-end, with per-layer metrics so failures localize to a root cause.

The diagram places retrieval at the top vertex, generation lower-left, end-to-end lower-right - each labeled with the one question its metrics answer.

text:
Layer 1 - Retrieval Metrics. Four metrics dominate the literature.

Recall@k = relevant_retrieved / total_relevant - what fraction of all the relevant docs were retrieved?

MRR = (1/N) * sum( 1 / rank_of_first_relevant ) - mean reciprocal rank of the first relevant doc per query.

Precision@k = relevant_retrieved / k - what fraction of the top-k retrieved are actually relevant?

nDCG@k = DCG@k / IDCG@k - normalised discounted cumulative gain, a rank-weighted relevance score.

Worked example: top-3 returned for 'How do I reset my password?': doc-1 (relevant), doc-23 (not), doc-7 (relevant) -> Precision@3 = 2 / 3 = 0.667.
summary: Four retrieval metrics - Recall@k, MRR, Precision@k, nDCG@k - measure whether search found the right docs.
queries: what is MRR mean reciprocal rank

text:
Layer 2 - Generation Metrics. Whether the model used retrieved context to produce a grounded answer. Faithfulness is the headline, paired with Answer Relevancy.

Faithfulness: Are the claims in the answer supported by the retrieved context? Example: answer claims '24-hour expiry'; retrieved doc-1 says 'the link expires after 24 hours' -> claim supported -> score 1.0.

Answer Relevancy: Does the answer actually address the question? Example: Question 'How do I reset my password?' Answer 'Click forgot password and check your email' -> relevancy 0.9.
summary: Generation layer measures faithfulness (claims supported by context) and answer relevancy (does the answer address the question).
queries: how to measure if model used retrieved context
are claims supported by context metric
does answer address the question metric
how to detect hallucination in RAG answer
what is groundedness in LLM answer

text:
Layer 3 - End-to-End Metrics. Whether the user actually got what they needed. Correctness compares to a gold answer; helpfulness asks the user on a 1-5 Likert scale.

Correctness (binary): Did the final answer match the gold answer? Example: Gold 'Click forgot password and check email.' Generated 'Click forgot password link and check your registered email.' -> 1.

Helpfulness (1-5 Likert): How useful was the answer to the user? Example: User rated this answer 4/5: 'Solved it but had to scroll'.
summary: End-to-end metrics measure user-facing quality: correctness against a gold answer (binary) and helpfulness as a 1-5 Likert rating.
queries: what is correctness metric in RAG
how to measure end-to-end RAG quality
did the answer help the user metric

text:
Why three layers? Failures trace to a layer. Walking the multi-hop query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' through three failure scenarios:

Scenario A - Retrieval Fault: Top-k returns doc-4 (refunds) for a password-reset query; doc-1 never surfaces. Recall@10 drops from 0.95 to 0.30. Root cause: retrieval. Fix in the retriever (12.14-12.21).

Scenario B - Generation Fault: Retrieval returns doc-1 + doc-7 correctly. The model ignores them and hallucinates a phone-support step. Faithfulness drops to 0.50. Root cause: generation. Fix the prompt / model / refusal logic (12.24).

Scenario C - Product Fault: Retrieval and generation both score above 0.9. Yet user reports 'this did not help'. Helpfulness drops to 2/5. Root cause: product / prompting / answer style. Fix online via 12.35.
summary: Three concrete failure scenarios show how the triangle pinpoints whether a bad answer is a retrieval, generation, or product problem.

text: Deprecated for RAG: BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR. These are word-overlap metrics: they count how many n-grams in the generated answer also appear in a reference answer. They were designed for machine translation and summarisation, where wording proximity matters. They do not measure faithfulness or groundedness. A perfectly faithful answer written in different words scores 0; a hallucinated answer that recycles reference words scores high. Use RAGAS (chapter 12.33) and LLM-as-judge (chapter 12.32) instead.
summary: BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR are word-overlap metrics built for translation; they miss faithfulness and should not be used for RAG.

text: Judge calibration before-and-after line chart. The x-axis is the judge score percentile (low to high); the y-axis is agreement with human labels. A green dashed horizontal line marks the 0.85 target. Before curve (red): roughly flat around 55-60% agreement across all judge-score percentiles, with a long divergence tail. After curve (green): rises from 0.78 at low percentile up to 0.92 at high percentile, crossing above the 0.85 target. Labels: 'Before: ~60%' under the red curve, 'After: ~92%' above the green curve. The after curve was produced by refining the rubric and applying position-bias and verbosity-bias mitigations, showing how calibration narrows the gap to human judgement.
summary: Diagram of the judge calibration line chart: red 'before' curve at ~60% agreement vs green 'after' curve reaching ~92%, crossing the 0.85 human-agreement target after rubric refinement and bias mitigations.

text: Chapter 12.32 covers LLM-as-judge: using one LLM to evaluate another LLM's outputs. It contrasts human annotation (slow, expensive, high quality), heuristics like BLEU/ROUGE (fast, free, low quality), and LLM-as-judge (fast, medium cost, near-human when calibrated, agreement 0.85+). It walks through the judge prompt artifact (framing, 1-5 criteria, JSON output), the rubric design that anchors integer scores to behaviours for Faithfulness, three biases (position bias - first slot wins; verbosity bias - longer answers preferred even when wrong; self-preference bias - judges favor their own model family), each with mitigations (randomize/swap order, weight conciseness or length-normalise, cross-family judging). Closes with calibration: spot-check 50-100 human-labeled cases until agreement with human rises from ~60% baseline to 0.85+ target (after curve hits ~92%). Use a stronger or cross-family judge model.
summary: Overview of LLM-as-judge for RAG eval: trade-offs vs human/heuristics, judge prompt, 1-5 rubric, three biases with mitigations, and human-spot-check calibration to 0.85+ agreement.

text: Why use an LLM to judge an LLM? Human annotation is the gold standard but does not scale. Word-overlap heuristics like BLEU and ROUGE are fast but miss faithfulness. LLM-as-judge sits between the two: fast, medium cost, and near-human quality on well-rubric'd tasks. Three methods compared: Human Annotation - speed Very Slow, cost Expensive, quality High, 1-2 minutes per example, costs scale with team size. Heuristic (BLEU / ROUGE) - speed Instant, cost Free, quality Low, n-gram overlap that misses faithfulness and groundedness entirely. LLM-As-Judge - speed Fast, cost Medium, quality Near-Human, seconds per example; agreement with human reaches 0.85+ when calibrated.
summary: Compares three eval methods - human annotation, BLEU/ROUGE heuristics, and LLM-as-judge - on speed, cost, and quality to justify LLM-as-judge as the practical middle path.

text: The judge prompt is a text artifact, not a code block. It frames the LLM as an evaluator, names three criteria with a 1-5 scale, and asks for a JSON response. Variable placeholders are filled in at eval time. Prompt text: 'You are an expert evaluator. You will be given a question, the retrieved context, and the candidate answer. Score the answer on three criteria, each 1 (terrible) to 5 (excellent), with one short justification per criterion. Faithfulness: are all claims in the answer supported by the retrieved context? Answer Relevancy: does the answer address the question directly? Helpfulness: would this answer actually help the user reach their goal? Question: {question} Retrieved Context: {retrieved_context} Candidate Answer: {generated_answer} Return JSON: { "faithfulness": <1-5>, "answer_relevancy": <1-5>, "helpfulness": <1-5>, "justification": "<one short paragraph>" }'
summary: Shows the actual judge prompt artifact - role framing, three criteria (faithfulness, answer relevancy, helpfulness) on a 1-5 scale, variable placeholders, and required JSON output schema.

text: Rubric design: 1-5 per criterion. The rubric is the actual calibration tool. Prose framing in the prompt sets tone; the rubric anchors every integer score to a concrete behaviour. Faithfulness rubric: Score 5 - All Claims Supported, None Missing: every factual statement in the answer is verifiable in the retrieved context. Score 4 - Mostly Supported, Minor Unsupported Detail: core claims grounded; one peripheral detail not in context. Score 3 - About Half Supported: a roughly even mix of supported and ungrounded statements. Score 2 - Mostly Unsupported; Some Grounded: most claims are made up; one or two are anchored in the context. Score 1 - Hallucinated; Nothing In Context Supports It: answer is a confident hallucination with no grounding at all. Rubric design beats prompt prose. The rubric is what makes scores reproducible.
summary: Defines a 5-point Faithfulness rubric where each integer score is anchored to a concrete behaviour, showing why the rubric (not the prose) is what produces reproducible judge scores.
queries: How to design a judge rubric?

How to define each score level

text: Bias 1: Position Bias. In pairwise judging, the order of the two candidates affects the verdict even when their content is identical. The first slot wins more often. Order 1 (A, B) - judge picks position 1 (A): same two answers, same judge model, the first slot wins. Order 2 (B, A) - judge picks position 1 (B): swap the order, the judge's pick swaps too. Identical content. Mitigation: Randomize Or Swap. Run both orders for every pair and average; or randomize position per query before passing to the judge.
summary: Explains position bias in pairwise LLM judging - the first slot wins regardless of content - and mitigates by swapping/averaging both orderings or randomizing position.
queries: What is position bias in LLM-as-judge?
Why does the first answer win in pairwise judging?
Does order matter in LLM judging?
Swap order trick for LLM judge
Why swap A and B in judge prompt

text: Bias 2: Verbosity Bias. Judges (especially weaker models) prefer longer answers, mistaking length for thoroughness. The longer answer can be wrong and still score higher than a tight correct one. Answer A - 50 Words, Accurate: 'Go to Settings, click Forgot Password, check your email, use the link within 24 hours.' All claims supported. No padding. Answer B - 200 Words, One Hallucination: 'To begin the secure password recovery process, navigate to your account dashboard... You may also call our 24/7 support line.' (Note: phone support claim is unsupported.) Longer feels thorough. Judges (especially weaker ones) score it higher. Mitigation: Weight Conciseness Or Length-Normalise. Add an explicit conciseness criterion to the rubric, or normalise by answer length before averaging scores.
summary: Demonstrates verbosity bias - judges prefer longer answers even when they hallucinate - using a 50-word accurate vs 200-word hallucinated example, and mitigates by adding conciseness criteria or length normalisation.
queries: What is verbosity bias in LLM judges?
Why do LLM judges prefer longer answers?
Do longer answers win in LLM eval?

text: Bias 3: Self-Preference Bias. Judges score outputs from their own model family higher than human-aligned scores would. The bigger the model, the bigger this effect typically gets. Examples: Judge = GPT-4o, Candidate = GPT-4o -> +12% vs Human (same family). Judge = GPT-4o, Candidate = Claude 3.5 -> -3% vs Human (different family). Judge = Claude 3.5, Candidate = Claude 3.5 -> +9% vs Human (same family). Mitigation: Third-Party / Cross-Family Judging. Pick a judge from a different model family than the candidates. Or average across multiple judge families to wash out single-family bias.
summary: Shows self-preference bias - judges score their own model family +9 to +12% above human-aligned scores - and mitigates with cross-family judging or averaging across families.
queries: What is self-preference bias in LLM judges?
Do LLM judges prefer their own model family?
How much does self-preference bias inflate scores?
Bias when judge and candidate are same model
Why not use the same model to judge itself

text: Calibrate: Spot-Check 50-100 Cases. A judge is a hypothesis. Before trusting its scores in production, verify agreement against 50-100 human-labeled cases. Refine the rubric and mitigations until correlation lands above 0.85. Calibration chart: x-axis is Judge Score Percentile (Low To High); y-axis is Agreement With Human. A horizontal target line is drawn at 0.85. Before curve (red) sits flat around 0.55-0.60 across all percentiles - labeled 'Before: ~60%'. After curve (green) rises from 0.78 at low percentile to 0.92 at high percentile - labeled 'After: ~92%'. The after curve crosses above the 0.85 target line. Judge Model Selection: use a stronger judge model than the candidate (e.g., judge with GPT-4 a candidate from a 7B model). Or use the same model with documented caveats. Cross-family judging is the strongest setup.
summary: Calibrate the judge against 50-100 human-labeled cases until agreement crosses the 0.85 target - the before-curve sits at ~60% agreement, the after-curve (rubric refined plus bias mitigations) rises to ~92%.
queries: How to calibrate an LLM judge?
How many human-labeled cases for calibration?

Before and after calibration of LLM judge

text: Chapter 12.33 introduces RAGAS (Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assessment), a 2023 reference-free metric suite that uses an LLM under the hood. Four core metrics: Faithfulness and Answer Relevancy cover generation; Context Precision and Context Recall cover retrieval. Faithfulness = verifiable_claims / total_claims_in_answer (worked example: 4/5 = 0.8). Answer Relevancy = mean cosine similarity between original query and n round-trip questions generated from the answer (0.94+0.91+0.89)/3 = 0.913. Context Precision = relevant_chunks_at_top_k / k (2/3 = 0.667). Context Recall = relevant_chunks_retrieved / all_relevant_chunks_in_corpus (2/2 = 1.0, or 1/2 = 0.5 if one missed). A per-query report card averages the four scores. BLEU and ROUGE are not RAGAS: n-gram overlap fails to measure faithfulness or groundedness.
summary: Complete tour of the four RAGAS metrics with formulas, worked numbers, and why BLEU/ROUGE are inadequate for RAG.
queries: how to evaluate RAG without a reference answer
why BLEU and ROUGE fail for RAG

text: RAGAS (Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assessment) is a 2023 metric suite that uses an LLM under the hood to compute eval scores without needing a reference answer. Four core metrics, two for retrieval and two for generation: Faithfulness (Are the claims supported?), Answer Relevancy (Does it answer the question?), Context Precision (Are top results actually relevant?), Context Recall (Did we retrieve everything?).
summary: Introduces RAGAS as a reference-free, LLM-powered evaluation suite with four metrics split across retrieval and generation.
queries: do I need a reference answer for RAGAS
how does RAGAS use an LLM
RAGAS retrieval metrics vs generation metrics
what questions do RAGAS metrics answer

text: Faithfulness asks whether every claim in the generated answer can be verified in the retrieved context. The LLM-judge extracts atomic claims and labels each as supported or not. Formula: Faithfulness = | verifiable_claims | / | total_claims_in_answer |. Worked example for the password reset query. Retrieved context (doc-1): 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security. Click Forgot Password. An email is sent to your registered address. The link expires after 24 hours.' Generated answer: 'Go to Settings > Security, click Forgot Password, check your email, and use the link within 24 hours. You can also call support.' Five extracted claims: (1) Go to Settings > Security - Supported; (2) Click Forgot Password - Supported; (3) Check your email - Supported; (4) Use the link within 24 hours - Supported; (5) You can also call support - Not Supported. Faithfulness = 4 / 5 = 0.8.
summary: Defines Faithfulness as supported-claims over total-claims and walks a 5-claim password-reset example to 0.8.
queries: how is faithfulness computed in RAGAS
is every claim supported by context

text: Answer Relevancy is a round-trip check: the LLM-judge reads the generated answer and writes n new questions that the answer would best answer. Each generated question is embedded and compared with cosine similarity to the original query. The average is the relevancy score. Formula: Answer Relevancy = (1/n) * sum( cosine( embed(q), embed(q_i_generated) ) ). Worked example with n = 3. Original query: 'How do I reset my password?' Round-trip questions: Q1 'What are the steps to reset my password?' cos 0.94; Q2 'How can I change my password if I forgot it?' cos 0.91; Q3 'How do I update my account password?' cos 0.89. Answer Relevancy = (0.94 + 0.91 + 0.89) / 3 = 0.913.
summary: Answer Relevancy uses LLM-generated round-trip questions and averages cosine similarity to the original query (worked to 0.913).
queries: what is answer relevancy in RAGAS
how relevant is the answer to the question
does the answer address the question RAGAS
average cosine over n round trip questions

text: Context Precision asks what fraction of the top-k retrieved chunks were actually relevant. RAGAS adds a rank-aware weighting in the full version, but the simplified ratio is taught here. Formula: Context Precision = relevant_chunks_at_top_k / k. Worked example for top-3 password reset query: rank 1 doc-1 chunk-1 Password Reset Intro Relevant; rank 2 doc-23 chunk-2 500 Error Troubleshooting Not Relevant; rank 3 doc-1 chunk-2 Password Reset Email Step Relevant. Context Precision = 2 / 3 = 0.667. Naming note: DeepEval and TruLens call this ContextualRelevancyScore - same idea under a different framework name. Older RAGAS shipped a separate 'Context Relevancy' metric; it was deprecated in favor of Context Precision plus Context Recall.
summary: Context Precision is relevant-top-k over k (worked to 0.667); cross-framework naming and deprecated metric noted.
queries: what is context precision in RAGAS
top-k relevant chunks divided by k
RAGAS context precision vs context relevancy
framework naming differences RAG eval metrics
context precision example password reset top-3

text: Context Recall asks what fraction of all the relevant chunks in the corpus were retrieved. It is the dual of precision: precision says 'are top results clean?', recall says 'did we miss anything?'. Formula: Context Recall = relevant_chunks_retrieved / all_relevant_chunks_in_corpus. Worked example: the corpus has 2 chunks relevant to 'How do I reset my password?' (doc-1 chunk-1 and doc-1 chunk-2). Both retrieved -> Context Recall = 2 / 2 = 1.0. Counter-example: if retrieval returned only doc-1 chunk-1 and missed doc-1 chunk-2, Context Recall = 1 / 2 = 0.5.
summary: Context Recall is retrieved-relevant over all-relevant-in-corpus, dual of precision, with full-recall (1.0) and missed-chunk (0.5) examples.
queries: dual of precision in RAG eval

text: Putting it together: a per-query report card. One query, four scores. The retrieval pair (precision + recall) covers the search step; the generation pair (faithfulness + answer relevancy) covers the model. Report card scores: Faithfulness 0.80, Answer Relevancy 0.91, Context Precision 0.67, Context Recall 1.00. Average = 0.845, the headline score per query. How RAGAS computes the scores: an LLM-judge extracts atomic claims for faithfulness, generates round-trip questions for relevancy, and labels each retrieved chunk as relevant or not for precision and recall. The triangle in chapter 12.31 splits the same way (retrieval vs generation).
summary: Combines the four scores into a per-query report card (avg 0.845) and summarizes how the LLM-judge produces each metric.
queries: faithfulness 0.8 answer relevancy 0.91 context precision 0.67 context recall 1
LLM judge across all four RAGAS metrics

text: Not RAGAS: BLEU and ROUGE. BLEU and ROUGE compute n-gram overlap between the generated answer and a reference answer. They were designed for machine translation and summarisation, where wording proximity matters. They do not measure faithfulness or groundedness. A perfectly faithful answer in different wording scores low; a hallucinated answer that recycles reference words scores high. Use RAGAS or LLM-as-judge instead (see chapters 12.31 and 12.32).
summary: Explains why n-gram metrics BLEU and ROUGE are inappropriate for RAG and points to RAGAS or LLM-as-judge as replacements.
queries: what to use instead of BLEU and ROUGE for RAG

The upper lane shows the LLM generating question-answer pairs grounded in a corpus document; the lower lane shows a human reviewer accepting, editing, or rejecting each pair. Illustrates how a small hand-built seed scales into a 500-2000 reviewed set without losing human oversight. LLM lane segments: Corpus Doc -> LLM Generates Q-A Pair -> Pair Candidate. Bridge arrow down to Human lane segments: Pair Candidate -> Reviewer Accept/Edit/Reject -> Golden Record.
summary: Diagram of the two-lane LLM-generate then human-review pipeline that scales golden datasets while keeping human oversight.

text: Chapter 12.34 Golden Datasets explains how to build and maintain the human-verified ground-truth set every other RAG eval metric depends on. Covers the structure of a golden record (question, expected answer, expected docs, refusal flag), starting with a 30-100 hand-written seed across query types, the five edge cases that must be covered (multi-hop, empty-context, ambiguous, refusal-required, time-sensitive), the regression set that captures every production bug as a permanent test, LLM-bootstrapped generation with mandatory human review to scale to 500-2000 records, and a monthly review cadence with coverage, freshness, and pass-rate health metrics.
summary: Overview of building, scaling, and maintaining golden datasets for RAG evaluation.

text: Golden Datasets are the ground truth every other eval metric depends on. RAGAS, LLM-as-judge, and recall@k all need human-verified labels. The golden dataset is the held-out, human-verified set of records run against every eval. Without it you have no signal; with a flaky one you have noise. One golden record has four fields: Question ('How do I reset my password?'), Expected Answer ('Click Forgot Password and check your registered email.'), Expected Docs ([doc-1]), Refusal (No). Multiply by 30-2000 records and you have a defensible eval set.
summary: A golden record is a tuple of question, expected answer, expected docs, and refusal flag - the ground truth for every other RAG metric.

text: Start with 30-100 hand-written examples. The goal of the first batch is breadth across query types, not volume. Three curation steps: (1) List query types from logs or product spec - mine a week of production queries (or the product spec) for the categories you must support. (2) Write 5-10 examples per type by hand - cover normal cases plus edge cases. (3) Write expected answer + expected docs per example - each tuple becomes one golden record. Week 1 tally by query type: Single-Doc Lookups 20, Multi-Hop 15, Aggregation 10, Refusal-Required 10, Empty-Context 10. Total Week 1 = 65.
summary: Curate the first 30-100 records by hand across query types from logs, aiming for breadth not volume.

text: Five edge cases that break naive RAG and must be covered in the first hand-written batch: (1) Multi-Hop - answer requires combining 2+ docs - example: 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' (2) Empty-Context - no doc in the corpus answers this, must refuse - example: 'What is your stock price?' (3) Ambiguous - two docs conflict, newest-wins or refusal - example: 'What is the refund window?' (doc-A: 14 days; doc-B: 30 days). (4) Refusal-Required - out-of-scope or unsafe query - example: 'Give me another customer's email.' (5) Time-Sensitive - answer depends on today's date - example: 'Is feature X live yet?' Skip any and you ship blind to a class of failures.
summary: Five edge cases - multi-hop, empty-context, ambiguous, refusal-required, time-sensitive - must be covered or you ship blind.
queries: five edge cases for golden dataset
what test cases must I cover

text: Regression Set: every production bug becomes a golden case. Anything that failed in production once must never silently fail again. The regression set is the self-healing memory of your eval suite. Four-step workflow: (1) Production user reports a bad answer. (2) Engineer reproduces; captures query, docs, answer, expected answer. (3) Add tuple to the regression-golden set. (4) Every future eval includes it; failure cannot silently re-emerge. Example regression tuples: 'Reset password forgot email' -> multi-hop missed, bot suggested support phone; 'Cancel and refund' -> refused incorrectly, user blocked from valid request; 'Pro plan SSO' -> hallucinated SSO inclusion in Pro tier.
summary: Every production failure is captured into the regression set so the same bug cannot ship twice.
queries: what is a regression set in RAG eval
how to handle production bugs in RAG
workflow for adding failures to golden set
capture production bug to test case
how to track production RAG failures

text: LLM-Bootstrapped pipeline: generate then human-review. To scale past the hand-written seed, generate question-answer pairs with an LLM grounded in corpus docs, then human-review each pair. Acceptance rate is 60-80% for clear docs. A 30-100 seed grows to 500-2000 reviewed records this way. Two-lane pipeline diagram: LLM Lane (Generate) flows Corpus Doc -> LLM Generates Q-A Pair -> Pair Candidate. Bridge arrow down. Human Lane (Review) flows Pair Candidate -> Reviewer Accept/Edit/Reject -> Golden Record. Caution: never deploy without human review - the LLM can encode its own bias, generating good test cases for itself while failing on adjacent phrasings. Acceptance rate matters; so does reviewer diversity.
summary: Two-lane pipeline where an LLM generates candidate Q-A pairs grounded in corpus docs and a human reviewer accepts, edits, or rejects each, scaling 30-100 to 500-2000 records.
queries: synthetic eval data with human review

human in the loop eval generation

growing eval set without losing quality

text: Monthly review cadence: archive obsolete, add new. Like code, golden sets rot. Monthly review keeps the set defensible: spot-check 20 cases, archive anything tied to deleted docs or changed product behavior, add new cases captured from the past month's production incidents. Each month: spot-check 20 random cases, archive obsolete to /archived/ (audit-kept), add new from production. Quarterly audit. Re-run regression to confirm green. Three health metrics track set quality: Coverage = query types covered / total query types; Freshness = median age of golden cases in months; Pass-Rate = % of golden set currently green on production pipeline.
summary: Monthly cadence spot-checks 20 cases, archives obsolete, adds new; coverage, freshness, and pass-rate measure set health.

how to know golden set is stale

text: Shadow-eval pipeline diagram. A user query splits into an old pipeline whose answer is served and a new pipeline whose answer is logged but not served. Both answers feed into an LLM-judge that scores them, producing an aggregate comparison without exposing users to the candidate pipeline.
summary: Diagram showing shadow eval: query forks to old (served) and new (logged) pipelines, both judged by an LLM rubric for safe comparison.

text: Closing-the-loop cycle diagram with four nodes connected by arrows: online finds a regression, capture failing cases into the golden regression set, offline reproduces and fixes, shadow plus A/B verify in production. The cycle returns to online detection, illustrating the offline-online feedback loop that production-grade RAG eval relies on.

text: Online eval and A/B testing complement offline eval. Offline gates merges; online detects regressions after deploy. Implicit signals (dwell time, copy-paste rate, follow-up rephrase, thumbs) form a noisy composite that tracks satisfaction. Explicit feedback (rating widget 2-8% capture, free-text) is high-signal low-volume - use for diagnosis with PII redaction under GDPR/CCPA. Shadow eval runs a candidate pipeline alongside production at zero user risk, judged by an LLM rubric. A/B testing splits 50/50, samples ~200 queries/day/bucket, runs rubric judge, hits p<0.05 in 7-14 days at effect size 0.1; guardrails auto-rollback on faithfulness drop or refusal spike. The whole system closes a loop: online regression -> golden regression set -> offline fix -> shadow + A/B verify -> online.
summary: Chapter 12.35 covers the online eval toolkit: implicit signals, explicit feedback, shadow eval, A/B testing, and the regression feedback loop that ties online to offline.

text: Offline eval vs online eval. Offline eval is controlled and reproducible across runs, allows fast iteration on a fixed golden set, but does not reflect the production query distribution and misses rare-but-bad cases real users hit. Online eval uses the real query distribution from real users and catches edge cases offline never sees, but the signal is noisy and needs aggregation over many sessions, plus there are privacy concerns when storing user feedback. You need both: offline gates merges before deploy; online catches regressions after deploy and feeds them back into the golden regression set.
summary: Offline eval and online eval are complementary - offline gates merges with reproducible golden-set scores while online catches real-world regressions and feeds them back.

two halves of RAG evaluation system

text: Implicit signals are behavioral traces from session telemetry that users emit without clicking anything. Four key signals: Thumbs up/down (explicit-light, most users don't click); Dwell time (long dwell = read carefully, very short = likely irrelevant); Copy-paste rate (user copied the answer = useful enough to reuse); Follow-up rephrase (user re-asks the same intent with different words = previous answer failed). Each signal alone is too noisy; combining three or more makes a reliable composite quality regression detector. Calibrate composite against explicit feedback.
summary: Implicit signals like dwell time, copy-paste rate, and follow-up rephrasing combine into a reliable composite quality detector for online eval.
