text: Explicit feedback comes in two forms: Rating widgets (post-answer thumbs / star rating / did-this-help yes/no) with capture rate 2-8% of sessions; and free-text feedback ("tell us what went wrong") which has higher signal but much lower volume and is the single best source of qualitative failure modes. Explicit feedback is highest signal per sample and lowest volume - use it for diagnosis, not as primary quality measurement alone. Privacy: redact PII (names, emails, account numbers) before retention; follow GDPR and CCPA; document the retention period. Store feedback with conversation context.
summary: Explicit feedback widgets and free-text comments give high signal but low volume; treat them as diagnostic and redact PII per GDPR/CCPA before storing.

text: Shadow eval runs a new candidate pipeline alongside the production one without serving its answers. Diagram: a user query splits into the old pipeline (served to user) and the new pipeline (logged, not served). Both answers feed into an LLM-judge rubric that scores them, producing an aggregate apples-to-apples comparison on real production traffic at zero user risk. Example week-1 shadow result: judge prefers new in 62% of cases, ties in 23%, regresses in 15%. This is the signal that graduates the new pipeline to A/B.
summary: Shadow eval routes real production traffic through both old and new pipelines, only serving the old, while an LLM-judge compares both at zero user risk.

text: A/B testing with rubric judging. Four steps: (1) Bucket users 50/50 into control and treatment; (2) Sample 200 queries per day per bucket; (3) Run LLM-judge rubric on every sampled (query, answer); (4) Compute mean rubric score per bucket and check significance. Statistical significance: at N=200/day per bucket and effect size 0.1 on a 1-5 scale, expect 7-14 days to reach p < 0.05. Do not peek; pre-register the analysis window. Guardrails and auto-rollback: monitor faithfulness drop and refusal-rate spike at high frequency; auto-rollback when any guardrail breaches threshold; don't wait for the analysis window.
summary: A/B tests split traffic 50/50, sample 200 queries per bucket per day, judge with the same rubric, reach significance in 7-14 days, and auto-rollback on guardrail breaches.
queries: how to A/B test RAG pipelines
200 queries per day per bucket sample size

text: Closing the loop: online -> offline -> online. Production-grade RAG eval is a feedback cycle. Four nodes around a cycle: (1) Online finds a regression (thumbs-down spike on a query type); (2) Capture to golden regression set (ties failing case into golden datasets from chapter 12.34); (3) Offline reproduces and iterates a fix (RAGAS plus judge re-score the offending case until green); (4) Shadow plus A/B verify the fix in production (promote only after both confirm no regression). Cycle returns to online detection. Offline gives reproducibility; online gives reality. The loop turns every production regression into a future regression-set entry so the same bug cannot ship twice.
summary: The eval feedback loop turns every online regression into a captured golden case, fixes it offline, verifies in shadow plus A/B, then returns to online monitoring.

Incoming query 'Reset my password?' -> embed query (1024-dim vector) -> cosine search in cache store comparing vs all cached queries -> diamond decision 'Score >= 0.93?' threshold -> YES branch returns cached answer (cost $0, no LLM call) -> NO branch runs full RAG and stores answer (cost full stack).

text: Horizontal bar chart comparing per-query cost across four cache configurations: no cache $0.0173 (0% saved); prompt cache only at 80% hit rate $0.0044 (74% saved); semantic cache only at 30% hit rate $0.0121 (30% saved); both stacked $0.003 (83% saved). Stacked configuration is the production target and delivers the lowest cost.
summary: Bar chart of per-query cost across four cache configurations showing stacked prompt+semantic cache delivers the lowest cost at $0.003 (83% saved).

text: Caching in RAG: prompt cache stores the static system+retrieved-doc prefix at a 90% discount with byte-exact match (works for stable prefixes, fails when docs change per query). Semantic cache stores (query, answer) pairs and serves hits when cosine similarity to a cached query is at or above a threshold like 0.93. Operations: LRU+TTL eviction, doc-ID tagged invalidation, high thresholds and held-out monitoring to control false hits. Stacked together: 83% per-query cost savings vs no cache. Production RAG systems that skip caching leave 50%+ of cost on the table.
summary: Prompt cache discounts a stable prefix and semantic cache reuses similar prior answers; stacked they cut RAG per-query cost by ~83%.
queries: summary of prompt and semantic cache
how to cut RAG cost with caching
what is the safety knob semantic cache

text: Why cache in RAG. Every production RAG query burns four cost lines: embedding ($0.0001), retrieval ($0.0002), LLM input for 4k tokens ($0.012), LLM output for 500 tokens ($0.005). Total per query = $0.0173. At 1k QPS over a day this stacks to ~$1,500/day. Caching cuts the bill 50-95% on repeated traffic.
summary: Production RAG queries cost ~$0.0173 each across four cost lines and scale to $1,500/day at 1k QPS, motivating caching.
queries: how much does a RAG query cost

LLM output token cost in RAG
do I need caching in RAG

text: Prompt cache: cache the static prefix. Provider-side prompt caching (Anthropic-style) keeps the system prompt and retrieved-context prefix on the inference server. Subsequent calls reuse it at a 90% discount as long as the prefix bytes match exactly. TTL ~5 minutes. The artifact has two regions: cached prefix (system prompt + retrieved docs) at 90% discount, and fresh suffix (user question + assistant) at full price. Cost comparison: full price no cache, 4000 input tokens at $3/1M = $0.012; with prompt cache, 3800 cached at $0.30/1M + 200 fresh at $3/1M = $0.00174 = 85% saved.
summary: Prompt cache stores the static system+retrieved-doc prefix on the inference server at a 90% discount with byte-exact match, saving ~85% on input tokens.
queries: how long does prompt cache last

text: When prompt cache applies. Prompt cache only wins when many requests share a long prefix. Cache HITS: support agent looping on a stable doc set; multi-turn conversation with same retrieved docs; RAG pipeline answering many queries with a stable system prompt. Cache MISSES: docs change per query (no shared prefix); query-time chunking (different chunks every time); cold-start every conversation.
summary: Prompt cache hits when many requests share a long stable prefix and misses when retrieved context or chunks change per query.
queries: does prompt cache work for changing docs
why is my prompt cache not hitting
when not to use prompt cache

text: Semantic cache: cache by query similarity. A semantic cache stores past (query, answer) pairs and serves a hit when an incoming query is similar enough to one already cached. Similarity is cosine on the query embedding; threshold is the safety knob (e.g. 0.93). Flow: incoming query -> embed query to 1024-dim vector -> cosine search in cache store vs all cached queries -> if score >= 0.93 return cached answer ($0 cost, no LLM call) -> else run full RAG, store answer (full cost). Example cosine scores vs cached query 'How do I reset my password?': 'How can I reset my password?' score 0.97 HIT; 'Why is my dashboard slow?' score 0.34 MISS; 'How do I change my password?' score 0.88 BORDERLINE.
summary: Semantic cache embeds incoming queries and serves cached answers when cosine similarity to a cached query crosses the threshold, skipping the LLM call.

text: Semantic cache operations: eviction, invalidation, false-hit risk. Eviction: LRU + TTL. Cache fills up; evict oldest unused entries; TTL forces refresh of any answer older than e.g. 24 hours. Invalidation: when the corpus updates (a doc changes), every cached answer that touched that doc is stale. Strategy: tag cache entries with doc-IDs and invalidate on doc update. False-hit risk: two queries with cosine 0.95 can still want different answers. Example: 'Cancel my subscription' vs 'Cancel my trial' - similar embedding, very different policy. Tune threshold high (0.95+) for safety; monitor false-hit rate on a held-out set.
summary: Semantic cache needs LRU+TTL eviction, doc-ID tagged invalidation on corpus updates, and high thresholds plus held-out monitoring to limit false hits.
queries: cancel subscription vs cancel trial cache
tag cache entries with doc id

text: Combined impact: prompt cache + semantic cache compound. Per-query cost for the same RAG pipeline under four cache configurations: no cache $0.0173 (0% saved); prompt cache only at 80% hit rate $0.0044 (74% saved); semantic cache only at 30% hit rate $0.0121 (30% saved); both stacked $0.003 (83% saved). Stacked $0.003/query = 83% savings, compounds prompt cache with semantic cache. Apply both. Tune thresholds. Monitor false-hit rate. Most production RAG systems leave 50%+ of cost on the table by skipping caching.
summary: Stacking prompt cache (80% hit) with semantic cache (30% hit) drops per-query cost from $0.0173 to $0.003 - 83% savings.

no cache vs both caches cost
do prompt and semantic cache add

text: Chapter 12.37 Cost Models teaches the per-RAG-query bill as five cost lines (LLM output, LLM input, reranker, vector search, embedding) totalling $0.0208, dominated ~94% by LLM tokens. At 1k QPS this scales to ~$539k/day or $16.2M/month. Five compounding levers cut cost: prompt cache, semantic cache, fewer chunks (top-k reduction), smaller LLM (Sonnet to Haiku), smaller embedding (Matryoshka). Stacking all five drops cost to $0.0012/query (94% reduction). A cost vs quality Pareto frontier shows which configs are optimal; never pick dominated configs.

text: A production RAG query bill is the sum of five cost lines, walked in order of typical magnitude. LLM output tokens: ~$15 per 1M tokens, about $0.0075 per query (500 output tokens, Claude Sonnet typical). LLM input tokens: ~$3 per 1M tokens, about $0.012 per query (4000 input tokens for system + retrieved + question). Reranker: ~$0.001 per query (cross-encoder, optional). Vector search: ~$0.0002 per query (operator cost, managed service). Embedding: ~$0.0001 per query (1024-dim, modern provider). Total per query = $0.0208. The biggest cost driver - LLM tokens - dominates and is visible first.

text: Five real levers to cut RAG cost, in order of compounding return. Smaller embedding: 1024 -> 384 dim with Matryoshka, -60% storage, no quality drop, small impact (embedding <1% of bill). Fewer chunks retrieved: top-20 -> top-5 to the LLM, -75% input tokens, ~44% of total bill. Smaller LLM: Sonnet -> Haiku, ~10x cheaper per token, ~80% quality on most RAG tasks, ~80% of LLM bill. Prompt cache: 80% prefix hit rate, prefix tokens at 90% discount, ~74% on cached prefix tokens. Semantic cache: 30% hit rate on hot queries, skip LLM entirely on hits, ~30% on affected queries. Apply in order: cache first (free quality), reduce chunks (free quality), then downsize LLM (quality tradeoff).
summary: Five levers cut RAG cost: smaller embedding, fewer chunks, smaller LLM, prompt cache, semantic cache - apply caches first since they are free quality.
queries: How do I reduce RAG cost?

text: Worked example stacking all five levers in sequence as a downward waterfall. Start - full price $0.0208. Apply prompt cache (80% hit): $0.0061, -71% on input tokens. Apply semantic cache (30% hit): $0.0043, -30% blended. Reduce top-k from 20 to 5: $0.0028, -35% remaining input. Switch LLM to Haiku: $0.0012, -57% on LLM bill. Final per-query cost $0.0012 = 94% reduction. At 1k QPS this saves about $1.69M per day versus no levers applied.
summary: Stacking five cost levers in sequence drops per-query cost from $0.0208 to $0.0012 (94% reduction, $1.69M/day saved at 1k QPS).

text: Chapter 12.38 teaches why and how to instrument production RAG with traces. Without per-stage instrumentation, four failure modes are undiagnosable: mystery slowness, silent quality drops, cost blowups, and hallucination hunts. A canonical RAG trace has one root span and five children (embed query, vector search, rerank, pack prompt, generate), each carrying attributes like model versions, doc-IDs, scores, token counts, and per-stage latency. Tool options include LangSmith, Helicone, OpenTelemetry, Arize Phoenix, and native provider tooling. Privacy demands hashing raw query text and logging doc-IDs not chunk text to satisfy GDPR/HIPAA/SOC2. A production dashboard surfaces P50/P99 latency, faithfulness, cost-per-query, the slowest trace, and signal-level facts like model version.
summary: Production RAG observability: span trees per query, per-stage attributes, tool landscape, privacy rules, and dashboard design.

text: Why trace RAG in production: a RAG system is opaque without traces. Four failure modes recur and each needs per-stage instrumentation to diagnose. (1) Mystery Slowness: user reports the system is slow; without per-stage latency you cannot tell whether retrieval or the LLM is the bottleneck. (2) Silent Quality Drop: faithfulness drops 5% week-over-week; no doc-IDs logged means you cannot see which docs were retrieved. (3) Cost Blowup: bill doubles; no per-query token count means you cannot tell which queries pulled in too much context. (4) Hallucination Hunt: user flags a bad answer; no model-version logged so you cannot reproduce the failure.
summary: Four production failure modes that prove RAG must be traced per stage: mystery slowness, silent quality drop, cost blowup, hallucination hunt.

text: The canonical RAG trace span tree: a single user query produces one root span (Query, total 180ms) and five child spans rendered with widths proportional to latency. Embed Query 12ms (model = bge-large-v1.5, dim = 1024). Vector Search 28ms (top-K = 20, index = hnsw, metric = cosine). Rerank 45ms (model = bge-reranker-v2, input = 20, output = 5). Pack Prompt 3ms (chunks packed = 5, total tokens = 4128). Generate 92ms (model = Sonnet 4.7, out tokens = 480, finish = stop). Rerank dominates wall time after Generate; optimization targets reducing input top-k or picking a faster reranker.
summary: Canonical RAG span tree with five child spans whose widths are proportional to per-stage latency, exposing the bottleneck visually.

text: Per-stage attributes to log on every RAG trace. Embed: query_text (hashed), embedding_model_version, dim, latency_ms. Vector Search: top_k_requested, index_name, distance_metric, retrieved_doc_ids, retrieval_scores, latency_ms. Rerank: reranker_model_version, input_count, output_count, reranker_scores, latency_ms. Pack: chunk_count_packed, total_prompt_tokens, citation_count, latency_ms. Generate: llm_model_version, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, finish_reason, latency_ms. Variable identifiers stay lowercase per code convention; column headers stay title-case. This is the minimum every production RAG trace MUST emit.
summary: Minimum attribute set every span must carry across embed, vector search, rerank, pack, and generate stages.

text: Tools that capture RAG traces in 2025. LangSmith: LangChain's official tracer; strong for LangChain users; tight integration with LangChain stack. Helicone: provider-agnostic; logs every LLM call with cost / latency dashboards; lightweight setup. OpenTelemetry: open standard; vendor-neutral spans; export to Datadog / Grafana / Jaeger / custom. Arize Phoenix: open-source; built for LLM eval + trace; strong dashboard for retrieval quality. Native Provider Tooling: Anthropic Console, OpenAI Dashboard; limited but free; first-party cost tracking. Choices span framework-native to vendor-neutral.
summary: Five tool options for capturing RAG traces, from framework-native LangSmith to vendor-neutral OpenTelemetry.
queries: framework native vs vendor neutral tracing

text: Privacy: what NOT to log in plain text. Regulations (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2) and contracts may forbid storing user query content. Use one-way hashes for queries; log doc-IDs not chunk text; redact PII upstream. Log freely: doc-IDs, retrieval scores, latency per stage, model version, token counts, embedding-model version, query length, anonymized user ID, response status code. Do NOT log in plain text: raw query text (hash it), raw retrieved chunks (log only IDs), PII inside the query, user account IDs (use opaque session IDs), API keys, prompt template contents that include secrets.
summary: Privacy rules for RAG telemetry: hash queries, log doc-IDs not chunks, redact PII, and never log secrets or account IDs in plain text.
queries: what not to log in RAG

text: A production RAG trace dashboard surfaces four headline tiles plus the most recent slow trace and three signal-level facts. Tiles: P50 Latency 180ms, P99 Latency 620ms, Faithfulness 0.91, Cost Per Query $0.012. Slowest trace last hour: 620ms with the span tree thumbnail showing each stage's contribution. Footer facts: Slowest Stage = Rerank (45ms P50), Top Retrieved Doc = Doc-7 (Password Reset), Model Version = Sonnet 4.7. A trace is the single source of truth when a user complains - every stage, every score, every model version.

text: Chapter 12.39 covers hallucination detection and drift monitoring in production RAG. It introduces four production hallucination signals (faithfulness score, citation coverage, refusal rate, out-of-index mentions), four drift types (data, embedding, eval, distribution drift), a 5-step claim-level hallucination detection pipeline that emits faithfulness scores to traces, drift detection via time-series charts with alert thresholds, what an actionable alert payload must contain (failing queries, scores, recommended action), and a unified ops dashboard combining faithfulness trend, refusal rate trend, drift status, and top hallucinated queries.
summary: How to detect hallucinations and four kinds of drift in production RAG using signals, pipelines, time-series alerts, and a unified dashboard.

text:
Hallucination Signals In Production. Four signals catch most hallucinations without manual review. Wire each into the trace; alert on rolling medians; investigate any spike inside the same shift.

1. Faithfulness Score: per-generation faithfulness from LLM-as-judge (12.32). Alert if rolling 7-day median drops below 0.85.
2. Citation Coverage: percent of claims with a [doc-X] citation. Alert if drops below 80%.
3. Refusal Rate: percent of queries refused with 'I don't know'. Alert on spike (too cautious) or dip (hallucinating when context was sufficient).
4. Out-Of-Index Mentions: regex or LLM check for entities mentioned that do not appear in any retrieved doc. Hard signal of hallucination.
summary: Four production signals catch most hallucinations: faithfulness score, citation coverage, refusal rate, and out-of-index entity mentions.
queries: what signals indicate hallucination in production RAG

alert thresholds for RAG quality signals

RAG hallucination automatic detection without humans
what is a good faithfulness alert threshold
dip in refusal rate hallucination cause

text:
Four Drift Types. Drift is the slow leak before the flood. Four kinds of drift afflict production RAG. Each has a different detection mechanism and a different fix.

- Data Drift: the corpus changes (new docs added, old docs removed). The embedding index becomes stale relative to the current corpus. Detect: re-index timestamp vs current doc set checksum.
- Embedding Drift: you re-embed with a new model. Old vectors and new vectors are not directly comparable. Mixed-version retrievals pollute top-K. Detect: log embedding_model_version per query; alert on mixed-version retrievals.
- Eval Drift: the LLM-as-judge model is upgraded. Faithfulness scores from old judge vs new judge are not directly comparable. Detect: lock judge version; rerun calibration on judge change.
- Distribution Drift: user query distribution shifts (new product launched, new failure mode). Old query embeddings cluster differently. Detect: cluster monitoring on query embeddings over time.
summary: Four drift types - data, embedding, eval, and distribution drift - each require a distinct detection mechanism in production RAG.
queries: what are the types of drift in RAG
how to detect stale embedding index
mixing old and new embedding vectors
why faithfulness scores change when judge model upgrades
lock judge version for eval consistency
slow leak before the flood drift

text:
Hallucination Detection Pipeline. A 5-step pipeline that runs per-generation. The LLM extracts atomic claims, the judge labels each as supported or unsupported, and a rolling-median alert fires below threshold.

Input: user query + retrieved chunks + generated answer.
Step 1: Extract Claims - LLM segments answer into atomic claims.
Step 2: Check Each Claim Against Retrieved Chunks - LLM-as-judge: is claim supported?
Step 3: Compute Faithfulness Score - supported_claims / total_claims.
Step 4: Log Score + Doc-IDs + Unsupported Claims - emit to trace.
Step 5: Alert If Rolling Median < 0.85 - page owner on drop.

Worked: 5 claims, 4 supported, 1 hallucinated -> Faithfulness = 4/5 = 0.80 -> below 0.85 threshold, ALERT.
summary: A 5-step per-generation pipeline extracts claims, checks each against retrieved chunks, computes faithfulness, logs to trace, and alerts when rolling median falls below 0.85.

text:
Drift Detection: Metrics Over Time. Three drift signals over 30 days. Each crosses its alert threshold on a different day. Without the chart you would have noticed the symptoms - a flood of unhappy users - 2-3 weeks late.

Faithfulness: series 0.92, 0.91, 0.91, 0.90, 0.90, 0.89, 0.88, 0.87, 0.86, 0.85, 0.84. Threshold 0.85. Alert day 28.
Recall On Golden Set: series 0.88, 0.87, 0.86, 0.85, 0.84, 0.83, 0.82, 0.81, 0.80, 0.80, 0.79. Threshold 0.82. Alert day 22.
Refusal Rate (%): series 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, 18. Threshold 15%. Alert day 25.

Three different metrics. Three different alert days. Each catches a different failure mode early.

text:
What An Alert Should Carry. An alert without examples is a blank page at 3am. Always include the failing queries, the failing scores, and a hint at the recommended action so the on-call engineer can start fixing immediately.

Alert payload example:
ALERT: rag.faithfulness.drop
Severity: WARNING
Trigger: 7-Day Median Faithfulness Dropped Below 0.85 (Currently 0.82)
First Crossed: 2026-05-14 03:21 UTC
Affected: Production - Customer Support Pipeline

Recent Examples (Last 24h):
  - Query: 'How Do I Reset My Password If I Forgot My Email?' Faithfulness: 0.60 | Cited Docs: doc-1, doc-3. Unsupported Claim: 'Support Team Will Email A Reset Link.'
  - Query: 'Cancel My Subscription And Get A Refund' Faithfulness: 0.50 | Cited Docs: doc-4. Unsupported Claim: 'Refunds Are Issued Within 24 Hours.'

Recommended Action: Review Recent Corpus Updates And Re-Run Golden Eval.
summary: An actionable alert payload must include severity, trigger threshold, failing queries with scores, unsupported claims, and a recommended action.

text: Chapter 12.40 walks through how to pick a RAG framework. Six options: No Framework, LlamaIndex, LangChain, LangGraph, Haystack, Vendor SDK. A decision matrix scores them on lock-in risk, API churn, build/hidden complexity, community size, and RAG fit. An honest LangChain take lists pros (huge ecosystem, fast prototyping) and cons (hidden abstractions, deprecations, API churn, lock-in). A fit table maps each framework to its ideal team and product profile. A three-question decision tree (Is RAG the primary feature? Mid-sized team? Multi-step agent? Explicit state? Single-provider OK?) lands on one of the six options. Finally, the framework-agnostic core: chunking, embedding choice, hybrid + reranker, prompt template, eval, and tracing are all decisions that survive any framework swap - aim for production RAG thin enough that switching frameworks is a 2-day job.

text: Six common framework options for building RAG in 2025: (1) No Framework - raw SDK (Anthropic / OpenAI / Cohere) plus simple Python or TypeScript orchestration; most popular for new production RAG in 2025. (2) LlamaIndex - RAG-focused framework with good retrieval defaults and less abstraction than LangChain. (3) LangChain - the original; large ecosystem with abstractions still evolving and some deprecated within the framework itself. (4) LangGraph - graph-based agent orchestration from the LangChain team; newer, explicit state machine. (5) Haystack - enterprise-leaning modular pipelines with a smaller community than LangChain. (6) Vendor SDK - OpenAI Agents, Anthropic native tool-use; tight integration with vendor lock-in. Brand-name spellings (LlamaIndex, LangChain, LangGraph) are official capitalizations, not title-case violations.
summary: Lists six framework options for production RAG in 2025 with one-line characterizations of each.

text: Honest take on LangChain. Pros: largest ecosystem; biggest community; tutorials everywhere; prototype in 30 minutes; integrations for every vector DB, embedding model, and LLM. Cons: abstractions hide what is actually happening (lost-in-middle invisible until you trace); some abstractions deprecated within the framework itself (Stuff / MapReduce / Refine, original AgentExecutor); fast-moving API churn requires upgrade cycles; framework lock-in concerns once production code depends on chains. LangChain is still actively developed and dominant for prototyping. For production-grade RAG, many teams move to raw SDK + LlamaIndex retriever, or stay on LangChain but carefully avoid deprecating abstractions.
summary: Weighs LangChain's pros (ecosystem, prototyping speed) against its cons (hidden abstractions, deprecations, API churn, lock-in).

text: When each framework fits. No Framework: small team, full control needed, production-grade RAG with custom orchestration; fits 2-3 engineers building a single product. LlamaIndex: RAG-heavy product, want good retrieval defaults, accept some framework dependency; fits teams shipping a RAG-centric app. LangChain: early prototyping, large team needs shared vocabulary, ecosystem integrations matter more than minimalism; fits teams that staff for framework upgrades. LangGraph: explicit agent state machine, multi-step workflows, less hidden magic than LangChain; fits teams building structured agents. Haystack: enterprise / regulated environment, modular pipelines, smaller-community tradeoff is acceptable; fits orgs with security review on every dependency. Vendor SDK: committed to one provider, lock-in is acceptable, tight integration is the priority; fits internal Anthropic / OpenAI shops. Use as a first-pass filter before opening any framework's documentation.
summary: Maps each of the six frameworks to the team profile and product profile it best fits.

NO (not a multi-step agent) -> Single-provider OK? YES -> Vendor SDK. NO -> No Framework. Three questions, six leaves, one choice. text: What stays the same regardless of framework. Every framework wraps the same six core decisions. Get the decisions right and the framework is replaceable. Production RAG code should be thin enough that a framework swap is a 2-day job, not a 2-month rewrite. The framework-agnostic decisions: (1) chunking strategy (12.7 - 12.13) is a data-pipeline decision, not a framework decision; (2) embedding model choice (12.14) is a data-pipeline decision; (3) hybrid + reranker cascade (12.16, 12.17) is a retrieval-quality decision; (4) prompt template + context packing (12.22) is a generation decision; (5) eval (12.31 - 12.35) is a measurement decision; (6) tracing (12.38) is an ops decision. Frameworks are wrappers; the decisions in 12.7-12.35 are agnostic to which one you pick.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Capstone Use Case: Q&A Over Case Law. Customer: a legal research firm. They need a RAG system for searching across published court cases. A fully-different domain from the customer support corpus. Design Brief: Corpus = 200,000 published court cases across 50 US jurisdictions. Metadata = jurisdiction tag, year, citation tree. Quality Bar = partner-lawyer rating >= 4 / 5 on legal research rubric. Latency Budget = 5-10 seconds (legal research is patient). Cost Budget = $0.05 / query. Audit Requirements = every answer cited, jurisdiction filter mandatory. Example Queries: 'What's the precedent for tortious interference in California state court?', 'Cases citing Smith v Jones about negligence in medical contexts', 'How has the standard for qualified immunity evolved 2010-2025?'. The framework will be walked decision by decision for this brief.
summary: Introduces the capstone legal case-law Q&A use case with corpus size, latency, cost, and audit constraints.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 1: Chunking Strategy (Chapters 12.8, 12.9). Choice: Hierarchical Chunking (12.8) + Contextual Retrieval (12.9) - per-paragraph chunks with parent (case-level) metadata and an LLM-injected 'this paragraph is from case X about Y' preamble. Why: case opinions have natural hierarchy (intro, facts, reasoning, holding). Hierarchical preserves parent-child relationships. Contextual Retrieval injects the per-chunk preamble that disambiguates citation-level retrieval. Plain fixed-size chunking would mid-sentence-split a court's analysis. Tradeoff: more storage (each chunk now has parent metadata). Higher index build cost (contextual augmentation is an LLM call per chunk). Worth it because case-law precision demands citation-level retrieval.
summary: Picks hierarchical + contextual chunking for case law and explains why fixed-size fails.

why fixed-size chunking fails for case opinions

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 2: Embedding + Index (Chapters 12.15, 12.16, 12.17). Choice: domain-adapted embedding (12.15) on top of a base Legal-BERT or Cohere Embed Multilingual. HNSW index with hybrid (BM25 + dense, 12.16). Jurisdiction stored as filter metadata. Cross-encoder cascade rerank (12.17). Why: general-purpose embeddings underweight legal jargon ('tortious', 'qualified immunity'). Hybrid catches both exact-citation lookups ('Smith v Jones') and semantic queries ('medical negligence cases'). Jurisdiction filter eliminates 96% of irrelevant cases before reranking. Cross-encoder lifts precision on legal-language matching. Tradeoff: domain-adapt requires labeled training pairs (expensive but one-time). Cross-encoder adds 50-100ms per query. Latency budget allows it.
summary: Selects domain-adapted Legal-BERT embedding, HNSW hybrid BM25+dense index, jurisdiction filter, and cross-encoder rerank.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 3: Query Transformation (Chapters 12.18, 12.19, 12.20, 12.21). Choice: Multi-Query Expansion (12.20) + Decomposition for complex multi-hop queries (12.21). HyDE (12.19) SKIPPED. Why: a query like 'Cases citing X about Y' naturally decomposes into 'find X' -> 'find cases citing X' -> 'filter for cases about Y'. Multi-query rewriting (legal synonyms: 'negligence' / 'duty of care' / 'breach of duty') improves recall on lexically-narrow legal language. HyDE skipped because case law is precise; fake hypothetical documents hurt more than help. Tradeoff: more retrievals per query (3-5x). Cost goes up linearly. Mitigated by aggressive deduplication after RRF merge.
summary: Uses multi-query expansion and decomposition; skips HyDE because precise legal text doesn't benefit from fake docs.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
summary: Packs 16-30k tokens with sandwich ordering, mandates inline [Case Name, Year] citations and an I-don't-know refusal.
queries: how to fight lost in middle

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 5: Advanced Retrieval Patterns (Chapters 12.25, 12.27, 12.28, 12.30). Choice: GraphRAG (12.28) for the case-citation graph (cases citing X / cases cited by X). Multi-hop retrieval (12.25) for 'cases citing X about Y' decomposition. Long-context (12.30) SKIPPED. Why: legal citation is a graph; GraphRAG indexes the relationships. Without it, 'cases citing X' requires querying every case in the corpus. With it, follow the citation edges. Multi-hop chains the steps for complex precedent queries. Long-context skipped because 200k cases is bigger than any context window so RAG is mandatory. Tradeoff: GraphRAG indexing is 10x slower than vector-only. Re-indexing the citation graph on corpus update is a nightly batch. Multi-hop is slower per query but precision gain on multi-hop queries is large.
summary: Adds GraphRAG for citation edges and multi-hop retrieval, skips long-context because corpus exceeds any window.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 6: Evaluation (Chapters 12.32, 12.33, 12.34, 12.35). Choice: LLM-as-Judge (12.32) with a legal-specific rubric. Golden Dataset (12.34) of 500 partner-curated query/expected-answer pairs. RAGAS metrics (12.33): faithfulness, citation precision, jurisdiction match. Online A/B (12.35) on partner-rated 4/5 score. Why: general-purpose RAG eval misses legal correctness. Partner-curated golden set is the ground truth because no automated metric reaches partner quality. RAGAS faithfulness catches hallucinations. Citation precision (% of cited cases that are real and on-point) is critical. Tradeoff: golden dataset is expensive to build (partner hours). 500 examples covers ~80% of query distribution; remaining 20% caught by online feedback. Rerun golden eval weekly on prod.
summary: Combines LLM-as-judge with legal rubric, 500-pair partner golden set, RAGAS faithfulness, and online A/B on 4/5 score.
queries: how big should golden dataset be

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 7: Production Operations (Chapters 12.36, 12.38, 12.39). Choice: Semantic cache DISABLED. Prompt cache ENABLED for system prompt + few-shot examples (12.36). Full tracing per query (12.38). Hallucination detection with legal-fact-check post-process (12.39). Drift monitoring on case-law updates. Why: semantic cache fails when 'negligence in CA' and 'negligence in NY' cosine to 0.96 - the answers differ entirely (false-hit risk too high). Prompt cache safe because system + few-shot are constant. Tracing every query is non-negotiable for audit. Tradeoff: no semantic cache = higher per-query cost, mitigated by prompt cache. Drift monitoring catches when judicial reasoning shifts (rare but high-impact).
summary: Disables semantic cache (false-hit risk), keeps prompt cache, traces every query, adds legal fact-check and drift monitoring.
queries: negligence CA vs NY cache collision

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Putting It All Together: The Capstone Stack. Five layers. (1) Ingest: hierarchical + contextual chunking; domain-adapted embedding; HNSW + BM25 hybrid index; jurisdiction metadata; case-citation graph index. (2) Retrieve: multi-query expansion; jurisdiction filter; hybrid retrieval; cross-encoder rerank; GraphRAG for citation queries. (3) Generate: 16-30k token budget; sandwich ordering; mandatory citations; I-don't-know refusal if no jurisdiction-matching cases. (4) Eval: partner-curated golden set (500 pairs); LLM-as-judge with legal rubric; RAGAS faithfulness; online A/B. (5) Ops: prompt cache (semantic cache disabled); full tracing per query; hallucination detection with legal-fact-check post-process; drift monitoring on case-law updates. Framework Choice (12.40): No Framework + LlamaIndex retriever for the RAG core + raw Anthropic SDK for generation. Cost ~$0.04 / query (under budget). Latency ~6 seconds. Every answer carries [Case Name, Year] citations. You now have everything to design production-grade RAG for any new domain. Apply this framework. Adjust per use case. Build.
summary: Final five-layer capstone stack (ingest, retrieve, generate, eval, ops) with framework choice and measured cost/latency.

text: Anatomy of an LLM Call: every call is an ordered list of role-tagged messages (system, user, assistant); the model processes tokens, not words, against a fixed context window (e.g. 128k or 200k); three sampling knobs - temperature, top-p, top-k - shape next-token selection; generation halts on one of four stop conditions (end_turn, length, stop_sequence, tool_use) exposed via finish_reason; the response object returns content, stop_reason, and usage (input_tokens + output_tokens). The full cycle is six steps: build messages, set parameters, send request, stream tokens, hit stop reason, deliver final response.
summary: Chapter-level overview of the LLM call: messages with roles, token-based context, sampling knobs, stop conditions, response shape, and the six-step lifecycle.

text: Every LLM call is a list of messages, not a chat. You send an ordered list of messages, each with a role; the model reads the whole list and writes the next assistant turn. Three roles: System sets the role, rules, and tone of the assistant (example: 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant. Be concise. Cite docs when possible.'). User is the end-user's turn, the thing the model must respond to (example: 'I can't log in - reset my password for alice@example.com.'). Assistant is the model's reply, which goes back into the messages list for the next turn (example: 'I'll help you reset it. I just sent a one-time link to alice@example.com.'). Every later request includes prior assistant replies so the model has the full conversation.
summary: An LLM call is an ordered messages list with three roles - system, user, assistant - and prior assistant replies are re-sent each turn.

text: The model doesn't see characters or words - it sees tokens, sub-word chunks from the tokenizer. Both input and output are billed and limited in tokens. Example user message 'I can't log in - reset my password' is 9 tokens: I, can, 't, _log, _in, _-, _reset, _my, _password. Example assistant reply 'I'll help you reset it.' is 7 tokens: I, 'll, _help, _you, _reset, _it, .. The context window is the total tokens across all messages: system + user + assistant history + new reply. A 200k context window holds roughly 150k English words. Cross the window and the oldest messages get dropped or the call fails.
summary: Models process tokens (sub-word chunks), not words, and the context window caps the total tokens across all messages.

text: Three sampling knobs shape how the model picks the next token from its probability distribution. Lower = safer and more repeatable, higher = more varied. Temperature controls sharpness of the distribution; common stops: 0.0 deterministic, 0.3 focused, 0.7 default, 1.0 creative, 2.0 wild. At T=0 the model always picks the most likely next token; at T=2 unlikely tokens get a real chance. Top-P (nucleus) keeps tokens until cumulative probability hits P. Example with top_p=0.9 on candidates: reset p=0.42 cum=0.42 kept; change p=0.21 cum=0.63 kept; update p=0.16 cum=0.79 kept; fix p=0.09 cum=0.88 kept; recover p=0.05 cum=0.93 cut; send p=0.04 cum=0.97 cut; create p=0.03 cum=1.00 cut - 4 tokens stay, 3 are cut. Top-K keeps the K most likely tokens. With top_k=5 the same list keeps reset, change, update, fix, recover and discards send, create before sampling.
summary: Temperature, top-p (nucleus), and top-k are sampling knobs that control how deterministic or varied the next-token choice is.

text: One LLM call doesn't 'just stop' - it stops for a specific reason and the response tells you which. Four stop conditions, four finish reasons. Max Tokens Reached (max_tokens: 1024) - hard ceiling on generated tokens, finish_reason: length. Stop Sequence Hit (stop: ['</answer>']) - model produced a configured stop string, useful for structured output, finish_reason: stop_sequence. End-Of-Turn Token (model decides on its own) - model emitted its special end-of-turn token, the natural end of a reply, finish_reason: end_turn. Tool Call Requested (only with tool use enabled) - model paused to ask for a tool, generation halts so your code can run the tool, finish_reason: tool_use. Always check finish_reason. A length stop means the answer got cut off mid-thought - retry with a higher max_tokens.
summary: Generation ends for one of four reasons - end_turn, length, stop_sequence, or tool_use - exposed via finish_reason.
queries: how to detect truncated LLM output

how do I know if response was cut off

text: The response is a small structured object. Three fields matter for almost every call: the generated text, why the model stopped, and the token usage you pay for. Response shape: { 'content': 'I'll help you reset it...', 'stop_reason': 'end_turn', 'usage': { 'input_tokens': 9, 'output_tokens': 7 } }. content is the generated assistant text - feed this back into the messages list for the next turn. stop_reason (some providers call this finish_reason, same idea: end_turn, length, stop_sequence, tool_use). usage = input_tokens + output_tokens - this is what gets billed and what counts against your context window.
summary: An LLM response object carries content (generated text), stop_reason (why it ended), and usage (input/output token counts for billing).

text: Every LLM call follows a six-step cycle. 1. Build Messages - combine system + history + new user turn into the messages list (roles assigned). 2. Set Parameters - pick temperature, top-p, top-k, max_tokens, stop (sampling locked). 3. Send Request - POST messages + params to the model endpoint (HTTPS POST). 4. Tokens Stream Back - model emits one token at a time over the wire (SSE chunks). 5. Stop Reason Reached - one of: end_turn, length, stop_sequence, tool_use (end_turn fired). 6. Final Response - content + stop_reason + usage delivered to your app. Typical latency: ~1.5s median for a 7-token response. Long replies stream tokens as they're generated. Messages in, parameters in, request out; tokens stream back until a stop reason fires; response object lands with content, stop_reason, and usage.
summary: Diagram of the full six-step LLM call cycle from building messages to receiving the final response with content, stop_reason, and usage.

text: Chapter 13.2 frames the system prompt as the agent's role contract - the constitution re-read by the model on every turn. It breaks the contract into four moving parts: persona (who I am, voice, role), capabilities (which tools I can call), constraints (the negative-space 'nevers'), and output rules (formatting, citations, follow-ups). Each part is illustrated with a customer-support assistant example (Alice's KB at docs.example.com, tools search_kb / lookup_customer / process_refund / escalate_human, $200 refund cap, escalate_human handoff). The chapter closes with the full ten-line system prompt that weaves all four parts together.

text: The system prompt is the role contract - the constitution every later message is interpreted under. The model re-reads it on every single turn, so anything not in it does not exist for the agent. Four moving parts: Persona (who am I? what is my voice and role?), Capabilities (what tools and data am I allowed to use?), Constraints (what must I never say, promise, or do?), Output Rules (how must my reply be formatted, cited, and structured?). Get all four right and the agent behaves predictably. Skip one and it goes off-script.

text: Persona answers 'who am I, what is my voice, and what is my role?' Vague before: 'You are a chatbot.' - no role, no voice, no domain, the model fills the gap with a generic helpful tone. Persona-specific after: 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant for the customer support knowledge base. You are friendly, concise, and you never invent policies.' Names the user (Alice), names the role (customer-support assistant), names the voice (friendly, concise), and one guardrail (no invented policies). Three rules of thumb: name the user, name the role, name one thing the assistant must never do.
summary: Persona names the user, role, voice, and one guardrail to avoid a generic chatbot tone.

text: Capabilities tell the model which tools are available, what each one does, and when to use it. Without this section, the model either refuses or hallucinates tool calls. Four tools in the example: search_kb (searches the customer support knowledge base at docs.example.com), lookup_customer (fetches the profile for a customer id like c-9924), process_refund (issues a refund up to a configured dollar limit), escalate_human (hands the conversation off to a human support agent). Declared in prompt as: 'Tools available to you: search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human.' The system prompt declares the tools; the API schema (chapter 13.8) actually wires them up. Both sides have to agree.
summary: Capabilities list tools and behaviors so the model neither refuses nor hallucinates calls.

text: Constraints are the negative-space rules. Every refund-gone-wrong, every leaked customer record, every invented policy traces back to a missing line here. Be explicit about the 'never's. Four constraints in the example: Never promise refunds over $200 without escalation. Never share another customer's data. Never claim policies not in the knowledge base. Never call tools without first looking up the customer. The $200 line and the escalation line live in the prompt, not in code. The model checks them on every reply, so a policy change is a one-line edit, not a deploy.
summary: Constraints are explicit nevers - the $200 refund cap and policy rules live in the prompt, not code.

text: Output rules shape the surface of every reply: tone, structure, citations, and what each turn must end with. They make the assistant feel consistent across thousands of conversations. Four output rules: 1) Always greet the customer by name. 2) Cite the knowledge base doc when quoting policy. 3) Wrap tool calls in <thinking> tags first. 4) End with a follow-up question or escalation offer. Citations and follow-up offers are not polish - they keep the agent honest and the conversation moving. Without them, replies dead-end and trust erodes.
summary: Output rules dictate greeting, citations, <thinking> wrapping, and ending with a follow-up.

text: Chapter 13.3 covers few-shot prompting and structured output for LLMs. Zero-shot describes the task in English; few-shot demonstrates it with 3-5 worked input-output pairs and reliably produces correctly-shaped output. JSON schemas with enums, required fields, and numeric ranges enforce structure that examples alone cannot guarantee. Together, few-shot teaches values and style while the schema locks structure - zero drift across calls. Production rules: 3-5 examples, diverse not duplicate, edge-case-heavy, span all enum values, hardest case last. The full ticket-classifier template (system + three examples + schema + ticket slot) is reusable, auditable, and tunable without retraining.
summary: Few-shot examples teach values and style; JSON schemas lock structure; together they produce drift-free classification.
queries: How to design a production LLM classifier
Best techniques for reliable LLM output

text: Zero-shot prompts describe the task. Few-shot prompts demonstrate the task with 3 to 5 worked examples first. The model is far better at pattern-matching examples than parsing English instructions. Zero-shot example: 'Classify this ticket as billing, product, or troubleshooting: "Charged twice for last month."' Output is unpredictable prose like 'This looks like a billing issue, possibly a duplicate charge...' - wrong shape. Few-shot example: three labeled pairs (Input: 'I can't log in.' -> billing, Input: 'Charged twice.' -> billing, Input: '500 errors.' -> troubleshooting) then the query. Output snaps to one token: 'billing'. Same model, same task, but examples raise classification accuracy from wobbly to reliable. Showing beats telling.
summary: Few-shot prompting beats zero-shot because the model pattern-matches examples better than it parses instructions.
queries: Why does few-shot work better than zero-shot
How to make LLM output a single label
Why does the model output prose instead of a label
How to constrain LLM output to one word

text: The anatomy of a few-shot block: a list of input-output pairs in identical format. The format is the lesson - the model learns the shape from these three, then applies it to the real query. Three examples, all identical format: Example 1 - Input: 'I can't log in - reset my password.' Output: Category: billing | Urgency: low. Example 2 - Input: 'Charged twice for last month.' Output: Category: billing | Urgency: high. Example 3 - Input: 'Dashboard shows 500 errors.' Output: Category: troubleshooting | Urgency: medium. Three categories (billing, product, troubleshooting) and three urgency levels (low, medium, high). Every example uses the same labels in the same slots. Consistency is the whole point.
summary: A few-shot block is an identically-formatted list of input-output pairs that teaches the model the response shape.

Why use the same format for all examples

Should examples follow the same format
How does the model learn the shape from examples

summary: A JSON schema constrains LLM output to a fixed shape via enums, required fields, and numeric ranges.
queries: How to force JSON output from an LLM
What does enum do in a schema

text: Few-shot and structured output do different jobs. Examples teach VALUES and STYLE. Schemas enforce STRUCTURE. Run them together and the model produces the same shape with the same vocabulary every single call. Few-shot teaches values + style: which categories exist, what urgency means here, how a typical ticket maps to a label. Schema enforces structure: field names, allowed enum values, types, and required keys; API rejects anything that doesn't match. Together: zero drift - same shape, consistent labels, every single call. Safe to feed straight into a database or downstream tool. Example output: { "category": "billing", "urgency": "high", "confidence": 0.85 }. Belt and suspenders - each technique catches a different failure mode, so together they leave almost nothing to chance.
summary: Combining few-shot examples with a JSON schema gives zero drift: examples teach the vocabulary, schema enforces the structure.
queries: What does few-shot teach vs schema

Examples teach values schema enforces structure

text: Five production rules for few-shot. Tip 1: Use 3 to 5 examples - more than 5 often hurts more than it helps, diminishing returns set in fast. Tip 2: Diverse examples beat similar ones - three near-duplicates teach the model one pattern, three different cases teach three. Tip 3: Edge cases improve robustness - a tricky example moves the needle more than a typical one, include the cases that break naive rules. Tip 4: Span all enum values - every category and urgency level should appear at least once across your examples. Tip 5: Put the hardest case last - the last example sets the strongest pattern, end on the case you most want the model to remember. Diverse, edge-case-heavy, enum-spanning, hardest-last. The ordering rule is the one teams forget most - and it matters more than the count of examples.
summary: Five production rules: 3-5 examples, diverse, edge-case-heavy, span all enums, put the hardest case last.
queries: How many few-shot examples should I use
Should examples cover all enum values
Why put the hardest example last
How does example order affect LLM accuracy

text:
All the pieces stitched into one prompt - system line on top, three few-shot examples in the middle, output schema, then the ticket slot. Template:
SYSTEM:
You classify support tickets for Alice's customer-support team. Return JSON only, matching the provided schema.

EXAMPLES:
Input:  "I can't log in - reset my password."
Output: { "category": "billing",         "urgency": "low",    "confidence": 0.91 }

Input:  "Charged twice for last month."
Output: { "category": "billing",         "urgency": "high",   "confidence": 0.95 }

Input:  "Dashboard shows 500 errors."
Output: { "category": "troubleshooting", "urgency": "medium", "confidence": 0.88 }

OUTPUT SCHEMA:
{
  "category":   enum["billing", "product", "troubleshooting"],
  "urgency":    enum["low", "medium", "high"],
  "confidence": number in [0, 1]
}

TICKET TO CLASSIFY:
"{{ ticket_text }}"
Three properties: Reusable - one template, thousands of tickets per day, identical output shape. Auditable - every field is logged, you can review wrong calls and tune examples. Tunable - swap one example, redeploy, measure, no model retraining required.
summary: Complete ticket-classifier template with system, three few-shot examples, output schema, and ticket slot - reusable, auditable, tunable.

text: Chapter 13.4 covers Chain of Thought (CoT) and Self-Consistency. CoT forces the model to show multi-step reasoning so mistakes can be caught - demonstrated with a $120 annual-plan refund where prorated $50 minus 10% fee yields $45. Zero-shot CoT uses trigger phrases like 'Let's think step by step' or 'Think carefully before answering. Show your work.' Few-shot CoT puts reasoning traces in the examples so the model imitates the reasoning style. Self-consistency samples the CoT N times at temperature 0.7 and takes a majority vote, collapsing variance. Tradeoffs: CoT is ~5x tokens and latency for +15% math accuracy; self-consistency (N=5) is 25x tokens, 5x-25x latency, +20% accuracy. Use CoT for multi-step arithmetic, multi-hop reasoning, ambiguous questions; skip for classification, lookups, single-fact Q&A. Reasoning models think internally so explicit CoT prompts become redundant.
summary: Chapter summary of CoT variants (zero-shot, few-shot, self-consistency), their cost/accuracy tradeoffs, and when to use vs skip them.
queries: chain of thought and self-consistency overview
zero-shot vs few-shot CoT vs self-consistency

text: Some questions look simple but hide multi-step math. Asking for the final number alone gives the model nowhere to recover when it slips. Chain of thought forces the work onto the page - and the work is what catches the mistake. Question: Ticket T4: customer on annual plan cancels in month 7. Annual plan is $120/year. Cancellation fee is 10%. What is the refund? Direct Answer (WRONG-style guess): $45 with no reasoning shown - the model guessed and stopped. Chain Of Thought (CORRECT): 1. Annual plan = $120 for 12 months. 2. 5 months remain (12 minus 7 used). 3. Prorated refund = 120 x 5 / 12 = $50. 4. Cancellation fee = 10% of $50 = $5. 5. Final refund = $50 minus $5 = $45. Same model, same question - only difference is whether the model shows its work. CoT exposes the prorating and the fee so the answer can be checked and corrected; the direct call had nothing to anchor to.
summary: Chain of thought makes the model show its work so multi-step math is checkable, illustrated by an annual-plan refund problem.
queries: what is chain of thought prompting
why ask the model to show its work
direct answer vs chain of thought
how does CoT help with math problems
why does direct answer fail multi-step math
exposing reasoning steps to catch mistakes
what does chain of thought look like
step by step reasoning for refunds
why CoT improves accuracy on arithmetic

text: The "Let's think step by step" trick. Zero-shot CoT is the cheapest reasoning upgrade you can buy: one extra sentence on the user turn. No examples, no system-prompt changes. Two phrases work reliably. Classic Trigger: User: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan cancelled month 7. What is the refund? User: Let's think step by step. Stronger Trigger: User: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan cancelled month 7. What is the refund? User: Think carefully before answering. Show your work. Zero examples, just a trigger phrase. The model has seen so much step-by-step reasoning in training that this single phrase flips it into reasoning mode for free.
summary: Zero-shot CoT works by appending a trigger phrase like 'Let's think step by step' or 'Think carefully, show your work' to the user turn.
queries: what is zero-shot chain of thought
let's think step by step prompt
cheapest way to get CoT reasoning
how to enable reasoning without examples
why does let's think step by step work
single sentence to flip model into reasoning
how training data enables let's think step by step

text: Show the reasoning in examples. Few-shot CoT raises the bar. Each example includes the reasoning trace, not just the answer. This transfers the reasoning STYLE, not just the answer format - so the model imitates how you think, not just what you output. Example 1 - Question: Ticket T1: annual plan $120, cancelled month 3. No fee. What is the refund? Reasoning: 1. 9 months remain out of 12. 2. Prorated refund = 120 x 9 / 12 = $90. 3. No fee applied. Answer: $90. Example 2 - Question: Ticket T4: annual plan $120, cancelled month 7. 10% cancellation fee. What is the refund? Reasoning: 1. 5 months remain out of 12. 2. Prorated refund = 120 x 5 / 12 = $50. 3. Fee = 10% of $50 = $5. Answer: $45. The example does the work. The model now knows: list months remaining, prorate, apply the fee, then state the answer. Few-shot CoT teaches that exact chain.
summary: Few-shot CoT shows reasoning traces in the examples so the model imitates the reasoning style, demonstrated with two refund problems.
queries: what is few-shot chain of thought
example with reasoning trace and answer
few-shot CoT for math word problems

text: Run N times, vote. Self-consistency is a simple trick: ask the same CoT question N times with temperature 0.7, collect the answers, and take the majority vote. Variance shrinks. Wrong runs get out-voted. Question: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan $120, cancelled month 7, 10% fee. Sample 5 times with temperature = 0.7. Run 1: Prorated $50 minus 10% fee = $45. Answer $45 (correct). Run 2: 5/12 x 120 = $50. Fee $5. Final $45. Answer $45 (correct). Run 3: Refund $50 prorated. Forgot the fee. Answer $50 (wrong). Run 4: $50 prorated, $5 fee, $45 refund. Answer $45 (correct). Run 5: Months left 5. Refund $50. Fee 10%. Answer $45 (correct). Majority Vote: $45 x 4 vs $50 x 1. Winner: $45. One run can slip and forget the fee. Five runs disagree, the correct answer wins by majority. Variance collapses without retraining anything.
summary: Self-consistency samples N CoT answers at temperature 0.7 and takes a majority vote so single-run mistakes get outvoted.
queries: how to reduce variance in LLM math

text: What CoT buys and what it costs. Reasoning isn't free. CoT generates extra tokens. Self-consistency multiplies that by N. The accuracy gain is real but the bill is real too. Tradeoff table: Direct Answer - tokens 1x, latency 1x, accuracy baseline. CoT Alone - tokens 5x, latency 5x, accuracy +15% on math. Self-Consistency (N=5) - tokens 25x, latency 5x (parallel) to 25x (serial), accuracy +20% on math. Self-consistency tradeoff: 5x to 25x the cost for ~5% extra accuracy over CoT alone. Run samples in parallel and latency drops back to 5x. Cost stays at 25x. Only worth it when accuracy is critical - billing, refunds, dosage, legal answers.
summary: Tradeoff table comparing token cost, latency and accuracy for direct, CoT, and self-consistency with N=5.
queries: tradeoff direct vs CoT vs self-consistency

text: When chain-of-thought wins. CoT is a Decision Matrix problem. The technique helps when there is real reasoning to do. It hurts when the task is a lookup or a one-step classification - extra tokens, extra latency, no payoff. USE COT (Reasoning Required): Multi-Step Arithmetic - refunds, prorating, tax breakdowns, discount stacking, any math with two or more steps. Multi-Hop Reasoning - questions that chain facts: who did X for the customer that signed up in 2023 and is in tier B. Ambiguous Questions - cases where the user's intent isn't obvious from the words alone; reasoning surfaces the assumption. SKIP COT (Direct Answer Is Better): Simple Classification - map ticket text to one of three labels; few-shot already nails it, CoT just adds latency. Lookup Tasks - fetch the customer's email or plan; no reasoning needed, it's a retrieval. Single-Fact Q&A - what time does support open; CoT will hallucinate a derivation where a direct answer suffices. Reasoning Models Change The Calculus: reasoning models like the ones from Section 10.4 Thinking do CoT internally before replying. You no longer prompt for 'step by step' - the model thinks first, then emits the answer. CoT prompts still help on non-reasoning models, but with a reasoning model the technique often becomes redundant. Match the technique to the task. Multi-step math gets CoT. A category lookup gets a direct answer. And when the model already reasons internally, save the tokens.
summary: Decision matrix for when CoT helps (multi-step math, multi-hop, ambiguous) vs when to skip (classification, lookup, single-fact), with a note that reasoning models obviate explicit CoT prompts.
queries: when to use chain of thought
do reasoning models need CoT prompts

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Chapter 13.5 separates Prompting, Fine-Tuning, RAG, and Agent by four axes: data freshness, capability gap, latency, and cost. Prompting triggers what's already in the base model; RAG fills factual gaps with vector-search retrieval at call-time; Fine-Tuning bakes new skills into weights; Agent uses tools in a loop to take actions. Decision tree: known skill -> Prompting; fresh facts -> RAG; missing specialized skill -> Fine-Tuning; needs side-effects via tools -> Agent. Production systems stack all four. Common anti-patterns: fine-tuning to add fresh facts, using RAG to teach style, agent loops for simple lookups, and giant prompts instead of fine-tuning a small model.
summary: Pick prompting, RAG, fine-tuning, or agent by walking four axes - freshness, capability, latency, cost - and stack them in production.
queries: Summary of prompting vs fine-tuning vs RAG vs agent
Four axes for choosing LLM approach

RAG vs fine-tuning vs prompting vs agents

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Four tools in the toolbox for shipping a customer-support feature: Prompting, Fine-Tuning, RAG, Agent. Prompting changes nothing in the model or data - write better messages, same base weights, same world knowledge, same call. Runtime: 1 LLM call. Fine-Tuning changes model weights, data is baked in - train on examples of new behavior, the new skill lives inside parameters. Runtime: 1 LLM call (specialized model). RAG retrieves data at call-time - vector search pulls fresh chunks into the prompt, same base model, new context per call. Runtime: 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call. Agent uses tools in a loop - LLM decides what to do, calls a tool, reads the result, loops until done. Runtime: multiple LLM calls + tool calls. These aren't competitors - they solve different problems.
summary: Four approaches differ by what changes (nothing, weights, retrieved data, or a tool loop) and by runtime call count.

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Data freshness axis is the first filter. If the answer depends on facts that move - prices, inventory, the ticket the user just filed - those facts cannot live inside the model weights. Freshness ranking: Fine-Tuning = Never to weekly (retraining is slow and expensive). Prompting = Never (whatever you wrote into the prompt is frozen). RAG = Hourly (re-index docs and the model sees them next call). Agent = Real-time per query (tools hit live APIs at the moment of the question). Rule of thumb: data that moves fast belongs OUTSIDE the model. Prompt and weights are frozen between calls; retrieval and tools are not.
summary: Data freshness determines whether facts can live in weights or must come from RAG or live tool calls.
queries: How fresh can each LLM approach keep its data
Why does fast-moving data not belong in weights

Why retraining is too slow for daily updates

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Capability gap axis: does the model already know how to do this, or is the skill itself missing? Prompting = already in base model (trigger the skill that's already there, cheapest path). RAG = knows the skill, missing the facts (reading-comprehension is in the model, your private docs are not). Agent = knows the skill, missing the actions (reasoning is in the model, side-effects need tools). Fine-Tuning = specialized skill never seen (style, domain jargon, structured output the base model can't imitate). RAG fills factual gaps. Agent fills action gaps. Fine-tuning fills skill gaps. Prompting only works when the skill is already inside the model and you just need to point at it.
summary: Different gaps need different medicine - facts use RAG, actions use Agent, skills use fine-tuning, existing capability uses prompting.
queries: What does RAG fix vs fine-tuning

Reading comprehension already in base model

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Latency and cost budgets are the third and fourth axes. Quadrants: Low Latency, Low Cost = Prompting (1 call, small prompt, cheapest and fastest by default). Low Latency, Higher Cost Upfront = Fine-Tuning (training is expensive, inference after that is short prompt + 1 call). Higher Latency, Low Cost = RAG (add vector search before the call, tokens stay small, freshness is free). Highest Latency, Variable Cost = Agent (each loop is another call, cost scales with tool steps). Ordering: Latency Prompting ~ Fine-Tune < RAG << Agent. Cost per call follows the same order, but Agent loops can balloon. Budgets break ties - if two approaches both work technically, the one that fits the latency floor and cost ceiling wins.
summary: Latency and cost rank Prompting fastest, Fine-Tuning expensive upfront, RAG cheap with extra hop, Agent slowest and most variable.
queries: Latency comparison prompting RAG fine-tune agent
Which LLM approach is cheapest at inference
When does cost balloon for agents

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Decision tree to pick an approach. Walk top to bottom; the first Yes is the answer or the starting point if stacking. Question 1: Does the model already know it well enough? Yes -> Prompting alone. Question 2: Knowledge needs to stay current? Yes -> RAG. Question 3: Need a specialized skill the model doesn't have? Yes -> Fine-Tuning (plus RAG if data also moves). Question 4: Need to take actions in the world via tools? Yes -> Agent. Decision tree, not flowchart. Each leaf is a single technique. If two questions answer Yes, combine them.
summary: Decision tree picks Prompting, RAG, Fine-Tuning, or Agent based on knowledge, freshness, skill, and action needs.

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Most production systems stack the four approaches into one customer-support assistant. Layer 1: Fine-Tuned Base Model - teaches the support voice and the refund-policy JSON shape. Layer 2: + RAG Layer - pulls today's order history, today's billing record, today's playbook. Layer 3: + Agent Loop - reads tickets, issues refunds, sends emails. Real side-effects. Layer 4: + Prompting / Few-Shot Per Task - system prompt + examples shape each individual call. Stack pattern: fine-tune + RAG + agent loop + prompting combine into one system. Not either/or. Knowing each layer's job is how you debug the stack when something breaks.
summary: Production systems layer fine-tuning, RAG, agent loops, and prompting - each layer covers what the one below cannot.
queries: Do production systems combine RAG and fine-tuning

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Common misuses (anti-patterns), each picking the wrong tool for an axis. 1) Fine-Tuning To Add Fresh Facts - retraining every time the pricing page changes. Fix: use RAG, facts that move belong outside the weights. 2) RAG For Behavior The Model Doesn't Know - stuffing style guides into the prompt and hoping the tone sticks. Fix: fine-tune, behavior lives in weights, not in retrieved chunks. 3) Agent Loop For A Simple Lookup - multi-step planning to answer 'what's the refund window?'. Fix: direct call + 1 tool, loops are for tasks that branch. 4) Massive Prompt Instead Of Fine-Tuning A Small Model - sending 30 examples on every call to a frontier model. Fix: fine-tune a small model once, cost runaway stops, latency drops. Pattern under every misuse: someone picked their favorite tool instead of walking the four axes.
summary: Four anti-patterns pick the wrong technique for the axis - fix by walking the freshness, capability, latency, cost axes first.

text: Context engineering is the meta-skill of packing a fixed 128k token context window on every LLM call. The chapter covers: the hard token budget split into system prompt, conversation history, retrieved docs, user input, and reserved output zones; the fixed top-to-bottom assembly stack (system prompt, few-shot examples, conversation history, retrieved chunks, user query at the bottom for recency); budget tradeoff strategies (Cram Everything, Trim History, Trim Retrieval); the lost-in-the-middle effect where recall dips to ~45% mid-context but peaks near 100% at start and end; ordering strategies (relevance-first for factual lookups, recency-first for multi-turn conversations); and the eviction ladder that drops older retrieved chunks first and never evicts system prompt or last user message.
summary: Overview of context engineering: budgeting, ordering, lost-in-the-middle, and eviction across a fixed 128k window.

text: Every model has a context window: a hard token budget the call has to live inside. A frontier model today is 128k tokens. Every prompt is a packing problem against that ceiling. The 128k budget is split into reserved zones: System Prompt 4k (rules + persona, always present), Conversation History 50k (prior turns, grows every turn), Retrieved Docs 69k (RAG chunks, refilled per query), User Input 1k (the new question, capped at 1k), and Reserved For Output 4k (headroom the model writes into). Anything past 128k gets dropped or summarized. Context engineering is the meta-skill of deciding what survives the cap on every single call.
summary: The 128k context window is a hard cap split into five reserved zones: system, history, retrieved docs, user input, and output.

what zones make up a prompt

text: The model reads top-to-bottom. Order is not cosmetic - it changes what the model attends to. The assembly stack is the same every call: Layer 1 System Prompt (persona, rules, tone, refusal policy, read first, anchors everything below); Layer 2 Few-Shot Examples (2-5 input + output pairs that pin the format, read before any real content); Layer 3 Conversation History (prior turns of this thread, older turns nearest the top of the section); Layer 4 Retrieved Chunks (RAG hits for this query, reranked closer-to-bottom = closer-to-query attention); Layer 5 User Query (the thing to actually answer, sits at the very bottom for maximum recency). Recency = bottom. Whatever you want the model to weight most goes nearest the user query. Same five layers every turn; history grows, chunks rotate, system prompt stays, but the order does not change.
summary: Five-layer assembly stack from system prompt at top to user query at bottom; recency means bottom.

text: 128k is a fixed pie. Every call is a tradeoff between more retrieval, more history, or more output. The same long support ticket can be packed three ways. Cram Everything (at-risk, lost-in-middle kicks in): System 4k, History raw 50k, Retrieved top-20 70k, User 1k, Output 3k. Trim History (wins for fact-heavy tickets, keeps room for retrieval): System 4k, History summary 8k, Retrieved 80k, User 1k, Output 35k. Trim Retrieval (wins for long multi-turn convos, keeps room for output): System 4k, History raw 50k, Retrieved top-3 8k, User 1k, Output 65k. Rule: summarize what you can compress, evict what you cannot, never starve the output. The trim and summarize moves are the two levers; which one wins depends on the ticket type, not on taste.
summary: Three budget strategies (Cram, Trim History, Trim Retrieval) show different ways to split 128k for a support ticket.

text: Even with a 128k window, the model does not attend evenly. Recall drops in the middle and spikes at the start and end. This is the lost-in-the-middle effect, and it is the single biggest reason long prompts fail. Sample recall accuracy: position 0 (Start) 93%, position 0.15 78%, position 0.3 60%, position 0.45 48%, position 0.5 (Middle) 45%, position 0.55 48%, position 0.7 62%, position 0.85 82%, position 1.0 (End) 95%. The curve is U-shaped. Rule: put the most important info at the START or END of context, never in the middle. Buried in the middle is buried full stop. If a fact has to land, place it next to the user query or inside the system prompt - those are the two recall peaks.
summary: Lost-in-the-middle: recall is U-shaped with ~95% at start and end but ~45% in the middle of a long context.
queries: what is lost in the middle effect
why do LLMs forget middle of context

why important info goes near user query
do models attend evenly to context
how to avoid lost in middle

text: Inside the retrieved-chunks and history layers, ordering is its own choice. Two strategies cover the common cases. Relevance-First: factual queries, one-shot lookups; rule is rerank retrieved chunks by similarity, top-3 sit closest to the user query; example ticket 'What's the refund window for plan Pro?' -> top-3 RAG hits next to the query. Recency-First: multi-turn conversations, long debugging threads; rule is latest history at the bottom (near query) for conversational coherence; example ticket with 6-turn back-and-forth -> last 2 user/assistant turns sit nearest the query. Factual lookup -> relevance-first. Long conversation -> recency-first. Hybrid -> rerank then recency-bias the last 2 turns. Same chunks, different order, different answer. Order is a free knob and it routinely costs nothing to flip.
summary: Relevance-first ordering wins for factual lookups; recency-first wins for multi-turn conversations; hybrid combines both.
queries: where to put last conversation turns

text: When the packed prompt exceeds 128k, something has to leave. Eviction is not random - there is a ladder. Drop the things whose loss costs the least, keep the things whose loss breaks the call. Rank #1 Older Retrieved Chunks (Beyond Top-3) - Evict First: rerank surfaced them but top-3 carry the answer almost always, drop the long tail. Rank #2 Mid-Conversation History (Raw Turns) - Summarize Then Evict: compress the middle 5+ turns into one summary line, keep newest 2 turns raw. Rank #3 Few-Shot Examples Beyond The First Two - Evict Soon: two examples pin the format, examples 3-5 are insurance and cost tokens. Rank #4 Top-3 Retrieved Chunks - Rarely Evict: these ARE the answer to this query, only drop if user input is huge. Rank #5 Latest Assistant Response - Rarely Evict: removing it loses the model's own context for what it just said. Rank #6 System Prompt + Last User Message - Never Evict: the rules of the game and the question itself, evicting either breaks the call. Rule: summarize before you evict. A 50-turn history compresses to a few hundred tokens with most of its signal intact.
summary: Six-rank eviction ladder: drop older retrieved chunks first, summarize mid-history, never evict system prompt or last user message.

Dashed return arc from Result back to Model labeled "Return: tool_result becomes the next input". Title: "One tool call: Decide -> Execute -> Return".

text: Tool use is how LLMs bridge from text generation to the real world. A bare LLM only knows training data; tools let it call live functions like search_kb, lookup_customer, or process_refund. Every tool has three parts: Name, Description, Parameters - Description being the most important. The flow is strict: Model decides which tool and what arguments, Runtime executes the real function, tool_result flows back as next input. Model = brain, Runtime = hands. A single call is the atom; an agent loops Reason -> Act -> Observe -> Reason until done. The chapter's support agent uses a fixed 8-tool inventory split into 3 read-only and 5 side-effectful, with refunds over $200 escalating instead of executing.
summary: Tools turn an LLM into an agent by letting it call functions through a strict decide-execute-return cycle that can repeat in a loop.
queries: overview of tool use in LLMs
single tool call vs agent loop summary
agent loop reason act observe summary

text: Pure LLM vs LLM with tools, using ticket T5: "What's the difference between Pro and Enterprise?". Pure LLM relies on training data, plans may be outdated, may hallucinate features. Flow: User asks T5 -> LLM guesses from memory -> Returns possibly-stale answer like "Pro has more features than Enterprise, I think..." - risky, facts can be wrong, no source. LLM + Tools calls search_kb to fetch the live comparison doc then answers from that source. Flow: User asks T5 -> LLM calls search_kb('Pro vs Enterprise') -> Knowledge base returns current doc -> LLM answers from doc. Answer: "Pro: 10 seats, $49/mo, email support. Enterprise: unlimited seats, SSO, dedicated CSM." Grounded, cites live docs, traceable to source. Same model, same prompt. Only difference: one can call search_kb. That single hook is the difference between a chatty box and a working agent.
summary: Tools let an LLM ground answers in live data instead of guessing from training data.

text: A tool is a function the model can call. You declare its signature like lookup_customer(email: string) -> Customer. The model decides when to call it. Your runtime executes the function for real. The result flows back into the conversation. Three-box flow: Model decides WHEN to call -> Runtime executes the real function -> Result flows back to the model. Tool_use block goes Model to Runtime, then Execute, then Return: tool_result becomes the next input. Model = brain. Runtime = hands. The brain never touches the database directly. Critical separation: the LLM never executes code itself. It only emits a tool_use block. Your runtime does the actual call. This boundary is what makes tool use safe enough to ship.
summary: A tool is a function with a declared signature; the model decides to call it but the runtime executes it.

text: Every tool definition has three parts you write. Part 1 Name: process_refund - the identifier the model emits when it decides to call this tool. Part 2 Description: "Refund a paid invoice. Refunds over $200 require human approval." - plain-English contract, what the model reads to decide WHEN to call. Part 3 Parameters: { invoice_id: string, reason: string } - the arguments the model must produce, validated before the runtime executes. The model reads all three at every turn. Description is the most important field - spend most of your editing time there. The model has never seen your codebase. The description is its only manual. If vague, the model guesses; if specific, the model rarely misfires.
summary: Every tool has Name, Description, and Parameters; Description is the most critical because it teaches the model when to call.
