text: The model decides, the runtime executes. Six-step flow. Step 1 User: sends a message "Refund my last invoice, the charge was wrong." Step 2 Model Reads: compares the message to every tool's description, looks for a match. Step 3 Model Decides: thinks "User asked for a refund - I should call process_refund." Or chooses NOT to call any tool. Step 4 Model Emits Tool_Use: generation halts, response is a tool_use block { name: 'process_refund', input: {...} }. Step 5 Runtime Executes: your code sees the block, validates inputs, calls the real function, gets a result. Step 6 Result Returns: runtime appends a tool_result message and re-invokes the model so it can continue. The model can also choose NOT to call any tool and just answer - valid outcome. At step 4 the model halts at tool_use; it does not execute anything itself. The runtime is the single chokepoint where every real-world side effect happens - which is exactly where you place auth, logging, and rate limits.
summary: Walks through the six-step decide-execute cycle from user message to tool_result, showing the model halts and the runtime acts.

text: One call or a loop of calls. A single tool call is the atom. An agent is what you get when you put that atom in a loop: reason, act, observe, then reason again with the new evidence. Triangle diagram: Reason (which tool?) at top, Act (call tool) bottom-right, Observe (read result) bottom-left, clockwise cycle Reason -> Act -> Observe -> Reason, loop until done. Reason: read messages so far, decide do I need a tool and which one. Act: emit a tool_use block, runtime calls the function for real. Observe: read the tool_result, did it succeed, was the info enough to answer. A single tool call is the atom. An agent loop is the molecule. Formalized in 13.20. One ticket might be a single search_kb call and done. Another might need lookup_customer, then lookup_subscription, then process_refund - three turns of the loop before the model finally answers.
summary: An agent is a Reason-Act-Observe loop repeating tool calls until the model has enough info to answer.

text: The 8 tools our support agent has. Fixed inventory used across the rest of Act 2. search_kb(query): search the customer support knowledge base. lookup_customer(email): find a customer by email address. lookup_subscription(customer_id): get current plan and status for a customer. reset_password(customer_id): send a password-reset email. change_email(customer_id, new_email): update the email on record. process_refund(invoice_id, reason): refund a paid invoice, refunds over $200 require escalation. escalate_human(transcript, urgency): hand off conversation to a human agent. send_email(template_id, vars): send a templated email. 8 tools: 3 read-only (search, lookup, lookup), 5 side-effectful (reset, change, refund, escalate, send). Read-only tools are cheap to retry; side-effectful tools must be guarded with auth, validation, and approvals. process_refund is the canary - any refund over $200 escalates instead of executing.
summary: The canonical 8-tool support agent inventory split into 3 read-only and 5 side-effectful tools, used throughout Act 2.

text:
Descriptions Are Read By The Model. The description field is plain language for the model. It is the most important text in your whole tool definition. Treat it like a manual entry the model will skim every turn. Five rules turn a bad description into a great one.

RULE 1: State what the tool does in plain language. One sentence the model can match against the user's request.
RULE 2: State the side effects. Does it mutate state? Send email? Cost money? The model needs to know before calling.
RULE 3: State when NOT to use the tool. Negative examples prevent over-eager calls. "Do not use for refunds over $200" saves real dollars.
RULE 4: Use the same vocabulary the user would use. If the user says cancel, do not name the tool terminate_subscription. Match the word.
RULE 5: Mention any constraints. Rate limits, dollar thresholds, retry semantics, idempotency - put them all in the description.

Description is the model's manual. Write it like you would write a runbook for a new hire. Spend ten times longer on the description than on the parameter names. A great description prevents wrong-tool calls, prevents the model from inventing arguments, and tells the model exactly when to call escalate_human instead of process_refund.
summary: Five rules for writing effective tool descriptions: state what it does, state side effects, state when not to use it, match user vocabulary, and mention constraints.

text:
One Sentence Changes Everything. Same tool, same parameters. The only difference between a refund that goes wrong and one that goes right is the description string. Read both versions of process_refund below.

BAD (Vague): "description": "Refund function." Why bad: No side effects mentioned. No dollar threshold. No when-not-to-use. The model will call it for anything that sounds refund-shaped.

GOOD (Specific): "description": "Refund a paid invoice. Use only when the customer has confirmed cancellation. Refunds over $200 require human approval - call escalate_human instead for those." Why good: What it does. When to use. When NOT to use. The $200 threshold. The fallback tool. The model now has a real contract.

Bad description: model refunds a $500 invoice. Good description: model calls escalate_human instead. The vague version costs you real dollars in mis-refunds. The specific version threads the $200 ceiling and the escalation fallback right into the model's prompt - so the model is choosing escalate_human before it ever attempts the refund.
summary: Side-by-side bad vs good description for process_refund, showing how a specific description with thresholds and fallback prevents the model from mis-refunding a $500 invoice.

text: Chapter 13.9 Tool Call Lifecycle walks the full journey of a tool call across six actors: User -> Model -> Runtime -> Tool -> Runtime -> Model. The model emits a tool_use block (type, id, name, input); the runtime validates and executes the real function; the runtime wraps the return in a tool_result block (type, tool_use_id, content, is_error) and appends it; the model re-reads everything and writes the final assistant message. A worked trace of ticket T1 ("I can't log in - reset my password") shows two tool calls (lookup_customer then reset_password) over three LLM turns. Streaming pauses on tool_use (which arrives as a whole JSON block, not token by token), runs the tool, then resumes. Latency budget for T1 is 3.4s total: 3.0s (88%) in LLM calls, 0.4s (12%) in tool calls - the model is the bottleneck.
summary: Full lifecycle of a tool call across six actors, with message shapes, a worked T1 trace, streaming behavior, and latency budget.

text: Swim-lane diagram: From Request To Final Answer. Six rows top to bottom showing the actors and messages: 1) User -> User message ("I can't log in - reset my password."). 2) Model -> tool_use block (reads context, picks a tool, emits name+input). 3) Runtime -> Function call (validates tool_use, looks up real function, calls it). 4) Tool -> Function return (runs real side effect on DB/email/refund, returns value or error). 5) Runtime -> tool_result block (wraps return value, appends to conversation). 6) Model -> Assistant message (re-reads conversation, writes final answer). Six actors. Two message kinds (tool_use, tool_result) carry the call from model to runtime and back. The model never touches the tool directly - the runtime is the single boundary where every real-world effect happens.
summary: Six-actor swim-lane showing the full path of a tool call from user message to assistant answer.

text:
Tool-Result: Runtime Returns The Outcome. After the runtime executes the real function, it wraps the return value into a tool_result block and appends it to the conversation. Canonical message:
{
  "type": "tool_result",
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_01abc...",
  "content": "Password reset email sent to alice@example.com.",
  "is_error": false
}
Fields: type is always "tool_result" - tells the model this block carries a return value. tool_use_id MUST match the id from the tool_use - this is how the model pairs request to response. content is the return value - usually a string, can be structured; this is what the model reads next. is_error is a flag - true means the runtime failed, the model should recover, not pretend it worked. tool_use_id pairs the response to the request. Same id in, same id out. If the tool failed (DB down, validation error, network blip) the runtime still produces a tool_result but flips is_error to true so the model adapts instead of assuming success.
summary: Anatomy of the tool_result block the runtime appends after executing a tool (type, tool_use_id, content, is_error).

text:
Trace: Ticket T1 Password Reset. Playing the whole lifecycle on a real ticket. T1 is "I can't log in - reset my password." The model loops twice (lookup_customer, then reset_password) before it can answer. Six steps:
Step 1 User: "I can't log in - reset my password." Plain English. No customer_id, just an intent.
Step 2 Model: tool_use { name: lookup_customer, input: { email: "alice@example.com" } }. Email comes from conversation header. Model needs customer_id before it can reset.
Step 3 Runtime: tool_result { content: { customer_id: "c-9924", plan: "Pro" } }. DB hit. Runtime turns function return into tool_result.
Step 4 Model: tool_use { name: reset_password, input: { customer_id: "c-9924" } }. Now it has c-9924 from step 3. Second turn of the loop.
Step 5 Runtime: tool_result { content: "Email sent." }. Side effect happens here. Runtime is the single chokepoint.
Step 6 Model: "Done - check alice@example.com for the reset link." Final assistant message. No more tool_use - model is satisfied.
T1: 2 tool calls (lookup_customer + reset_password), 3 LLM turns, 1 final answer. The model never has c-9924 in step 2 - it only learns the id from step 3's tool_result. Each turn unlocks the next call by giving the model new evidence.
summary: End-to-end six-step trace of ticket T1 password reset showing two tool calls across three LLM turns.

text: Tools While Streaming. In production you usually stream assistant tokens to the UI for the typewriter effect. Tool calls fit cleanly inside a stream - but with one twist: text streams token by token; tool blocks arrive whole. Seven phases left to right: 1) Stream begins - assistant tokens start arriving one by one. 2) Partial text streams - "Let me look that up for you..." appears word by word. 3) Stream pauses - model decides to call a tool, token stream halts. 4) tool_use block emitted - block arrives as one complete JSON object, NOT token-by-token. 5) Runtime executes - your code runs the real function, could take 200ms or 2s. 6) tool_result delivered - result message appended; model re-invoked to continue. 7) Stream resumes - new assistant tokens flow again, building the final answer. Tool blocks aren't streamed token-by-token; they arrive complete. The UI shows partial text as it streams but holds the spinner during tool execution. When the tool_result lands and the stream resumes, the next batch of tokens picks up where it left off.
summary: How tool calls interleave with a streaming assistant response: text streams token by token but tool_use blocks arrive whole and pause the stream.

text:
Where The Seconds Go. Ticket T1 took 3.4 seconds end-to-end. Latency budget breakdown:
LLM call 1 (reads user, emits lookup_customer): 1200ms
lookup_customer tool execution: 200ms
LLM call 2 (reads result, emits reset_password): 1100ms
reset_password tool execution: 200ms
LLM call 3 (reads result, emits final answer): 700ms
Total: 3400ms = 3.4 seconds.
LLM calls: 3.0s (88%). Tool calls: 0.4s (12%). The model is the bottleneck. Every extra LLM call adds about 1 second. Speeding up T1 means cutting LLM turns, not tool latency. Parallel tool calls let you fire lookup_customer and lookup_subscription in one turn instead of two - shaving a whole LLM call from the budget.
summary: Latency budget for T1: 3.4s total, with 88% spent in LLM calls and only 12% in tool execution.

text: Chapter 13.10 Parallel Tools + Tool Choice. Two control levers for agent runtimes: (1) Parallel tool calls - the model emits multiple tool_use blocks in the same assistant turn when calls are independent; runtime fans out concurrently and appends multiple tool_results. Halves tool latency and removes an LLM turn. Only works when calls don't read each other's outputs - dependent calls (e.g., process_refund needing customer_id) must serialize across turns. (2) tool_choice field with four modes: auto (model decides), required (must call something), none (must not call), specific {type:"tool", name:X} (force a named tool). Auto is default and covers chitchat + lookups + parallel calls. Ticket T2 shows independent lookups going parallel; Ticket T4 measures 31% savings (6.8s -> 4.7s) for three lookups + one mutation. Split between parallel and serial is determined by data flow, not the model.
summary: Chapter recaps parallel tool calls (independent fans out, dependent serializes) and the four tool_choice modes (auto, required, none, specific) with T2/T4 latency examples.

text: Ticket T2: "Reset my password but my email also changed to new@example.com". Needs two facts before acting: who is this customer, and what is their subscription. Serial runtime: LLM call 1 (1200ms) -> lookup_customer (Tool, 200ms) -> LLM call 2 (1200ms) -> lookup_subscription (Tool, 200ms). Total 400ms tool work + 2 LLM turns. Parallel runtime emits both tool_use blocks in the SAME assistant turn: LLM call 1 (1200ms) -> lookup_customer + lookup_subscription concurrent (200ms). Total 200ms tool work + 1 LLM turn. Same two tool calls, half the tool latency, one fewer LLM turn. Parallel only works when the two calls do not read each other's output.

text: Independent? Parallel. Dependent? Serial. Rule: if call B needs an argument from call A's result, you cannot parallelize - the model has to wait for A before forming B. If neither call reads the other, fan out. INDEPENDENT (Verdict: Parallel): lookup_customer(email) and lookup_subscription(email). Neither call reads the other's output. Both take an email and return a record. Both tool_use blocks emitted in the SAME assistant turn; runtime fans out, awaits both, returns both tool_results. DEPENDENT (Verdict: Serial): lookup_customer(email) and process_refund(customer_id, ...). process_refund needs customer_id from lookup_customer's result. Second call cannot be formed until first returns. Two assistant turns required: Turn 1 = lookup, Turn 2 = refund (after tool_result is appended). Rule: parallelize when calls don't read each other's outputs.
summary: The parallelization rule: independent calls fan out in one turn; dependent calls where B needs A's output must run in separate turns.

text: Tool Choice = Auto (Default). By default the model decides whether to call a tool, which tool, and how many tools. Set tool_choice: "auto" (or leave unset) and let the model route. Config: tool_choice: "auto". The model decides per-message. Scenarios: (1) Ticket T5: "Pro vs Enterprise - what's different?" -> Model picks search_kb(query="Pro vs Enterprise"). Knowledge question - model picks the docs tool. (2) User: "Hey, good morning!" -> No tool. Plain assistant reply. Chitchat - calling a tool would be wasted latency. (3) Ticket T2: "Reset my password and email also changed." -> lookup_customer AND lookup_subscription. Two tool_use blocks in one turn. Auto still picks - and it can pick more than one. Auto is default because it covers chitchat (no tool), single lookups, and parallel lookups all from one config.
summary: Auto tool_choice lets the model route per-message and can return zero, one, or multiple tool_use blocks per turn.

text: Four Modes Of Tool Choice. tool_choice is a single field with four shapes; each locks down behavior a different amount. (1) AUTO - tool_choice: "auto". Behavior: Model decides whether to call a tool, and which one(s). Use for most flows - chitchat, mixed Q&A, anything where next step depends on input. (2) REQUIRED - tool_choice: "required". Behavior: Model MUST call at least one tool; plain-text replies are blocked. Use when intent is already routed (e.g., user clicked "Run a search") - forces the tool path. (3) NONE - tool_choice: "none". Behavior: Model MUST NOT call any tool; can only emit plain text. Use for final summarization, drafting a reply from already-gathered context, or pure chitchat. (4) SPECIFIC - tool_choice: { type: "tool", name: "search_kb" }. Behavior: Force a specific tool; model can ONLY call this one named tool. Use for deterministic routing - your classifier already decided which API to call; the model fills in arguments. Summary: auto = decide. required = must call something. none = must not call. specific = force this tool.
summary: The four tool_choice modes (auto, required, none, specific) progressively lock down the model from full discretion to forced single-tool calls.

text: Numbers: How Much Does Parallel Save? Ticket T4: "Cancel my subscription, refund this month, and have someone call me." Three independent lookups (customer, subscription, refund policy) feed one mutation (process_refund). SERIAL (4 turns): LLM call 1 (1500ms) + lookup_customer (200ms) + LLM call 2 (1500ms) + lookup_subscription (200ms) + LLM call 3 (1500ms) + lookup_refund_policy (200ms) + LLM call 4 (1500ms) + process_refund (200ms). Summary: 4 LLM calls of 1.5s + 4 tool calls of 200ms = 6.8s total, 4 LLM calls. PARALLEL (2 turns): LLM call 1 (1500ms) + lookup_customer + lookup_subscription + lookup_refund_policy concurrent (200ms) + LLM call 2 (1500ms) + process_refund (200ms) + LLM call 3 (1300ms). Summary: 3 LLM calls + 3 lookups concurrent + 1 mutation = 4.7s total, 3 LLM calls. Savings: 6.8s -> 4.7s (31% faster, one fewer LLM call). Each LLM turn costs ~1.5s and you also save the stacked tool latency. For thousands of tickets a day, 31% is real money and real customer wait time.
summary: Ticket T4 with three independent lookups + one mutation drops from 6.8s serial (4 turns) to 4.7s parallel (2 turns), a 31% speedup.

three lookups one mutation parallel example

text: Trace: Ticket T2 With Parallel Lookups. End-to-end trace - turn 1 parallel (two lookups), turns 2 and 3 sequential because change_email must complete before reset_password sends the link to the new address. STEP 1 User: "Reset my password but my email also changed to new@example.com". Two intents in one ticket. STEP 2 Model (turn 1) [PARALLEL]: tool_use [lookup_customer(email=old) AND lookup_subscription(email=old)]. Both blocks emitted in SAME assistant turn; runtime fans them out in parallel. STEP 3 Runtime [PARALLEL]: tool_result [customer_id=c-9924 AND subscription=active]. Both finish at ~200ms concurrent; two tool_result blocks appended in parallel. STEP 4 Model (turn 2): tool_use { name: change_email, input: { customer_id: "c-9924", new_email: "new@example.com" } }. change_email must complete before reset_password (reset link goes to NEW email). STEP 5 Runtime: tool_result { content: "Email updated." }. Side effect: customer record mutated. STEP 6 Model (turn 3): tool_use { name: reset_password, input: { customer_id: "c-9924" } }. Reset link sent to new@example.com because prior turn already updated the record. STEP 7 Runtime: tool_result { content: "Reset email sent." }. STEP 8 Model (turn 4): "Updated your email to new@example.com and sent a reset link there." Final assistant message, no more tool_use. Split determined by data flow, not the model: independent reads share one turn; dependent mutations (change_email then reset_password) must serialize.
summary: Complete T2 trace shows turn 1 firing both lookups in parallel, then change_email and reset_password serializing across turns 2 and 3 before the final assistant reply.

An arrow points down to the runtime Schema Validation stage (green box) which checks types, required fields, enums, and formats. The stage branches two ways: an Invalid branch (purple, left) returns a malformed error immediately with is_error: true and error_class: "malformed" - no execution, no HTTP call. A Valid branch (cyan, right) proceeds to Execute Tool Function - the expensive step with HTTP call, DB write, etc. Footer caption: "Cheap validation saves expensive function calls." The diagram shows that schema validation runs in microseconds and gates tool execution that costs hundreds of milliseconds.

text: Chapter 13.11 covers Tool Errors, Retries, and Validation. The core idea: errors are data, not exceptions. Every tool failure fits one of four classes - Transient, Permanent, Malformed, Business-rule - and each class has its own retry budget (3 with exponential backoff, 0, 1, 0). Errors are returned as structured tool_result messages with is_error: true and an error_class field so the model can route on them without special-case code. Schema validation gates expensive tool execution by catching malformed arguments in microseconds before any HTTP or DB call. Mutations that may be retried require idempotency keys derived from stable inputs (hash(invoice_id), request_id, client UUID) to prevent double-spending. The Ticket T4 trace demonstrates the full loop: a business_rule denial of process_refund triggers the model to adapt and call escalate_human, producing a calm user-facing resolution.
summary: Chapter 13.11 summary: classify tool errors into four classes, retry per class, validate against schema, use idempotency keys for safe mutations, return errors as data so the model adapts.

text: Production tools fail and the retry strategy depends on WHY the call failed. Every tool error fits one of four classes: Transient, Permanent, Malformed, Business-rule. Transient (yellow, error_class: "transient") covers rate limit HTTP 429, network timeout, upstream 503 - retry with exponential backoff because the call would probably succeed if you tried again in a moment. Permanent (red, error_class: "permanent") covers auth failed HTTP 401, customer not found, missing record - surface to user, do not retry, retrying changes nothing because state not the network is the problem. Malformed (purple, error_class: "malformed") covers schema validation failed, missing required field, wrong type - return the error to the model and let it self-correct because the model wrote bad arguments and given the error message usually fixes them on the next turn. Business-rule (orange, error_class: "business_rule") covers refund $350 > $200 auto-approve cap, blocked customer, outside policy window - return structured "rule violated" reason so the model adapts or escalates because the call is syntactically valid but policy says no and the model needs the WHY to pick a different path. Classify on the way out of the tool, attach the class to the tool_result, and the rest of the agent loop knows what to do.
summary: Four error classes (Transient, Permanent, Malformed, Business-rule) determine retry strategy for every tool failure.

text: Errors are data, not exceptions. The runtime does not throw a Python exception up the stack. It returns a structured tool_result with is_error set to true and an error_class field. The model reads those same fields it reads on a successful call and adapts. Example tool_result message: { "type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_01xyz...", "content": "Refund denied: amount $350 exceeds $200 auto-approve cap.", "is_error": true, "error_class": "business_rule" }. The is_error: true flag tells the model to treat content as the failure reason, not the answer. The error_class: "business_rule" tells the model whether to wait and retry, fix its arguments, escalate, or apologize - same payload shape, different next action. $350 over the $200 cap = structured business_rule error, not a stack trace. Because the error is data, the model can route on it. The next assistant turn picks a different tool (escalate_human) on its own. You did not write "if business_rule then escalate" - the model inferred it from the message.
summary: Errors are returned as structured tool_result messages with is_error and error_class fields so the model can adapt on its own.

text: Match the retry to the error class. One retry policy across all errors is wrong - retrying a permanent auth failure burns three round-trips for no reason, and not retrying a transient 503 fails a perfectly good call. The class drives the retry budget, the backoff schedule, and who handles it. Transient: 3 retries, exponential backoff 200ms, 400ms, 800ms - brief downtime usually clears in under a second. Permanent: 0 retries, surface immediately, no backoff, no second attempt - re-asking the same impossible question wastes turns and tokens. Malformed: 1 retry, return schema error to model, then escalate - the model fixes its own arguments on the second try about 95% of the time. Business-rule: 0 retries, return policy reason, model chooses an alternate tool or escalates - retrying never flips a policy, the model must adapt the plan. Transient retry budget: 3 attempts with exponential backoff (200ms, 400ms, 800ms). Exponential backoff matters because thundering-herd retries amplify outages. Each retry waits twice as long, which spreads load and gives the dependency a chance to recover before you slam it again.
summary: Each error class has its own retry budget and backoff strategy - transient 3 with exponential backoff, permanent 0, malformed 1, business-rule 0.

text: Validate before calling. Most malformed errors never need to hit the tool function. The runtime already has the JSON schema from the tool definition - it can check the model's arguments against required fields, types, enums, and formats in microseconds, before it ever opens an HTTP socket or starts a transaction. The validation gate has three stages: Step 1 - Model emits tool_use, the assistant turn includes a tool_use block with input arguments. Step 2 - Runtime validates against schema, checking required fields, types, enums, formats - cheap and instant. Step 3 - Branch on result: if invalid, return malformed error immediately; if valid, execute the tool function. Schema check (microseconds) gates the tool call (hundreds of milliseconds). Schema-level validation also gives the model a precise reason - "missing required field invoice_id" - which is much easier to fix than a generic 400 from the API. One retry usually does it.
summary: Runtime validates tool arguments against the JSON schema before executing, catching malformed calls in microseconds before any expensive HTTP or DB work.

text: Safe-to-retry tools need idempotency keys. Reads are naturally safe to retry - they just return the same data. Mutations are different. process_refund without an idempotency key can double-refund if the runtime retries on a flaky 503 that actually succeeded. The fix is a key derived from a stable input so the backend can detect and dedupe the retry. Without Idempotency Key (unsafe to retry): signature process_refund(invoice_id, reason). Scenario: runtime retries on a 503 but the first call actually succeeded. Outcome: customer gets refunded TWICE - $700 charged back instead of $350. Rule: never auto-retry a mutation without an idempotency contract. With Idempotency Key (safe to retry): signature process_refund(invoice_id, reason, idempotency_key). Scenario: key = hash(invoice_id), backend stores it and dedupes any retry. Outcome: second call returns the SAME refund_id as the first - customer charged once. Rule: derive the key from a stable input (invoice_id, request_id) so retries collide cleanly. idempotency_key = hash(invoice_id) - retries collide, no double refund. The pattern is universal: any mutation that can be retried needs a deterministic key. For refunds, invoice_id works. For password resets, request_id. For new orders, a client-generated UUID. Without it, retries are unsafe and you must either give up retries or risk double-spending.
summary: Mutations need an idempotency key derived from a stable input so retries dedupe instead of double-charging.
queries: Why do mutations need idempotency keys?
How does idempotency prevent double refunds?

text: Trace: Ticket T4 with errors and recovery. Ticket T4: "Cancel my subscription and refund my last invoice ($350)." Step 1 - User: ticket contains two intents and a refund amount over policy. Step 2 - Model turn 1: tool_use { name: lookup_customer, input: { email: "..." } } - read first, need customer_id before any mutation. Step 3 - Runtime: tool_result { content: "customer_id: c-7741", is_error: false } - clean read. Step 4 - Model turn 2: tool_use { name: process_refund, input: { invoice_id: "inv-882", reason: "customer requested" } } - model attempts refund directly, amount $350 over $200 cap but model doesn't know yet. Step 5 - Runtime: tool_result { is_error: true, error_class: "business_rule", content: "Refund $350 exceeds $200 auto-approve cap." } - BUSINESS-RULE ERROR, TRIGGERS ADAPT, no retry, structured reason is the signal. Step 6 - Model turn 3: tool_use { name: escalate_human, input: { customer_id: "c-7741", reason: "refund $350 over policy cap", urgency: "medium" } } - adapts: switches plan to escalate_human with full context. Step 7 - Runtime: tool_result { content: "Escalation queued: ticket-9921 assigned to billing-team." } - successful escalation. Step 8 - Model turn 4: "I've escalated your refund to a human agent who can approve the $350. You'll hear back within one business day." - final reply, no more tool_use. Step 5 business_rule error -> step 6 escalate_human. Same loop, structured recovery. The user never sees the raw policy error - they see a calm escalation message.
summary: End-to-end T4 trace shows a business_rule error in step 5 triggering the model to switch from process_refund to escalate_human in step 6.

text: Protocols exist because connecting M agents to N tools ad-hoc requires M x N connectors (5 agents x 6 tools = 30). A shared protocol turns this into M + N (5 + 6 = 11) via a hub-and-spoke contract, switching cost from O(M x N) to O(M + N). The three protocols in the wild: MCP (Anthropic, agent <-> tools, production today), A2A (Google, agent <-> agent, multi-agent), and HTTP+OpenAPI (legacy transport). Decision rule: adopt a protocol when you cross 3 tools and 3 agents. Beyond wiring, a protocol enforces a sandbox contract - a trust boundary where capabilities are listed, tool calls authorized, and results returned, making it safe to plug untrusted tool servers into a trusted host holding user data.
summary: Protocols solve the M x N tool wiring explosion, standardize agent-tool communication (MCP, A2A, OpenAPI), and enforce a trust boundary between host and untrusted tool servers.

text: Ad-hoc tools don't scale. Every agent needs to call every tool. Without a shared protocol you write a custom connector for each pair. With 5 agents and 6 tools that is already 30 bespoke integrations. Each new tool forces every agent to learn it; each new agent forces every tool's API to be re-wrapped. The diagram shows 5 agents x 6 tools = 30 hand-wired connectors, a tangle visualizing the M x N sprawl. Connectors grow as M x N - five times six is the small case and it already looks like a tangle.
summary: Without protocols, connecting M agents to N tools requires M times N custom connectors, which scales badly.

text: A protocol turns M x N into M + N. A protocol is a shared contract that sits in the middle. Each agent speaks it once; each tool speaks it once. Same 5 agents, same 6 tools, but now 5 + 6 = 11 clean lines instead of 30. The hub-and-spoke shape is what makes the system additive instead of multiplicative. Add a 6th agent -> 1 new connector. Add a 7th tool -> 1 new connector. No agent rewrites a tool API ever again. Same number of components, drastically different cost of change. The math turns from O(M x N) to O(M + N).
summary: A shared protocol acts as a hub, reducing connector count from M times N to M plus N.

text: MCP, A2A, OpenAPI. Three protocols you will run into. MCP (Model Context Protocol): Anthropic-led open protocol, connects Agent <-> Tools, Resources, Prompts. Lets any agent host discover and call any compliant tool server without bespoke glue. Production today. A2A (Agent To Agent): Google-driven open spec, connects Agent <-> Agent. Lets a planner agent delegate work to specialist agents over a shared message envelope. Useful when one agent does not own all the tools. Newer, Google-driven. HTTP + OpenAPI: connects Service <-> Service (legacy transport). The HTTP and OpenAPI spec the rest of the internet already speaks. Agents can call these too, but the agent host has to translate every endpoint into a tool definition by hand. Legacy, still common. MCP is the one most production agent stacks use today for tool access. A2A is newer and built for multi-agent systems. OpenAPI is not agent-specific - it is the underlying HTTP transport.
summary: Three agent protocols: MCP for tools (Anthropic), A2A for agent-to-agent (Google), and OpenAPI for legacy HTTP services.

text: Production sign: 3+ tools or 3+ agents. Decision grid: Few Tools / Few Agents (1-2 tools, 1-2 agents): hand-wired connectors still readable, skip the protocol layer. Verdict: Ad-Hoc Fine. Many Tools / Few Agents: tool list grows but agent count stable, standardize the tool side so new tools self-describe to the same agent. Verdict: Protocol On Tool Side. Few Tools / Many Agents: handful of high-value tools but many agents (user, planner, evaluator), standardize the agent side so every agent reads the same tool catalog. Verdict: Protocol On Agent Side. Many Tools / Many Agents: production scale, both sides must converge on the same wire format or every release introduces O(M x N) integration work. Verdict: Full Protocol Mesh. Decision rule: cross 3 + 3 (3 tools and 3 agents) and you want a protocol. The exact number depends on rate of change. If new tools land monthly you cross the threshold sooner; if your tool set is frozen at 6, an ad-hoc setup can survive longer.
summary: Use a protocol once you have 3+ tools or 3+ agents; below that threshold ad-hoc connectors are fine.

text: Protocol = sandbox contract. Beyond reducing wiring, a protocol enforces a trust boundary. On the inside sits the host: your user data, the model, and the mutations you are willing to authorize. On the outside sits the tool server: third-party code you do not control. The protocol is the only channel that crosses the boundary, and it dictates what may pass. The diagram shows concentric rings: outer trusted host (user data, model, allowed mutations), inner untrusted server. Arrows cross the boundary: Capabilities Listed (server -> host), Tool Calls Authorized (host -> server), Results Returned (server -> host). The protocol is the only legal crossing. The protocol defines what the inner can ask for and what the outer must approve. That sandbox boundary is what makes it safe to plug a third-party tool server into an agent that holds user data.
summary: Protocols enforce a trust boundary between the trusted host (user data, model) and the untrusted tool server, defining the only legal crossing.

text: MCP (Model Context Protocol) splits the agent stack into three roles - host, client, server - that communicate using JSON-RPC over a chosen transport (stdio, HTTP, or SSE). The host is the user-facing app (Claude Desktop, IDE, custom agent), it launches one client per server connection, and each server is a separate process or remote service exposing tools, resources, and prompts. A session follows a fixed lifecycle: host launches client, client connects to server, initialize handshake exchanges protocol version and capabilities, list returns available tools/resources/prompts, then the host calls a named tool and receives a result. Discovery via tools/list returns each tool's name, description, and JSON input schema, which the host merges across servers into a single tool catalog the model sees.
summary: MCP architecture: host/client/server roles, transports (stdio/HTTP/SSE), connect-handshake-list-call lifecycle, and tools/list discovery merging into one model-visible catalog.

text: MCP splits the agent stack into three roles. Host: the application the user runs - owns the UI, the model, and the user data. The host always owns the trust boundary; the server always sits on the other side; the client is the in-process adapter that connects them.

text: The typical MCP topology is a hub. One host launches many clients - one per server it wants to talk to. Each client maintains its own connection. The host stitches all the listed tools into a single catalog the model can pick from. Example: a single host hosts 3 clients wired to 3 servers - Postgres tool, Linear tool, Filesystem tool. Each client owns one server connection; the host is the hub. The host adds or drops servers without the servers ever talking to each other. The Postgres server has no idea the Linear server is also connected, and the model sees one merged toolset from all three.
summary: MCP topology is hub-and-spoke: one host runs many clients each owning a single server connection, and the host merges all server tool catalogs into one model-visible set.

text: The MCP wire format is the same JSON-RPC regardless of how the bytes travel. Three transports are in common use. Stdio - local only: host launches the server as a child process and pipes JSON-RPC over stdin and stdout. Fastest, no network. Used by Claude Desktop and local MCP servers. HTTP - network: standard request/response over HTTPS, supports remote servers across the internet, needs auth headers and TLS. Used when the server is a hosted service. SSE (Server-Sent Events) - network plus streaming: a one-way streaming channel from server to host on top of HTTP, lets the server push intermediate updates without polling. Used when the server needs to push partial results, progress, or long-running notifications. Pick stdio for same-machine zero-network-surface, HTTP for remote, SSE on top of HTTP when the server streams progress back.
summary: MCP runs the same JSON-RPC over three transports - stdio (local child process), HTTP (remote request/response), and SSE (streaming server-push over HTTP) - chosen by locality and streaming need.

text: An MCP session follows a fixed five-step lifecycle: 1) Host launches client - the host creates a client connection per server. 2) Client connects to server - stdio child process or HTTP/SSE socket opens. 3) Initialize handshake - both sides exchange protocol version and capabilities. 4) List available tools/resources/prompts - server tells the host what it can do. 5) Call a tool, get a result - host invokes a named capability and receives the response. The lifecycle matters because the host cannot just call a tool blind. It must finish the initialize handshake (so both sides agree on protocol version) and then call list before it knows any tool names. Only after list returns does the model see a populated tool catalog.
summary: MCP session lifecycle: launch client, connect, initialize handshake (version + capabilities), list available tools/resources/prompts, then call a tool - discovery must precede invocation.

text: Chapter 13.14 introduces the three MCP primitives - Tools, Resources, and Prompts - and shows when to use each. Tools are named actions with typed input schemas that mutate state and are invoked by the model (e.g., process_refund). Resources are read-only data exposed at server-defined URIs (e.g., kb://articles/password-reset) that the host fetches into context. Prompts are parameterized templates (e.g., summarize_ticket with ticket_id required and max_words optional) that users invoke as slash-commands, with the host filling arguments. The decision rule: tool if side-effecting, resource if read-only data, prompt if a template the user picks. Each primitive has a distinct caller - model, host, or user - and mis-assigning capabilities breaks the contract.
summary: Overview of MCP's three primitives and the decision framework for mapping any capability to the right one based on who calls it and whether it mutates state.

text: An MCP server exposes three kinds of capabilities: Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Tools are things the model can do - callable actions like process_refund(invoice_id, reason) that the model picks and invokes. Resources are things the model can read - data like kb://articles/password-reset that the host pulls in and shows to the model. Prompts are templates the host can offer - parameterized templates like summarize_ticket(ticket_id, max_words) where the user picks one and the host fills the slots. The split matters because each primitive has a different access pattern: the model decides when to call a tool, the host decides when to read a resource, and the user picks which prompt to invoke.
summary: MCP servers expose three primitives - Tools (actions the model calls), Resources (data the host reads), and Prompts (templates the user picks).

text: A Tool is a named action with a typed input schema. It usually has side effects: it mutates state, calls an external API, or spends money. The model picks one from the tool list and emits a tool call with the required arguments. Example tool process_refund has inputSchema: { invoice_id: string, reason: string }. The side effect is mutation: $-amount returned to the customer's card. The model chooses when to call. Tools always go through validation before the server actually runs the action. The inputSchema is how the host and the model know what arguments are required and what type each one must be.
summary: A Tool is a typed, side-effecting action the model invokes by name with arguments, validated against an inputSchema.

text: A Resource is read-only data the server exposes under a URI. The host fetches it on demand and feeds the content into the model's context, the same way you might attach a PDF in a chat. The model never calls a resource itself. Example URI: kb://articles/password-reset which resolves to the Markdown body of the doc (read-only). The host decides when to read - e.g., before calling the model on a ticket. Resources are READ-ONLY by convention. The URI scheme is server-defined: it could be kb://, file://, github://, postgres://, or anything else the server exposes. The host treats it as an opaque identifier and asks the server to resolve it when needed.
summary: A Resource is read-only data exposed at a URI that the host fetches and injects into the model's context.

text: When you design an MCP server, every capability falls into exactly one bucket. Ask what the model or host needs to DO, READ, or REUSE, and the right primitive is obvious. Decision rows: Need to DO something (mutate state, call API)? -> Tool. Need to READ something (fact, doc, file)? -> Resource. Need a reusable prompt template the user can invoke? -> Prompt. Decision rule: tool if side-effecting; resource if read-only data; prompt if template the user picks. Putting a read-only doc behind a tool wastes a model turn. Putting an action behind a resource hides it from the model entirely. Putting a one-shot question behind a prompt hides it from end users. The split is the contract.
summary: Decision framework: side-effecting action becomes a Tool, read-only data becomes a Resource, user-invokable template becomes a Prompt - and mis-mapping breaks the contract.

text: Chapter 13.15 walks through building an MCP server: it is a short program that declares an identity (name + version), registers tools, resources, and prompts, then starts listening on a transport. Each registration takes a name/description plus a handler that runs server-side. The server moves through four lifecycle states - REGISTERED, LISTENING, ACTIVE, CLOSED - via connect, initialize, and shutdown events. Most time is spent in ACTIVE handling list/call/read/prompt-get requests. Before wiring to a real host, test handlers as plain unit tests, verify list responses include declared items, check the transport handshake, and use a mock host to avoid burning tokens.

text:
Resource: kb://articles/{id}. Resources are addressed by URI patterns. The server registers a template like kb://articles/{id}; the host requests a concrete URI; the server matches the template and runs the handler to produce the content.

Server.registerResource({
  uriPattern: "kb://articles/{id}",
  description: "Knowledge base article by ID.",
  handler: async ({ id }) => readMarkdown(id)
})

Example flow: host requests kb://articles/password-reset. Server matches the template, reads the markdown doc, returns the body.

text: Test Before Connecting. An MCP server is just a process with handlers and a transport - test every piece in isolation before plugging into a real host with a real model. Four strategies: 1) Test handler functions as plain unit tests - call them directly with sample inputs and assert the returned value. 2) Test list responses - assert all declared tools, resources, and prompts appear in the catalog. 3) Test transport connection - spin the server up over stdio or HTTP and assert the initialize handshake completes. 4) Use a mock host before connecting to a real LLM - drive list and call from a scripted host to catch contract bugs without paying for tokens. Production hygiene: test the server in isolation, then wire it to the host.
summary: Four testing strategies for MCP servers: unit-test handlers, verify list catalogs, test transport handshake, and use mock hosts before real LLMs.

text: MCP Security stacks four defenses around untrusted third-party server code. Sandbox: the host spawns the server in its own OS process with transport-only communication, no filesystem access except granted directories, and no outbound network by default. Capability scope: per-host allow/deny lists at three levels - tool names, resource URI prefixes, and a global read-only flag. OAuth: HTTP servers require user-granted access tokens that ride along on every request and get scope-checked by the server; stdio servers skip this under local trust. Consent + audit: the host blocks side-effecting calls behind approve/deny prompts and logs every call with timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and decision. Sandbox keeps the server from reaching outside; scope keeps it from doing more than allowed; OAuth proves the user granted access; consent and audit keep the user in control of every effect.
summary: MCP security combines sandbox process isolation, per-host capability scope, OAuth for HTTP servers, and consent prompts plus audit logs to contain untrusted server code.

text: An MCP server is third-party code. It might be open source, vendor-supplied, or written by a teammate, but the host never assumes it is safe. The host holds the user data, the model, and every mutation worth caring about. The server holds the tool implementation. The boundary between them is what makes the whole architecture safe. The host owns the user data and the model. The server runs third-party tool code. The protocol crossing the boundary is the only thing the host has to police. Trust boundary: outer ring is Host (Trusted) with User Data, Model, Allowed Mutations; inner ring is Server (Untrusted) running third-party tool code. Arrows crossing the boundary: List (server to host), Call (host to server), Result (server to host). The MCP transport is the only legal crossing.
summary: MCP servers are untrusted third-party code, so the host polices the trust boundary and treats the MCP transport as the only legal crossing.

text: Sandbox: Process Isolation. The host launches the server in its own OS process. The host can spawn it, kill it, and shape what it sees, but it never shares memory with it. Whatever capability the server wants, it has to ask for over the transport. Sandbox rules: (1) Transport Only - the server communicates with the host only over the MCP transport. No back channel. (2) Filesystem Walled Off - no filesystem access except the directories the host explicitly grants. (3) Network Off By Default - no outbound network unless the host opts in for this server. The sandbox is the OS-level fence. Even if the tool code is buggy or hostile, it cannot reach beyond the resources the host hands it.
summary: The host runs the MCP server in an isolated OS process sandbox with no shared memory, transport-only communication, walled filesystem, and network off by default.

text: Capability Scope Per Host. Even when a server declares ten tools and a hundred resources, the host can narrow what this particular agent is allowed to touch. Scope is a subset of what the server advertises, configured per host. Three dimensions: ALLOWED TOOLS - subset of declared tools, the host picks which tools each agent may call, example: deny `escalate_human` for junior agents. ALLOWED RESOURCES - prefix-matched URIs, resource access is gated by URI prefix patterns, example: allow `kb://articles/*` but not `kb://internal/*`. ALLOWED MUTATION - read-only mode flag, a flag forces every tool call to be non-mutating regardless of declaration, example: read-only mode for evaluation runs. Capability scope is allow/deny lists at three levels: tool names, resource URI prefixes, and a global read-only flag.
summary: Hosts narrow an MCP server's declared capabilities with allow/deny lists on tool names, resource URI prefixes, and a global read-only mutation flag.

text: OAuth: Who Granted What. When the server lives over HTTP, the host has to prove the user authorized it. OAuth is the standard answer: the user grants scopes once, the host exchanges that grant for an Access Token, and that token rides along on every server request. OAuth flow (four steps): 1) User Authorizes, 2) Host Gets Access Token, 3) Token Passes To Server, 4) Server Checks Scope. Grant -> Token -> Request -> Check. The Access Token is the proof the server demands on every call. Stdio servers skip OAuth because they run under local trust. HTTP servers require it so the host can prove which user authorized the call.
summary: HTTP MCP servers use OAuth so the user authorizes once, the host gets an access token, and the server checks scope on every call; stdio servers skip OAuth under local trust.

text: Consent + Audit: User Sees Everything. Sandbox, scope, and OAuth handle the machine side. Consent and audit handle the human side. The host asks the user before any side-effecting call, and writes every call to a log the user can review later. Consent Prompts: the host blocks the call until the user clicks approve or deny. Example prompt: 'About to call process_refund / For invoice INV-9924 / Reason: "Customer Requested" / Allow? [Approve] [Deny]'. Required for side-effecting tools. Audit Log: every call is recorded with timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and the consent decision. Example rows: 14:02:11 lookup_customer {id:"C-104"} OK alice@co Auto; 14:02:34 process_refund {invoice:"INV-9924"} OK alice@co Approved; 14:03:02 send_email {to:"cust@x"} OK alice@co Approved; 14:05:50 delete_account {id:"C-104"} Blocked alice@co Denied. The audit log makes every action traceable to a user, a tool, and a decision.
summary: Consent prompts block side-effecting tool calls until the user approves and an audit log records timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and decision for every call.

text: Chapter 13.17 covers the A2A (Agent-to-Agent) Protocol. MCP handles agent-to-tool calls; A2A handles agent-to-agent delegation where one agent hands an entire problem to a peer that runs its own loop, model, memory, and tools. Agents discover each other via an agent.json card listing name, description, skills, endpoint, and auth. A real delegation classifies the request, looks up a peer with the right skill, packages context, sends the request, and waits for a result. Long tasks stream intermediate progress back so the caller stays informed without polling. Decision rule: function → MCP Tool, document → MCP Resource, whole agent needed → A2A Delegation. A2A is heavier than MCP - use it only when the work needs the receiving agent's own loop.
summary: A2A is the protocol for one agent to delegate an entire problem to another agent, with JSON-based discovery, full-context handoff, streaming progress updates, and a clear decision rule versus MCP.

text: MCP and A2A handle two directions of agent communication. MCP: agent reaches out to a tool and gets a value back. A2A (Agent-to-Agent): agent hands an entire problem to a different agent and lets that peer run its own loop. Same wire idea, very different scope. With MCP, the agent stays in charge and just borrows a tool. With A2A, the agent hands the wheel to a peer that runs its own model, memory, and tools to finish the job.

text:
A delegated task is not a single function call. The receiving agent may need minutes, human approval, or several tool calls of its own. A2A solves this by letting the receiving agent stream intermediate updates back instead of going silent until the final result is ready.

Streaming updates from billing agent (example):
- Looked up customer...
- Processing refund...
- Refund > $200, need approval (yellow flag)
- Final result returned (green)

Streaming lets triage show the user progress in real time and react if an intermediate update needs human approval before the long task finishes. Triage stays informed without polling.
summary: A2A supports streaming intermediate progress updates from the delegated agent back to the caller so long tasks don't go silent and the caller can react to approvals.

text:
MCP and A2A live side by side, not in competition. The decision is about what you need from the other side: a function, a document, or an entire collaborator.

Decision rules:
- Need to call a function? → MCP Tool
- Need to read a doc? → MCP Resource
- Need an entire other agent to handle a sub-problem? → A2A Delegation

A2A is heavier than MCP. Use it when the delegated work needs the receiving agent's own loop, memory, and tools - not just a single function call.
summary: Choose MCP Tool for function calls, MCP Resource for documents, and A2A Delegation when you need another agent's full loop, memory, and tools.

text: Workflow vs Agent: a workflow is a fixed DAG you wire in advance; an agent is an open loop where the model picks each next step. Pick workflow when steps are knowable, outputs predictable, and cost/latency must be bounded - example: Classify -> Route -> Handle for tickets. Pick agent when you can't pre-draw the graph and the model must decide tools and stopping point - example: summarize all customer issues. Most production systems are hybrid: outer deterministic DAG (Classify, Reply, Log) with one inner agent step (Handle). Failure shape differs: workflow fails loud with bounded cost; agent soft-fails with unbounded iterations. Decision rule: workflow when you can; agent when you must.
summary: Workflows are fixed DAGs with bounded cost; agents are open loops with variable cost; production systems combine them with agents used only where needed.

text: Two shapes of control: a workflow is a fixed DAG where you wire the steps and the model follows the wires. An agent is an open loop where the model picks the next step every iteration. Agent side: a central Decide node with arrows out to Tool 1, Tool 2, Tool 3, Tool 4 and a self-loop, different runs take different paths. Workflow is a fixed graph you draw. Agent is an open loop where the model picks the next move. Choose the shape that matches how much variance the task actually has.

text: Workflow: known inputs, known steps. Workflow is the right shape when you already know what to do. The model is a smart cell inside a bigger pipeline you wrote, not the conductor. Pick this when the task is repeatable and the steps are knowable in advance. Pick Workflow When: You can list the steps in advance. Each step's output type is predictable. You need to guarantee certain steps execute. The cost / latency must be bounded. Example: when a ticket comes in: Classify -> Route -> Handle. Always those 3 steps. The model still calls tools and writes text. But you, not the model, decide which step runs in which order. The output of each step is predictable enough to wire to the next.
summary: Pick a workflow when steps are knowable in advance, outputs are typed, and cost/latency must be bounded.

text: Agent: variable steps, variable tools. Agent is the right shape when you can't pre-draw the graph. The model is the conductor: it decides which tool to call, in what order, and when to stop. You pay for that flexibility with cost and unpredictability. Pick Agent When: You don't know the steps in advance. The model needs to decide which tools to call and in what order. The task could take 2 calls or 20. You're willing to pay for flexibility. Example: research-style task: 'Summarize all customer issues from the past week.' Agent decides which docs to read, which to skip, and when it has enough. One ticket might need 2 tool calls. The next might need 20. You can't draw that graph in advance because the shape of the task only becomes visible after the model starts digging.
summary: Pick an agent when steps are unknown ahead of time and the model must dynamically choose tools and stopping point.

text: Most production systems are hybrid. Real systems are rarely pure. The common pattern is a workflow with one agent step inside. You keep the outer skeleton deterministic and let the model loose only inside the one node that actually needs creativity. Hybrid shape: outer DAG, inner agent loop. Example four step pipeline: Classify (deterministic) -> Handle (agent step, marked with ?) -> Reply (deterministic) -> Log (deterministic). The Classify step is deterministic. The Handle step is an agent. Reply and Log are deterministic again. This is the hybrid shape almost every production agent ships with. You get the safety and predictability of a workflow around the parts you can fully spec, and the freedom of an agent only where you actually need it.
summary: Most production systems wrap one agent step inside an outer deterministic workflow to get safety plus flexibility where needed.

text: Predictable cost vs variable cost. The biggest practical difference between workflow and agent is the shape of failure. Workflow failures are loud and bounded. Agent failures are quiet and unbounded - the agent keeps trying and the bill keeps climbing. Workflow: bounded and predictable. Cost: fixed per run. Latency: bounded by slowest step. Failure: hard crash, loud error. Agent: variable and soft-failing. Cost: variable, depends on iterations. Latency: variable, 2 calls or 20. Failure: soft-fail, gets stuck instead of crashing. Decision rule: workflow when you can; agent when you must. The cost of an extra workflow node is one more tool call. The cost of an extra agent iteration is an unbounded number of tool calls. That asymmetry is why teams default to workflow and reach for an agent only on the steps that genuinely need it.
summary: Workflows fail loud with bounded cost; agents fail soft with unbounded cost, so default to workflow and use agents only where needed.

text: Chapter 13.19 introduces the three workflow primitives that compose every production LLM pipeline: chaining (step A's structured output feeds step B's input via JSON), routing (a classifier LLM call picks one of N branches), and parallelization (fan out N concurrent workers, merge results, latency = max not sum). Real workflows stack all three: chain into a classifier, route to a branch, parallelize heavy retrieval inside the branch, chain the merged result into the final answer. The chapter walks a customer support example where step 2 routes by intent, step 3 chains lookup_customer -> lookup_subscription -> respond, step 4 parallelizes search_kb for top-3 hypotheses, and step 5 escalates directly. A workflow is wired in advance; the LLM is a smart cell inside a graph the engineer controls.
summary: Overview of chaining, routing, parallelization as composable workflow primitives stacked in real pipelines.

text: Workflows are built from three composable primitives. Chaining wires step A's output into step B. Routing classifies the input and picks one branch. Parallelization fans out N workers and merges their results. Every production workflow stacks these three. Card 1 CHAIN: A -> B -> C, sequential steps. Card 2 ROUTE: classifier picks branch, one of N branches. Card 3 PARALLEL: fan-out + merge, concurrent workers. These are the lego bricks. Chaining is a sequence. Routing is a fork. Parallelization is a fan-out plus merge. Real workflows stack them in layers.

text: Chaining is the simplest primitive: the structured output of one LLM call becomes the structured input of the next. The glue is JSON. Step A returns a typed object, your code reads it, and feeds the next step. Example chain: Ticket (User Text) -> Classifier (LLM Call) -> Handler (LLM Call). Edges carry JSON: {"text": "can't bill"} then {"category": "billing"}. Step A Output: { "category": "billing", "confidence": 0.92 }. Step B Input: { "category": "billing", "ticket": "Can't bill my card." }. Because each step returns a typed object (JSON schema from chapter 13.8), the next step reads fields like ticket.category by name. Chaining is normal function composition with LLM calls as the functions.
summary: Chaining wires one LLM call's JSON output into the next call's input like function composition.

text: Routing classifies the input and picks one branch. The classifier is itself an LLM call that returns a typed label. Your code dispatches to the matching handler. Only the chosen branch runs. Example: Intent Classifier picks one of four branches - Billing, Product, Troubleshooting, Chitchat. The classifier uses few-shot examples (Section 13.3). One classification call replaces a hand-written if/else tree. The model labels the input, your code dispatches the matching branch handler. Adding a new branch is one new few-shot example plus one new handler.
summary: Routing uses an LLM classifier to pick one branch from N handlers, replacing hand-written if/else trees.

text: Parallelization runs N independent calls concurrently and aggregates the results. Total latency is the time of the slowest worker, not the sum. Cheapest way to cut wait time on independent sub-tasks. Example fan-out: Query -> Worker 1 (180ms), Worker 2 (140ms), Worker 3 (210ms) -> Aggregator. Timing bars show concurrent execution. Formula: Latency = max(worker time), Not Sum. If three retrieval calls each take 150-210ms, running them in series costs ~540ms. Running them concurrently costs ~210ms, the slowest one. The aggregator merges all results into one structured object.
summary: Parallelization fans out N concurrent workers and merges; total latency equals the slowest worker not the sum.

text: Chapter 13.20 The Agent Loop. An agent is a loop with three beats: REASON (model thinks, reads full history), ACT (tool_use emitted), OBSERVE (tool_result returned). Zoomed in it is a state machine with four primary states (WAITING, REASONING, ACTING, OBSERVING) and three terminal states (DONE, FAILED, ESCALATED). After each OBSERVE the runtime runs a cheap termination check: final answer -> DONE, max iterations -> ESCALATE, budget exceeded -> ABORT. Cost per iteration is roughly 1 LLM call (~$0.02 for 5k-token reasoning context + 200-token response) + 1 tool call; reasoning context grows each iteration because full history is replayed (40k tokens by iter 8). Worked trace ticket T2 ('forgot password and email changed') runs 4 iterations: lookup_customer, change_email, reset_password, final answer; agent discovers ordering through reasoning, not hard-coding. Decision rule: if the second tool's input depends on the first tool's output, you need a loop; otherwise use a single call or fixed workflow.
summary: Full overview of the three-beat agent loop, its state machine, termination check, per-iteration cost, the T2 worked trace, and when a loop is actually needed.

text: The Three-Beat Cycle. An agent is not a single call. It is a loop with exactly three beats: the model REASONS, the runtime ACTS, the runtime OBSERVES. Then the model reasons again with the new observation glued to the history. Every agent system is some shape of this cycle. REASON = Model Thinks. ACT = tool_use emitted. OBSERVE = tool_result returned. Reason reads the entire history so far. The model's next decision is always conditioned on every previous tool call and result, not just the original prompt.
summary: Introduces the three-beat REASON / ACT / OBSERVE cycle that defines every agent loop.

text: Agent As State Machine. Zoom in on a single iteration and the loop becomes a state machine with four primary states the agent moves through: WAITING (idle, awaiting input), REASONING (model call in flight), ACTING (tool call in flight), OBSERVING (got tool result). Transitions: User Input moves WAITING to REASONING, LLM Response moves REASONING to ACTING, Tool Result moves ACTING to OBSERVING, then the loop edge runs from OBSERVING back to REASONING for the next iteration. Three terminal states end the run: DONE (final answer emitted), FAILED (unrecoverable error such as tool throw or schema break), ESCALATED (budget or max-iter hit, the safety net). Primary states loop. Terminal states end the run. Every iteration checks whether it should cross the bottom boundary. DONE is the happy path. FAILED is an unrecoverable error. ESCALATED catches runaway agents.
summary: Models the agent as a state machine with four primary states cycling and three terminal states ending the run.

text: Check After Each Beat. Every iteration the runtime asks: should we keep looping? Without this guardrail an agent can spin forever on a tool that keeps almost-working. The check happens after OBSERVE and before the next REASON. A diamond decision node labeled Terminate? sits between OBSERVE and the next REASON with three outgoing branches: Final Answer Emitted? Yes -> DONE (green). Max Iterations Exceeded? Yes -> ESCALATE (amber). Budget Exceeded? Yes -> ABORT (red). No -> Next Iteration loops back to REASON. The termination check is cheap (no model call) and happens on every iteration. It is the difference between a system you can run in production and a runaway model that bills your card flat. Each condition is formalized in chapter 13.23 (Loop Termination).
summary: Describes the per-iteration termination check with three branches DONE, ESCALATE, ABORT that prevents runaway agents.

text: What Each Beat Costs. One iteration is one full lap around the cycle. REASON: 1 LLM call, roughly $0.02 for a 5k-token reasoning context plus 200-token response. ACT: 0 model cost (tool_use emitted in reason's response), 1 tool execution cost (varies). OBSERVE: 0 model cost, result appended to history. TOTAL: roughly 1 LLM call + 1 tool call per iteration. Rule of thumb: Cost Per Iteration = 1 LLM call + 1 tool call. Scale linearly with iteration count. Reasoning context grows every iteration because the full history is replayed. By iteration 8 you might be paying for 40k tokens of context per call. This is why long agent runs get expensive fast, even when each individual call looks small.
summary: Breaks down per-beat cost: 1 LLM call plus 1 tool call per iteration, with context growing linearly.
queries: how much does an agent iteration cost
cost of reason act observe beats
how many LLM calls per agent iteration

text: Loop Trace: Ticket T2. Ticket T2: 'I forgot my password and my email also changed.' Two problems, order matters. The agent loop discovers the order through reasoning, not hard-coded steps. Iter 1: REASON 'Need customer info before anything else.' ACT lookup_customer(email="ada@example.com"). OBSERVE got customer_id c-9924, Plan=Pro, Email Verified=False. Iter 2: REASON 'Email outdated, update first before reset lands in the wrong inbox.' ACT change_email(customer_id="c-9924", new_email="ada+new@example.com"). OBSERVE email updated, verification pending. Iter 3: REASON 'Now reset the password.' ACT reset_password(customer_id="c-9924"). OBSERVE reset email sent to ada+new@example.com. Iter 4: REASON 'Done. Summarize for the user.' ACT emit final answer, no tool call. OBSERVE TERMINATE, loop exits. Outcome: 4 iterations, 3 tool calls, 1 final answer. Loop terminates cleanly because the model emitted a final response instead of a tool call. You never told the agent 'update email before reset'. It worked that out in iteration 2 from the observation in iteration 1.
summary: Walks through the T2 password-reset ticket as a 4-iteration trace showing the agent discovering tool ordering from observations.

text: When You Need The Loop. Not every task needs a loop. A single tool call is cheaper, faster, and easier to debug. SINGLE CALL: one tool, one response, done. Example: 'What is the weather in Tokyo?' -> get_weather('Tokyo') -> return the result. No adaptation needed. LOOP: multiple iterations, model adapts based on intermediate results. Example: T2 above. Iter 1's observation changes what iter 2 decides to do. Decision Rule: If the second tool's input depends on the first tool's output, you need a loop. Otherwise stay with a single call or a fixed workflow. That dependency is the whole reason the loop exists. The first tool's output reshapes what the model knows, and the model must re-reason before picking the second tool. No loop, no adaptation, no agent.
summary: Decision rule for when to use a loop vs a single call: dependency between tool outputs and next tool inputs.

text: Chapter 13.21 ReAct Pattern. ReAct = Reasoning + Acting. Model writes Thought, Action, Observation as visible structured output each iteration of the agent loop instead of hiding reasoning internally. Thought explains why next action is right. Action is exactly one tool call matching JSON schema. Observation is the runtime-supplied tool result (model does not invent). Loop repeats until model emits an Answer instead of an Action. Worked example: Ticket T2 password reset with changed email runs 4 cycles, 3 tool calls (lookup_customer, change_email, reset_password), then final answer. Trade-off: each Thought costs tokens and slows response - modern tool_use APIs already give structured calls without forcing written reasoning. Use ReAct when you need to audit reasoning, debug failures, fine-tune smaller models on traces, or build user trust.
summary: ReAct forces the agent to write Thought, Action, Observation each loop so reasoning is visible for audit, debug, fine-tuning, and trust.

text: Make The Reasoning Visible. ReAct stands for Reasoning + Acting. Instead of hiding the model's reasoning inside its head and only emitting a tool call, ReAct asks the model to write the thought out loud, then the action, then the observation, in that order, every iteration. The loop is the same; what changes is that each block is now visible structured output. Three labeled blocks: Thought = what the model is thinking about the next step. Action = which tool to call and with which arguments. Observation = what the tool returned, fed back into the next thought. Key idea: Thought is INSIDE the model's response now, not hidden. Same three beats as the agent loop. The Thought becomes a written block in the response, so evaluators, debuggers, and downstream tools can read it directly.
summary: ReAct = Reasoning + Acting: model writes Thought, Action, Observation as visible blocks each loop iteration instead of hiding reasoning internally.

text: Thought: Why The Next Action. The Thought block answers one question: why is the next action the right next action? It names the plan in plain language before any tool fires. A good Thought references the user's request, the state so far, and the order of remaining steps. Example: 'Thought: The user asked to reset their password but mentioned the email changed. I should first lookup_customer with the OLD email, then change_email to the NEW one, then reset_password.' Why it matters: the Thought makes the plan visible for evals and debugging. When a reset later goes wrong, you can open the trace and read the model's plan in English. You learn whether the failure was bad reasoning (wrong plan) or bad execution (right plan, wrong tool argument). That split is impossible without a written Thought.
summary: The Thought block is a plain-language plan justifying the next action, enabling separation of reasoning failures from execution failures.
queries: What goes in a ReAct Thought block

What question does the Thought answer
Reading model reasoning in a trace

text: Action: The Concrete Step. After the Thought, the model emits exactly one Action. It is a tool name plus an argument object that matches the tool's JSON schema. No second tool, no batching - one call, one step. Example: 'Action: lookup_customer({"email": "alice@example.com"})'. Rule: one Action per iteration. Why only one? Because the next Thought must be free to react to whatever this Action returned. Batching two tool calls into one Action makes the model commit to a second step before it has seen the first result, which is exactly the trap the loop is supposed to avoid.
summary: Each ReAct iteration emits exactly one Action - a tool name plus JSON-schema-matching args - so the next Thought can react to the actual result.
queries: What is an Action block in ReAct
Can ReAct batch multiple tool calls
Why only one Action per iteration
Why not batch tool calls in agent loop
What does an Action look like in ReAct
Avoiding premature commitment in agent loops

text: Observation: What Came Back. The Observation block holds the result the runtime received from the tool. The runtime writes this in - the model does not invent it. The Observation is appended to the conversation so the next Thought has the new ground truth to reason over. Example: 'Observation: { customer_id: "c-9924", tier: "pro", primary_email: "alice@example.com" }'. The model reads this and decides the next Thought. Notice the structure - keys like customer_id, tier, primary_email. The model is reading a JSON-shaped result, not a free-text paragraph. Keeping the Observation strictly structured is what lets the next Thought pull out specific fields like c-9924 instead of paraphrasing them.
summary: The Observation is the runtime-supplied, JSON-structured tool result appended to the conversation as ground truth for the next Thought.
queries: What is an Observation in ReAct
How tool results feed next Thought
Why observations should be JSON not prose

text: ReAct Trace: Ticket T2. Full four-cycle ReAct trace. Cycle 1: Thought - Customer wants reset, mentioned old email. Look them up first. Action - lookup_customer({"email": "alice@example.com"}). Observation - { customer_id: "c-9924", tier: "pro", primary_email: "alice@example.com" }. Cycle 2: Thought - Email on record is outdated. Update it before the reset link goes to the wrong inbox. Action - change_email({"customer_id": "c-9924", "new_email": "alice+new@example.com"}). Observation - { ok: true, primary_email: "alice+new@example.com", verification: "pending" }. Cycle 3: Thought - Email is fixed. Trigger the password reset now so the link lands at the new address. Action - reset_password({"customer_id": "c-9924"}). Observation - { ok: true, reset_link_sent_to: "alice+new@example.com" }. Cycle 4: Thought - All steps done. Write the user-facing reply and stop the loop. Answer - 'I updated your email to alice+new@example.com and sent a fresh reset link there. Click it within 30 minutes to set a new password.' Outcome: 4 cycles, 3 tool calls. Cycle 4 ends with an Answer instead of an Action, so the loop stops. A human reviewer can audit every decision, and a downstream evaluator can score the Thoughts independently from whether the final answer was right.
summary: Worked four-cycle ReAct trace for a password reset ticket: lookup, change email, reset password, then final answer terminates the loop.

text: When To Force ReAct. ReAct is not free. Each Thought costs tokens and slows the response. Modern tool-use APIs already produce structured tool_use blocks without forcing the reasoning to be written out. Plain tool-use: model emits tool_use blocks, reasoning stays in the model's head, invisible to user / observability. ReAct: same loop, but model required to emit Thought + Action explicitly, reasoning written out as text. When to use ReAct: when you need to audit reasoning; when you need to debug failures; when you train a smaller model on the traces; when you need to build trust with the user. Once the trace is written down, it becomes a teaching signal. You can fine-tune a smaller model on big-model traces, you can show the Thoughts in a UI to make the agent feel transparent, and you can write evals that score the plan separately from the final answer.
summary: ReAct adds token cost and latency - force it only when you need audit, debug, distillation, or user trust; otherwise plain tool_use is enough.

text: Decide up front or step by step. ReAct chooses the next move iteration by iteration. Plan-Execute does the opposite: the model writes the full plan tree up front, then a simple executor walks the leaves. Same tools, same model, very different control shape. ReAct card: reactive chain S1 -> S2 -> S3 -> S4 with question-mark arrows; model picks next action each turn; no plan until step N+1 is chosen. Plan-Execute card: a Root Plan node fans out to four leaves L1, L2, L3, L4; whole plan drawn before any leaf runs; deliberate, plan tree is visible up front. Use ReAct when the next step truly depends on what just happened. Use Plan-Execute when you can name all the steps up front and just want them ordered, parallelized, or audited.
summary: ReAct decides iteratively; Plan-Execute writes the whole plan tree up front then walks it.

text: What a plan looks like. A plan is a tree. The root names the overall goal. Each leaf names a concrete tool call with the data it needs. Plan tree for ticket T4 - Resolve T4: Cancel + Refund (Root Goal) with four leaves: 1. lookup_customer (to get customer_id), 2. lookup_subscription (to get invoice_id), 3. cancel (subscription), 4. process_refund (business_rule Error -> Escalate, marked red). Tree drawn before any tool fires. Key idea: every leaf names a tool and the data it will need. The plan is structured output, not free text. The model emits four leaf objects, each with a tool name, an argument template, and a note about which earlier leaf supplies each argument. The fourth leaf flags a known business_rule snag the planner anticipates.
summary: A plan is a tree with a goal root and tool-call leaves; ticket T4 example has four leaves with the refund leaf flagged for escalation.

text: Walk the leaves. Once the plan is set, a thin executor walks the leaves in order. Each leaf calls its tool, captures the result, and feeds the result into the next leaf's arguments. The model only re-enters the loop when something the plan did not anticipate happens. Executor walking leaves 1-4 + adapted leaf 5: 1. lookup_customer -> customer_id: c-9924 (Ok). 2. lookup_subscription -> invoice_id: inv-7741 (Ok). 3. cancel -> status: cancelled (Ok). 4. process_refund -> Error: business_rule (Err). Executor adapts and adds leaf 5. escalate_human, reason: business_rule (Done). When plans break: executor catches the business_rule error and appends step 5 escalate_human. The executor did not throw the entire plan away. Leaves 1, 2, and 3 already succeeded - their results are kept. Only the failed leaf triggers a replan, and the replan appends one new leaf instead of rebuilding the tree from scratch.
summary: Executor walks plan leaves passing results forward; on failure it appends a corrective leaf rather than discarding the whole plan.
