text: Reflection: did that answer land? Reflection sits on top of any loop. After the model writes a draft, the same model (or a separate critic call) grades it. If the grade is low, the model revises. It is a cheap, model-only feedback loop that catches confidently wrong answers before they ship. Three steps: 1. Model writes draft answer - first pass, free text or structured output, may be confidently wrong. 2. Model (or critic) scores the draft 0-10 with reasoning - grade covers accuracy, format, completeness, tone; reasoning spells out the misses. 3. If score < 7, model revises and re-evaluates - revision uses the critique as a diff list; re-score decides whether to stop or loop again. Safety valve: one revision cap typical, more triggers escalation. Critic prompt sketch: `Critic: Grade the draft 0-10 on accuracy, format, completeness. Return JSON: { "score": int, "reasoning": str, "fix_list": [str] }. If Score < 7, the orchestrator runs the model again with the fix_list pasted into the prompt. Second draft is re-scored. Two failed revisions in a row escalate_human.` Cost is one extra model call per draft, plus another full call if a revision is needed. Win is catching bugs the original pass missed: math errors, missing fields, tone mismatches. Bound revisions so reflection cannot spiral into an infinite revise loop.
summary: Reflection adds a draft-score-revise loop with a score threshold of 7, a one-revision cap, and a critic that returns JSON with score, reasoning, and fix_list.
queries: What is reflection in an agent loop?

text: Pick the right loop pattern. Four shapes, one decision matrix. Plot the task on two axes: task complexity (simple vs complex) and need for auditability (low audit vs high audit). Cells: Simple Task + Low Audit -> Plain Tool-Use (cheap single-call flow). Simple Task + High Audit -> ReAct (simple task, visible reasoning). Complex Task + Low Audit -> Plan-Execute (complex plan, light audit). Complex Task + High Audit -> Plan-Execute + Reflection (complex plan, heavy audit, self-check). Task complexity grows top to bottom. Decision shortcut: single tool, trusted output -> Plain Tool-Use; few steps, need visible reasoning -> ReAct; many steps, order known -> Plan-Execute; high-stakes answer, must self-check -> Plan-Execute + Reflection. Loops are not a ladder where bigger is always better. Each extra structure costs tokens, latency, and review surface. Pick the smallest shape that handles the task and graduate only when the task forces you to.
summary: 2x2 matrix on complexity x auditability picks among Plain Tool-Use, ReAct, Plan-Execute, and Plan-Execute + Reflection.

Step 1 (red): 'Loop Terminates' - any non-success stop (max-iters/budget/halt). Step 2 (amber): 'Agent Emits Final Message To User' - 'I wasn't able to fully resolve this. A human agent will follow up.' Step 3 (cyan): 'Call escalate_human With Partial Transcript' - hands off the full chain of reasoning so the human can resume. Caption: 'Fail-Safe Pattern - Triggered By ANY Non-Success Termination'.

text: Chapter 13.23 covers loop termination. An agent loop has exactly four stop conditions: Success (model emits text without tool_use, API stop_reason becomes end_turn), Max-Iters (a hard counter cap of 10-20 force-terminates runaway loops), Budget (token/dollar cap, e.g., $0.50/run, safer than iteration count because per-step cost can vary 10x), and Explicit Stop (a tool returns halt:true or the user cancels). Success is the only one that does not need escalation. Every non-success termination must trigger the fail-safe pattern: emit a user-visible message saying a human will follow up, then call escalate_human with the partial transcript so the human can resume with full reasoning context. Production rule: no silent failures.
summary: Four loop stop conditions (success, max-iters, budget, explicit stop) and a mandatory fail-safe escalation with partial transcript for any non-success case.
queries: all ways an agent loop can end
how to end an agent loop safely

text: An agent loop must stop. There are exactly four reasons it does: Success (model emits final answer, no more tool calls), Max-Iters (hit the cap, e.g., 10), Budget (token/cost exhausted), and Explicit Stop (a tool returns a 'halt' signal, or the user cancels). Three of the four are non-success cases (out of room, out of money, or told to halt). Every production agent must detect each one and respond differently. Knowing which condition fired tells you what to do next.
summary: Four stop conditions end every agent loop: success, max-iters, budget, and explicit stop.
queries: how does an agent loop terminate
what makes an agent stop looping
how to know when agent finishes

text: Success is the subtlest stop condition. The model never says 'I'm done' out loud. It just stops asking for tools. The runtime watches the most recent response and detects the absence of any tool_use block. Iteration N-1: model emits tool_use plus text - still working, runtime runs the tool, feeds result back, loops again. Iteration N: model emits ONLY text (no tool_use). That's success - the model decided no more tools are needed. The stop reason from the API is 'end_turn' instead of 'tool_use'. The final text is the answer to the user. This is the only stop condition that does not require an escalation message; everything else is partial completion and the user must be told what happened.
summary: Success is detected when the model emits text without any tool_use block, signaled by API stop_reason end_turn.
queries: how does agent detect successful completion
how do you know the agent finished
API stop reason after agent loop

text: The max-iter cap (also called max-iters) is the dumbest safety net but the most important. A counter ticks up every loop pass. When it hits the cap, the runtime force-terminates the loop without asking the model. Production rule: 10-20 typical max-iter cap. Higher = pay-to-think; lower = miss answers. The cap protects against runaway loops where the model can't make progress and keeps re-trying the same tool. Without a cap a single buggy ticket can bill thousands of dollars in an hour.
summary: A hard iteration cap of 10-20 force-terminates runaway loops without consulting the model.
queries: how to prevent runaway agent loops
how many iterations should an agent run

text: Iteration count is a proxy for cost; the real number is dollars. A budget cap measures the total tokens (or dollars) spent across all iterations and stops the loop when the wallet is empty. Sample budget: $0.50 per agent run. At 30% ($0.15) iter 1-2 consumed; at 60% ($0.30) iter 3-4 consumed; at 100% budget exhausted - STOP at $0.50. Budget is the SAFER cap than iterations because real cost can vary 10x per iteration. One iteration that triggers a thinking burst can spend 10k tokens; the next might spend 500. Iteration count doesn't see that. A dollar budget does.
summary: A token/dollar budget cap is safer than iteration count because per-iteration cost can vary 10x.

text: Some tools are terminal by design. An escalate_human tool hands the conversation to a human and there is nothing left for the agent to do. The tool result includes a halt flag, and the runtime respects it. Tool result shape: { "tool": "escalate_human", "result": { "ticket_id": "T4", "human_assigned": true }, "halt": true }. The loop respects this signal and terminates cleanly. Similar pattern: a user cancellation injects a halt event. Explicit stops are the cleanest non-success case: the model doesn't get confused, the runtime doesn't guess, and there is a clear handoff record in the transcript.
summary: Terminal tools (e.g., escalate_human) return a halt:true flag that the runtime respects to cleanly stop the loop.
queries: how to design a halting tool

text: Any non-success termination (max-iters, budget, or explicit halt that wasn't a successful resolution) must trigger the same fail-safe pattern. Step 1: Loop terminates on any non-success stop. Step 2: Agent emits a final message to the user - 'I wasn't able to fully resolve this. A human agent will follow up.' Step 3: Call escalate_human with partial transcript - hands off the full chain of reasoning so the human can resume. Production rule: No silent failures. Every non-success termination produces a user-visible message AND an escalate_human call with the full partial transcript. Silent failures are how agent bugs hide in production for weeks. The transcript is the load-bearing artifact: it carries the agent's reasoning, every tool call, every result, so the human picking up the ticket has full context. No re-litigating from scratch.
summary: Every non-success termination must emit a user message and call escalate_human with the partial transcript - no silent failures.
queries: what to do when agent gives up
how to escalate agent to human
what happens when budget runs out

text: Chapter 13.24 introduces the agent memory taxonomy. Agent memory splits into two layers: short-term working memory (lives in active context window, discarded at session end) and long-term persistent memory (lives in external store, persists across sessions). Long-term further splits three ways: episodic (past events with timestamps), semantic (facts as key-value or vectors), and procedural (skills, recipes, cached workflows). A concrete ticket T2 snapshot (Alice, password reset + email change) shows each layer holding different information: working tracks current task state, episodic recalls past ticket #4521, semantic stores Pro tier + signup date, procedural caches the rule 'Refund > $200 -> escalate_human'. Each layer earns its place because each has different shape, retrieval pattern, update rule, and lifespan.
summary: Overview of the four-layer agent memory taxonomy: working, episodic, semantic, procedural.

text: Two Memory Layers. An agent has memory in two very different places. Short-term memory lives inside the active context window of the current conversation. Long-term memory lives outside that window and persists across sessions. SHORT-TERM (Working Memory): Lives in the active context window. The model reads and writes it every loop turn. Discarded when the session ends. LONG-TERM (Persistent Memory): Lives outside the context window in an external store. Persists across sessions and conversations. Gets loaded back in on demand. Short = this conversation. Long = everything before.
summary: Agent memory splits into short-term working memory inside the context window and long-term persistent memory in external storage.

text: Long-Term Splits Three Ways. Long-term memory is not one thing. It separates by what kind of information it holds. Three sub-types - episodic, semantic, procedural - each answer a different question. Episodic: Past Events (timestamps + content). Example: 'Past ticket #4521: failed password reset escalated for MFA bug.' Semantic: Facts (key-value or vector). Example: 'Alice = Pro tier, signed up 2024-08, prefers email.' Procedural: Skills (recipes / cached workflows). Example: 'Refund > $200 -> escalate to human.' Events answer when, facts answer what, skills answer how.
summary: Long-term memory has three sub-types: episodic for events, semantic for facts, procedural for skills.

text: The Agent Memory Taxonomy tree: one root (Agent Memory), two branches (Short-Term, Long-Term), four leaves. Short-Term has one leaf: Working (scratchpad, chapter 13.25). Long-Term has three leaves: Episodic (events, 13.26), Semantic (facts, 13.27), Procedural (skills, 13.28). Each leaf becomes its own chapter. Summary and context-management close out at 13.29. The tree is the map for the rest of the memory chapters.
summary: Visual taxonomy tree showing Agent Memory root branching into short-term and long-term, then long-term into episodic, semantic, and procedural.

Semantic: 'Alice = Pro tier, signed up 2024-08, prefers email contact, USD billing.' Procedural: 'Refund > $200 -> escalate_human. Always confirm identity before change_email.' Customer ID c-9924 and refund cap $200 reappear across memory chapters. The agent does not re-derive these each turn; it loads them from memory.

text: Each Layer Solves A Different Problem. Why have four layers and not one? Because each holds a different shape of information, with different retrieval patterns, different update rules, and different lifespans. Working: Holds the current task state across loop iterations. Episodic: Gives the agent recall of specific past events. Semantic: Provides stable customer facts without re-asking. Procedural: Caches learned routines so the agent does not re-derive them every time. Working memory tracks the current task state. Episodic memory recalls specific past events. Semantic memory stores stable facts. Procedural memory caches routines. Each layer earns its place.
summary: Each memory layer exists because it has a distinct shape, retrieval pattern, update rule, and lifespan.

text: Working memory is the in-context scratchpad the agent reads on every REASON step and updates on every OBSERVE step inside the prompt context window. It is a structured object with five stable fields (customer_context, current_goal, completed_steps, next_step, constraints) that anchor the running task. It evolves each iteration: a password-reset ticket walks from empty state through lookup_customer, change_email, reset_password, until next_step becomes null and the loop terminates. The scratchpad is discarded at task end, so anything worth keeping must be promoted to long-term memory first. Working memory lives in the prompt and is fast, cheap, ephemeral, capped at a few hundred tokens, written by the model each turn; long-term memory lives in an external store (Vector DB / Postgres), is slower and more expensive but persistent across sessions, holds millions of facts, and is written by explicit promotion logic.
summary: Chapter recap: working memory is the structured in-prompt scratchpad updated each loop turn and discarded at task end, contrasted with persistent long-term memory.
queries: how does working memory compare to long-term
what fields are in agent scratchpad
ephemeral vs persistent agent memory recap

text: Working memory is the in-context scratchpad. The agent reads it on every REASON step and updates it after every OBSERVE step. It lives inside the prompt context window itself, not in any external store. The loop around the note pad: 1. REASON: the model reads the scratchpad to decide the next tool. 2. ACT: the tool runs. 3. OBSERVE: the result comes back and the scratchpad is updated. 4. LOOP: back to step 1, this time with the updated note pad. The scratchpad is the agent's short-term reasoning trace. Lose it, and the agent has to re-derive what it was doing from scratch.
summary: Working memory is an in-context scratchpad the agent reads on REASON and writes on OBSERVE inside the prompt window.

text:
The scratchpad is not free-form text. It is a structured object with stable field names so the model can read and write specific slots. Five fields anchor the running task:
{
  "customer_context": "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro",
  "current_goal": "Reset password (email also changed)",
  "completed_steps": ["lookup_customer", "change_email"],
  "next_step": "reset_password",
  "constraints": ["refund cap $200"]
}
customer_context answers 'who', current_goal answers 'what', completed_steps answers 'what so far', next_step answers 'what next', and constraints keep the agent within policy. Same five fields appear in 13.42 observability traces.
summary: Working memory is a structured JSON object with five stable fields: customer_context, current_goal, completed_steps, next_step, constraints.

text:
Watch the scratchpad evolve across four loop turns of ticket T2 (Alice: password reset + email change). Each row is the state right after the iteration's OBSERVE step.
Iteration 1: customer_context: "" (not yet looked up), completed_steps: [], next_step: "lookup_customer".
Iteration 2: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer"], next_step: "change_email".
Iteration 3: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer", "change_email"], next_step: "reset_password".
Iteration 4: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer", "change_email", "reset_password"], next_step: null (task complete).
By iteration 4, next_step is null and the task is done. The scratchpad terminated cleanly, which is how loop termination (13.23) detects success.
summary: Scratchpad walkthrough across four iterations of a password-reset ticket, ending with next_step=null signaling task completion.

text: When the loop terminates, the scratchpad is discarded. Anything worth keeping must be promoted to long-term memory BEFORE that discard. Otherwise it is gone. The timeline: Task Start (Created) -> Iter N (Updated) -> Promote (To Long-Term) -> Task End (Discarded). A promote arrow lifts entries out of the timeline up to the Long-Term Store before discard. 'Resolved by email reset link' is a working memory entry. After promotion to episodic memory, the next session can recall it. Skip the promote, and the next session asks Alice the same question from scratch.
summary: Working memory is discarded at task end, so valuable entries must be promoted to long-term memory before the loop terminates.

text:
Side-by-side comparison of in-context scratchpad and external long-term store. Different lifespan, different capacity, different writer.
Lives In: Working = Prompt Context Window; Long-Term = External Store (Vector DB / Postgres).
Written By: Working = Model On Each Loop Turn; Long-Term = Explicit Promotion Logic.
Capacity: Working = A Few Hundred Tokens; Long-Term = Millions Of Facts / Events.
Lifespan: Working = Discarded When Task Ends; Long-Term = Persistent Across Sessions.
Working memory is fast, cheap, and ephemeral. Long-term memory is slower, more expensive, but persistent. Agents need both, and the bridge between them is the promote step covered in the next chapters.
summary: Working memory and long-term memory differ in location, writer, capacity, and lifespan; agents need both connected by an explicit promote step.

Stage 1 (write, top row, yellow): Event 'Failed Password Reset' box -> arrow -> Embed [1024 floats] box -> arrow -> Vector DB box (purple, labeled Section 11.7 / 11.8). Stage 2 (read, bottom row, cyan): Query 'What past issues?' box -> arrow -> Embed (Same Encoder) box -> arrow -> ANN Search Top-K Events box (purple). Shows that events and queries pass through the same encoder before vector DB write or ANN retrieval.

text: Chapter 13.26 covers episodic memory: time-stamped past events stored as embeddings in a vector database. Episodes (e.g., Alice's four past tickets) are embedded with the same encoder used for queries, then retrieved via ANN search (HNSW/IVF). Pattern: on new conversation, host retrieves top-3 relevant episodes and injects into system prompt - this is how ChatGPT Memory creates appearance of memory through retrieve-and-inject. Each entry is structured (id, customer_id, timestamp, summary, tools_used, outcome, embedding) not raw transcripts. Pruning is mandatory: drop old clean resolutions, keep escalations, summarize duplicates, honor compliance deletion.
summary: Episodic memory = time-stamped events embedded in a vector DB, recalled via ANN search and injected into prompts, with structured entries and a pruning policy.
queries: how agent memory of past events works
vector DB plus retrieve inject pattern
what does episodic memory consist of

text: Episodic memory holds specific past events. Each event has a timestamp and short description of what happened. Example: agent has four episodes on file for Alice the customer. 2026-03-04 Failed Password Reset - escalated due to legacy MFA bug. 2026-04-12 Slow Dashboard - complained about loading time, resolved with cache fix. 2026-05-01 Invoice Request - requested May invoice, sent successfully. 2026-05-15 Password + Email Reset (T2) - current ticket. Episodes answer 'what happened, when?'. They are NOT the customer's stable facts (that is semantic memory) and NOT how-to recipes (that is procedural memory).
summary: Episodic memory stores time-stamped past events distinct from semantic facts or procedural recipes.
queries: what is episodic memory in agents

text: Episodic memory lives in a vector database. Each event becomes an embedding; a search query becomes an embedding; an ANN index returns the most similar past events. Stage 1 (write): Event 'Failed Password Reset' -> Embed [1024 floats] -> Vector DB. Stage 2 (read): Query 'What past issues?' -> Embed (same encoder) -> ANN Search -> Top-K Events. Vector storage substrate covered in Section 11.7 (IVF) / 11.8 (HNSW). Here used as layer below episodic memory: write once, search by similarity from then on.
summary: Episodic events are embedded and stored in a vector database, then retrieved via ANN search using a query embedding from the same encoder.
queries: how do agents search past events
same encoder for query and event
how do agents recall past episodes

text: Recall before reasoning. When a new conversation starts, the agent retrieves relevant past episodes BEFORE reasoning about the current ticket. Host system fetches top-3 events and injects them into the system prompt as context. Steps: 1. User message arrives - T2: 'I forgot my password and my email changed.' 2. Build recall query - Query = 'current ticket summary + customer_id c-9924'. 3. Retrieve top-3 episodes - ANN search returns 3 most similar past events. 4. Inject into system prompt - episodes added to prompt as context block. 5. Agent reasons with past context - past MFA-bug episode informs current approach. ChatGPT Memory works similarly: host fetches relevant facts on each new conversation and injects them. The model does not 'remember' - the host system gives it the appearance of memory through retrieval-and-inject.
summary: Host retrieves top-3 relevant past episodes via ANN search and injects them into the system prompt so the agent can reason with past context.
queries: how does agent recall past events
how is past context given to LLM

text:
Event log entry shape. Each episode entry is a structured record. Agent does not store raw transcripts - stores summaries + tools used + outcome + embedding for retrieval.

{
  "id": "ev-7821",
  "customer_id": "c-9924",
  "timestamp": "2026-03-04T14:22:00Z",
  "summary": "Failed password reset escalated due to legacy MFA bug.",
  "tools_used": ["reset_password", "escalate_human"],
  "outcome": "human_resolved",
  "embedding": "[1024 floats]"
}

`summary` is what the model reads. `embedding` is what ANN search uses. `tools_used` and `outcome` let analytics ask 'which past escalations involved MFA bugs?'.
summary: An episodic entry is a structured JSON record with id, customer_id, timestamp, summary, tools_used, outcome, and embedding - not raw transcripts.

text: Memory has to forget. Unbounded growth wrecks retrieval quality. Pruning policy decides what to keep and drop. Four rules cover most cases: (1) Age > 12 months AND outcome = resolved cleanly -> Drop. (2) Age > 12 months AND outcome = escalated -> Keep (rare patterns valuable). (3) Aggregated similar events -> Summarize into one parent and drop children. (4) Manual customer request -> Delete on demand (compliance). Compliance-driven deletion is non-negotiable. A customer can request their entire episodic log be wiped, and the agent must honor it within whatever window the regulation requires.
summary: Pruning policy drops old clean resolutions, keeps old escalations, summarizes duplicates, and honors compliance deletion requests.
queries: what to keep vs drop in agent memory
how do agents forget old events
right to be forgotten agent memory

text: Chapter 13.27 Semantic Memory - Learned Facts. Covers: (1) semantic memory holds stable 'currently true' facts, distinct from time-stamped episodic events; (2) the customer profile card shape with typed fields like customer_id, tier, preferred_contact, billing_currency, and a nested preferences bag; (3) two storage substrates - key-value/Postgres for typed exact-match fields and vector store for free-text similarity facts, used together in production; (4) profile growth from Day 1 to Day 30 to Day 90 via confirmed observations only, never single-message guesses; (5) the 6-months test for what counts as a fact: stable preferences, identity, constraints go in; transient mood, one-time requests, and guesses stay out.
summary: Chapter overview: semantic memory stores long-lived facts in structured plus vector stores, growing via confirmed observations and gated by the 6-months test.
queries: how semantic memory works end to end
what is semantic memory in LLM agents

text: Semantic memory holds stable facts about a customer. Different from episodic memory: an episode is 'this happened on this date'; a fact is 'this is currently true'. Episodic fact (time-stamped event): On 2026-03-04, Alice opened a ticket about a password reset. Semantic fact (stable state): Alice prefers email contact. Same person, different memory layer. Episodic answers 'what happened?', semantic answers 'what is true?'. Both are needed; they live in different stores with different update rules.
summary: Semantic memory stores stable current-truth facts about a user, distinct from time-stamped episodic events.
queries: what is semantic memory in agents

current state vs historical event memory

summary: Semantic memory is shaped as a customer profile card with typed fields plus a nested preferences bag.
queries: typed fields vs nested fields agent

text: Key-Value Or Vector. Two storage substrates serve different fact shapes. Most production agents use BOTH: structured for the spine, vector for the long-tail freetext. Structured (Key-Value / Postgres): use when fields are typed, predictable, queried by exact match. Example: customer_id -> profile lookup. Vector (Embedding + Similarity): use when facts are free-text, queried by meaning. Example: 'User has a dairy allergy' embedded for retrieval. The spine (typed profile) is small and exact. The long-tail (free-form preferences and constraints) is large and fuzzy. Use the right tool for each shape.
summary: Semantic memory uses key-value storage for typed exact-match fields plus vector storage for free-text similarity retrieval.
queries: key-value vs vector storage agent memory
when to use vector store agent
exact match vs semantic match storage

text: How The Profile Fills Up. Semantic memory grows over time. Alice's profile at signup has 4 fields. By day 30 it has 5. By day 90 it has nested preferences. Growth comes from confirmed observations. Day 1 (Signup): customer_id: c-9924, tier: Pro, signed_up: 2024-08, primary_email: alice@example.com. Day 30: (all Day-1 fields) + preferred_contact: email, learned from 'please email me at...'. Day 90: (all Day-30 fields) + preferences.skip_onboarding_emails: true, learned from user opt-out click. Profile fills only on CONFIRMED observations. A guess from one ambiguous message does not get written. A repeated preference observed across sessions does.
summary: The profile grows incrementally from 4 to 5 to nested fields as the agent confirms observations across days.
queries: Day 1 vs Day 30 vs Day 90 profile
when to write fact to memory
agent learning user preferences over time

text: What Counts As A Fact? Rule of thumb: if it would still be true in 6 months, write it to semantic memory. Otherwise leave it in the working scratchpad or skip it entirely. Write to semantic: stable preferences (always email), confirmed identity (Alice is account owner), constraints (dairy allergy). Do not write: transient mood ('frustrated today'), one-time requests ('send a copy this once'), guesses (uncertain inferences). 6-Months Test: would knowing this still help six months from now? If yes, write it. If no, it belongs in working memory or the chat history and should expire naturally.
summary: The 6-months test decides what to write: stable preferences, identity, constraints go in; transient moods, one-offs, and guesses stay out.

Stage 5 Use Recipe (scaffolding). Arrows connect each stage left to right. New ticket text is embedded, ANN search runs against the recipe library trigger embeddings, and the top-one match is surfaced for the agent to use as scaffolding.

text: Procedural memory stores skills (how-to recipes) separately from facts. The customer-support agent maintains a small library of named recipes, each with a trigger embedding, an ordered step list, success_rate, and uses count. New tickets are embedded and matched via ANN search against trigger embeddings; the top-1 match becomes a scaffold that the agent adapts to the ticket. No match means fall back to first-principles reasoning, which can be mined into a future recipe. Versus baking instructions into the system prompt, procedural memory is retrieved on demand (lower token cost), externally stored, and updatable from outcomes (success_rate drives rewrites). Use prompting for universal rules and procedural memory for task-specific recipes.
summary: Chapter shows procedural memory as a retrievable, updatable library of how-to recipes that complements semantic facts and static system prompts.
queries: what is procedural memory in LLM agents
how do agents learn and reuse skills
what is the full procedural memory workflow
how does procedural memory work end to end

text: Procedural memory encodes skills (HOW to do something), not facts about the world. Contrast: a semantic fact says 'refund cap is $200' (WHAT is true). A procedural skill says 'When a refund > $200 is requested, look up customer tier first. Enterprise customers self-approve up to $500. Otherwise call escalate_human with urgency=Medium' (WHAT TO DO). The agent uses both: the fact gives the $200 cap, the procedure says what to do when the cap is exceeded.
summary: Procedural memory stores how-to recipes (skills) while semantic memory stores what-is-true facts, and agents use them together.

text: The recipe library is a small set of named workflows the customer-support agent reuses. Each recipe has a trigger condition and an ordered step list. Four example recipes: 1) Handle Refund Request - trigger: Customer asks for refund, 3 steps. 2) Resolve Password Issue - trigger: Login / password error, 4 steps. 3) Escalate Billing Dispute - trigger: Disputed invoice or charge, 2 steps. 4) Resolve Sync Failure - trigger: Data out-of-sync across devices, 5 steps. Each used to be a prompt-engineering exercise; once it stabilized, it got moved into procedural memory.
summary: A recipe library is a set of named, trigger-and-step workflows that an agent reuses for common tasks.

text: Recipe retrieval pipeline: a new ticket gets embedded; ANN search runs against the recipe library's trigger_embedding field; the top-1 match is surfaced (or 'no match' if similarity is below threshold). Five stages: New Ticket (free text) -> Embed (1024 floats) -> ANN Search (vs library) -> Top-1 (or no match) -> Use Recipe (scaffolding). The agent uses the retrieved recipe as a scaffold then adapts to specifics. If no recipe matches, agent falls back to first-principles reasoning, and that run becomes a candidate to mine into a new recipe.
summary: Agent retrieves a relevant recipe by embedding the ticket and running ANN search against stored trigger embeddings, falling back to reasoning when no match exists.

text:
A recipe is a structured record. Example shape:
{
  "id": "recipe-refund",
  "name": "Handle refund request",
  "trigger_embedding": "[1024 floats]",
  "steps": [
    "lookup_customer to get customer_id and tier",
    "if tier == Enterprise: auto-approve up to $500; else $200",
    "lookup_subscription for invoice_id",
    "process_refund(invoice_id, reason); if business_rule error: escalate_human"
  ],
  "success_rate": 0.92,
  "uses": 187
}
Trigger embedding is for retrieval; steps are for execution; success_rate and uses drive analytics and self-improvement. success_rate = 0.92 means 92% of runs produced a clean outcome. uses = 187 means the agent has run the recipe 187 times. A falling success_rate triggers a rewrite review.
summary: A recipe record stores an id, name, trigger embedding, ordered steps, success_rate, and uses count - enabling retrieval, execution, and self-improvement.

text: Prompting vs procedural memory comparison: 1) Where it lives - Prompting: inside the system prompt. Procedural: external store, retrieved on demand. 2) Token cost - Prompting: every conversation pays the prompt tokens. Procedural: only retrieved when relevant. 3) Can learn? - Prompting: static, cannot update from outcomes. Procedural: updatable, success_rate drives rewrite. 4) Best for - Prompting: universal rules (tone, format). Procedural: task-specific recipes. Prompting stays great for universal rules - tone, format, safety constraints. Procedural memory wins for task-specific recipes that change with real-world outcomes. Use both deliberately.
summary: Prompting is best for static universal rules while procedural memory is best for retrievable, learnable task-specific recipes - and serious agents use both.

text: Rolling summary technique in three stages: Stage 1 conversation at fifty percent capacity, Stage 2 oldest half summarized into a small purple summary block, Stage 3 capacity drops back to twenty five percent so the conversation can continue. Arrows connect the three stages. Repeats every time the window hits 50%. The summary itself grows slowly as older summaries are re-compressed.

text: Hierarchical summary tree: raw turn batches at the bottom layer (leaves of 10 turns each, labeled T1, T3, T5, ...) become per-batch summaries at the middle layer (Batch Summary 1, 2, 3), which combine into a single meta-summary at the top covering the whole conversation. Three branches feed into the meta-summary.
summary: Tree diagram showing leaves of raw turn batches feeding per-batch summaries feeding a single meta-summary at the top.
queries: batch summary to meta summary chart

text: Four threshold bands across a context window utilization bar: twenty five percent Do Nothing (green - plenty of room), fifty percent Compress Half (yellow - summarize oldest 25%), seventy five percent Aggressive Summarize (orange - summarize older half + start hybrid retrieval), ninety percent Panic Drop (red - drop everything except summary + last 5 turns + top retrieved). Each band labeled with its percentage above and action below.
summary: Diagram of four context utilization bands - 25% do-nothing, 50% compress, 75% aggressive, 90% panic - with escalating actions.
queries: do nothing compress aggressive panic diagram
green yellow orange red threshold bar

text: Chapter 13.29 covers summary memory and context window management. Long conversations overflow context (8k fills by turn 30 at 250 tokens/turn). Rolling summary compresses the oldest half at 50% capacity into a 2-paragraph block, dropping usage back to 25%. Hierarchical summary scales to week-long sessions with leaves (raw batches), mid (per-batch summary), and top (meta-summary). Selection strategies after summarization: recency (last N), relevance (similar to query), hybrid (top-K recent + top-K relevant) - hybrid wins in production. Threshold bands: 25% do nothing, 50% compress half, 75% aggressive + hybrid retrieval, 90% panic drop. Final prompt stack: system prompt, memory block, conversation summary, recent turns, retrieved turns, working memory, current user message.
summary: Chapter summary: techniques and thresholds for compressing conversation history (rolling, hierarchical, recency/relevance/hybrid) and assembling the final prompt under a token budget.
queries: summary memory and context window management overview
all techniques to manage long chat context
what stays in prompt after summary

text: 100 turns won't fit. Long conversations are not free. With an 8k token context window and ~250 tokens per turn, the window fills by turn 30. Past that, something has to give. A horizontal bar diagram shows the 8k token context filling up: by turn 30 the bar is at capacity, leaving turns 31-100 unable to fit. Past turn 30 the agent must compress old turns, drop them, or both. The rest of the chapter is about HOW to manage that ceiling.
summary: Long conversations exceed context windows: at 250 tokens per turn an 8k window fills by turn 30, forcing compression or dropping.
queries: why do long conversations run out of context
how many turns fit in 8k context window
what happens when context window fills
agent memory must compress or drop

text: Compress the oldest half: the rolling-summary technique. When the window hits 50% capacity, summarize the oldest half into a 2-paragraph block. The summary replaces the raw turns. Capacity drops to 25%. Repeat every 50%. Three stages: Stage 1 - 50% capacity reached. Stage 2 - summarize oldest half into a small summary block. Stage 3 - back to 25% capacity. The model itself usually generates the summary (a 2-paragraph LLM call per compression cycle). The summary lives in the conversation history at the spot where the raw turns used to be. The summary itself grows slowly as older summaries are re-compressed.
summary: Rolling-summary compresses the oldest half of a conversation into a short summary block whenever capacity hits 50%, returning to 25% used.
queries: two paragraph summary of old turns
LLM call to summarize older turns
summary lives where raw turns were

text: Summaries of summaries. For week-long conversations, flat rolling summary degrades. The hierarchical approach organizes raw turns into batches (leaves), per-batch summaries (mid), and a meta-summary of the whole conversation (top). Leaves = raw turn batches (10 turns each). Mid = per-batch summary. Top = meta. Hierarchical wins for week-long conversations because the meta-summary stays small while you can still zoom into specific batches when needed.
summary: Hierarchical summarization layers raw turn batches under per-batch summaries under a meta-summary, scaling better than flat rolling summary for long sessions.
queries: tree structure for long chat memory

text: Most recent vs most relevant. After summarization, what stays in the prompt? Three strategies, each with a different tradeoff between continuity and topical fit. Recency: keep most-recent N turns raw, drop summary of older turns; good for continuity-heavy conversations. Relevance: keep turns most similar to current query, drop summary of less-relevant turns; good for long-range topical recall. Hybrid: keep top-K recent + top-K relevant, drop summary of everything else; best default in practice. Hybrid wins in production because most conversations have BOTH continuity needs (the user said "this" earlier) AND topical recall needs (the user asked a similar thing 80 turns ago).
summary: Three selection strategies for what stays in the prompt after summarization - recency, relevance, and the hybrid mix which works best in production.
queries: what turns to keep in prompt
hybrid retrieval recent and relevant turns
top k recent plus top k relevant
drop older turns or keep relevant

text: Summarize at 50% capacity. Production agents trigger compression at fixed thresholds, not on demand. Four bands cover the production rule. 25% - Do Nothing - plenty of room. 50% - Compress Half - summarize oldest 25%. 75% - Aggressive - summarize older half + start hybrid retrieval. 90% - Panic - drop everything except summary + last 5 turns + top retrieved. Threshold-based compression is production-friendly because the cost is predictable. On-demand compression can hide behind a single user turn that suddenly explodes the window.
summary: Production agents compress context at four fixed utilization thresholds - 25%, 50%, 75%, 90% - escalating from do-nothing to panic-drop for predictable cost.
queries: fixed thresholds vs on demand compression
panic mode last 5 turns plus summary
summarize oldest 25 percent at half full

text: Context is where real work happens. Memory plus context-engineering decide what the model sees on EVERY turn. Chapter 13.6 covered the prompt-assembly stack. This chapter closes out by tying the memory layers into that stack. The assembly order: 1. System Prompt - static + recipe from procedural memory. 2. Memory Block - retrieved episodes + profile card from semantic. 3. Conversation Summary - rolling / hierarchical summary of old turns. 4. Recent Turns - last N raw turns (recency). 5. Retrieved Turns - top-K relevant turns from older history (hybrid). 6. Working Memory - current scratchpad state. 7. Current User Message - what the user just said. Every layer comes from somewhere. Memory chapters 13.24-13.28 supplied the sources; 13.6's assembly stack joins them into a single prompt; this chapter closes the loop with summarization that keeps it all under the token budget.
summary: Final prompt is assembled in a 7-layer order combining system prompt, memory sources, conversation summary, recent/retrieved turns, scratchpad, and user message.
queries: what goes into agent prompt every turn
system prompt then memory block order
where does conversation summary go in prompt
recent turns vs retrieved turns in prompt
current user message position in prompt
how memory ties into context engineering

text: Diagram: single agent loaded with five different jobs (retrieve, classify, refund, troubleshoot, escalate) clustered around one central agent box labeled '5 Jobs On One System Prompt'. Visualizes the crowded role assignment that creates a performance ceiling for one agent.
summary: Diagram of one overloaded agent surrounded by five conflicting jobs.
queries: retrieve classify refund troubleshoot escalate diagram
what does overloaded agent look like

text: Diagram: parallel execution. A central Dispatcher hub dispatches three independent sub-tasks to three separate agents running in parallel - Agent A (lookup_customer, 1.2s), Agent B (lookup_subscription, 1.4s), Agent C (search_kb, 1.6s). Wall-clock time equals the slowest agent (1.6s) rather than the sum (4.2s).
summary: Diagram of a dispatcher fanning out to three parallel agents with max-latency wall clock.
queries: Agent A B C parallel timing

text: Diagram: two-layer planner/worker architecture. Planner Agent at the top decomposes the task and dispatches sub-tasks via arrows to two worker agents below - Worker 1 (Lookup Customer) and Worker 2 (Process Refund). Workers execute and return results to the planner.
summary: Diagram of a planner agent on top dispatching to two worker agents below.

text: Why multi-agent. One generalist agent hits a precision ceiling when its system prompt covers many roles. Three reasons to go multi-agent: specialization (one focused agent per role with 1-2 tools each), parallelism (independent sub-tasks run concurrently so wall-clock = max not sum, e.g. 4.2s sequential collapses to 1.6s parallel), and planner/worker layering (planner decomposes, workers execute, neither crosses the line). But multi-agent costs LLM calls, state, and failure surface, so avoid it for single-step tasks, context-continuity tasks, latency-critical loops, and small dev teams.
summary: Use multi-agent for specialization, parallelism, or planner/worker; otherwise stick with one agent.
queries: one agent vs many agents tradeoff

text: Why one agent sometimes isn't enough. A single agent loaded with all customer-support roles (retrieve, classify, refund, troubleshoot, escalate) has a crowded system prompt. Each role pulls the prompt in a different direction, creating a performance ceiling. Every new role added to the system prompt costs precision on every other role. At some point, splitting roles into separate agents beats stuffing them into one.
summary: Stuffing many roles into one agent's system prompt creates a precision ceiling.
queries: problem with single agent doing everything
why split agent into multiple agents
does adding roles hurt agent accuracy
when does one LLM agent break down
why customer support agent needs splitting

text: Specialization: one agent per role. Replace the crowded single agent with 3 focused agents: Billing Specialist (tools: lookup_subscription, process_refund; handles refunds, invoices, tier changes), Troubleshooting Specialist (tools: search_kb, lookup_customer; diagnoses errors, outages, sync failures), and Triage Router (tool: lookup_customer; classifies each ticket and routes to specialist). Each specialist has 1-2 tools, not the full 8-tool inventory. Smaller surface = fewer wrong-tool errors. The triage router picks which specialist a ticket goes to.
summary: Replace one generalist with focused specialist agents, each with tight prompt and tiny tool set.
queries: billing specialist troubleshooting specialist triage router
why smaller tool set per agent
how specialist agents reduce wrong-tool errors

text: Run independent tasks at the same time. Some sub-tasks have no data dependencies; run them on separate agents concurrently. Wall-clock cost is max(sub-task latency), not the sum. Example: Agent A runs lookup_customer in 1.2s, Agent B runs lookup_subscription in 1.4s, Agent C runs search_kb in 1.6s. Sequential = 1.2 + 1.4 + 1.6 = 4.2s. Parallel = max(1.2, 1.4, 1.6) = 1.6s. Almost 3x faster. The win compounds as you add more independent sub-tasks.
summary: Parallel agents on independent sub-tasks cut wall-clock time from sum to max.
queries: wall clock time max vs sum
how much faster is parallel agents

text: Planner vs Worker. Some agents are good at decomposing tasks; others are good at executing single steps. Separating produces a 2-layer setup: Planner Agent at top decomposes the task and dispatches sub-tasks; Worker 1 (e.g., lookup_customer) and Worker 2 (e.g., process_refund) execute single steps. The planner does NOT execute steps. The workers do NOT decompose. Each agent stays on its layer. This separation makes both layers more reliable than one mixed agent.
summary: Split planning from execution into a two-layer planner/worker architecture for reliability.

text: Don't multi-agent a small problem. Multi-agent costs more LLM calls, more state to track, more places for failure. Four anti-patterns where one agent is right: Single-Step Tasks (just call the tool; adding a second agent doubles latency for zero gain), Context Continuity Tasks (if the same context carries through every step, hand-off loses information), Latency-Critical Loops (each agent adds 1-2 seconds of reasoning; a 100ms SLA loop cannot afford this), Small Dev Teams (multi-agent failures in 13.35 are hard to debug; a 2-person team will drown). Decision Rule: Multi-Agent IF Specialization OR Parallelism OR Planner/Worker fits the workload. Otherwise single agent.
summary: Four anti-patterns where multi-agent hurts, plus the decision rule for when to use it.
queries: decision rule multi-agent vs single agent

text: Hub and spoke topology diagram: an orchestrator agent box at the top center labeled 'Orchestrator: Plans + Aggregates' has bidirectional arrows to three worker agents (Worker 1, Worker 2, Worker 3) at the bottom, each labeled 'One Sub-Task'. There are no edges between the workers themselves - all communication flows through the orchestrator.
summary: Diagram of the hub-and-spoke orchestrator-worker topology with bidirectional arrows and no worker-to-worker edges.

text: Chapter 13.31 covers the orchestrator-worker multi-agent pattern: a central orchestrator runs three phases (Plan, Dispatch, Aggregate) over N workers in a hub-and-spoke topology. Workers run their own ReAct loops on isolated sub-tasks and return structured results without talking to each other. A T3 ticket trace shows parallel dispatch where latency equals max worker time plus plan plus aggregate. Three aggregation patterns - concatenation, voting, synthesis - cover the production design space, with synthesis as the natural-language default.
summary: Summarizes the orchestrator-worker pattern - hub-and-spoke planning, parallel worker dispatch, and three aggregation strategies.
queries: what is the orchestrator worker design
key ideas in orchestrator worker chapter

text: The orchestrator-worker pattern is the most common production multi-agent shape. A central orchestrator plans and aggregates; workers each handle one sub-task. Hub-and-spoke topology: workers only talk to the orchestrator, never to each other. The orchestrator owns coordination cost; workers stay simple. No edges between workers means no peer-to-peer chatter and no shared-state corruption.
summary: Introduces the orchestrator-worker hub-and-spoke topology where one planner coordinates N workers that never talk to each other.
queries: hub and spoke multi agent topology
why workers don't talk to each other
most common multi agent production pattern
orchestrator role in multi agent system
peer to peer agent communication problems

text: The orchestrator runs three phases per task. Plan: break the incoming task into 3-5 sub-tasks each workable in isolation. Dispatch: send each sub-task to its assigned worker agent. Aggregate: when workers return, merge results into a single coherent answer. Each phase is a separate LLM call with a distinct system prompt - the same agent loops the model through three different mindsets. The 3 phases give the orchestrator 3 separate minds: one to plan, one to dispatch, one to aggregate. Each is simpler than a single do-everything prompt.
summary: Breaks down the orchestrator's three phases - Plan, Dispatch, Aggregate - each a separate LLM call with its own mindset.
queries: how does orchestrator break down tasks
why use multiple LLM calls for orchestrator
simpler prompts vs do everything prompt
how many sub tasks should orchestrator create

text: Each worker takes one sub-task description, runs its own agent loop within its tool set, and returns a structured result. Example: input sub-task 'Lookup customer tier for c-9924' goes into a worker agent that runs an internal Reason -> Act -> Observe loop within its tool set only, and returns { tier: "Pro", usage: "heavy" }. Workers do NOT talk to each other. If Worker A's result feeds Worker B's input, the orchestrator does that chaining. This is what makes parallel dispatch safe.
summary: Explains how a single worker executes its sub-task via its own ReAct loop and returns a structured result, never talking to peers.
queries: how does a worker agent execute
can workers talk to each other
how does orchestrator chain worker results

text: Trace for Ticket T3 = 'Dashboard slow + 500 errors'. Step 1 Orchestrator: receives T3, plans 3 sub-tasks: A) search_kb for 'dashboard slow'; B) search_kb for '500 errors'; C) lookup_customer for usage tier. Step 2 Worker A: returns top-3 knowledge base articles on dashboard performance. Step 3 Worker B: returns top-3 knowledge base articles on 500 errors. Step 4 Worker C: returns Alice's tier (Pro) + usage stats (heavy dashboard user). Step 5 Orchestrator: aggregates - composes a single answer combining knowledge base hits + customer context, recommends dashboard optimization and escalates 500 errors to engineering. Workers A, B, and C ran in parallel - steps 2-4 happened roughly at the same time. Total latency = max(worker time) + plan + aggregate, not sum.
summary: Walks through a concrete T3 ticket trace showing 3 parallel workers dispatched and aggregated, with latency = max not sum.

text: Aggregation - merging worker outputs into a single answer - has three common patterns. Concatenation: stitch all worker outputs together in order. Simple but verbose. Best for quick prototypes; outputs already well-sectioned. Voting: workers each propose; orchestrator picks majority. Good for classification. Best for categorical outputs; hallucination reduction. Synthesis: orchestrator reads all worker outputs and writes a single coherent answer. Most common, most expensive. Best as production default for natural-language answers. Synthesis is the production default for natural-language answers. Voting wins when workers are doing classification. Concatenation is fine for structured outputs that need no rephrasing.
summary: Compares three aggregation patterns - concatenation, voting, synthesis - and when to pick each.

text: Three-level agent hierarchy diagram: at the top, a single green Supervisor box. It connects down with edges to three middle-level Sub-Supervisor boxes positioned at x=120, 280, 440 in the viewBox. Each Sub-Supervisor connects down to two Leaf specialist boxes offset by -40 and +40 from the parent's x. Edges are planar with no crossings. Caption: Top = Supervisor. Mid = Sub-Supervisors. Leaves = Specialists (Tools).
summary: Diagram showing the three-level supervisor / sub-supervisor / leaf tree with planar parent-child edges.
queries: what does the agent hierarchy diagram show

where do tools sit in hierarchy

text: Hierarchical multi-agent is a tree of supervisors delegating to sub-supervisors and leaf specialists, with three levels as the sweet spot. Each supervisor at every level runs the same five-step loop: Receive, Plan, Route Down, Aggregate, Return Up, with routing as the new responsibility versus orchestrator-worker. Pick hierarchical when the domain has genuine sub-specialties (billing, troubleshooting); flatten when depth exceeds 3. A concrete customer-support tree has Support Supervisor over Billing, Troubleshooting, and Escalation sub-supervisors with narrow-tool leaves. Failures bubble up: a leaf escalates to its supervisor, which may retry or escalate further, eventually reaching the Escalation Supervisor that calls escalate_human - the safety net that prevents silent stalls.
summary: Chapter overview of hierarchical multi-agent: tree topology, per-supervisor five-step loop, decision rule vs orchestrator-worker, support-agent example, and bottom-up escalation safety net.

text: Hierarchical multi-agent is a tree with multiple levels of delegation. A top supervisor delegates to sub-supervisors which delegate to leaf specialists. Each leaf has 1-2 narrow tools. This is different from orchestrator-worker which has only a single level of workers. Three levels is the practical sweet spot. Two levels is just orchestrator-worker renamed. Four levels usually signals over-engineering.
summary: Hierarchical agents form a multi-level tree of supervisors delegating down to leaf specialists, with three levels as the sweet spot.
queries: how many levels in agent hierarchy
why three levels in hierarchical agents
what is a leaf specialist agent
when is two-level hierarchy just orchestrator-worker

text: At each level a supervisor does five things: Receive a sub-task from its parent. Plan whether a single child handles it or break it into smaller sub-tasks. Route Down by picking the child agent(s) best matched to the sub-task. Aggregate by waiting for children and combining their results. Return Up the aggregated result to the parent supervisor. Every supervisor performs the same five-step loop at its own level. Routing is the new responsibility versus orchestrator-worker - a supervisor must know which child agent fits which sub-task type.
summary: Each supervisor runs a five-step loop - Receive, Plan, Route Down, Aggregate, Return Up - with routing as the new responsibility.
queries: what does a supervisor agent do at each level
what is route down in agents
supervisor loop receive plan route aggregate return
how to pick child agent for sub-task
supervisor decides single child or split

text: Choosing hierarchical over orchestrator-worker is a domain shape question. Pick Orchestrator-Worker when workers are peer specialists at one level, example: 3 knowledge base search workers run in parallel. Pick Hierarchical when the domain has clear sub-domains, example: billing has refund / invoice / tier-change as sub-areas. Avoid Hierarchical when tree depth > 3 levels - usually means over-engineering; flatten the design. Most production customer-support agents fit hierarchical because billing and troubleshooting are genuinely different sub-domains with different tools and policies.
summary: Pick hierarchical when the domain has real sub-specialties; pick orchestrator-worker for peer workers; flatten anything past three levels.
queries: when to use hierarchical agents vs orchestrator-worker
when domain has sub-specialties use hierarchical
avoid hierarchical when tree too deep

text: The customer-support agent fits hierarchical cleanly. Root: Support Supervisor - all-tickets entry point that classifies and routes. Sub-supervisors: Billing Supervisor with leaves Refund Specialist, Invoice Specialist, Tier-Change Specialist. Troubleshooting Supervisor with leaves Database Specialist, Network Specialist. Escalation Supervisor with leaf Human Hand-Off (escalate_human). Each leaf specialist uses 1-3 tools from the canonical 8-tool inventory: refund specialists use process_refund + lookup_subscription, network specialists use search_kb, escalation calls escalate_human.
summary: Concrete support tree: Support Supervisor routes to Billing, Troubleshooting, Escalation sub-supervisors, each with specialist leaves using narrow tool sets.
queries: example customer support hierarchical agent tree
what tools does refund specialist use

text: A leaf specialist that cannot finish escalates up to its supervisor. The supervisor decides: retry with a different leaf, or escalate to its own parent. Eventually a top-level escalation reaches the Escalation Supervisor which calls escalate_human. Worked trace: Refund Specialist hits business_rule error on a $250 refund and escalates up. Billing Supervisor considers Tier-Change Leaf, rules it out, escalates up. Support Supervisor routes to Escalation Supervisor. Escalation Supervisor calls escalate_human with full trace. Escalation up the tree is the safety net; without it a leaf failure becomes a silent stall.
summary: Failures bubble up the tree - a refund leaf error escalates through billing and root supervisors until the Escalation Supervisor calls escalate_human.

text: Hand-off ring topology diagram: four agents arranged in a circle - Triage at top, Billing at right, Escalation at bottom, Troubleshooting at left. Each agent is connected by bidirectional arrows to its two neighbors forming a ring, plus one diagonal dashed cross-edge connecting Triage to Troubleshooting. Shows peer-to-peer transfer with no central parent and illustrates that any agent can route directly to allowed peers via its handoffs list.

text: Hand-offs (Swarm pattern) let an agent finish its part and return the next agent, with the runtime switching control directly - no central orchestrator. Each agent declares a handoffs list of allowed peers. The hand-off package carries full conversation history, a working memory snapshot, and a hand-off reason so the receiver picks up cleanly. Example T4 ticket flows Triage to Billing to Escalation, each step carrying full context. Topology is a peer ring/graph - fitting equal-level domains, in contrast with tree topology for nested domains.
summary: Chapter on Swarm-style hand-offs: agents return the next agent, pass a full context package, and form peer ring topologies versus orchestrator trees.
queries: ring vs tree agent topology overview
what does a hand-off package contain

text: Hand-offs use the Swarm pattern (now in the OpenAI Agents SDK): instead of an orchestrator routing sub-tasks, an agent finishes its part and RETURNS the next agent. The runtime then switches control directly. No central coordinator. Orchestrator-Worker style (delegation): orchestrator picks who handles next, waits for result, picks again - central control. Swarm/Hand-off style: agent A returns 'next = agent B', loop switches control directly, no central coordinator. Hand-off is decentralized; orchestration is centralized. Both ship to production - the pick depends on whether you want a single brain or peer-to-peer routing.
summary: Hand-offs are the Swarm pattern where an agent returns the next agent to run, decentralizing control versus a central orchestrator.
queries: what is the swarm pattern in agents
how does OpenAI Agents SDK do hand-offs
what does it mean to return the next agent
central coordinator vs no coordinator agents
when to pick swarm over orchestrator

text:
Agents as routes. Each agent declares which other agents it can hand off to via a handoffs field - similar to routes in a web framework. Shape:

Agent {
  name: "triage",
  instructions: "Classify intent. Hand off to billing for refund/invoice, troubleshooting for errors.",
  handoffs: [billing_agent, troubleshooting_agent]
}

name and instructions are this agent's own identity. handoffs is the set of other agents this agent can transfer control to - basically a small DAG of allowed transitions.
summary: Each Swarm agent declares a handoffs list naming the other agents it is allowed to transfer control to, forming a small DAG of routes.
queries: how do you define an agent hand-off list
how to wire allowed agent transitions
agent as route web framework analogy
what fields does an Agent have in swarm

text: What travels with the hand-off. When agent A hands off to agent B, B does not start fresh. It receives a hand-off package with everything it needs to pick up cleanly. Package parts: (1) Full Conversation History - all turns since session start; (2) Working Memory Snapshot - current scratchpad state at hand-off time; (3) Hand-Off Reason - why the sender decided to hand off (e.g. 'Classified As Billing'). The receiving agent reads the entire package before its first reasoning step. No re-litigation from scratch. The conversation history plus working memory plus reason is the contract.
summary: A hand-off carries full conversation history, working memory snapshot, and a hand-off reason so the receiving agent can pick up without restarting.
queries: what is passed in an agent hand-off

text:
Trace: T4 with Triage and Billing hand-off. Ticket T4 = 'Cancel my subscription + refund last invoice.' Three agents, three hand-offs, one ticket.
Step 1 - Triage Agent: Receives T4 ('Cancel Subscription + Refund Last Invoice').
Step 2 - Triage Agent: Classifies as billing. Returns hand-off to billing_agent. Loop switches.
Step 3 - Billing Agent: Receives full conversation history. Processes cancel.
Step 4 - Billing Agent: Refund > $200; business_rule error. Returns hand-off to escalation_agent.
Step 5 - Escalation Agent: Receives full trace. Calls escalate_human with cancel-and-refund context.
Each agent did its specialty. The hand-off package carried full context so escalation_agent did not need to ask 'what happened before'.

text: Ring when all agents are peers. Hand-off topology is a ring (or general graph) - any agent can transfer to any other, subject to the handoffs list. Different from tree topology, which has fixed parent-child relationships. Tree topology fits when domains are nested (billing has refund/invoice as sub-areas). Ring topology fits when domains are equal peers (triage, billing, troubleshooting, escalation are at the same level, no nesting). Diagram shows four peer agents (Triage, Billing, Escalation, Troubleshooting) in a ring with bidirectional edges plus one diagonal cross-edge.
summary: Hand-off topology is a peer-to-peer ring or graph fitting equal-level domains, contrasted with tree topology for nested domains.

text: Chapter 13.34 covers Critic / Debate / Reflection-as-Multi-Agent patterns. A critic is a separate agent that reviews a worker agent's draft - same idea as self-reflection (13.22) but with isolation since the critic does not share working memory. The critic-revise loop is bounded: worker drafts, critic scores 0-10 with reasoning, if below threshold (e.g. 7) the worker revises, critic re-scores, loop until score is high enough or retry cap is hit (avoids cost runaway from 13.35). Debate is a 3-agent variant: Agent Pro argues for, Agent Con argues against, Judge agent reads both and picks. Concrete example: a refund agent drafts $180 refund, policy critic catches invoice is >30 days old, refund revised down to $120 (60% policy). Costs: critic adds +1 LLM call per draft, debate adds +3 LLM calls per round. Use critic for high-stakes mutations (refunds, escalations, contract changes), debate for contested decisions with multiple readings, skip both for simple lookups/classifications. Rule of thumb: if wrong answer is worth less than extra LLM cost, skip; if wrong answer costs $200 in refund overage, a $0.01 critic call is trivial.
summary: Overview of critic, critic-revise loops, and debate as multi-agent reflection patterns with costs and when to use each.

text: A Second Agent Checks The First. Critic is reflection-as-multi-agent: instead of one agent self-critiquing (13.22), a separate critic agent reviews the worker's draft. Same idea, more isolation - the critic does not share working memory with the worker. The diagram shows a Worker Agent on the left producing a draft answer for the customer, with the draft flowing right into a Critic Agent that reads draft plus question and scores or rewrites. The critic has a different system prompt - it is told to be SKEPTICAL of the worker's draft. The separation makes it less likely the model just rubber-stamps its own earlier output.
summary: Introduces critic as a separate agent (not self-reflection) that reviews a worker's draft with a skeptical system prompt.

text: Loop: Draft - Critique - Revise. The critic-revise loop is bounded. Worker drafts; critic scores 0-10 with reasoning; if below threshold, worker revises with critic's feedback; critic re-scores. Loop until score is high enough OR the retry cap is hit. Four stations in a circle: Worker Drafts, Critic Scores 0-10, If < 7 Revise, Critic Re-Scores, then back to worker. The retry cap matters. Without it, a worker that can't please the critic loops forever - one of the cost-runaway failure modes covered in 13.35.
summary: Explains the bounded draft-critique-revise loop with 0-10 scoring, revise threshold, and mandatory retry cap.
queries: how does critic revise loop work
how to score critic output 0 to 10
why need retry cap on critic loop

text: Two Agents Argue, Judge Decides. Debate is a 3-agent variant. Agent Pro argues for a position; Agent Con argues against; Judge agent reads both arguments and the original question and picks. Useful when the answer is contested or high-stakes enough to be worth two views. Debate triples the LLM bill vs single-agent. Use it sparingly - high-stakes contested decisions where you genuinely want two voices, not noise-reduction on a routine task.
summary: Defines the 3-agent debate pattern (Pro, Con, Judge) and warns it triples LLM cost so use only for contested high-stakes decisions.
queries: when to use debate multi agent
how many LLM calls per debate
agent arguing for and against position
debate vs critic when to choose

text: Policy Critic For The Refund Agent. Concrete example: a refund agent makes a draft decision; a policy critic catches that the invoice is >30 days old; the refund gets revised down. STEP 1 - CUSTOMER: Requests refund for invoice #INV-9924, $180. STEP 2 - REFUND AGENT: "Approved, processing $180 refund now." STEP 3 - POLICY CRITIC: "Hold. Check if invoice was paid >30 days ago. If yes, partial refund only (60% policy for >30 days)." STEP 4 - REFUND AGENT (REVISED): "Confirmed >30 days. Approved for $120 (60% policy)." The critic caught $60 the worker would have over-refunded. Multiplied across thousands of refunds per month, the policy critic pays for its own LLM bill many times over.
summary: Worked example: a policy critic catches a refund agent over-refunding $60 by flagging the >30-day partial-refund policy.
queries: step by step critic trace refund
how much money does critic save

text: Critic Adds Cost; Pick Battles. Critic and debate are not free. Each extra agent doubles or triples the LLM bill and adds latency. Pick where they earn their cost, skip where they do not. USE CRITIC: high-stakes mutations (refunds, escalations, contract changes), +1 LLM call per draft. USE DEBATE: contested decisions with multiple readings (policy edge cases, customer-vs-business tension), +3 LLM calls per round. SKIP BOTH: simple low-stakes tasks (lookups, classifications, factual recall), no overhead. Rule of thumb: if a wrong answer is worth less than the extra LLM cost, skip the critic. If a wrong answer can cost $200 in refund overage, paying $0.01 for a critic call is the easiest trade you will ever make.
summary: Decision framework comparing critic, debate, and skip-both based on stakes, examples, and LLM call overhead.
queries: when to use critic vs debate vs neither
rule of thumb critic worth it
