text: Chapter 13.48 LangGraph: LangGraph models an agent as a state machine on a directed graph - nodes are steps, edges are transitions, and a shared state object threads through every node. Three primitives: add_node, add_edge (optionally with a condition), and a state schema. Conditional edges replace switch statements by routing based on fields like state.intent. State is checkpointed at every node boundary so async, long-running, or human-in-the-loop tool calls can pause and resume without re-running earlier nodes or rebuilding context. Use LangGraph when the agent is naturally a graph, state must survive across LLM/tool calls, and you want visualizable, debuggable flow control; skip it for one-shot tool use. Tradeoff: small abstraction tax for predictable, checkpointable, debuggable runs.
summary: Chapter overview: LangGraph builds agents as stateful directed graphs with add_node/add_edge/state primitives, conditional routing, automatic checkpointing for async and human-in-the-loop work, and fits multi-step agents at the cost of a small abstraction tax.
queries: how LangGraph differs from raw API calls
LangGraph nodes edges checkpoints conditional summary

text: LangGraph treats an agent as a state machine on top of a graph. Each node is a step (classify, lookup, refund, respond). Each edge is a transition. A shared state object threads through the graph and every node reads and writes it. For a customer-support agent the shared state object holds { ticket, customer, intent, lookup_result, refund_id, resolution }. The graph is the program. The state is the variables. The runtime walks edges for you. That is the LangGraph mental model.
summary: LangGraph models an agent as a directed graph of step nodes connected by edges, with a shared state object threaded through every node.
queries: how does LangGraph model an agent
what is shared state in LangGraph
what does each node do in LangGraph
how does state flow in LangGraph
graph as a program agent framework

text: LangGraph exposes three primitives: add_node registers a step function, add_edge wires steps together (optionally with a condition), and a state schema declares what every node can read and write. Pattern: graph.add_node("classify", classify_fn); graph.add_node("billing_handler", billing_fn); graph.add_node("respond", respond_fn); graph.add_edge("classify", "billing_handler", condition=is_billing); graph.add_edge("billing_handler", "respond"); state = { ticket: ..., customer: ..., resolution: ... }. add_node registers a Python function. add_edge wires it. The condition argument on the edge picks the next node from state. The state object is the only thing passed between nodes.
summary: LangGraph's three primitives are add_node to register step functions, add_edge to wire transitions with optional conditions, and a state schema declaring shared fields.
queries: how to define LangGraph state schema
what is the LangGraph code shape
how do you wire nodes in LangGraph
what argument routes between nodes LangGraph
what is passed between LangGraph nodes

text: Conditional edges are LangGraph's routing primitive. After classify writes state.intent, conditional edges fork into billing_handler, troubleshooting_handler, or escalation_handler. The router function reads state.intent and returns the next node name. Billing_Handler uses refund and invoice tools. Troubleshooting_Handler uses knowledge-base tools. Escalation_Handler hands off to a human. The conditional edge replaces a switch statement. Routing logic lives on the edge, not buried inside the next node, so the graph stays readable.
summary: Conditional edges in LangGraph branch from a classifier node into intent-specific handler nodes based on state, replacing buried switch statements with explicit graph routing.
queries: what are conditional edges in LangGraph
how does routing work in LangGraph
how to route based on state intent
where does routing logic live LangGraph
how to keep graph readable LangGraph

text: LangGraph checkpoints state at every node boundary by default. If a tool call is long-running, async, or needs human approval, the graph pauses, persists state, and resumes from the checkpoint when the result arrives. No re-running earlier nodes. No rebuilt context. Sequence: Step 1 agent runs three nodes and hits a long-running tool (async approval request) with working memory built up. Step 2 checkpoint state, persist to store, graph pauses, wait for result. Step 3 tool result arrives hours or days later, graph resumes from the checkpoint. Without checkpoints, async tool calls force you to either block the agent (kills throughput) or rebuild the conversation from scratch (loses context). Checkpointing makes long-running flows feel like one continuous run, crucial for async, long-running, and human-in-the-loop workflows.
summary: LangGraph checkpoints state at every node boundary so async, long-running, or human-in-the-loop tool calls can pause and resume without re-running earlier work or rebuilding context.
queries: what is a checkpoint in LangGraph
human in the loop with LangGraph
how to pause an agent and resume
agent pause and resume from store

text: LangGraph fits when your agent looks like a directed graph and state is the star of the show. Use it when: you can describe the flow as a directed graph (nodes are steps, edges are transitions, state is the payload); state needs to survive across LLM / tool calls (working memory belongs in one object that threads through every node); you want visualizable, debuggable flow control (the graph renders, you can point at the failing edge); you can pay a small abstraction tax for structure (a few extra lines of setup in exchange for predictable runs). Avoid LangGraph when: simple one-shot tool use, a single classify-and-respond call - just call the API directly without graph machinery. The cost is structure tax: extra node and edge setup. The payoff is visualizable, checkpointable, debuggable agent flows. Worth it when the agent has more than two steps.
summary: Pick LangGraph when the agent maps to a directed graph with persistent state needing visual debuggable flow control, and skip it for simple one-shot tool calls.
queries: should I use LangGraph for my agent
when is graph framework worth it

text: CrewAI and AutoGen are two multi-agent frameworks built on different abstractions. CrewAI is role-based: each Agent has a role, goal, backstory, and tools, and a Crew object delegates tasks to the best-fit agent. AutoGen is conversational: AssistantAgents and a UserProxyAgent talk in a GroupChat, with a GroupChatManager choosing the next speaker. A ticket T4 refund example runs cleanly through both - CrewAI hands a task from triage to billing; AutoGen routes messages through a moderator. Pick CrewAI when you can list distinct roles with clear goals, pick AutoGen when agents need free-form conversation like debate or review, and skip both when one agent plus tools is enough. Framework choice matters less than agent design quality.
summary: Chapter overview of CrewAI vs AutoGen abstractions, code shapes, traces, and a decision rule for which to use.
queries: which multi-agent framework should I use
when to use multi-agent at all
team of specialists vs group chat

text: CrewAI and AutoGen tackle the same problem (multiple agents working together) with two different abstractions. CrewAI is role-based: each agent has a role, goal, backstory, and tools. AutoGen is conversational: agents talk to each other through a manager that routes messages. CrewAI primitive is Role-Based with fields Role + Goal + Backstory + Tools; runtime is a Crew running tasks against agents; feel is job descriptions then hire. AutoGen primitive is Conversational with fields System Message + Speaker/Listener; runtime is a Manager routing messages between agents; feel is group chat with a moderator. Same problem, two different abstractions. The pick depends on whether your mental model is team of specialists or group chat with a moderator.
summary: Introduces CrewAI (role-based) and AutoGen (conversational) as two abstractions for multi-agent systems.
queries: what fields does a CrewAI agent have

text:
A CrewAI agent looks like a job description. Role names the position. Goal states the outcome. Tools is the kit. The Crew object ties agents to tasks and runs them. The runtime tries to match each task to the best-fit agent. Example code:

triage_agent = Agent(
  role="Support Triage Specialist",
  goal="Classify the ticket and route to the right specialist.",
  tools=[classify_intent]
)
billing_agent = Agent(
  role="Billing Specialist",
  goal="Resolve billing-related requests including refunds and invoices.",
  tools=[lookup_customer, lookup_subscription, process_refund, escalate_human]
)
crew = Crew(agents=[triage_agent, billing_agent], tasks=[handle_ticket_task])

The role and goal fields are not cosmetic. The runtime feeds them into the agent's system prompt and uses them to pick which agent to delegate a task to. Backstory is optional flavor that shapes voice.
summary: Shows the CrewAI Agent/Crew code shape with role, goal, tools, and how the runtime uses role/goal for delegation.
queries: how to define a CrewAI agent
what does the role field do in CrewAI
what does the goal field do in CrewAI
how CrewAI picks an agent for a task

text:
AutoGen models agents as participants in a chat room. Each AssistantAgent has a name and system_message. A UserProxyAgent stands in for the human or upstream system. A GroupChatManager moderates: it sees every message and picks who speaks next. Example code:

triage = AssistantAgent(
  name="triage",
  system_message="You classify support tickets and tag the right handler."
)
billing = AssistantAgent(
  name="billing",
  system_message="You handle billing requests: refunds, invoices, subscriptions."
)
user_proxy = UserProxyAgent(name="user_proxy")

manager = GroupChatManager(
  groupchat=GroupChat(agents=[triage, billing, user_proxy])
)

The manager is the orchestration core. It reads message context to choose the next speaker. This makes back-and-forth conversation natural (debate, review, brainstorm) but adds a routing layer you have to tune.
summary: Shows the AutoGen AssistantAgent / UserProxyAgent / GroupChatManager code shape and how the manager routes messages.
queries: how to define an AutoGen agent
how does AutoGen pick the next speaker

text:
Ticket T4 (cancel and refund) runs through both frameworks. Same outcome. Different orchestration shape. CrewAI hands tasks. AutoGen routes messages.

CrewAI Trace - Ticket T4:
Step 1: Triage Agent classifies ticket T4 -> Intent = Billing.
Step 2: Triage Agent hands the task to Billing Agent.
Step 3: Billing Agent runs lookup_customer(c-9924) -> Pro tier, in good standing.
Step 4: Billing Agent runs process_refund(INV-9924, $150).
Step 5: Billing Agent calls escalate_human because refund > auto-approve threshold.

AutoGen Trace - Ticket T4:
Step 1: user_proxy posts ticket T4 to group chat.
Step 2: Triage speaks: classify -> Intent = Billing, tag @Billing.
Step 3: Billing joins: lookup_customer(c-9924), process_refund(INV-9924, $150).
Step 4: Escalation joins: approve the refund (manager routes message).
Step 5: Triage closes: replies to user_proxy with resolution.

CrewAI feels like the triage agent passes a baton to the billing agent. AutoGen feels like they discuss it in chat with a moderator. Both arrive at the same resolution. Pick the one that matches how you describe the work in English.
summary: Compares CrewAI baton-pass vs AutoGen group-chat traces for the same refund ticket T4.
queries: how does CrewAI route a ticket
how does AutoGen route a ticket

text:
Framework choice matters less than agent design quality. A well-designed CrewAI agent beats a poorly-designed AutoGen agent every time. Pick whichever abstraction makes your team faster.

When to pick what:
Pick CrewAI when you can list distinct roles with clear goals. Example: Customer Support Engineer, Researcher, Writer, Reviewer.
Pick AutoGen when agents need free-form conversation. Example: Debate, Brainstorm, Multi-Reviewer Code Review.
Skip both when you have one agent + tools (no second agent needed). Example: Most customer-support bots live here.

Pick the abstraction your team can reason about fastest and ship a working agent before debating the perfect runtime.
summary: Decision rule: pick CrewAI for clear roles, AutoGen for free-form conversation, skip both for single-agent + tools.
queries: when not to use a multi-agent framework
do I need multi-agent at all

text: Chapter 13.50 compares two vendor-native agent SDKs: Claude Agent SDK (loop primitive, built around the Claude API) and OpenAI Agents (hand-off primitive, successor to Swarm, built around OpenAI tool-calling). Claude SDK fits single agents with deep tool use; OpenAI Agents fits multi-role workflows with routing. Both ship native vendor features (prompt caching, MCP, Assistants API, built-in function tools) but carry high lock-in and low portability. Use them when committed to one vendor for 12-18+ months and the abstractions match your agent shape; avoid for multi-vendor strategies or when you need LangGraph-style checkpointing.
summary: Vendor-native SDKs (Claude Agent SDK loop, OpenAI Agents hand-off) trade portability for native vendor features.

text: When the model vendor ships the framework. Vendor-native SDKs are tightly integrated with one model provider. Claude Agent SDK is built around the Claude API and exposes a loop primitive. OpenAI Agents (the successor to Swarm) is built around OpenAI tool-calling and exposes a hand-off primitive. Both ship native support for that vendor's other features. Claude Agent SDK: accent cyan, Loop Primitive, API: Run_Agent_Loop With System Prompt + Tools, Native: Prompt Cache, Structured Output, MCP, Fits: Single Agent Doing Deep Tool Use. OpenAI Agents: accent purple, Hand-Off Primitive, API: Agent.Handoffs = [Other_Agent, ...], Native: Assistants API, Built-In Function Tools, Fits: Multi-Role Workflows With Routing. Both vendors ship the abstractions they think their model is best at. Claude invests in long iterative loops with deep tool use. OpenAI invests in multi-agent hand-off chains. Pick the one whose abstractions match your agent shape.
summary: Introduces the two vendor SDKs and contrasts their primitives, native features, and ideal use cases.
queries: What is a vendor-native agent SDK?
Why does Claude SDK use a loop?
Why does OpenAI Agents use hand-offs?
Native features of Claude Agent SDK
Single agent deep tool use framework
What does prompt cache mean in Claude SDK?

text:
Loop Primitive. The Claude Agent SDK exposes AgentLoop: a configured loop that takes a system prompt, a tool inventory, an iteration cap, and an on-message callback. Inside the loop, the SDK runs reason-act-observe for you and returns the final message once the model stops asking for tools. Framework Pattern (Shape) - Claude Agent SDK:

loop = AgentLoop(
  system_prompt=SYSTEM,
  tools=[lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human],
  max_iterations=10,
  on_message=log_message
)
result = loop.run(user_message="I want a refund for INV-9924")

The loop is the abstraction. You hand the SDK the model, tools, and a cap, then call run. Reason-act-observe lives inside the SDK. You never write the while loop yourself.
summary: Shows AgentLoop code shape: configure system prompt, tools, iteration cap, then call run; SDK hides reason-act-observe.
queries: How does AgentLoop work in Claude Agent SDK?
Does Claude SDK hide the while loop?
Tool inventory parameter Claude Agent SDK
How to call loop.run in Claude SDK?
Where does the agent loop live?

text:
Hand-Off Primitive. OpenAI Agents (the successor to Swarm) exposes hand-offs. Each agent declares which other agents it can hand off to. The Runner walks the chain: each agent either resolves the request or returns the next agent. The chain terminates when an agent returns a final response. Framework Pattern (Shape) - OpenAI Agents:

triage = Agent(
  name="triage",
  instructions="Classify and route.",
  handoffs=[billing_agent, troubleshooting_agent]
)
result = Runner.run_sync(triage, "Refund my last invoice")

The handoffs field is the routing graph. The Runner reads each agent's response to decide if it is a final answer or a hand-off to the next agent. No loop on your side. Just declare who can pass the baton to whom.
summary: Shows OpenAI Agents hand-off code: each Agent declares handoffs list, Runner walks the chain until final response.
queries: How do OpenAI Agents handoffs work?
What is the Runner in OpenAI Agents?
How do agents pass the baton?

text: Two Primitives, Two Mental Models. Loop versus hand-off is the headline difference. Lock-in and portability are the production trade-offs you carry forever. Moving to another vendor means rewriting the agent harness, not just swapping a model. Comparison table: Core Primitive - Claude: Loop (Single Agent, Multi-Iteration); OpenAI: Hand-Off (Multi-Agent Chain). Best Fit - Claude: Stand-Alone Agents With Deep Tool Use; OpenAI: Multi-Role Workflows With Routing. Lock-In - Claude: High - Bound To Claude API + Abstractions; OpenAI: High - Bound To OpenAI API + Abstractions. Portability - Claude: Low - Switching Vendor Means Rewriting The Loop; OpenAI: Low - Switching Vendor Means Rewriting The Hand-Off Chain. High lock-in is not automatic disqualification. If you are sure about the vendor for the next 18 months, lock-in is the price of native features. If you are not sure, the price is rewriting the agent harness later.
summary: Compares Claude SDK vs OpenAI Agents across primitive, fit, lock-in, and portability; both high lock-in, low portability.

text: Use The Vendor SDK When... Vendor SDKs are the right pick when the vendor relationship is long-term and its abstractions fit your shape. Avoid them when you need multi-vendor support or features the SDK does not ship (LangGraph-style checkpointing, for example). Fit signals: (1) Committed To One Vendor Long-Term - You picked Claude or OpenAI and are not switching for 12+ months. (2) Want The Vendor's Latest Features Day One - Prompt caching, new tool-calling modes, built-in memory land faster here. (3) Vendor Abstractions Match Your Agent Shape - Single-agent loop -> Claude SDK. Multi-role hand-off -> OpenAI Agents. Avoid Vendor SDK When: A multi-vendor strategy is required, you anticipate switching models, or you need framework features the vendor does not ship (e.g. LangGraph-style checkpointing across long-running calls). Lock-in is a deliberate trade. You buy speed and native features. You pay in portability. Make the trade once, eyes open, before you have 50 agents in production.
summary: Decision rules: pick vendor SDK for long-term commitment, day-one features, matching abstractions; avoid for multi-vendor or missing features like checkpointing.

NO - USE FRAMEWORK: 'One-Off Prototype, Ship This Week' (ship fast, you can rebuild later if it sticks), 'Team Of Fewer Than 3 Engineers' (you cannot maintain a custom harness AND ship features). text: Chapter 13.51 Custom / No-Framework. Three reasons to go custom: control, cost (50-200ms framework overhead per iteration), and avoiding vendor lock-in. The minimum-viable agent loop is ~11 lines of Python: history list, llm.call with tools, dispatch tool calls, terminate on no-tool response or max_iter. Going custom means you own every framework feature: observability, retries with exponential backoff, and eval harness are must-build; checkpointing and multi-agent hand-offs can be skipped initially. Decision tree: go custom for >10K/sec traffic, P95 <2s, or multi-vendor strategy; use framework for one-off prototypes or teams under 3 engineers. Most production teams end up hybrid - buy scaffolding/schema/retries from a framework, build custom LLM adapter, observability integration, and domain-specific tool gating.
summary: Chapter-level overview of when and how to build a custom agent without a framework, including the loop code, must-build pieces, decision signals, and the hybrid pattern most teams use.
queries: when to skip an agent framework

text: Three reasons to roll your own agent instead of using a framework. (1) Control: you want exact control of every behavior - no framework magic. Frameworks hide decisions: when to retry, when to truncate, what to cache. (2) Cost: frameworks add 50-200ms overhead per iteration. For high-volume traffic, that is millions of dollars per year at scale. (3) Avoid vendor lock-in: you anticipate moving across LLM providers. A custom LLM adapter lets you swap Claude for OpenAI for Llama without rewrites. Custom is not a default - it's what you reach for when one of these three reasons applies and you can afford the engineering cost. Otherwise pick a framework and ship.
summary: Three production trade-offs justify a custom no-framework agent: control, cost overhead, and vendor lock-in avoidance.

text: Missing pieces you now own when you go custom. Frameworks ship more than the loop - every framework feature becomes your build list. Must build: (1) Observability (spans, traces, metrics) - without traces you cannot debug production failures. (2) Retries with exponential backoff per tool error class - transient network errors are real, permanent business-rule errors are not retryable. (3) Eval harness integration - without evals you ship regressions (see 13.39 LLM-as-Judge and 13.40 Trace Evals). Can skip: (1) Checkpointing for long-running / human-in-the-loop - skip if every run is sub-second, build only if you need async resumes. (2) Hand-off between agents - skip if you have one agent, build only for multi-agent workflows. Must-build is the floor for production. Can-skip is the ceiling deferable until traffic justifies. Misjudge the floor and you ship a fragile agent that breaks the first time a flaky tool surfaces.
summary: Lists framework features you must rebuild (observability, retries, evals) versus ones you can defer (checkpointing, hand-offs) when going custom.

text: Decision tree for staying custom - five signals. Three say YES go custom: (1) High-volume traffic > 10K/sec - framework overhead becomes the bottleneck. (2) Tight latency budget P95 < 2s - strip every wrapper, inline hot paths. (3) Multi-vendor strategy required - vendor-agnostic adapter saves the migration later. Two say NO use framework: (1) One-off prototype, ship this week - ship fast, you can rebuild later if it sticks. (2) Team of fewer than 3 engineers - you cannot maintain a custom harness AND ship features. When signals conflict, default to a framework and optimize only when you can prove framework overhead is the bottleneck. Prototypes and small teams = framework. Production scale and vendor portability = custom.

text: Designing a production agent is nine decisions, top to bottom. Strategy at the top (what approach, what loop). Operations at the bottom (which framework, which observability stack). The full decision stack: 1. Approach (Prompt / Tune / RAG / Agent, chapter 13.5). 2. Loop Pattern (Workflow / ReAct / Plan-Execute / Reflection, 13.18-13.22). 3. Memory Layers (Working + Which Long-Term, 13.24-13.29). 4. Multi-Agent (Single / Orchestrator / Supervisor / Hand-Off, 13.30-13.34). 5. Tools (Which Tools, What Scope, 13.7-13.11). 6. Protocols (MCP / A2A / Bespoke, 13.12-13.17). 7. Eval (Dimensions + Judge + Traces, 13.37-13.41). 8. Production (Observability + Cost + Guardrails + Injection + Security, 13.42-13.47). 9. Framework (LangGraph / CrewAI / Vendor SDK / Custom, 13.48-13.51). Any agent that ships needs a concrete pick at every layer with a reason traceable to the chapter that taught it.

text: Top three layers for the IT agent. Layer 1 Approach: pick Agent. Why: mutating actions (password reset, hardware order) need tool calls plus a loop; pure prompting or RAG cannot take actions. Layer 2 Loop Pattern: pick Workflow With An Agent Step. Why: most tickets are deterministic (classify, route, handle); the handler itself is an agent loop with tool use; pure ReAct is overkill. Layer 3 Memory Layers: pick Working + Episodic + Semantic. Why: Working = current ticket; Episodic = past employee tickets (90-day window); Semantic = employee profile (department, hardware history, software stack).
summary: Top three layers: Agent approach, workflow-with-agent-step loop, and all three memory layers (working, episodic, semantic).

text: Layers 4 and 5 for the IT agent. Layer 4 Multi-Agent: pick Orchestrator-Worker. Why: triage classifier routes to specialist handlers (password / software / hardware); hand-off when a handler needs another specialist's tools. Layer 5 Tools + Capability Scope: pick 8 internal tools, scoped per handler. Why: password handler gets reset_password and audit tools; hardware handler gets order_hardware bounded by $500 cap; capability scope is per-role. Orchestrator-Worker fits because routes are well-defined and bounded. Capability scope per handler keeps the password agent from ever calling order_hardware - bounded blast radius even under jailbreak attempts.
summary: Pick Orchestrator-Worker multi-agent shape with 8 internal tools scoped per handler for bounded blast radius.
queries: orchestrator worker pattern for IT agent

text: Layers 6 and 7 for the IT agent. Layer 6 Protocols: pick MCP for SSO + Ticketing, no A2A. Why: MCP servers wrap SSO and the ticketing system; the IT team can swap backends without touching the agent; no A2A because this is a single-team agent, not a cross-org mesh. Layer 7 Eval Strategy: pick 4-axis eval + LLM-as-judge + trace evals. Why: eval axes are Correctness, Latency, Cost, Safety; LLM-as-judge on resolutions; trace evals on every refund or escalation step; eval set: 50 golden + 20 adversarial + growing regression set. MCP gives clean separation between agent and IT-system internals; eval set sizes are small enough to ship this quarter and growable as production traffic exposes new failure modes.
summary: Use MCP (not A2A) for SSO/ticketing integration and a 4-axis eval with 50 golden + 20 adversarial cases.

text: Layers 8 and 9 for the IT agent. Layer 8 Production Hardening: pick OTel + LangSmith + prompt cache + guardrails + injection defense. Why: OTel + LangSmith for observability; prompt cache on the long system prompt; latency target P95 < 5s; hardware-order guardrail enforces the $500 cap; input layer runs prompt-injection defense per 13.46. Layer 9 Framework: pick LangGraph. Why: the workflow-with-agent-step shape maps cleanly to LangGraph nodes and edges; conditional edges handle routing; checkpoints cover async manager-approval steps for hardware orders. LangGraph wins because the graph maps to classify node, route node, handler node, escalation node, respond node.
summary: Production stack: OTel+LangSmith+prompt cache+guardrails+injection defense; LangGraph as the framework with checkpoints for async approvals.

text: Section 13 walked from the anatomy of a single LLM call to a complete production agent decision framework. The three concrete capabilities you now have: Decide - every layer above has a concrete decision in front of you, not a fog. Diagnose - when the agent fails in production, you know which layer to investigate. Defend - in a design review, you argue your choices from mechanics, not feel. Every layer traces back to a chapter you worked through. The decision stack is the map. The chapters are the terrain.
summary: Closing capabilities: Decide, Diagnose, Defend - layer-by-layer agent design lets you ship, troubleshoot, and defend choices.
